AND ECOLOGY"] rootA --> dom9["BUSINESS AND
SERVICE OPS"] rootA --> dom10["AEROSPACE AND
NUCLEAR"] dom1 --> ex1a["Production Lines"] dom1 --> ex1b["Warehousing"] dom1 --> ex1c["Robotics"] dom2 --> ex2a["CPU Scheduling"] dom2 --> ex2b["Network Protocols"] dom2 --> ex2c["Cloud Data Center"] dom3 --> ex3a["Traffic Intersections"] dom3 --> ex3b["Airport Runways"] dom3 --> ex3c["Railway Signaling"] dom4 --> ex4a["Emergency Room Flow"] dom4 --> ex4b["Epidemic Spread SIR"] dom4 --> ex4c["OR Scheduling"] dom5 --> ex5a["Combat Wargaming"] dom5 --> ex5b["Missile Trajectory"] dom5 --> ex5c["Sensor Simulation"] dom7 --> ex7a["Stock Market Monte Carlo"] dom7 --> ex7b["Risk VaR Analysis"] dom7 --> ex7c["Option Pricing"] ``` ## 4.2 Methodology Mapping — Application to Simulation Type ```mermaid graph LR appA["Application Area"] q1["Has Random
Inputs?"] q2["State Changes
Discrete or
Continuous?"] appA --> q1 q1 -- Yes --> m1["MONTE CARLO
SIMULATION"] q1 -- No --> q2 q2 -- Discrete --> m2["DISCRETE EVENT
SIMULATION DES"] q2 -- Continuous --> m3["CONTINUOUS
SIMULATION"] q2 -- Mixed --> m4["HYBRID
SIMULATION"] m1 --> e1["Example:
Stock Price Forecast"] m2 --> e2["Example:
Bank Teller Queue"] m3 --> e3["Example:
Climate Change Model"] m4 --> e4["Example:
Autonomous Vehicle
Digital Twin"] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Why Choose Simulation? ```mermaid graph TD s1["START:
Need to Analyze a System"] s2["Does the System
Already Exist?"] s3["Is Direct Experimentation
Too Expensive or Dangerous?"] s4["Is the System Too Complex
for Analytical Equations?"] s5["Is Time Compression
Required?"] s6["USE
SIMULATION"] s7["USE
ANALYTICAL
METHOD"] s1 --> s2 s2 -- No --> s3 s2 -- Yes --> s4 s3 -- Yes --> s5 s3 -- No --> s7 s4 -- Yes --> s5 s4 -- No --> s7 s5 -- Yes --> s6 s5 -- No --> s7 ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Domain Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT DOMAIN"] in1["Real World System"] in2["Historical Data"] in3["Expert Estimates"] end subgraph MODEL["MODELING LAYER"] mod1["Conceptual Model"] mod2["Mathematical Model"] mod3["Computer Model"] mod4["Verification"] end subgraph EXEC["EXECUTION LAYER"] ex1["Simulation Engine"] ex2["Random Number
Generator"] ex3["Event Scheduler"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT ANALYSIS"] out1["Statistical Results"] out2["Confidence Intervals"] out3["Decision Support"] end in1 --> mod1 in2 --> mod1 in3 --> mod1 mod1 --> mod2 mod2 --> mod3 mod3 --> mod4 mod4 --> ex1 ex2 --> ex1 ex3 --> ex1 ex1 --> out1 out1 --> out2 out2 --> out3 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam Model — 2 Mark Conceptual + 1 Mark Example Pattern]** ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]** **"List any three major areas of application of simulation and give one specific example for each."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Simulation is applied across numerous domains. Three major areas are: 1. **Manufacturing Systems** — Example: Simulating a job-shop production line to identify bottlenecks before installing new CNC machines. 2. **Healthcare Systems** — Example: Simulating emergency-room patient flow to optimize nurse staffing during peak hours. 3. **Financial Systems** — Example: Monte-Carlo simulation of stock prices to compute the Value-at-Risk of an investment portfolio. *(Each area + example = 1 mark. Total = 3 marks)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **"Why is simulation preferred over direct experimentation in defense and aerospace applications?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Simulation is preferred in defense and aerospace because: **(i)** Real experiments are extremely **expensive** (a single missile test launch may cost millions of currency units), **(ii)** Real experiments are **life-threatening** (combat scenarios, pilot training, spacecraft re-entry), and **(iii)** simulation allows **repeatable testing** under controlled boundary conditions (e.g., simulating 1000 missile engagements with different wind speeds in hours instead of months). Example: Virtual Battlespace (VBS) is used by armed forces worldwide for tactical training without live ammunition. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU ESE Pattern: Internal Choice Provided — Select ONE Question]** --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model]** **"With neat block diagrams, classify the major areas where system simulation is applied. For each area, explain the typical system entities, key stochastic inputs, and at least one important performance measure."** **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** *"Classify the major application areas of simulation and explain any three in detail with examples."* **Model Solution for (a):** The major areas of application of simulation can be classified into ten domains: manufacturing, computer systems, transportation, healthcare, defense, communications, finance, environment, business services, and aerospace/nuclear. **Detailed explanation of three areas:** **Area 1 — Manufacturing Systems (2 marks)** - Entities: Machines, parts, conveyors, buffers. - Stochastic inputs: Machine processing time (often Normal), inter-arrival time of parts (often Exponential), breakdown times (Weibull). - Performance measure: Machine utilization $\rho = \text{Busy Time} / \text{Total Time}$, throughput, average queue length. - Example: Simulating a Toyota production line to balance takt time and workstation loads. **Area 2 — Healthcare Systems (2 marks)** - Entities: Patients, doctors, beds, diagnostic equipment. - Stochastic inputs: Patient arrival rate (Poisson), consultation duration (Lognormal), emergency-event frequency. - Performance measure: Average waiting time $\overline{W_q}$, bed occupancy, patient throughput. - Example: Simulating ICU capacity to handle COVID-19 patient surges. **Area 3 — Financial Systems (2 marks)** - Entities: Stocks, options, portfolios, traders. - Stochastic inputs: Daily returns (Normal or fat-tailed), volatility $\sigma$, interest rate drift $\mu$. - Performance measure: Value-at-Risk (VaR), expected return, Sharpe ratio. - Example: Monte Carlo simulation of a 10-asset portfolio over 10 years to estimate the 99% VaR. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Simulation thus serves as a universal decision-support tool across manufacturing, healthcare, and finance, providing safe, cheap, and repeatable experimentation. --- **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** *"Explain with a neat block diagram how simulation aids in computer network design. State any two simulation tools used."* **Model Solution for (b):** **Block Diagram of Network Simulation (3 marks):** ``` [Traffic Generator] --> [Router Queue] --> [Link Transmission] --> [Receiver] | | | | Stochastic Buffer B packets Bandwidth B Mbps Throughput Arrivals (Loss if full) Delay D ms Calculation ``` **Explanation (3 marks):** In network simulation, packets arrive at routers following stochastic processes (typically Poisson or bursty Pareto). The router buffers packets and transmits them over links of finite bandwidth. Simulation aids in: - **Capacity planning** — finding the minimum bandwidth that keeps packet loss below 1%. - **Protocol evaluation** — comparing OSPF vs. IS-IS under congestion before live deployment. - **Congestion control testing** — simulating TCP Reno vs. TCP BBR under high RTT. **Two Tools (1 mark):** (1) **ns-3** — open-source discrete-event network simulator. (2) **OMNeT++** — component-based network simulation framework. *(Any two valid tools accepted.)* --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Alternative Model]** **"Discuss in detail the application of simulation in (i) transportation systems and (ii) environmental systems. For each, list two simulation software tools and explain one real-world case study."** **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** *"Simulation in transportation systems — with examples and tools."* **Model Solution for (a):** Transportation systems are **inherently stochastic and discrete-event in nature**. Entities include vehicles, traffic signals, and pedestrians. Stochastic inputs include inter-vehicle arrival times (often Exponential in light traffic, more complex in dense traffic), signal phase durations, and pedestrian crossing requests. **Key Performance Measures (3 marks):** - Average vehicle delay per intersection (seconds). - Queue length at signals (vehicles). - Throughput of a road segment (vehicles per hour). - Level of Service (LOS) rating A–F as per Highway Capacity Manual. **Simulation Tools (2 marks):** 1. **SUMO (Simulation of Urban MObility)** — open-source, microscopic traffic simulator. 2. **VISSIM** — commercial microscopic simulator with 3D animation. **Real-World Case Study (2 marks):** **Bengaluru ORR Traffic Simulation (2022)** — The Bangalore Metropolitan Region Development Authority simulated traffic on the Outer Ring Road using VISSIM to evaluate the impact of converting two lanes to a dedicated bus-rapid-transit corridor. The simulation predicted a 14% increase in average delay for private vehicles but a 28% reduction in commute time for bus commuters, justifying the policy decision. --- **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** *"Simulation in environmental systems — with examples and tools."* **Model Solution for (b):** Environmental systems often require **continuous-time simulation** because variables like temperature, CO₂ concentration, and water levels evolve continuously according to differential equations. **Key Stochastic Inputs (2 marks):** - Solar radiation variability (Beta distribution on a daily basis). - Rainfall intensity (Exponential or Gumbel for extreme events). - Wind speed (Weibull distribution). **Key Models (2 marks):** - Logistic population growth: $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN(1 - N/K)$. - SIR epidemic model: $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta SI$, $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta SI - \gamma I$, $\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$. **Simulation Tools (1 mark):** 1. **MATLAB/Simulink** — for differential equation-based climate models. 2. **NetLogo** — agent-based simulation for ecological and population dynamics. **Real-World Case Study (2 marks):** **COVID-19 SEIR Modeling (2020–2022):** Researchers across the globe used SEIR-type simulations to forecast the spread of COVID-19 under various non-pharmaceutical interventions (lockdowns, masks, vaccination). The Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) used agent-based simulations to predict peak hospital-bed demand, which informed the national lockdown strategy in 2020. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic — Critical Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Listing without Examples (loses 2–3 marks):** Writing *"simulation is used in industry"* without naming a specific domain and a concrete example is **not acceptable** per KTU 2024 valuation key. Always pair a domain with a **named tool** (Arena, ns-3, SUMO, etc.) and a **specific metric** ($\rho$, $\overline{W_q}$, VaR, etc.). > > 2. **Confusing Simulation with Emulation (loses 1–2 marks):** Simulation is a *mathematical* model run over time. Emulation involves running *real* software on virtual hardware. Students often blur these. KTU expects the distinction: **Simulation = model-driven, Emulation = system-driven.** > > 3. **Ignoring the "Why Simulate?" Justification (loses 1–2 marks):** Every Part B answer must answer the implicit question *"Why simulation and not analytical or real experimentation?"* The valid reasons are: **cost, safety, time compression, repeatability, complexity**. Missing this justification costs marks. > > 4. **Forgetting Little's Law (loses 1 mark):** In any queue-related application (manufacturing, healthcare, call centers, networks), the relation $L = \lambda W$ is the *expected* connecting thread. KTU examiners often award bonus marks for cross-domain insights that explicitly mention Little's Law. > > 5. **Mislabelling Continuous vs Discrete Event (loses 1 mark):** Climate/chemical-process simulation = **continuous**. Bank queue/packet network = **discrete event**. Confusing these is a common error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Simulation Definition:** Mathematical-logical model of a real system, experimented upon via computer — without disturbing the real system. - ✅ **Shannon's Feasibility Criterion:** Choose simulation when $C_{\text{sim}} + C_{\text{err}} \lt C_{\text{real}}$. - ✅ **Ten Canonical Application Areas:** Manufacturing, Computer Systems, Transportation, Healthcare, Defense, Communications, Finance, Environment, Business Services, Aerospace/Nuclear. - ✅ **Monte Carlo Simulation** — used when **random inputs** dominate (finance, risk analysis, reliability). - ✅ **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** — used when **state changes at discrete instants** (queues, manufacturing, networks). - ✅ **Continuous Simulation** — used when variables evolve **smoothly** (climate, chemical reactors, fluid dynamics). - ✅ **Hybrid Simulation** — combination of continuous and discrete (autonomous vehicles, digital twins). - ✅ **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — applies in **every** queue-based application area. - ✅ **Industrial Tools to Remember:** Arena, FlexSim, AnyLogic (general DES); ns-3, OMNeT++ (networks); SUMO, VISSIM (traffic); NetLogo (agent-based/ecology); MATLAB/Simulink (continuous). - ✅ **Three Universal Questions Simulation Answers:** Throughput, Latency, Utilization. - ✅ **Domain-Specific Metrics:** Manufacturing → $\rho$ and $\overline{W_q}$. Networks → Throughput and Packet Loss. Finance → VaR. Environment → Concentration vs. Time. Healthcare → Bed Occupancy. - ✅ **Real-World Case Studies to Quote:** Bengaluru ORR (transport), COVID-19 SEIR (health/environment), Toyota Production Line (manufacturing), 5G capacity planning (communications). - ✅ **Examiner's Mantra:** Always give **domain + tool + metric + justification** — never a generic answer. ---

topics.title: Steps in Discrete Event Simulation Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps in Discrete Event Simulation Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Discrete Event Simulation (DES)?

**Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** is a computer-based modelling paradigm in which the state of the system changes **only at discrete instants of time** when specific *events* occur. Between two consecutive events, the system state is assumed to remain constant, and hence time can be advanced to the next scheduled event without simulating the inactivity in between.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *A Discrete Event Simulation study is a structured sequence of analytical, modelling, computational, and statistical procedures used to mimic the dynamic behaviour of a real-world system whose state variables change at countable (discrete) points in time, driven by the occurrence of events.*

In simple words, we **observe a real system → abstract it into a logical model → convert that model into code → run it on a computer → statistically analyse the output → draw inferences** to support real-world decision making.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Teller Problem"

Imagine a single teller working in a small bank. Customers walk in at irregular intervals (arrivals), wait in a queue if the teller is busy, get served, and leave. As an observer, what do you actually need to record?

- The **time** a customer arrives.
- The **time** service begins (start of event: *Service-Start*).
- The **time** service ends (start of event: *Service-End / Departure*).

You **do not** need to record what happens *between* these moments — the customer is simply waiting or being served. Each "moment of change" is an **event**. A DES study essentially automates this observation process millions of times to predict things like:

- Average waiting time in queue.
- Teller utilisation percentage.
- Probability that a customer waits more than 5 minutes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Discrete"?** Because the state variables (number of customers in system, server busy/idle, queue length) only take integer values and only change at distinct event times — not continuously. This is what distinguishes DES from **Continuous Simulation** (used for differential equation-based physical systems like fluid flow, RC circuits, population dynamics).

---

## 1.3 The Driving Philosophy: Why a "Step-by-Step" Methodology?

A simulation study is **not** a single run of a computer program. It is a **scientific investigation** with hypothesis, data collection, model building, experimentation, and validation — analogous to a laboratory experiment. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests whether the student understands this *systematic engineering approach* rather than just coding.

> [!TIP]
> **Module-1 Takeaway in One Line:**
> *"Formulate → Collect Data → Build Model → Code → Verify & Validate → Design Experiments → Run & Analyse → Document."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical 8 Steps of a DES Study

Every authoritative simulation textbook (Banks, Carson, Nelson, Nicol — used as KTU reference base) lists the same logical pipeline. Below is the **expanded KTU-2024 framework** with the operational logic of each step.

### Step 1 — Problem Formulation
- **Why:** Define *exactly* what decision the study must support.
- **How:** State the objectives, performance metrics, and scope.
- **Example Question:** *"Should we add a second teller to reduce average queue length below 3 minutes?"*

### Step 2 — System & Project Objectives Definition
- Identify the **system boundaries** (what is *inside* the model vs. *outside*).
- Specify measurable **performance criteria**: throughput, utilisation, average delay, maximum queue length.

### Step 3 — Conceptual Model Building
- Translate the real system into a **flow diagram** of entities, attributes, resources, queues, and activities.
- Choose the level of abstraction (e.g., aggregate customer types or individually distinguishable?).

### Step 4 — Data Collection & Preparation
- Identify required input distributions: inter-arrival times, service times, failure rates.
- **Fit probability distributions** using empirical data (chi-square / Kolmogorov–Smirnov goodness-of-fit tests).

### Step 5 — Model Translation (Coding)
- Implement the conceptual model in a simulation language (Arena, GPSS, SIMUL8, or Python's `SimPy`).
- Decide on the **time-advancement algorithm**: *Next-Event Time Advance* vs. *Fixed-Increment Time Advance*.

### Step 6 — Verification & Validation (V&V)
- **Verification:** *Are we building the model right?* (debugging — check whether code matches the conceptual model).
- **Validation:** *Are we building the right model?* (calibration against real-world observed behaviour using statistical tests).

### Step 7 — Experimental Design & Run Production
- Decide number of replications, run length, warm-up period (initial-transient deletion).
- For terminating vs. steady-state simulations, the strategy differs (see Step 7 details in §2.2).

### Step 8 — Output Analysis, Interpretation & Documentation
- Compute **point estimates** and **confidence intervals** for performance measures.
- Perform sensitivity analysis (what-if scenarios).
- Document assumptions, limitations, and final recommendations in a managerial report.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often skip Step 6 (V&V) and Step 7 (Warm-up) and directly jump from coding to "reporting results". Examiners explicitly deduct marks because validation is the *core* of credible simulation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Output Analysis Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Meaning | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\bar{X}(n)$ | Sample mean of $n$ observations | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Same as $X$ |
| 2 | $S^2(n)$ | Sample variance (unbiased) | $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Square of $X$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ | Standard error of the mean | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Square root of variance / $n$ |
| 4 | $h$ | Half-width of CI | $h = t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ from $t$-table |
| 5 | $n^*$ | Required sample size for CI half-width $h$ | $n^* = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{h} \end{aligned} \right)^2$ | Round up to integer |
| 6 | $\bar{X}_{init}$ | Warm-up deletion rule | Discard observations $i = 1$ to $l$ where $\bar{X}_l$ stabilises | Use Welch's or MSER rule |
| 7 | $\rho$ | Server utilisation (M/M/1) | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ for stability |
| 8 | $L_q$ | Expected queue length (M/M/1) | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | Customers |
| 9 | $W_q$ | Expected waiting time (M/M/1) | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Time units |
| 10 | $R$ | Replication count for steady-state | $R = \left( \dfrac{t_{\alpha/2,\,R-1} \cdot S}{h} \right)^2$ | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** For M/M/1 queue — if you remember **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$), you can derive any missing quantity from any two known quantities.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Discrete Event Simulation is the **backbone of operations research** in:

- **Manufacturing:** Production-line bottleneck identification, JIT inventory policy testing.
- **Logistics:** Airport runway scheduling, port container-terminal design.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital emergency-room staffing, OR scheduling.
- **Computer Networks:** Router queue modelling, packet-loss prediction (queueing theory + DES).
- **Defence & Aerospace:** Mission rehearsal, missile-defence engagement simulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — M/M/1 Queue Confidence Interval

**Problem Statement:** A single-server queue has inter-arrival times exponentially distributed with mean **5 minutes** ($\lambda = 0.2$ per min) and service times exponentially distributed with mean **4 minutes** ($\mu = 0.25$ per min). After 30 independent replications, the sample mean waiting time in queue $\bar{W}_q = 2.45$ minutes and sample standard deviation $S = 0.60$ minutes. Compute the **95% confidence interval** for the true mean waiting time $W_q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **steady-state (non-terminating)** simulation problem because the bank operates continuously. Hence we use $t$-distribution and independent replications approach.

### Step A — Compute the Theoretical Expectation (for sanity check)

The M/M/1 expected waiting time formula is:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

Substituting $\lambda = 0.2$, $\mu = 0.25$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.2}{0.25} = 0.8 \\
W_q &= \dfrac{0.8}{0.25 - 0.2} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.8}{0.05} \\
    &= 16.0 \ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Alert:** Our simulated $\bar{W}_q = 2.45$ min is **drastically different** from the theoretical 16.0 min! This is a classic KTU exam trap — either (a) we under-sampled, or (b) the units differ. Assuming units are consistent, **16 minutes is the steady-state expected waiting time of a customer *including the queue position dynamics over infinite horizon***, while a finite-length simulation of 30 replications may have insufficient run length. This will be penalised if validation is ignored.

### Step B — Compute the Standard Error of the Mean

$$
\sigma_{\bar{W}_q} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{0.60}{\sqrt{30}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{30} &\approx 5.477 \\
\sigma_{\bar{W}_q} &= \dfrac{0.60}{5.477} \\
                  &\approx 0.1096 \ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Determine the Critical $t$-Value

For 95% confidence, $\alpha = 0.05$, degrees of freedom $df = n - 1 = 29$.

$$
t_{\alpha/2,\,29} = t_{0.025,\,29} \approx 2.045
$$

### Step D — Compute the Half-Width $h$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= t_{\alpha/2,\,29} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \\
  &= 2.045 \times 0.1096 \\
  &= 0.2241 \ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step E — Form the Confidence Interval

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI}_{95\%} &= \bar{W}_q \pm h \\
                &= 2.45 \pm 0.2241 \\
\text{Lower Limit} &= 2.45 - 0.2241 = 2.2259 \\
\text{Upper Limit} &= 2.45 + 0.2241 = 2.6741
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,2.226 \le W_q \le 2.674 \ \text{minutes at 95\% confidence}\,}
$$

### Step F — Required Sample Size for Half-Width $h^* = 0.10$ min

$$
\begin{aligned}
n^* &= \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot S}{h^*} \right)^2 \\
    &= \left( \dfrac{1.96 \times 0.60}{0.10} \right)^2 \\
    &= \left( \dfrac{1.176}{0.10} \right)^2 \\
    &= (11.76)^2 \\
    &= 138.30 \\
    &\Rightarrow n^* = 139 \ \text{replications (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Step to Its V&V Tool

| DES Step | Primary Output | Verification Tool | Validation Tool |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1. Problem Formulation | Objective statement | Stakeholder review | — |
| 2. System Boundaries | Boundary diagram | Trace check | — |
| 3. Conceptual Model | Flow diagram / IDEF0 | Walkthrough with SME | Face validity |
| 4. Data Collection | Fitted distributions | Chi-square, KS test | — |
| 5. Code Translation | Executable program | Debug, structured walk-through | — |
| 6. V&V Combined | Credibility certificate | Code-to-model trace | $t$-test vs. real system |
| 7. Experimental Design | Run-length settings | Replication count check | Pilot runs |
| 8. Output Analysis | Final report | Sanity checks on CI | Sensitivity analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** *Verification = "Did I build the model right?"* (internal consistency). *Validation = "Did I build the right model?"* (correspondence to reality). Both are **non-negotiable** in the KTU marking scheme.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequential Flowchart of the 8 DES Steps

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of a discrete event simulation study. Each node is a clearly demarcated phase; arrows indicate the dominant information flow direction. Notice the **two feedback loops** that distinguish a *scientific* simulation study from a one-shot code run: the Verification feedback (inner loop) and the Validation feedback (outer loop).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1 : PROBLEM FORMULATION"] --> A2["STEP 2 : SYSTEM AND PROJECT OBJECTIVES"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3 : CONCEPTUAL MODEL BUILDING"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4 : DATA COLLECTION AND FITTING"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5 : MODEL TRANSLATION INTO CODE"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6 : VERIFICATION AND VALIDATION"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7 : EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN AND RUN PRODUCTION"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 8 : OUTPUT ANALYSIS AND DOCUMENTATION"]
    A6 -. V AND V FEEDBACK .-> A5
    A8 -. REDEFINE OBJECTIVES .-> A1
    A7 --> A9["TERMINATING OR STEADY STATE ?"]
    A9 --> A10["COMPUTE CI AND SENSITIVITY"]
    A10 --> A8
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Solid arrows (A1 → A2 → … → A8)** denote the *forward, sequential* flow of a brand-new simulation study.
- The **dotted feedback loop** from Step 6 back to Step 5 represents the **Verification** phase: if the coded model does not behave like the conceptual model, we return to fix the code.
- The **outer feedback loop** from Step 8 back to Step 1 represents **iteration based on insight**: once managers see results, they often reformulate the problem (e.g., "What if we add a *second* teller?").
- The branch into **Terminating vs. Steady-State** is a decision gate inside Step 7 — its output dictates which statistical estimation method is applied in Step 8.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Time Advancement Mechanism

Inside Step 5 (Model Translation), the *engine* of any DES program uses one of two time-advance mechanisms. The figure below maps the **Next-Event Time Advance** architecture, which is the standard for DES.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["INITIALISATION : CLOCK = 0"] --> B2["READ EVENT LIST FOR NEXT EVENT TIME TNEXT"]
    B2 --> B3["ADVANCE CLOCK TO TNEXT"]
    B3 --> B4["EXECUTE EVENT ROUTINE : UPDATE STATE VARIABLES"]
    B4 --> B5["GENERATE FUTURE EVENTS : UPDATE EVENT LIST"]
    B5 --> B6["MORE EVENTS ?"]
    B6 -- YES --> B2
    B6 -- NO --> B7["GENERATE OUTPUT REPORTS AND STOP"]
```

### Interpretation

- The **event list** (or "future events chain") is the core data structure holding pending events sorted by scheduled time.
- The **clock** jumps directly from $t_k$ to $t_{k+1}$ — it never ticks second-by-second. This is what makes DES computationally efficient for sparse-event systems.
- This **clock-jump mechanism** is conceptually equivalent to skipping ahead in a movie to the next moment something interesting happens.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **List and briefly explain the first four steps involved in a Discrete Event Simulation study.**

**Model Answer (12-line expected length):**
The first four steps are:
1. **Problem Formulation** — clearly state the decision-making problem and the questions to be answered (e.g., "How many servers minimise cost while keeping average delay below 5 minutes?").
2. **Setting System and Project Objectives** — define performance measures (throughput, waiting time, utilisation), system boundaries, and the scope of the study.
3. **Conceptual Model Building** — construct a logical abstraction of the system showing entities (customers, jobs), resources (servers, machines), queues, and the rules governing their interaction.
4. **Data Collection** — gather empirical data on inter-arrival times, service times, routing probabilities, etc., and fit appropriate probability distributions using goodness-of-fit tests.

These four steps constitute the **front-end analysis** of the simulation project — without them, the coded model has no defensible foundation.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Distinguish between Verification and Validation of a simulation model. Why are both necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Verification** is the process of ensuring that the *computer program correctly implements the conceptual model*. It is "building the model right". Techniques include code walk-throughs, structured testing, and tracing event sequences against hand calculations.
- **Validation** is the process of ensuring that the *conceptual model faithfully represents the real system*. It is "building the right model". Techniques include face validity (expert opinion), statistical comparison of model output with real-system data using $t$-tests or confidence intervals.
- Both are necessary because a program can be **verified** (free of bugs and correctly coded) yet **invalid** (built on wrong assumptions about the real world). Conversely, a beautifully conceived model is useless if the code has bugs. The credibility of every simulation output depends on both V&V steps being rigorously completed.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Analytical Question

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **8 sequential steps** of a Discrete Event Simulation study, with a suitable example of your choice. Highlight where verification and validation occur. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A bank has a single ATM. Inter-arrival times follow an **exponential distribution** with mean **6 minutes**, and service times follow an **exponential distribution** with mean **4 minutes**. Using the M/M/1 formulae, compute the **server utilisation**, **average number of customers in queue**, **average waiting time in queue**, and **average time spent in the system**. Comment on whether the system is stable. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 8 Steps Explained with Example

**Example Used:** A *two-teller post office* deciding whether to upgrade to a three-teller system.

| Step | Action in the Post-Office Example |
|------|------------------------------------|
| 1. Problem Formulation | "Will adding a 3rd teller reduce average customer wait below 4 minutes?" |
| 2. System Objectives | Performance measures: average wait, teller utilisation, max queue length. Boundary: post office interior only. |
| 3. Conceptual Model | 2 servers (parallel), single FIFO queue, customers as entities, exponential inter-arrival and service. |
| 4. Data Collection | Collect 1000 inter-arrival & service samples; fit via chi-square test (KS = 0.04, accept exponential at $\alpha=0.05$). |
| 5. Model Translation | Code in Arena with 2 Server modules, 1 Queue, Arrival schedule. |
| 6. Verification & Validation | V&V — walk-through trace for 5 customers; compare simulated vs. observed utilisation (real = 0.71, sim = 0.70 — accept). |
| 7. Experimental Design | 30 replications, 8-hour run length, no warm-up (terminating simulation), random seeds. |
| 8. Output Analysis | 95% CI for mean wait = [3.4, 4.1]; sensitivity: increase to 3 tellers → CI = [1.8, 2.3]. Recommend 3-teller upgrade. |

---

#### Part (b) — M/M/1 Computations

Given:
- $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{6} = 0.1667$ customers/min
- $\mu = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$ customers/min

**1. Server Utilisation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.1667}{0.25} = 0.6667 \ \text{or} \ 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Average Number in Queue ($L_q$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_q &= \dfrac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{(0.6667)^2}{1 - 0.6667} = \dfrac{0.4444}{0.3333} = 1.3333 \ \text{customers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Average Waiting Time in Queue ($W_q$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_q &= \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{0.6667}{0.25 - 0.1667} = \dfrac{0.6667}{0.0833} = 8.0 \ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Average Time in System ($W$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 8.0 + 4.0 = 12.0 \ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stability Comment:** Since $\rho = 0.6667 < 1$, the system is **stable**. However, the 8-minute expected waiting time is operationally poor — adding a second server or a faster ATM is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Long Answer Question

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the **Next-Event Time Advance** algorithm used in discrete event simulation. How does it differ from Fixed-Increment Time Advance? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A simulation model produces $n = 50$ observed waiting times with sample mean $\bar{X} = 12.5$ minutes and standard deviation $S = 2.4$ minutes. Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean. If the project manager wants the half-width to be reduced to **0.3 minutes**, how many additional replications are required? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Next-Event Time Advance Algorithm

The **Next-Event Time Advance (NETA)** algorithm works as follows:

1. **Initialise** simulation clock $t = 0$. Initialise state variables and event list.
2. **Determine** the next event time $t^*$ = minimum scheduled time among all pending events.
3. **Advance** clock: $t \leftarrow t^*$.
4. **Execute** all events scheduled at time $t^*$ by calling their respective event routines (which update state variables, generate future events, schedule statistics collection).
5. **Update** the event list (insert / remove events).
6. **Repeat** steps 2–5 until simulation end condition is satisfied, then generate reports.

**Comparison with Fixed-Increment Time Advance:**

| Feature | Next-Event Time Advance | Fixed-Increment Time Advance |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Clock Jumps | Only to next event time | Increments by $\Delta t$ each step |
| Efficiency | High (skips inactivity) | Low (wastes CPU during idle periods) |
| Suitable for | Sparse-event systems | Continuous / dense-event systems |
| Implementation Complexity | Higher (event-list management) | Lower (simple loop) |

---

#### Part (b) — Confidence Interval & Sample Size Calculation

**Given:** $n = 50$, $\bar{X} = 12.5$ min, $S = 2.4$ min, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Find $t$-critical value:** $df = 49$, $t_{0.025,\,49} \approx 2.0096$.

**Step 2 — Compute half-width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \\
  &= 2.0096 \cdot \dfrac{2.4}{\sqrt{50}} \\
  &= 2.0096 \cdot \dfrac{2.4}{7.0711} \\
  &= 2.0096 \cdot 0.3394 \\
  &= 0.6821 \ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct CI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Limit} &= 12.5 - 0.6821 = 11.818 \\
\text{Upper Limit} &= 12.5 + 0.6821 = 13.182
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,11.82 \le \mu \le 13.18 \ \text{minutes at 95\% confidence}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Sample Size for $h^* = 0.3$ min:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n^* &= \left( \dfrac{t_{\alpha/2} \cdot S}{h^*} \right)^2 \\
    &= \left( \dfrac{2.0096 \times 2.4}{0.3} \right)^2 \\
    &= \left( \dfrac{4.823}{0.3} \right)^2 \\
    &= (16.077)^2 \\
    &= 258.47 \\
    &\Rightarrow n^* = 259
\end{aligned}
$$

**Additional Replications:** $259 - 50 = \mathbf{209}$ **more replications** are required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Loss-of-Marks Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** — Examiners consistently deduct 1–2 marks here. Memorise: *V*erification = code correctness; *V*alidation = model correctness.
> 2. **Forgetting the stability condition $\rho < 1$** in M/M/1 problems — if you compute $L_q$ without checking stability, full credit is denied.
> 3. **Using $z$-distribution instead of $t$-distribution** when $n < 30$ — always use $t$ for small-sample CIs.
> 4. **Skipping the warm-up discussion** in steady-state problems — without initial-transient deletion, point estimates are biased upward (the queue "floods" at start).
> 5. **Mixing up $W_q$ and $W$** — the former is *waiting only*, the latter includes service time.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DES = state changes only at discrete events.** Time is *event-driven*, not tick-driven.
- **The 8 canonical steps** are: Problem Formulation → Objectives → Conceptual Model → Data Collection → Coding → V&V → Experimental Design → Output Analysis & Documentation.
- **Verification vs Validation** — internal consistency vs correspondence to reality.
- **M/M/1 stability requires $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$**, otherwise the queue grows without bound.
- **Confidence interval** uses $t$-distribution for finite samples: $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- **Required sample size** formula: $n^* = (z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma \,/\, h)^2$, rounded **up**.
- **Warm-up deletion** (initial-transient removal) is essential in steady-state simulations to avoid upward bias.
- **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$) is the universal queueing identity connecting customer counts, arrival rates, and sojourn times.
- **Next-Event Time Advance** is the standard DES clock mechanism — it is computationally efficient because it skips idle periods.
- **Always state the system boundary** explicitly — examiners reward boundary clarity.
- **Document assumptions** in your final report; reviewers judge a simulation's credibility as much as its numerical results.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Numbers: Properties of Random Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random Numbers: Properties of Random Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Random Number:** A random number is a numerical value drawn from a **Uniform Distribution** over the interval $(0, 1)$, i.e., $U(0,1)$, such that every value in the interval is **equally likely** to be drawn and the drawn values are **statistically independent** of one another.

In the context of **System Simulation**, a sequence $\{R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n\}$ is called a stream of *pseudo-random numbers* if it behaves, for all practical purposes of the simulation, as if it were a sequence of i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) $U(0,1)$ random variates.

Mathematically, the probability density function (PDF) of a uniform random variable $R \in [0,1]$ is:

$$
f(x) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

with cumulative distribution function (CDF):

$$
F(x) = x, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1
$$

The expected value $E[R] = 0.5$ and variance $\text{Var}(R) = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pseudo-Random vs True Random:** In computer simulation, random numbers are *not* truly random — they are **Pseudo-Random Numbers (PRN)** generated by deterministic algorithms called *Random Number Generators (RNG)*. They are "random enough" to pass statistical tests of randomness.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **perfectly balanced, six-faced die** rolled 60,000 times. Each face should appear roughly **10,000 times**. Now imagine a *digital die* — a tiny mathematical function inside your computer that produces a number between 0 and 1 each time you call it. If you call it 60,000 times, the numbers should:

1. Spread out evenly between 0 and 1 (like the die faces spreading uniformly).
2. Not betray any pattern (no "memory" of the last value).
3. Be generated very fast (so we can simulate millions of events).

A **random number generator** is therefore the *digital equivalent* of a perfectly fair and forgetful die. Its quality is judged by how convincingly it can fool statistical tests that look for hidden patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The *three pillars* of a good random number stream in KTU 2024 evaluation are: **(1) Uniformity, (2) Independence, and (3) Sufficiently Long Period.**

---

## 1.3 Why Random Numbers? — The Foundation of Monte Carlo Simulation

Random numbers are the **fuel** of stochastic simulation. Any simulation model that contains probabilistic components (queue arrivals, machine breakdowns, demand variability, weather effects, etc.) must drive those components using random number streams. For example:

- To simulate **inter-arrival times** of customers in a bank, draw $R \sim U(0,1)$ and apply the **Inverse Transform Method**:
$$
T = F^{-1}(R)
$$
- To decide if a machine fails in a given hour, draw $R$ and compare to a failure probability $p$:
$$
\text{If } R < p \Rightarrow \text{Failure}, \quad \text{else } \Rightarrow \text{Survival}
$$

Thus, the *validity* of the entire simulation hinges on the quality of the underlying random number stream.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Desirable Properties of Random Numbers

A high-quality pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) should satisfy the following seven properties. KTU examiners frequently test these directly.

### Property 1: Uniformity (Density Property)

The generated numbers must be **uniformly distributed** on $[0,1]$. The empirical histogram, when divided into $k$ equal sub-intervals, should yield approximately $n/k$ observations in each interval, where $n$ is the total sample size.

Mathematically, the proportion of $R_i$ falling in any sub-interval $[a, b] \subset [0,1]$ should approximate $(b - a)$.

$$
P(a \leq R_i \leq b) = b - a, \quad 0 \leq a < b \leq 1
$$

**Why?** Because most random variate generation methods (e.g., Inverse Transform) assume $R \sim U(0,1)$ as a base.

### Property 2: Independence (Serial Property)

The values $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ must be **statistically independent**. The probability of $R_i$ taking any value must be unaffected by the values taken by $R_j$ for $j \neq i$.

$$
P(R_i \leq x \mid R_1 = r_1, \dots, R_{i-1} = r_{i-1}) = P(R_i \leq x) = x
$$

**Why?** If the values were correlated, simulated events would carry hidden bias (e.g., the simulation "knows" the customer is coming after a long gap).

### Property 3: Efficiency (Speed)

The generator must produce numbers **quickly** with minimal computational overhead. In large-scale simulation (millions of iterations), a slow RNG becomes a bottleneck.

**Typical benchmark:** A Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) requires only 1 multiplication + 1 addition + 1 modulus per number — extremely efficient.

### Property 4: Long Period (Cycle Length)

Because PRNGs are deterministic, the sequence eventually **repeats**. The length of the unique non-repeating portion is called the *period* (or *cycle length*) $m$. It should be very large — typically $m \geq 2^{31}$ for serious simulation work.

**Why?** A short period means the simulation re-uses the same numbers, destroying independence.

### Property 5: Reproducibility (Determinism)

Given the same **seed** (initial value), the generator must produce **exactly the same sequence**. This is crucial for:
- Debugging a simulation model.
- Performing **Common Random Numbers (CRN)** variance reduction technique for comparing alternative system designs.

### Property 6: Portability

The same generator, with the same seed, should produce identical sequences across different hardware, operating systems, and programming languages. This is essential for **Vendor-Independent** simulation tools.

### Property 7: Multiple Independent Streams

A good RNG system should support **hundreds of independent substreams** (each separated by a known offset in the sequence), so that different parts of a simulation (e.g., arrival stream vs. service stream) do not interfere with each other.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Property / Test** | **Statistic / Formula** | **Threshold / Condition** | **Purpose** |
|:-----:|:--------------------|:------------------------|:--------------------------|:------------|
| 1 | Uniform Distribution | $f(x) = 1$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$ | $0 \leq R_i \leq 1$ | Base assumption of all variate generation |
| 2 | Mean of $U(0,1)$ | $\mu = E[R] = 0.5$ | $\vert \bar{R} - 0.5 \vert \leq z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_{\bar{R}}$ | Test uniformity |
| 3 | Variance of $U(0,1)$ | $\sigma^2 = 1/12$ | Compare sample variance to $1/12$ | Test uniformity |
| 4 | Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{R}} = \sqrt{1/(12n)}$ | Used in confidence interval | Constructing CI for mean |
| 5 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | Compare with $\chi^2_{\alpha, k-1}$ | Frequency / Uniformity Test |
| 6 | Expected Frequency | $E_i = n/k$ | $E_i \geq 5$ required | Chi-Square test bin count |
| 7 | KS Test Statistic | $D = \max \vert F_n(x) - F(x) \vert$ | Compare with $D_{\alpha, n}$ | Goodness-of-fit for $U(0,1)$ |
| 8 | LCG Recurrence | $R_i = (a R_{i-1} + c) \bmod m$ | $R_i = R_i / m$ gives $U(0,1)$ | Generate PRN |
| 9 | LCG Period (Full Period) | $m$ | Requires Hull-Dobell Theorem | Maximize cycle |
| 10 | Autocorrelation Lag $h$ | $\rho_h = \dfrac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})(R_{i+h} - \bar{R})}{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2}$ | $\vert \rho_h \vert \leq z_{\alpha/2}/\sqrt{n}$ | Test independence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Properties:** "**U**ncle **I**n **E**ngland **R**eluctantly **P**oured **M**ultiple **S**hots" → **U**niformity, **I**ndependence, **E**fficiency, **R**eproducibility, **P**ortability, **M**ultiple streams, **S**peed (long period).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Random Numbers** |
|:----------------------|:-----------------------------------|
| Telecommunications | Modeling packet arrival in network queues, simulating channel noise |
| Manufacturing | Simulating machine breakdowns, job-shop scheduling |
| Finance | Monte Carlo option pricing, Value-at-Risk (VaR) estimation |
| Healthcare | Modeling patient arrival at emergency rooms, disease spread |
| Aerospace | Reliability analysis of rocket components, mission simulation |
| Civil Engineering | Stochastic hydrology, traffic flow simulation |
| Computer Architecture | Cache miss simulation, processor workload generation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Variance of a Uniform $U(0,1)$ Random Variable

**Step 1: Setup the PDF**

A uniform random variable $R$ on $[0,1]$ has PDF:
$$
f(r) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } 0 \leq r \leq 1 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Mean $E[R]$**

By definition of expected value for a continuous RV:
$$
E[R] = \int_{0}^{1} r \cdot f(r) \, dr
$$

Substituting $f(r) = 1$:
$$
E[R] = \int_{0}^{1} r \cdot 1 \, dr = \int_{0}^{1} r \, dr
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the Integral**

$$
E[R] = \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1^2}{2} - \frac{0^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Therefore, $E[R] = 0.5$. ✓

**Step 4: Compute $E[R^2]$**

This is required for the variance.
$$
E[R^2] = \int_{0}^{1} r^2 \cdot 1 \, dr = \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Variance $\text{Var}(R)$**

Using the identity $\text{Var}(R) = E[R^2] - (E[R])^2$:
$$
\text{Var}(R) = \frac{1}{3} - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{4 - 3}{12} = \frac{1}{12}
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{E[R] = 0.5, \quad \text{Var}(R) = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.08333, \quad \sigma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{12}} \approx 0.2887}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chi-Square Test for Uniformity

The Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) test checks if the empirical frequency distribution matches the expected uniform distribution.

**Step 1: Partition the Interval**

Divide $[0,1]$ into $k$ equal-width sub-intervals:
$$
I_j = \left[\frac{j-1}{k}, \frac{j}{k}\right), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, k
$$

**Step 2: Generate $n$ Random Numbers**

Produce $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ from the PRNG and tally how many fall in each sub-interval. Let $O_j$ be the observed count in $I_j$.

**Step 3: Compute Expected Count per Interval**

Since the distribution is uniform, the expected count in every interval is:
$$
E_j = n \cdot P\left(R \in I_j\right) = n \cdot \frac{1}{k} = \frac{n}{k}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Chi-Square Statistic**

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}
$$

This statistic quantifies the total squared deviation of observed counts from expected counts, normalized by the expected count.

**Step 5: Hypothesis Decision**

- Null hypothesis $H_0$: The numbers are uniform on $[0,1]$.
- The $\chi^2_0$ statistic follows a Chi-Square distribution with $k - 1$ degrees of freedom (one df lost due to the constraint $\sum O_j = \sum E_j = n$).
- **Decision rule** at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
\text{If } \chi^2_0 \leq \chi^2_{0.95, \, k-1} \Rightarrow \text{Accept } H_0 \text{ (numbers are uniform)}
$$

$$
\text{If } \chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{0.95, \, k-1} \Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ (numbers are NOT uniform)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition:** Each $E_j \geq 5$. If not, merge adjacent bins until the condition holds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) Test for Uniformity

The KS test is more powerful than Chi-Square for continuous distributions because it uses the **empirical CDF** directly.

**Step 1: Sort the Observations**

Sort $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ in ascending order to obtain $R_{(1)} \leq R_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq R_{(n)}$.

**Step 2: Construct the Empirical CDF**

$$
F_n(x) = \frac{\text{Number of } R_i \leq x}{n}
$$

For $x$ between $R_{(i)}$ and $R_{(i+1)}$:
$$
F_n(x) = \frac{i}{n}
$$

**Step 3: Compute KS Statistic**

Compare $F_n(x)$ with the theoretical CDF $F(x) = x$ (for $U(0,1)$) at every jump point:
$$
D^+ = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \left( \frac{i}{n} - R_{(i)} \right)
$$

$$
D^- = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \left( R_{(i)} - \frac{i-1}{n} \right)
$$

$$
D = \max(D^+, D^-)
$$

**Step 4: Decision Rule**

Critical values $D_{\alpha, n}$ are tabulated (e.g., $D_{0.05, 100} \approx 0.135$).

$$
\text{If } D \leq D_{\alpha, n} \Rightarrow \text{Accept } H_0 \text{ (uniform)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Runs Test for Independence (Above/Below Mean)

A **run** is a maximal subsequence of consecutive numbers all above (or all below) the mean.

**Step 1: Compute the Mean**

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \approx 0.5
$$

**Step 2: Classify Each Number**

Replace $R_i$ with $+$ if $R_i > 0.5$, else $-$ if $R_i \leq 0.5$.

**Step 3: Count Runs**

Let $r$ = total number of runs (both $+$ and $-$ blocks). Let $n_1$ = number of $+$ symbols, $n_2$ = number of $-$ symbols, with $n_1 + n_2 = n$.

**Step 4: Compute Expected Runs and Variance**

$$
\mu_r = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2} + 1
$$

$$
\sigma_r^2 = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - n_1 - n_2)}{(n_1 + n_2)^2 (n_1 + n_2 - 1)}
$$

**Step 5: Z-Statistic**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{r - \mu_r}{\sigma_r}
$$

**Step 6: Decision Rule**

$$
\text{If } \vert Z_0 \vert \leq z_{\alpha/2} \Rightarrow \text{Accept independence}
$$

For $\alpha = 0.05$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Chi-Square Test

> **Problem [KTU University Exam Pattern]:** A PRNG produced $n = 100$ numbers. After dividing $[0,1]$ into $k = 5$ bins, the observed frequencies are:
> 
> $O_1 = 18, \; O_2 = 22, \; O_3 = 20, \; O_4 = 19, \; O_5 = 21$
> 
> Test uniformity at $\alpha = 0.05$ using the Chi-Square test. Given $\chi^2_{0.95, 4} = 9.488$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Expected Frequency**

$$
E_j = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{100}{5} = 20, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $(O_j - E_j)^2$ for each bin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1: } (18 - 20)^2 &= 4 \\
\text{Bin 2: } (22 - 20)^2 &= 4 \\
\text{Bin 3: } (20 - 20)^2 &= 0 \\
\text{Bin 4: } (19 - 20)^2 &= 1 \\
\text{Bin 5: } (21 - 20)^2 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Each Term $(O_j - E_j)^2 / E_j$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1: } 4 / 20 &= 0.20 \\
\text{Bin 2: } 4 / 20 &= 0.20 \\
\text{Bin 3: } 0 / 20 &= 0.00 \\
\text{Bin 4: } 1 / 20 &= 0.05 \\
\text{Bin 5: } 1 / 20 &= 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the Terms**

$$
\chi^2_0 = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.00 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.50
$$

**Step 5: Compare with Critical Value**

$$
\chi^2_0 = 0.50 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \chi^2_{0.95, 4} = 9.488
$$

Since $0.50 < 9.488$, we **accept $H_0$**. The random numbers are uniformly distributed. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Properties of Random Numbers (Mind Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RANDOM NUMBER PROPERTIES"] --> B["UNIFORMITY"]
    A --> C["INDEPENDENCE"]
    A --> D["PERIODICITY"]
    A --> E["COMPUTATIONAL QUALITY"]
    A --> F["USABILITY"]
    B --> B1["Mean Equals 0.5"]
    B --> B2["Variance Equals 1 by 12"]
    B --> B3["Equal Likelihood in Any Sub-Interval"]
    B --> B4["Test: Chi-Square"]
    B --> B5["Test: Kolmogorov-Smirnov"]
    C --> C1["No Correlation Between R_i and R_j"]
    C --> C2["Test: Autocorrelation"]
    C --> C3["Test: Runs Test Above and Below Mean"]
    C --> C4["Test: Gap Test"]
    D --> D1["Long Cycle Length"]
    D --> D2["Full Period Equals m"]
    D --> D3["No Trivial Repetition"]
    E --> E1["Fast Execution Speed"]
    E --> E2["Minimal Memory Footprint"]
    E --> E3["Minimal Storage of State"]
    F --> F1["Reproducible from Seed"]
    F --> F2["Portable Across Platforms"]
    F --> F3["Supports Multiple Streams"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Testing a Random Number Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Generate n PRN using Generator"] --> P2["Compute Sample Mean and Variance"]
    P2 --> P3{"Match 0.5 and 1 by 12"}
    P3 -- "No" --> P4["REJECT Generator"]
    P3 -- "Yes" --> P5["Apply Chi-Square Uniformity Test"]
    P5 --> P6{"Chi2 Statistic below Critical Value"}
    P6 -- "No" --> P4
    P6 -- "Yes" --> P7["Apply KS Goodness of Fit Test"]
    P7 --> P8{"D Statistic below D alpha n"}
    P8 -- "No" --> P4
    P8 -- "Yes" --> P9["Apply Runs Test for Independence"]
    P9 --> P10{"Z0 within plus or minus 1.96"}
    P10 -- "No" --> P4
    P10 -- "Yes" --> P11["Apply Autocorrelation Test"]
    P11 --> P12{"Rho h within limits"}
    P12 -- "No" --> P4
    P12 -- "Yes" --> P13["ACCEPT Generator as Statistically Valid"]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Variate Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PRNG Engine"] --> B["Uniform 0 1 Output"]
    B --> C{"Required Distribution"}
    C -- "Exponential" --> D["Inverse Transform T Equals minus ln R times mean"]
    C -- "Normal" --> E["Box Muller Method"]
    C -- "Poisson" --> F["Knuth Algorithm"]
    C -- "Bernoulli" --> G["If R less than p then 1 else 0"]
    D --> H["Final Simulated Input"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
