# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*List any three properties that a sequence of pseudo-random numbers must satisfy.*

**Model Answer:**

A good sequence of pseudo-random numbers must satisfy the following three properties:

1. **Uniformity:** The numbers must be uniformly distributed over the interval $[0, 1]$. The expected value should be $0.5$ and variance should be $1/12 \approx 0.0833$.

2. **Independence:** The numbers $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ must be statistically independent. The probability of $R_i$ taking any value should not be influenced by any of the previously generated values.

3. **Long Period (or Sufficient Cycle Length):** The generator should produce a long non-repeating sequence before cycling. The period should be very large (typically $m \geq 2^{31}$) to ensure that the simulation does not exhaust the cycle during a run.

*(Other acceptable properties: Efficiency, Reproducibility, Portability, Multiple Streams — 3 marks awarded for any three with one-line justification each.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Why is uniformity of random numbers important in simulation?*

**Model Answer:**

Uniformity is the foundational property of random numbers because **almost every random variate generation technique** (Inverse Transform Method, Rejection Method, Composition Method, etc.) begins by assuming we can draw a perfect $U(0,1)$ sample.

If the underlying random numbers are not uniform, the resulting variates (e.g., inter-arrival times, service times) will carry hidden bias, causing the simulation to **systematically over-estimate or under-estimate** system performance metrics such as average queue length, waiting time, or utilization.

In short, **non-uniform random numbers corrupt the entire simulation output**, regardless of how well the model is built. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The internal choice is **between two completely independent alternatives** (Question A OR Question B). Answer any ONE.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the desirable properties of pseudo-random numbers in detail. Why is each property important for simulation experiments?*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *A Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) is defined as $R_i = (5 R_{i-1} + 3) \bmod 16$ with seed $R_0 = 7$. Generate the first 10 random numbers $R_1$ to $R_{10}$ and verify if the period matches the modulus.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The seven desirable properties of pseudo-random numbers are:

1. **Uniformity** — The numbers should be uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$ with PDF $f(x) = 1$ and CDF $F(x) = x$. This ensures that the Inverse Transform method produces unbiased variates from any target distribution. *Importance: Foundation of all variate generation.*

2. **Independence** — Each $R_i$ must be statistically independent of $R_{i-1}, R_{i-2}, \dots$. *Importance: Guarantees that the simulated event sequence contains no hidden correlation pattern.*

3. **Long Period** — The unique non-repeating sequence length $m$ should be large. *Importance: Avoids recycling the same numbers during long simulation runs.*

4. **Efficiency** — Generation should be fast (few multiplications, additions, modulus operations per number). *Importance: Real-time or large-scale simulations cannot afford slow PRNGs.*

5. **Reproducibility** — Given the same seed, the generator must reproduce the same sequence. *Importance: Essential for debugging, validation, and Common Random Numbers (CRN) technique.*

6. **Portability** — Identical output across hardware/operating systems. *Importance: Simulation studies must be reproducible on different machines.*

7. **Multiple Independent Streams** — Should support hundreds of substreams. *Importance: Each source of randomness (arrivals, services, breakdowns) needs a non-overlapping stream.*

*(7 marks — 1 mark per property + 0.5 for importance of each)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Given:** $R_i = (5 R_{i-1} + 3) \bmod 16$, $R_0 = 7$

**Step 1: Compute each $R_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= (5 \times 7 + 3) \bmod 16 = (35 + 3) \bmod 16 = 38 \bmod 16 = 6 \\
R_2 &= (5 \times 6 + 3) \bmod 16 = (30 + 3) \bmod 16 = 33 \bmod 16 = 1 \\
R_3 &= (5 \times 1 + 3) \bmod 16 = (5 + 3) \bmod 16 = 8 \bmod 16 = 8 \\
R_4 &= (5 \times 8 + 3) \bmod 16 = (40 + 3) \bmod 16 = 43 \bmod 16 = 11 \\
R_5 &= (5 \times 11 + 3) \bmod 16 = (55 + 3) \bmod 16 = 58 \bmod 16 = 10 \\
R_6 &= (5 \times 10 + 3) \bmod 16 = (50 + 3) \bmod 16 = 53 \bmod 16 = 5 \\
R_7 &= (5 \times 5 + 3) \bmod 16 = (25 + 3) \bmod 16 = 28 \bmod 16 = 12 \\
R_8 &= (5 \times 12 + 3) \bmod 16 = (60 + 3) \bmod 16 = 63 \bmod 16 = 15 \\
R_9 &= (5 \times 15 + 3) \bmod 16 = (75 + 3) \bmod 16 = 78 \bmod 16 = 14 \\
R_{10} &= (5 \times 14 + 3) \bmod 16 = (70 + 3) \bmod 16 = 73 \bmod 16 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Tabulate and Convert to $U(0,1)$**

Since $R_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, 15\}$ and $m = 16$, the uniform random number is $U_i = R_i / m$:

| $i$ | $R_i$ | $U_i = R_i / 16$ |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | 6 | 0.3750 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.0625 |
| 3 | 8 | 0.5000 |
| 4 | 11 | 0.6875 |
| 5 | 10 | 0.6250 |
| 6 | 5 | 0.3125 |
| 7 | 12 | 0.7500 |
| 8 | 15 | 0.9375 |
| 9 | 14 | 0.8750 |
| 10 | 9 | 0.5625 |

**Step 3: Verify Period**

The full period for an LCG is at most $m = 16$. The sequence contains all 16 possible values (0–15) before repeating — confirmed once the sequence returns to $R_0 = 7$. Hence, the period equals $m = 16$. *(7 marks — 1 per step + 1 for table + 1 for period verification)*

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the Chi-Square test for testing uniformity of random numbers. A PRNG produces 200 numbers, and they are classified into 8 equal intervals. The observed frequencies are: 22, 25, 24, 26, 25, 23, 28, 27. Test uniformity at $\alpha = 0.05$. Given $\chi^2_{0.95, 7} = 14.067$.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Explain the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test for testing uniformity of random numbers. How does it differ from the Chi-Square test?*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Theory (2 marks):**

The Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) test for uniformity works as follows:

1. Divide the interval $[0,1]$ into $k$ equal sub-intervals.
2. Generate $n$ random numbers and count observed frequencies $O_j$ in each sub-interval.
3. Compute expected frequency $E_j = n/k$ under the null hypothesis $H_0$ (numbers are uniform).
4. Compute the test statistic:
$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}
$$
5. If $\chi^2_0 \leq \chi^2_{\alpha, k-1}$, accept $H_0$ (uniform); otherwise reject.

**Numerical (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute Expected Frequency
$$
E_j = \frac{200}{8} = 25, \quad \forall j = 1, \dots, 8
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(O_j - E_j)^2 / E_j$ for each interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
j=1: \; (22 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 9/25 = 0.36 \\
j=2: \; (25 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 0/25 = 0.00 \\
j=3: \; (24 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 1/25 = 0.04 \\
j=4: \; (26 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 1/25 = 0.04 \\
j=5: \; (25 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 0/25 = 0.00 \\
j=6: \; (23 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 4/25 = 0.16 \\
j=7: \; (28 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 9/25 = 0.36 \\
j=8: \; (27 - 25)^2 / 25 &= 4/25 = 0.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the Terms
$$
\chi^2_0 = 0.36 + 0.00 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.00 + 0.16 + 0.36 + 0.16 = 1.12
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with Critical Value
$$
\chi^2_0 = 1.12 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \chi^2_{0.95, 7} = 14.067
$$

Since $1.12 \ll 14.067$, we **accept $H_0$**. The random numbers are uniformly distributed. ✓

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test (5 marks):**

The Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test compares the **empirical cumulative distribution function** $F_n(x)$ of the sample with the **theoretical CDF** $F(x) = x$ of $U(0,1)$.

**Procedure:**

1. Sort the $n$ random numbers to obtain $R_{(1)} \leq R_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq R_{(n)}$.
2. Compute the KS statistic:
$$
D = \max \left\{ D^+, D^- \right\}
$$
where:
$$
D^+ = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \left( \frac{i}{n} - R_{(i)} \right)
$$
$$
D^- = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \left( R_{(i)} - \frac{i-1}{n} \right)
$$
3. Compare $D$ with the tabulated critical value $D_{\alpha, n}$.
4. If $D \leq D_{\alpha, n}$, accept $H_0$ (uniformity holds); otherwise reject.

**Differences from Chi-Square Test (2 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Chi-Square Test** | **KS Test** |
|:-----------|:--------------------|:------------|
| Statistic Type | Binned (discretized) | Uses raw continuous data |
| Information Loss | Yes — bins lose detail | No — uses every observation |
| Sample Size | Works for both small and large $n$ | More powerful for small $n$ |
| Distribution | Applicable to any discrete grouping | Applicable only to continuous distributions |
| Critical Values | Chi-Square table | Kolmogorov-Smirnov table |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $R_i$ and $U_i$:** Many students forget to divide the LCG output by $m$ to get $U(0,1)$. The raw LCG integer is *not* a uniform $[0,1]$ variate. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the $(k-1)$ degrees of freedom:** Students often use $k$ instead of $k-1$ when looking up the critical $\chi^2$ value. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Skipping the validity check $E_j \geq 5$:** Always state this assumption explicitly in the answer. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **For Independence Test, omitting the Runs Test or Autocorrelation Test:** KTU often asks for *any test* of independence — pick one and explain it fully. Don't just name-drop. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Writing "random number" instead of "pseudo-random number":** In a computer simulation context, they are always pseudo-random. Examiners deduct marks for this terminology slip. **(-0.5 mark)**
> 6. **Miscounting sub-part marks:** Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks = 14 marks total. Don't write 10-mark answers in each part. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam):**

- **Random Number (RN):** A number drawn from $U(0,1)$. In simulation, it is **pseudo-random** (deterministic algorithm output).
- **Three core properties** (must-know for KTU): **Uniformity, Independence, Sufficiently Long Period.**
- **Four supporting properties:** **Efficiency, Reproducibility, Portability, Multiple Streams.**
- **Mean of $U(0,1)$** = $0.5$. **Variance of $U(0,1)$** = $1/12$. **Standard deviation** = $1/\sqrt{12}$.
- **Chi-Square Test for Uniformity:** $\chi^2_0 = \sum (O_j - E_j)^2 / E_j$ with $k-1$ degrees of freedom and $E_j \geq 5$.
- **KS Test for Uniformity:** $D = \max(D^+, D^-)$ where $D^+ = \max(i/n - R_{(i)})$ and $D^- = \max(R_{(i)} - (i-1)/n)$.
- **Runs Test for Independence:** Count runs above/below the mean; compare $Z_0 = (r - \mu_r)/\sigma_r$ with $\pm 1.96$.
- **Autocorrelation Test for Independence:** Compute lag-$h$ correlation $\rho_h$ and check $\vert \rho_h \vert \leq z_{\alpha/2}/\sqrt{n}$.
- **LCG Formula:** $R_i = (a R_{i-1} + c) \bmod m$, then $U_i = R_i / m$ to get $U(0,1)$.
- **Full period of LCG** = $m$ (requires Hull-Dobell Theorem conditions: $\gcd(c, m) = 1$, $a - 1$ divisible by all prime factors of $m$, $a - 1$ divisible by 4 if $m$ is divisible by 4).
- **Inverse Transform Method:** Given $R \sim U(0,1)$, the variate $T = F^{-1}(R)$ follows the distribution with CDF $F$.
- **Bernoulli Decision Rule:** If $R < p$ then event occurs; else it does not.
- **KTU Golden Rule:** Always verify **both** uniformity **and** independence — uniformity alone is not enough.
- **Most common KTU exam trap:** Students compute Chi-Square correctly but fail to **interpret the result** (accept/reject decision). Always end with a clear conclusion statement.
- **Numbers to memorize:** $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $1/12 \approx 0.0833$, $1/\sqrt{12} \approx 0.2887$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generation of Pseudo-Random Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generation of Pseudo-Random Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pseudo-Random Numbers (PRN):** A sequence of numbers generated by a **deterministic algorithmic process** that *appears* to be statistically random, but is entirely reproducible given the same initial seed value $X_0$. They are *not* truly random — they are "fake" random numbers produced by a mathematical recurrence relation.

**In the context of System Simulation:**
Pseudo-random numbers are the **fundamental input fuel** for any stochastic (probabilistic) simulation model. Since real randomness cannot be generated by a deterministic digital computer, simulation engineers rely on **Pseudo-Random Number Generators (PRNGs)** to produce Uniform $(0,1)$ variates, which are then transformed into samples from other probability distributions (exponential, normal, Poisson, etc.) using techniques like the **Inverse Transform Method** or **Acceptance-Rejection Method**.

A formal definition for the **KTU board perspective**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A sequence $\{R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots\}$ of pseudo-random numbers is a sequence of real numbers in the interval $(0, 1)$ that satisfies two properties:
> 1. **Uniformity:** The numbers are uniformly distributed over $(0, 1)$.
> 2. **Independence:** The numbers are statistically independent of each other.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Cookie Cutter" Analogy:** Imagine you are baking millions of cookies (your random numbers). You cannot bake *truly unique* cookies because you have only one cookie-cutter mold (your algorithm). However, this mold is designed so cleverly that the cookies **look, taste, and behave** as if each one was hand-shaped uniquely. Anyone tasting the cookies one-by-one cannot tell they came from the same mold. The mold's *pattern* is the algorithm, and the *initial dough shape* ($X_0$) is the **seed**.

**Plain-English Intuition:**
- A **True Random Number** = Rolling a fair, physical die. The outcome is governed by quantum-level unpredictability.
- A **Pseudo-Random Number** = Asking a calculator to compute a complex formula like $X_{i+1} = (7 \cdot X_i + 5) \bmod 100$. The result is *mathematically deterministic*, but the sequence is so chaotic-looking that it passes every statistical test of randomness.

**Why "Pseudo" and not "Real"?**
The word "Pseudo" (Greek for *false*) is crucial. Given the *same starting seed* $X_0$, the **exact same** sequence will be reproduced every time. This is actually an **advantage** in simulation — it enables **reproducibility** of experiments for debugging and validation.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Glossary:**
>
> | Term | Standard KTU Definition |
> |------|-------------------------|
> | **Seed ($X_0$)** | The starting value of the recurrence relation. Determines the entire sequence. |
> | **Modulus ($m$)** | The integer against which all arithmetic is reduced. Determines the **period length** ceiling. |
> | **Multiplier ($a$)** | The multiplicative coefficient in the recurrence. |
> | **Increment ($c$)** | The additive constant (zero in multiplicative generators). |
> | **Period ($P$)** | The number of distinct values before the sequence repeats. |
> | **Full Period** | A period equal to $m$, the maximum possible. |
> | **U(0,1) variate** | A uniform random number in the open interval $(0,1)$. |

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Modulus Choices in Literature:** $m = 2^{31} - 1$ (Mersenne prime) is a very popular value.
- **Maximal Period Theorem (Hull-Dobell):** The conditions under which an LCG achieves the **full period $m$**.
- **Statistical Tests:** Chi-Square ($\chi^2$), Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Runs Test, Autocorrelation Test, Poker Test, Gap Test.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Architecture of a PRNG

A PRNG operates in three logical stages:

1. **Selection of Parameters:** Choose the recurrence parameters $(X_0, a, c, m)$ satisfying the **Hull-Dobell conditions** for maximal period.
2. **Generation of Integer Stream:** Compute the deterministic integer sequence $\{X_i\}$ using modular arithmetic.
3. **Normalization:** Convert each $X_i$ to a uniform $(0,1)$ variate via the transformation $R_i = \dfrac{X_i}{m}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why Behind Each Step:**
> - *Why modular arithmetic?* It naturally creates a finite, cyclic sequence (period $P \le m$).
> - *Why a large $m$?* It guarantees a long period before repetition.
> - *Why normalize?* Simulation models need $(0,1)$ inputs for probability transformations.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Pseudo-Random Number Generators

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of the following five classical generators:

#### A. Midsquare Method (Von Neumann, 1949)
- **Logic:** Square the current number, then extract the middle digits.
- **Formula:** If $X_i$ has $d$ digits, then $X_{i+1}$ is the middle $d$ digits of $X_i^2$.

#### B. Mid-Product Method
- **Logic:** Multiply two previous numbers, extract middle digits.
- **Formula:** $X_{i+1} = \text{middle } d \text{ digits of } (X_i \cdot X_{i-1})$.

#### C. Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) / Mixed Congruential Method
- **The workhorse of simulation.** Formula:
$$X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$$
- When $c \neq 0$, it is called the **Mixed LCG**.
- Normalized output: $R_i = \dfrac{X_i}{m}$.

#### D. Multiplicative Congruential Generator (MLCG)
- A special case of LCG with $c = 0$:
$$X_{i+1} = (aX_i) \bmod m$$
- Faster than mixed LCG (one fewer addition), but maximum period is $m-1$ (not $m$, since $X=0$ is absorbing).

#### E. Additive Congruential Generator (Fibonacci-type)
- Uses *two* previous values:
$$X_{i+1} = (X_i + X_{i-n}) \bmod m$$
- Can achieve extremely long periods; used in IBM's **RANDU**-style generators.

---

### 2.3 The Hull-Dobell Theorem (Maximal Period Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull-Dobell Theorem (1962):** The mixed LCG $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$ achieves the **full period $m$** (i.e., all integers $0, 1, 2, \dots, m-1$ appear exactly once) **if and only if** all four conditions are satisfied:
>
> 1. $m$ and $c$ are **relatively prime** (i.e., $\gcd(c, m) = 1$).
> 2. $a - 1$ is divisible by **every prime factor** of $m$.
> 3. $a - 1$ is divisible by **4** if $m$ is divisible by 4.
> 4. $m > 0$ (trivially satisfied).

For the **multiplicative** LCG, the full period is $m-1$ if and only if:
- $m$ is prime, and
- $a$ is a **primitive root modulo $m$**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Generator | Recurrence Relation | Period Condition | Output $(0,1)$ |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Midsquare | $X_{i+1} = \text{mid}(X_i^2)$ | Not guaranteed | $R_i = X_i / 10^d$ |
| 2 | Mid-Product | $X_{i+1} = \text{mid}(X_i \cdot X_{i-1})$ | Empirical | $R_i = X_i / 10^d$ |
| 3 | Mixed LCG | $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$ | $P = m$ (Hull-Dobell) | $R_i = X_i / m$ |
| 4 | Multiplicative LCG | $X_{i+1} = (aX_i) \bmod m$ | $P = m-1$ if $m$ prime, $a$ primitive root | $R_i = X_i / m$ |
| 5 | Additive LCG | $X_{i+1} = (X_i + X_{i-n}) \bmod m$ | $P = m^n$ (theoretical max) | $R_i = X_i / m$ |

| # | Hull-Dobell Condition | Mathematical Form | Why It Matters |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Relatively prime | $\gcd(c, m) = 1$ | Ensures all residues visited |
| 2 | Prime factor divisibility | $a \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for every prime $p \mid m$ | Avoids "stuck" subcycles |
| 3 | Power of 2 divisibility | $a \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ if $4 \mid m$ | Handles trailing bits correctly |
| 4 | Positivity | $m > 0$ | Trivial but mandatory |

| Test Name | Purpose | Statistic Used |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) | Tests uniformity | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ |
| Kolmogorov-Smirnov | Tests CDF fit | $D = \max \vert F_n(x) - F(x) \vert$ |
| Runs Test | Tests independence | Counts sign-changes |
| Autocorrelation | Tests independence | $\rho_k = \frac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})(R_{i+k} - \bar{R})}{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2}$ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Does Industry Care?**
> - **Monte Carlo Finance:** Pricing derivatives (Black-Scholes) needs billions of $U(0,1)$ samples.
> - **Telecommunications:** Modeling packet arrival in queueing networks.
> - **VLSI Chip Design:** Simulating thermal noise and process variability.
> - **Cryptography (note: NOT this type!):** Crypto-secure PRNGs use different math (e.g., AES-CTR_DRBG), because statistical PRNGs are *predictable*.
> - **Game Development:** Procedural content generation uses simple LCG variants.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Walkthrough — Mixed LCG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given Problem:** Generate 5 pseudo-random numbers using the Mixed LCG with parameters:
> $X_0 = 7, \quad a = 5, \quad c = 3, \quad m = 16$.

**Step 0: Verify Hull-Dobell conditions.**

- $\gcd(c, m) = \gcd(3, 16) = 1$ ✓
- Prime factors of $m = 16$: only $2$. Check: $a - 1 = 4$, and $2 \mid 4$ ✓
- $m = 16$ is divisible by $4$, check: $4 \mid (a-1) = 4$ ✓
- $m > 0$ ✓
- **Conclusion:** Full period $P = m = 16$ is achievable.

**Step 1: Compute $X_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= (a \cdot X_0 + c) \bmod m \\
X_1 &= (5 \cdot 7 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_1 &= (35 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_1 &= 38 \bmod 16 \\
X_1 &= 38 - 2 \cdot 16 \\
X_1 &= 38 - 32 \\
X_1 &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Multiply the previous value $X_0 = 7$ by the multiplier $a = 5$ to get $35$. Add the increment $c = 3$ to get $38$. Divide $38$ by the modulus $m = 16$; the quotient is $2$ with remainder $6$. Therefore $X_1 = 6$.

**Step 2: Compute $X_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_2 &= (5 \cdot 6 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_2 &= (30 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_2 &= 33 \bmod 16 \\
X_2 &= 33 - 2 \cdot 16 \\
X_2 &= 33 - 32 \\
X_2 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Continue the recurrence. $30 + 3 = 33$. $33 / 16 = 2$ remainder $1$. So $X_2 = 1$.

**Step 3: Compute $X_3$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_3 &= (5 \cdot 1 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_3 &= 8 \bmod 16 \\
X_3 &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $5 \cdot 1 + 3 = 8$. Since $8 < 16$, the remainder is $8$ itself.

**Step 4: Compute $X_4$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_4 &= (5 \cdot 8 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_4 &= 43 \bmod 16 \\
X_4 &= 43 - 2 \cdot 16 \\
X_4 &= 11
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $40 + 3 = 43$. $43 - 32 = 11$.

**Step 5: Compute $X_5$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_5 &= (5 \cdot 11 + 3) \bmod 16 \\
X_5 &= 58 \bmod 16 \\
X_5 &= 58 - 3 \cdot 16 \\
X_5 &= 58 - 48 \\
X_5 &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $55 + 3 = 58$. $58 - 48 = 10$.

**Step 6: Normalize to $U(0,1)$ variates using $R_i = X_i / m$.**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $R_i = X_i / 16$ |
|-----|-------|------------------|
| 1   | 6     | 0.3750           |
| 2   | 1     | 0.0625           |
| 3   | 8     | 0.5000           |
| 4   | 11    | 0.6875           |
| 5   | 10    | 0.6250           |

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Walkthrough — Midsquare Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given Problem:** Generate 4 numbers via Midsquare starting with $X_0 = 4172$ (a 4-digit number, so $d = 4$).

**Step 1: Compute $X_0^2$.**

$$
X_0^2 = 4172^2 = 17{,}405{,}584
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Square the seed. The result is a 7- or 8-digit number. We need the middle 4 digits.

**Step 2: Pad with leading zeros to make it 8 digits.**

$$
X_0^2 = 17{,}405{,}584 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{write as } \; 17405584
$$

**Step 3: Extract middle 4 digits.**

The 8-digit number $17405584$: take digits 3 through 6 (0-indexed: positions 2,3,4,5):
- Position 0: 1
- Position 1: 7
- Position 2: 4  ← start
- Position 3: 0
- Position 4: 5
- Position 5: 5  ← end
- Position 6: 8
- Position 7: 4

Therefore $X_1 = 4055$.

**Step 4: Compute $X_1^2$ and extract middle 4 digits.**

$$
X_1^2 = 4055^2 = 16{,}443{,}025 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; X_2 = 4430
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $16443025$, middle 4 digits = $4430$.

**Step 5: Compute $X_2^2$ and extract middle 4 digits.**

$$
X_2^2 = 4430^2 = 19{,}624{,}900 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; X_3 = 6249
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $19624900$, middle 4 digits = $6249$.

**Step 6: Compute $X_3^2$ and extract middle 4 digits.**

$$
X_3^2 = 6249^2 = 39{,}050{,}001 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; X_4 = 0500 = 500
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $39050001$, middle 4 digits = $0500$. Note the leading zero — this is why the **Midsquare method can collapse to zero**!

**Step 7: Normalize using $R_i = X_i / 10^d$ with $d = 4$.**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $R_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 1   | 4055  | 0.4055 |
| 2   | 4430  | 0.4430 |
| 3   | 6249  | 0.6249 |
| 4   | 500   | 0.0500 |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** If $X_3 = 500$, then $X_3^2 = 250000$ and the middle 4 digits are $5000$. Then $X_4 = 5000$, $X_4^2 = 25000000$, middle 4 digits are $0000$, and the sequence **collapses to 0 forever**. This is the major weakness of the Midsquare method.

---

### 3.3 Period Verification (Hull-Dobell)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem Proof Sketch (For KTU Board):**
> To prove the mixed LCG has full period $m$, we must show that the sequence $\{X_0, X_1, \dots, X_{m-1}\}$ visits every integer in $\{0, 1, \dots, m-1\}$ exactly once. This is equivalent to showing the recurrence is a **bijection** on $\mathbb{Z}_m$.
>
> The function $f(X) = (aX + c) \bmod m$ is bijective on $\mathbb{Z}_m$ if and only if:
> - $\gcd(a, m) = 1$ (ensures $f$ is one-to-one, because if $aX_1 \equiv aX_2 \pmod m$, then $X_1 \equiv X_2 \pmod m$).
> - Hull-Dobell's three additional conditions ensure the cycle starts from the smallest possible point and visits all $m$ elements without any internal sub-cycles.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Mixed LCG | Multiplicative LCG | Additive LCG | Midsquare |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|-----------|
| Speed | Fast | Fastest | Medium | Slow |
| Period | $m$ (max) | $m-1$ (max) | $m^n$ (theoretical) | Unpredictable |
| Implementation Ease | Easy | Easiest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Reproducibility | Yes (deterministic) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Statistical Quality | Good (if Hull-Dobell) | Good | Excellent | Poor |
| Risk | Sub-cycles if $a, c$ bad | Absorbing state at 0 | Correlations | Collapse to 0 |
]<]minimax[>[<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PRNG Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SEED INPUT X0"] --> B["PARAMETER STORE a, c, m"]
    B --> C["RECURRENCE ENGINE X i plus 1 = aX i + c mod m"]
    C --> D["OUTPUT INTEGER X i"]
    D --> E{"IS X i ZERO?"}
    E -- YES --> F["WARNING COLLAPSE RISK"]
    E -- NO --> G["NORMALIZATION R i = X i / m"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["UNIFORM 0 1 VARIATE OUTPUT R i"]
    H --> I{"DISTRIBUTION TRANSFORM NEEDED?"}
    I -- YES --> J["INVERSE TRANSFORM OR REJECTION METHOD"]
    I -- NO --> K["DIRECT INPUT TO SIMULATION MODEL"]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Hull-Dobell Validation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START CHECK LCG PARAMETERS"] --> B["COMPUTE gcd c, m"]
    B --> C{"IS gcd EQUAL TO 1?"}
    C -- NO --> Z1["REJECT FULL PERIOD IMPOSSIBLE"]
    C -- YES --> D["FACTORIZE m INTO PRIMES p1 p2 ... pk"]
    D --> E["VERIFY a MINUS 1 DIVISIBLE BY EVERY pi"]
    E --> F{"ALL CHECKS PASS?"}
    F -- NO --> Z2["PARTIAL PERIOD ONLY"]
    F -- YES --> G["CHECK IF 4 DIVIDES m"]
    G --> H{"IS m DIVISIBLE BY 4?"}
    H -- YES --> I["VERIFY 4 DIVIDES a MINUS 1"]
    I --> J{"PASSES?"}
    J -- NO --> Z2
    J -- YES --> K["ACCEPT FULL PERIOD m GUARANTEED"]
    H -- NO --> K
```

### 4.3 Comparison of Three Generators (Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MIXED["MIXED LCG"]
        M1["aX i + c"] --> M2["mod m"] --> M3["X i plus 1"]
    end
    subgraph MULTIPLICATIVE["MULTIPLICATIVE LCG"]
        N1["aX i"] --> N2["mod m"] --> N3["X i plus 1"]
    end
    subgraph ADDITIVE["ADDITIVE LCG"]
        P1["X i plus X i minus n"] --> P2["mod m"] --> P3["X i plus 1"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Statistical Testing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GENERATE N PRNs R1 R2 ... RN"] --> B["UNIFORMITY TEST CHI SQUARE KS TEST"]
    B --> C{"PASS UNIFORMITY?"}
    C -- NO --> Z["REJECT GENERATOR"]
    C -- YES --> D["INDEPENDENCE TEST RUNS TEST AUTOCORRELATION"]
    D --> E{"PASS INDEPENDENCE?"}
    E -- NO --> Z
    E -- YES --> F["ACCEPTED FOR SIMULATION USE"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
> **Define a Pseudo-Random Number. Why is the term "pseudo" used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A Pseudo-Random Number is a number generated by a deterministic algorithm (e.g., Linear Congruential Generator) that *appears* to be statistically random, i.e., it satisfies the properties of **uniformity** in $(0, 1)$ and **statistical independence** between successive values.
- **Why "pseudo" (1 mark):** The term *pseudo* (meaning "false") is used because the numbers are **not truly random**; they are entirely determined by the algorithm and the initial seed $X_0$. Given the same seed, the exact same sequence is reproduced.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**  
> **State the Hull-Dobell Theorem for the period of a Mixed Linear Congruential Generator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The mixed LCG $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$ achieves the **full period $m$** if and only if:
1. $\gcd(c, m) = 1$ (increment and modulus are relatively prime). **(1 mark)**
2. $a - 1$ is divisible by every prime factor of $m$. **(1 mark)**
3. If $4$ divides $m$, then $4$ must also divide $a - 1$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Important:** As per KTU 2024 ESE rules, Module 1 carries ~20% weightage. A 14-mark question may be split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Full LCG Problem

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**  
> **(a)** Generate the first 5 pseudo-random numbers in $(0,1)$ using the **Mixed Linear Congruential Generator** with seed $X_0 = 17$, multiplier $a = 5$, increment $c = 3$, and modulus $m = 32$. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Verify whether the generator satisfies the **Hull-Dobell conditions** for achieving full period. State the actual period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply the recurrence $X_{i+1} = (5 X_i + 3) \bmod 32$ iteratively.**

For $i = 0$ to $4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= (5 \cdot 17 + 3) \bmod 32 \\
X_1 &= (85 + 3) \bmod 32 \\
X_1 &= 88 \bmod 32 \\
X_1 &= 88 - 2 \cdot 32 \\
X_1 &= 88 - 64 \\
X_1 &= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $85+3 = 88$; $88 - 64 = 24$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_2 &= (5 \cdot 24 + 3) \bmod 32 \\
X_2 &= 123 \bmod 32 \\
X_2 &= 123 - 3 \cdot 32 \\
X_2 &= 123 - 96 \\
X_2 &= 27
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $120+3 = 123$; $123 - 96 = 27$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_3 &= (5 \cdot 27 + 3) \bmod 32 \\
X_3 &= 138 \bmod 32 \\
X_3 &= 138 - 4 \cdot 32 \\
X_3 &= 138 - 128 \\
X_3 &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $135+3 = 138$; $138 - 128 = 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_4 &= (5 \cdot 10 + 3) \bmod 32 \\
X_4 &= 53 \bmod 32 \\
X_4 &= 21
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $50+3 = 53$; $53 - 32 = 21$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_5 &= (5 \cdot 21 + 3) \bmod 32 \\
X_5 &= 108 \bmod 32 \\
X_5 &= 108 - 3 \cdot 32 \\
X_5 &= 108 - 96 \\
X_5 &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $105+3 = 108$; $108 - 96 = 12$.

**Step 2: Normalize to $R_i = X_i / 32$.**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $R_i = X_i / 32$ |
|-----|-------|-------------------|
| 1   | 24    | 0.7500            |
| 2   | 27    | 0.8438            |
| 3   | 10    | 0.3125            |
| 4   | 21    | 0.6563            |
| 5   | 12    | 0.3750            |

**(7 marks distributed: 1 mark for formula, 5 marks for iteration, 1 mark for normalization table.)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Check Condition 1: $\gcd(c, m) = \gcd(3, 32)$.**  
- $32 = 10 \cdot 3 + 2$, $3 = 1 \cdot 2 + 1$, $2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$ → $\gcd(3, 32) = 1$. **✓ Satisfied. (2 marks)**

**Step 2: Check Condition 2: Prime factors of $m = 32 = 2^5$.**  
- The only prime factor is $p = 2$. Is $2 \mid (a-1) = 4$? **Yes ✓. (2 marks)**

**Step 3: Check Condition 3: $4 \mid m$?**  
- $m = 32$ is divisible by 4. Is $4 \mid (a-1) = 4$? **Yes ✓. (2 marks)**

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark).**  
- All three conditions are satisfied. The generator has the **full period $P = m = 32$**. Every integer in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 31\}$ will appear exactly once before repetition.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **Midsquare method** for generating pseudo-random numbers. Generate 4 numbers starting with seed $X_0 = 3187$ (4-digit). **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Compare the **Midsquare method** and the **Linear Congruential Generator (LCG)** in terms of period, simplicity, and statistical quality. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Explanation of Midsquare Method (3 marks):**  
The Midsquare method, proposed by **John von Neumann (1949)**, generates the next pseudo-random number by:
1. Squaring the current number $X_i$.
2. Extracting the **middle $d$ digits** of the squared value, where $d$ is the number of digits in $X_i$.
3. If leading zeros appear, they are preserved (e.g., $0500$ for $d=4$).

**Mathematical Form:** $X_{i+1} = \text{mid}_d(X_i^2)$.

**Iterative Generation (4 marks):**

**Iteration 1:** $X_0 = 3187$.  
$X_0^2 = 3187^2 = 10{,}157{,}969$. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{1579}$. So $X_1 = 1579$.

*Logic:* Square $3187$ to get $10,157,969$. Take digits 2 through 5 (middle four of eight positions): $1579$.

**Iteration 2:** $X_1 = 1579$.  
$X_1^2 = 1579^2 = 2{,}493{,}241$. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{4932}$. So $X_2 = 4932$.

*Logic:* $2,493,241$ has 7 digits; pad to 8: $02493241$. Middle 4 digits: $4932$.

**Iteration 3:** $X_2 = 4932$.  
$X_2^2 = 4932^2 = 24{,}324{,}624$. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{4324}$. So $X_3 = 4324$.

*Logic:* $24,324,624$ → middle four = $4324$.

**Iteration 4:** $X_3 = 4324$.  
$X_3^2 = 4324^2 = 18{,}696{,}976$. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{8696}$. So $X_4 = 8696$.

*Logic:* $18,696,976$ → middle four = $8696$.

**Summary Table:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $X_i^2$ | Middle 4 digits |
|-----|-------|---------|------------------|
| 1   | 1579  | 2,493,241   | 1579 → 4932 |
| 2   | 4932  | 24,324,624  | 4932 → 4324 |
| 3   | 4324  | 18,696,976  | 4324 → 8696 |
| 4   | 8696  | 75,620,416  | 8696 → 6204 |

(Showing first 3 iterations in detail; the 4th follows the same logic.)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Midsquare Method | Linear Congruential Generator |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Period (2 marks)** | Cannot be predicted; sequence can collapse to zero (e.g., if $X_i = 2500$, $X_i^2 = 6,250,000 \rightarrow$ middle = $2500$, then $X_{i+1}^2 = 6,250,000$ again → stuck). | Guaranteed period up to $m$ (Hull-Dobell conditions). |
| **Simplicity of Logic (2 marks)** | Very simple — just square and extract. No parameters like $a, c, m$. | Simple recurrence, but requires careful parameter selection. |
| **Statistical Quality (2 marks)** | Poor — exhibits serial correlation and high failure rate on independence tests. | Good to excellent if Hull-Dobell conditions are met; widely used in practice. |
| **Implementation Speed (1 mark)** | Slow (requires string manipulation for digit extraction). | Very fast (single multiply-add-mod operation). |
| **Risk (1 mark)** | High risk of degenerate sequences. | Low risk if parameters are chosen carefully. |

**Conclusion:** The LCG is **superior** to the Midsquare method in nearly every practical dimension. Midsquare is now considered obsolete except for pedagogical purposes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take modulus at every step.** Students often write $X_1 = 88$ instead of $X_1 = 88 \bmod 32 = 24$. *Loss: 2–3 marks per occurrence.*
> 2. **Not normalizing.** Generating $X_i$ is only half the work. KTU explicitly asks for $U(0,1)$ variates. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Mis-stating Hull-Dobell conditions.** Writing "all of $a, c, m$ should be prime" is **wrong**. The conditions involve divisibility and gcd. *Loss: up to 3 marks.*
> 4. **In Midsquare, dropping leading zeros.** Writing $X_4 = 500$ instead of $X_4 = 0500$ (which equals $500$ numerically, but the *position of extraction* is the testable concept). *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 5. **Confusing Multiplicative and Mixed LCG.** Multiplicative has $c = 0$; the maximum period is $m-1$, not $m$. Many students lose marks by writing "period = $m$" for the multiplicative case. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Omitting the "Why" in design questions.** When asked "Why is LCG better than Midsquare?", listing properties *without justifying with reasoning* loses the application-level marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for Last-Minute Reading**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** PRN = Deterministic sequence *appearing* random; reproducible via seed.
>
> ✅ **Key Parameters:** $X_0$ (seed), $a$ (multiplier), $c$ (increment), $m$ (modulus).
>
> ✅ **Mixed LCG Formula:** $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$.
>
> ✅ **Multiplicative LCG Formula:** $X_{i+1} = (aX_i) \bmod m$.
>
> ✅ **Additive LCG Formula:** $X_{i+1} = (X_i + X_{i-n}) \bmod m$.
>
> ✅ **Midsquare Formula:** $X_{i+1} = \text{middle-}d\text{-digits of } (X_i^2)$.
>
> ✅ **Normalization:** Always $R_i = X_i / m$ to get $U(0,1)$.
>
> ✅ **Hull-Dobell Theorem (Full Period $m$):**
>   - $\gcd(c, m) = 1$
>   - Prime factors of $m$ divide $a - 1$
>   - $4 \mid m \Rightarrow 4 \mid (a-1)$
>
> ✅ **Multiplicative LCG Period = $m - 1$** (not $m$); requires $m$ prime and $a$ a primitive root.
>
> ✅ **Midsquare is OBSOLETE** — risks collapse to zero.
>
> ✅ **Two Required Properties:** Uniformity + Independence.
>
> ✅ **Tests:** Chi-Square (uniformity), Runs Test (independence), KS Test (CDF fit), Autocorrelation.
>
> ✅ **Order of generation:** Compute $X_i$ first, then derive $R_i$ by division.
>
> ✅ **Reproducibility** is a feature, not a bug — it enables debugging.
>
> ✅ **Caveat for KTU:** Always show modular reduction explicitly in numerical problems; never skip a step.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Number Generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random Number Generators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Number Generator (RNG):** A computational or mathematical procedure that produces a sequence of numbers (or symbols) that lack any predictable pattern, mimicking the properties of random samples drawn from a uniform distribution $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$. In the context of **System Simulation**, RNGs are the foundational input drivers (also called **random variate generators**) that supply the stochastic inputs required to imitate the behavior of complex real-world stochastic systems.

Formally, a sequence $\\{U_1, U_2, U_3, \ldots, U_n\\}$ is considered a stream of **pseudo-random** numbers if it satisfies the following three necessary conditions:

1. **Uniformity:** Each $U_i$ is independently and uniformly distributed over the interval $(0, 1)$.
2. **Independence:** The value of $U_i$ is statistically independent of all preceding values $U_{i-1}, U_{i-2}, \ldots, U_1$.
3. **Reproducibility:** The same sequence can be regenerated using the same **seed** value, enabling controlled, repeatable simulation experiments.

Because pure physical randomness (e.g., radioactive decay) cannot be replayed, **System Simulation** always relies on **Pseudo-Random Number Generators (PRNGs)** — deterministic algorithms whose output *appears* random under statistical testing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Mechanical Dice Roller with a Memory":**
> Imagine a robotic arm that rolls a 1,000-sided die, writes down the outcome, then uses a *secret mathematical recipe* (deterministic formula) to roll again. Even though the recipe is fixed, the outcomes look completely chaotic. The **seed** is like the very first roll the robot makes — change the seed, and the entire infinite roll sequence changes. Use the *same* seed, and the *same* sequence is produced. This is how a computer — which is fundamentally deterministic — fakes randomness.

## 1.3 Why Random Number Generators are Critical to Simulation

- **Driving stochastic processes:** Queues, inventory demands, machine breakdowns, and weather patterns are all stochastic; RNGs generate the timeline of these events.
- **Monte Carlo integration:** Solving multi-dimensional integrals numerically (e.g., estimating $\pi$, portfolio risk in finance).
- **Statistical sampling:** Drawing representative samples from probability distributions (Normal, Exponential, Poisson, etc.).
- **Cryptographic simulation:** Entropy sources, key generation, protocol testing.
- **Optimization heuristics:** Simulated annealing and genetic algorithms depend on RNGs for stochastic exploration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric — Period Length ($p$):** The total number of unique values the generator can produce before the sequence **cycles** and begins repeating. A "good" generator must have an astronomically large period (e.g., $p \geq 2^{31} - 1 \approx 2.1 \times 10^9$) to avoid correlation artifacts in long simulations.

## 1.4 The Two Generation Philosophies

| Philosophy | Method | Speed | Reproducibility | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pseudo-Random (Deterministic)** | Mathematical recurrence (e.g., LCG) | Extremely fast ($\mu$s) | Perfect via seed | Simulation, Monte Carlo |
| **True Random (Non-Deterministic)** | Physical entropy (noise, decay) | Slow (ms) | Impossible | Cryptography, lotteries |

> **KTU Board Tip:** 90% of exam questions on this topic deal with the **Pseudo-Random (LCG / Mid-Square)** family. Always mention *period*, *seed*, and *modulus* in definitions to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Families of PRNGs (KTU Focus)

### A. Mid-Square Method (Von Neumann, 1949)

This is the simplest, oldest, and most illustrative PRNG. The operational algorithm is:

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Select a Seed** $X_0$ with exactly $d$ digits (commonly $d = 4$).
2. **Square** the current number: $X_i^2$.
3. **Pad** the squared result with leading zeros so it has exactly $2d$ digits.
4. **Extract the middle $d$ digits** — these form $X_{i+1}$.
5. **Normalize** by dividing by $10^d$ to obtain a Uniform $(0,1)$ variate: $U_i = X_i / 10^d$.
6. **Repeat** from Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** The squared number has information spread across all $2d$ digits. Picking the middle $d$ digits discards the deterministic influence of the high-order digits (carry propagation) and the low-order digits (parity effects), producing a value that *appears* decorrelated from its predecessor.

### B. Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) — Industry Standard

The most widely deployed PRNG in system simulation libraries. The recurrence relation is:

$$X_{i+1} = (a \cdot X_i + c) \bmod m$$

Where:
- $m$ — the **modulus** ($m > 0$)
- $a$ — the **multiplier** ($0 < a < m$)
- $c$ — the **increment** ($0 \leq c < m$)
- $X_0$ — the **seed** ($0 \leq X_0 < m$)

**Three Sub-Families (Critical for Board Exam):**

| Variant | Condition | Full Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Mixed LCG** | $c \neq 0$ | Hull-Dobell Generator |
| **Multiplicative LCG** | $c = 0$ | Pure Multiplicative Generator |
| **Additive LCG** | $a = 1$ | Fibonacci-like generator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull-Dobell Theorem (Full Period Condition):** An LCG will achieve the **maximum possible period** $p = m$ if and only if:
> 1. $\gcd(c, m) = 1$ (i.e., $c$ and $m$ are co-prime)
> 2. $a - 1$ is divisible by every prime factor of $m$
> 3. $a - 1$ is divisible by **4** if $m$ is divisible by 4

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **LCG Recurrence** | $X_{i+1} = (a X_i + c) \bmod m$ | Core generation equation |
| **Normalization to $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$** | $U_i = X_i / (m - 1)$ | Scales to $[0, 1]$ |
| **Period (Mixed LCG)** | $p = m$ | If Hull-Dobell conditions hold |
| **Period (Multiplicative LCG)** | $p \leq m - 1$ | Maximum when $m$ is prime |
| **Mid-Square Normalization** | $U_i = X_i / 10^d$ | For $d$-digit seeds |
| **Multiplicative LCG Recurrence** | $X_{i+1} = (a \cdot X_i) \bmod m$ | When $c = 0$ |
| **Random Integer in $[a, b]$** | $I = a + \lfloor (b - a + 1) U_i \rfloor$ | Discrete uniform sampling |
| **$\chi^2$ Test Statistic** | $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | Tests uniformity goodness-of-fit |
| **KS Test Statistic** | $D = \max \vert F_n(x) - F(x) \vert$ | Tests uniformity goodness-of-fit |
| **Standard Normal via Box-Muller** | $Z = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2)$ | Generates $N(0,1)$ from $U_1, U_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world **discrete-event simulation packages** (Arena, FlexSim, AnyLogic), the default PRNG is a 64-bit Mersenne Twister (period $2^{19937} - 1$). The LCG is the **pedagogical ancestor** of these industrial generators and remains a critical building block for understanding statistical properties.

## 2.3 Generation of Random Variates (Inverse Transform Principle)

Once we have a $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$ generator, any distribution $F(x)$ with a closed-form inverse $F^{-1}(u)$ can be sampled as:

$$X = F^{-1}(U)$$

| Target Distribution | $F^{-1}(u)$ Expression | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Exponential($\lambda$)** | $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(1 - U)$ | Inter-arrival times in queues |
| **Weibull($k,\lambda$)** | $X = \lambda (-\ln(1 - U))^{1/k}$ | Equipment failure times |
| **Discrete Uniform** | Map $U$ to nearest bin | Lottery, dice |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Mid-Square Method (Board Favorite)

**Problem:** Generate the first 5 pseudo-random numbers in $[0,1)$ using the **Mid-Square Method** with a **4-digit seed** $X_0 = 5497$.

> **Step 0 — Initialization.** We have seed $X_0 = 5497$, with $d = 4$ digits.

**Step 1 — Compute $X_1$:** Square the seed.

$$X_0^2 = 5497^2 = 30,217,009$$

Pad to $2d = 8$ digits: `30217009`. Extract the **middle 4 digits**: $\mathbf{0217}$.

$$X_1 = 0217 \quad\Rightarrow\quad U_1 = \frac{217}{10000} = 0.0217$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Squaring a 4-digit number can produce up to 8 digits. The middle 4 are extracted by counting 2 digits in from both ends.

**Step 2 — Compute $X_2$:** Square $X_1$.

$$X_1^2 = 217^2 = 47{,}089$$

Pad to 8 digits: `00047089`. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{0470}$.

$$X_2 = 0470 \quad\Rightarrow\quad U_2 = 0.0470$$

**Step 3 — Compute $X_3$:** Square $X_2$.

$$X_2^2 = 470^2 = 220{,}900$$

Pad: `00220900`. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{2209}$.

$$X_3 = 2209 \quad\Rightarrow\quad U_3 = 0.2209$$

**Step 4 — Compute $X_4$:** Square $X_3$.

$$X_3^2 = 2209^2 = 4{,}879{,}681$$

Pad: `48796881`. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{7968}$.

$$X_4 = 7968 \quad\Rightarrow\quad U_4 = 0.7968$$

**Step 5 — Compute $X_5$:** Square $X_4$.

$$X_4^2 = 7968^2 = 63{,}479{,}024$$

Pad: `63479024`. Middle 4 digits: $\mathbf{4790}$.

$$X_5 = 4790 \quad\Rightarrow\quad U_5 = 0.4790$$

**Consolidated Output Table:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $X_i^2$ (Padded) | Middle 4 Digits | $U_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5497 | 30217009 | — | — |
| 1 | 0217 | 00047089 | 0217 | 0.0217 |
| 2 | 0470 | 00220900 | 0470 | 0.0470 |
| 3 | 2209 | 04879681 | 2209 | 0.2209 |
| 4 | 7968 | 63479024 | 7968 | 0.7968 |
| 5 | 4790 | 22944100 | 9441 | 0.4790 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Pitfall (called out by KTU Examiners):** Students often forget to **pad with leading zeros** before extracting the middle digits. For example, in Step 1, $217^2 = 47089$ MUST be written as `00047089` to extract the middle correctly. Missing the pad → lose 1 mark per instance.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Linear Congruential Generator

**Problem:** Given $a = 5, \ c = 3, \ m = 16, \ X_0 = 7$. Generate the first 6 pseudo-random numbers in $[0,1)$.

**Recurrence:** $X_{i+1} = (5 X_i + 3) \bmod 16$.

**Step 0:** $X_0 = 7$.

**Step 1:**
$$X_1 = (5 \times 7 + 3) \bmod 16 = 38 \bmod 16 = 6$$

**Step 2:**
$$X_2 = (5 \times 6 + 3) \bmod 16 = 33 \bmod 16 = 1$$

**Step 3:**
$$X_3 = (5 \times 1 + 3) \bmod 16 = 8 \bmod 16 = 8$$

**Step 4:**
$$X_4 = (5 \times 8 + 3) \bmod 16 = 43 \bmod 16 = 11$$

**Step 5:**
$$X_5 = (5 \times 11 + 3) \bmod 16 = 58 \bmod 16 = 10$$

**Step 6:**
$$X_6 = (5 \times 10 + 3) \bmod 16 = 53 \bmod 16 = 5$$

**Consolidated Output Table:**

| $i$ | $X_i$ | $U_i = X_i / (m-1) = X_i / 15$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 7 | 0.4667 |
| 1 | 6 | 0.4000 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.0667 |
| 3 | 8 | 0.5333 |
| 4 | 11 | 0.7333 |
| 5 | 10 | 0.6667 |
| 6 | 5 | 0.3333 |

> **Hull-Dobell Check (KTU Bonus Mark):**
> - $m = 16 = 2^4$, so its only prime factor is 2.
> - $a - 1 = 4$, divisible by prime 2. ✓
> - $m$ is divisible by 4, so we need $a - 1$ divisible by 4. $4$ IS divisible by $4$. ✓
> - $\gcd(c, m) = \gcd(3, 16) = 1$. ✓
> - **Therefore, the period is $p = m = 16$ (maximum possible).**

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Exponential Variate Generation

**Problem:** Inter-arrival times in a queue follow Exponential($\lambda = 2$). Generate one variate using $U_1 = 0.65$.

Using $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(1 - U)$:

$$X = -\frac{1}{2} \ln(1 - 0.65) = -0.5 \times \ln(0.35) = -0.5 \times (-1.0498) = 0.5249$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The inverse transform $F^{-1}(u) = -\lambda^{-1} \ln(1-u)$ arises from solving $u = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing an RNG

| Criterion | Mid-Square | LCG (Mixed) | LCG (Multiplicative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Period | Very short, unpredictable | Up to $m$ | Up to $m - 1$ |
| Speed | Slow (large integer ops) | Very fast | Very fast |
| Statistical Quality | Poor | Good (with proper params) | Good |
| Implementation Ease | Trivial | Trivial | Trivial |
| Use in Modern Tools | None | Historical / Teaching | Mersenne Twister ancestor |
| **KTU Weightage** | **High (easy scoring)** | **Highest** | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Inside a Linear Congruential Generator

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how an LCG transforms one random state into the next.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seedX["Seed X0"] --> mul["Multiplier: a times X"]
    mul --> add["Adder: plus c"]
    add --> mod["Modulus Operation: mod m"]
    mod --> outX["New State X1"]
    outX --> norm["Normalization: divide by m minus 1"]
    norm --> outU["Uniform U1 in 0 to 1"]
    outX --> feed["Feedback Loop"]
    feed --> mul
```

## 4.2 Operational Flow: Mid-Square Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Start: Choose 4-digit Seed X0"]) --> square["Square X0: compute X0 squared"]
    square --> pad["Pad Result to 8 digits"]
    pad --> mid["Extract Middle 4 Digits"]
    mid --> store["Set X1 equal to middle digits"]
    store --> uval["Compute U1: X1 divided by 10000"]
    uval --> check{"Has period been reached?"}
    check -- "No" --> square
    check -- "Yes" --> stop(["Stop: Output the sequence"])
```

## 4.3 RNG Pipeline: From Seed to Stochastic Simulation Input

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG["Stage 1: PRNG Core"]
        seed["Seed"] --> lcg["LCG Algorithm"]
        lcg --> u01["Uniform 0 to 1"]
    end
    subgraph TG["Stage 2: Transformation"]
        u01 --> inv["Inverse Transform F inverse"]
        inv --> target["Target Distribution X"]
    end
    subgraph SIM["Stage 3: Simulation"]
        target --> events["Stochastic Events"]
        events --> out["System Output Metrics"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Chi-Square Test Workflow (Uniformity Validation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Collect n random numbers from PRNG"] --> B["Divide 0 to 1 into k equal intervals"]
    B --> C["Count observations O sub i in each interval"]
    C --> D["Compute Expected count: E equals n divided by k"]
    D --> E["Compute Chi-square statistic: sum of O minus E squared divided by E"]
    E --> F["Lookup critical Chi-square value at alpha and k minus 1"]
    F --> G{"Statistic less than Critical?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Pass: Sequence is uniform"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Fail: Reject generator"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice in Section 4.1 how the output $X_1$ is *fed back* into the multiplier. This **closed-loop feedback** is the defining architectural feature distinguishing an LCG from a one-way hash function — it is also why the sequence is **deterministic** and **reproducible** by seed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Pseudo-Random Number Generator. List the two essential properties a sequence of pseudo-random numbers must satisfy for use in simulation.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)** is a deterministic algorithm that, given an initial value called a **seed**, produces a sequence of numbers that *appears* random and satisfies standard statistical tests of randomness.

The two essential properties are:

1. **Uniformity:** Each $U_i$ must be uniformly distributed on $(0, 1)$, i.e., $f(u) = 1$ for $0 \leq u \leq 1$.
2. **Independence:** The successive values $U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n$ must be statistically independent (no auto-correlation).

*(Optional third property for 3-mark depth: Reproducibility — same seed gives the same sequence.)* — **3 marks**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the period of a random number generator? State the Hull-Dobell theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

The **period** of a PRNG is the number of distinct values generated before the sequence begins to repeat. A longer period is essential to avoid correlation in long simulations.

> **Hull-Dobell Theorem:** A Linear Congruential Generator $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$ achieves full period $p = m$ if and only if:
> 1. $\gcd(c, m) = 1$
> 2. $(a - 1)$ is divisible by every prime factor of $m$
> 3. $(a - 1)$ is divisible by $4$ if $m$ is divisible by $4$ — **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1). `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Mid-Square Method** of random number generation with its algorithm. What are its main limitations? **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

> The **Mid-Square Method**, proposed by **Von Neumann (1949)**, is the earliest and conceptually simplest PRNG.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose a $d$-digit seed $X_0$.
2. Compute $Y = X_i^2$.
3. Pad $Y$ to $2d$ digits using leading zeros.
4. Extract the **middle $d$ digits** as $X_{i+1}$.
5. If $X_{i+1} = 0$, terminate (degenerate case).
6. Compute $U_{i+1} = X_{i+1} / 10^d$.
7. Set $i \leftarrow i + 1$, go to Step 2.

**Example Trace (with $X_0 = 5497, d = 4$):**
- $X_0^2 = 30{,}217{,}009$ → Middle 4 digits: $0217$ → $X_1 = 217$, $U_1 = 0.0217$
- $X_1^2 = 47{,}089$ → Padded: $00047089$ → $X_2 = 470$, $U_2 = 0.0470$
- $X_2^2 = 220{,}900$ → Padded: $00220900$ → $X_3 = 2209$, $U_3 = 0.2209$

**Limitations:**
- **Short period:** Many seeds rapidly decay to zero (the famous "**vanishing seed**" problem).
- **Statistical weakness:** Fails standard randomness tests (KS, Chi-square) for large samples.
- **Dependence on seed selection:** Period is unpredictable a priori.
- **Not suitable for high-fidelity simulation** — supplanted by LCGs in practice.

**— 7 marks**

---

**Q: (b)** Generate the first 5 pseudo-random numbers using the **Linear Congruential Generator** with parameters $a = 3, \ c = 5, \ m = 32, \ X_0 = 7$. Verify the Hull-Dobell conditions. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Recurrence:** $X_{i+1} = (3X_i + 5) \bmod 32$

| $i$ | Calculation | $X_i$ | $U_i = X_i / 31$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Given | 7 | 0.2258 |
| 1 | $(3 \times 7 + 5) \bmod 32 = 26$ | 26 | 0.8387 |
| 2 | $(3 \times 26 + 5) \bmod 32 = 83 \bmod 32 = 19$ | 19 | 0.6129 |
| 3 | $(3 \times 19 + 5) \bmod 32 = 62 \bmod 32 = 30$ | 30 | 0.9677 |
| 4 | $(3 \times 30 + 5) \bmod 32 = 95 \bmod 32 = 31$ | 31 | 1.0000 |
| 5 | $(3 \times 31 + 5) \bmod 32 = 98 \bmod 32 = 2$ | 2 | 0.0645 |

**Hull-Dobell Verification (for full period $p = 32$):**
- $m = 32 = 2^5$. Prime factors: $\{2\}$.
- $\gcd(c, m) = \gcd(5, 32) = 1$. ✓
- $a - 1 = 2$. Is $2$ divisible by prime factor $2$? **Yes.** ✓
- $m = 32$ is divisible by $4$. Is $a - 1 = 2$ divisible by $4$? **No.** ✗
- **Conclusion:** Hull-Dobell condition 3 is violated → period $p < 32$ (sub-maximal).

**— 7 marks**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice). `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Linear Congruential Generator** in detail. Compare **Mixed LCG** and **Multiplicative LCG** with examples. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

> An **LCG** generates a sequence of integers using the modular recurrence $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$, where $a, c, m$ are constants and $X_0$ is the seed.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Mixed LCG | Multiplicative LCG |
|---|---|---|
| Recurrence | $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$ | $X_{i+1} = (aX_i) \bmod m$ |
| Increment $c$ | $c \neq 0$ | $c = 0$ |
| Maximum Period | $p = m$ (with Hull-Dobell) | $p = m - 1$ (requires $m$ prime) |
| Statistical Quality | Better | Slightly weaker |
| Example | $a=5, c=3, m=16$ | $a=5, c=0, m=31$ (prime) |

**Mixed LCG Example:** $a = 5, c = 3, m = 16, X_0 = 7$ (worked in §3.2 above).

**Multiplicative LCG Example:** $a = 13, m = 100, X_0 = 1$.
- $X_1 = (13 \times 1) \bmod 100 = 13$
- $X_2 = (13 \times 13) \bmod 100 = 169 \bmod 100 = 69$
- $X_3 = (13 \times 69) \bmod 100 = 897 \bmod 100 = 97$

**— 7 marks**

---

**Q: (b)** Explain the **Inverse Transform Method** for generating random variates from a non-uniform distribution. Generate one variate from the **Exponential distribution** with mean $4$, using $U = 0.73$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

> The **Inverse Transform Method** is the most general technique for converting $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$ samples into samples from any continuous distribution whose CDF $F(x)$ is invertible.

**Algorithm:**
1. Generate $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1)$.
2. Compute $X = F^{-1}(U)$, where $F^{-1}$ is the inverse of the target CDF.
3. $X$ is the desired random variate.

**Mathematical Justification:** If $F$ is continuous and strictly increasing, then $F(X) \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1)$. Therefore setting $X = F^{-1}(U)$ with $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$ gives $X$ with CDF $F$.

**Exponential Derivation:** For Exponential($\lambda$), $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$.

$$u = 1 - e^{-\lambda x} \quad\Rightarrow\quad e^{-\lambda x} = 1 - u \quad\Rightarrow\quad x = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(1 - u)$$

**Numerical Calculation:** Mean = 4 implies $\lambda = 1/4 = 0.25$. With $U = 0.73$:

$$X = -\frac{1}{0.25} \ln(1 - 0.73) = -4 \times \ln(0.27) = -4 \times (-1.3093) = 5.2373$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We used $\ln(0.27) = -1.3093$ from standard logarithm tables. The minus sign flips it positive. Multiplying by $-4$ gives the variate.

**Result:** $X = 5.2373$ units (e.g., hours, minutes, depending on application).

**— 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting padding in Mid-Square:** A student who writes $217^2 = 47089$ and then directly takes the "middle 4 digits" as $4708$ (wrong!) loses 1–2 marks. You **must** explicitly state padding.
> 2. **Confusing Mid-Square normalization:** Use $U_i = X_i / 10^d$, not $U_i = X_i / 10^{d-1}$.
> 3. **Missing Hull-Dobell verification:** In any LCG question, always state whether full period is achieved. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 4. **Sign error in Exponential transform:** The minus sign inside the logarithm is critical; missing it yields a negative variate.
> 5. **No mention of Seed:** A 14-mark LCG answer that omits $X_0$ (seed) loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Modulus misuse:** $83 \bmod 32 = 19$ (since $32 \times 2 = 64$, $83 - 64 = 19$). Students often incorrectly write $83 \bmod 32 = 51$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Random Number Generators**

- **PRNG vs TRNG:** Pseudo = deterministic, reproducible; True = physical, non-reproducible. Simulations use PRNGs.
- **Three core properties:** Uniformity, Independence, Reproducibility.
- **LCG Recurrence (must memorize):** $X_{i+1} = (aX_i + c) \bmod m$.
- **Mid-Square Rule:** Square → Pad to $2d$ → Take middle $d$ digits → Divide by $10^d$.
- **Normalization formula:** $U_i = X_i / (m - 1)$ for LCG, $U_i = X_i / 10^d$ for Mid-Square.
- **Hull-Dobell Theorem (3 conditions):** Co-primality of $c$ and $m$; divisibility of $a-1$ by all prime factors of $m$; divisibility of $a-1$ by $4$ if $m$ is divisible by $4$.
- **Full Period (Mixed LCG):** $p = m$. **Full Period (Multiplicative LCG):** $p = m - 1$ when $m$ is prime.
- **Inverse Transform:** $X = F^{-1}(U)$; for Exponential($\lambda$): $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(1 - U)$.
- **Test for Uniformity:** Chi-Square test statistic $\chi^2_0 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$.
- **Test for Independence:** Runs test, Autocorrelation test.
- **Practical seed values:** 0, 1, current time (in nanoseconds).
- **Mersenne Twister:** Modern standard with period $2^{19937} - 1$.
- **Multiplication factor for marks:** Always show **padding steps** in Mid-Square and **Hull-Dobell checks** in LCG — these are the two top mark-grabbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests for Random Numbers: Frequency and Poker Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tests for Random Numbers: Frequency and Poker Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Random Numbers in System Simulation

A **Random Number** in the context of discrete-event system simulation is a sequence of real numbers $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ generated independently and identically (i.i.d.) from a continuous Uniform distribution on the interval **[0, 1)**. These numbers form the **stochastic engine** of any Monte Carlo or simulation model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Random numbers are the basic building blocks of simulation. Since the validity of a simulation output is critically dependent on the quality of the random numbers used, every random number generator (RNG) must be subjected to statistical tests to confirm that the produced sequence possesses the two essential properties of randomness:*
> 1. **Uniformity (Distribution Property)** — verified by the **Frequency Test**.
> 2. **Independence (Serial Property)** — verified by the **Poker Test**, Runs Test, Autocorrelation Test, etc.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dice Factory"

Imagine a factory that claims to manufacture perfectly fair dice.

- **Frequency Test** is like rolling the die **10,000 times** and checking whether each face (1 to 6) shows up approximately 1/6th of the time. If one face appears far more often, the die is "loaded" — i.e., the RNG is biased.
- **Poker Test** is like studying **groups of five consecutive rolls** and checking whether the patterns (like "three 4's and two 7's" — analogous to "three of a kind") occur with the expected probability. If the die is "loaded" or if one roll influences the next (a "memory" die), the pattern distribution will be skewed.

Thus, **Frequency Test = "Are the numbers fair?"** and **Poker Test = "Do the numbers remember each other?"**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | Level of Significance | **0.05** (commonly used in KTU) |
| $k$ | Number of class intervals | Typically **10, 20, 50, or 100** |
| $n$ | Sample size (number of RNs) | Generally $n \geq 1000$ |
| $m$ | Digits per group in Poker Test | **3, 4, or 5** (most common: **5**) |
| $d.o.f.$ | Degrees of Freedom | $k-1$ for Frequency Test |

> [!NOTE]
> The null hypothesis $H_0$ for both tests is: *"The generated sequence is uniformly distributed and independent."* We fail to reject $H_0$ if the computed test statistic does not exceed the critical value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Frequency Test (Test for Uniformity)

The **Frequency Test** checks whether each number in the generated sequence is equally likely to occur in any sub-interval of $[0, 1)$. It is the most fundamental empirical test of randomness.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Partition the interval** $[0, 1)$ into $k$ non-overlapping sub-intervals (classes) of equal width:
$$I_j = \left[\frac{j-1}{k},\ \frac{j}{k}\right),\quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$
2. **Generate $n$ random numbers** $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$.
3. **Count the observed frequency** $O_j$ = number of $R_i$ falling in interval $I_j$.
4. **Compute the expected frequency** under perfect uniformity:
$$E_j = \frac{n}{k},\quad \text{for all } j$$
5. **Compute the Chi-square statistic** ($\chi_0^2$):
$$\chi_0^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}$$
6. **Decision Rule**: Compare $\chi_0^2$ with the tabulated critical value $\chi_{\alpha,\, k-1}^2$.
   - If $\chi_0^2 \leq \chi_{\alpha,\, k-1}^2$ → **Accept $H_0$** (sequence is uniform).
   - If $\chi_0^2 > \chi_{\alpha,\, k-1}^2$ → **Reject $H_0$** (sequence is non-uniform).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (from Banks, Carson, Nelson, Nicol):** Each class should have an expected frequency $E_j \geq 5$. If $n = 1000$ and $k = 10$, then $E_j = 100$, which is safely above 5.

---

## 2.2 Poker Test (Test for Independence / Serial Randomness)

The **Poker Test** examines the digits of random numbers in groups to detect any serial dependence. It is named after the card game *poker*, where hand patterns (one pair, full house, etc.) are classified.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Choose the group size** $m$ (commonly $m = 3, 4,$ or $5$).
2. **Group the random numbers** into batches of $m$ digits each.
   - If using actual decimals, take the first $m$ digits of each $R_i \in [0,1)$ after the decimal point.
   - Total groups formed: $N = \lfloor n / m \rfloor$.
3. **Classify each group** into one of the **poker categories** (for $m = 5$):

| Category | Description | Probability $P_i$ (for $m=5$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| All Different | 5 distinct digits | **0.3024** |
| One Pair | Exactly two digits identical, rest distinct | **0.5040** |
| Two Pairs | Two distinct pairs + one distinct digit | **0.1080** |
| Three of a Kind (Trips) | Three identical + two distinct | **0.0720** |
| Full House | Three of a kind + a pair | **0.0090** |
| Four of a Kind (Quads) | Four identical + one distinct | **0.0045** |
| Five of a Kind (Quints) | All five identical | **0.0001** |

4. **Count observed frequencies** $O_i$ for each category.
5. **Compute expected frequencies** $E_i = N \cdot P_i$.
6. **Compute Chi-square statistic**:
$$\chi_0^2 = \sum_{i} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$
7. **Decision Rule**: With $k_c$ categories used (often we merge rare ones so $E_i \geq 5$), the degrees of freedom = $k_c - 1$. Compare with $\chi_{\alpha,\, k_c-1}^2$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Meaning | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_j = n/k$ | Expected frequency per class | Frequency Test |
| $\chi_0^2 = \sum \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}$ | Chi-square test statistic | **Both Tests** |
| $d.o.f. = k - 1$ | Degrees of freedom (Frequency) | Frequency Test |
| $N = \lfloor n/m \rfloor$ | Number of poker groups | Poker Test |
| $E_i = N \cdot P_i$ | Expected frequency per category | Poker Test |
| $P(\text{All Different}) = \frac{10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |
| $P(\text{One Pair}) = \frac{5 \cdot \binom{10}{1} \cdot \binom{9}{1} \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot \binom{5}{2}}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |
| $P(\text{Two Pairs}) = \frac{\binom{5}{2} \cdot \binom{10}{2} \cdot 8}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |
| $P(\text{Three of Kind}) = \frac{\binom{5}{3} \cdot \binom{10}{1} \cdot 9 \cdot 8}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |
| $P(\text{Full House}) = \frac{\binom{5}{3} \cdot \binom{10}{1} \cdot \binom{9}{1} \cdot \binom{2}{1}}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |
| $P(\text{Four of Kind}) = \frac{\binom{5}{4} \cdot \binom{10}{1} \cdot 9}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |
| $P(\text{Five of Kind}) = \frac{10}{10^5}$ | Probability formula | $m = 5$ Poker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sum Check:** $0.3024 + 0.5040 + 0.1080 + 0.0720 + 0.0090 + 0.0045 + 0.0001 = 1.0000$ ✓

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications**: Validating pseudo-random sequences used in CDMA spread-spectrum codes.
- **Cryptography**: Statistical vetting of cryptographic PRNGs before deployment in TLS/SSL handshakes.
- **Manufacturing & Quality Control**: Monte Carlo simulation of assembly-line bottlenecks.
- **Queueing Systems**: Validating inter-arrival and service-time samples in bank, hospital, and airport simulations.
- **Computational Finance**: Pricing derivatives via Monte Carlo (e.g., Asian options) — biased RNs produce wrong prices.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Probability Expressions for the Poker Test (m = 5)

### A. P(All Different) — All 5 digits are distinct

We are placing 5 digits from $\{0,1,\ldots,9\}$ such that no two are equal. Since the digits are independent and each has 10 choices:

$$P(\text{All Different}) = \frac{10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6}{10^5} = \frac{30240}{100000} = 0.3024$$

### B. P(One Pair) — Exactly 2 digits are identical, the other 3 are distinct

**Combinatorial reasoning**:
- **Step 1:** Choose which 2 of the 5 positions form the pair: $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ ways.
- **Step 2:** Choose the digit value for the pair: $10$ choices.
- **Step 3:** Choose the 3 distinct digits for the remaining positions from the other 9 digits: $9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7$ ways (ordered).
- **Step 4:** Permute the 3 distinct digits into the 3 remaining positions: $3! = 6$ ways.

Therefore:
$$\text{Favourable outcomes} = \binom{5}{2} \cdot 10 \cdot (9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7) \cdot 3!$$
$$= 10 \cdot 10 \cdot 504 \cdot 6 = 302400$$

$$P(\text{One Pair}) = \frac{302400}{100000} = 0.5040$$

### C. P(Two Pairs) — Two different pairs + 1 distinct digit

- **Step 1:** Choose which 2 of the 5 positions form the first pair, then which 2 of the remaining 3 form the second pair: $\dfrac{\binom{5}{2}\binom{3}{2}}{2!} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 3}{2} = 15$ ways. (Division by $2!$ removes the double-counting of which pair is "first".)
- **Step 2:** Choose the 2 digit values for the two pairs: $10 \cdot 9$ ways.
- **Step 3:** Choose the digit value for the lone remaining position: $8$ ways.
- **Step 4:** Place the lone digit — only 1 position left, so $1$ way.

$$\text{Favourable outcomes} = 15 \cdot 10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 = 10800$$

$$P(\text{Two Pairs}) = \frac{10800}{100000} = 0.1080$$

### D. P(Three of a Kind) — Exactly 3 identical digits + 2 distinct

- **Step 1:** Choose 3 of 5 positions for the triplet: $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ ways.
- **Step 2:** Choose the digit value for the triplet: $10$ ways.
- **Step 3:** Choose 2 distinct digit values from the remaining 9: $9 \cdot 8$ ways.
- **Step 4:** Permute the 2 distinct digits into 2 positions: $2! = 2$ ways.

$$\text{Favourable outcomes} = 10 \cdot 10 \cdot 72 \cdot 2 = 14400$$

$$P(\text{Three of Kind}) = \frac{14400}{100000} = 0.0720$$

### E. P(Full House) — Three of a kind + a separate pair

- **Step 1:** Choose 3 of 5 positions for the triplet: $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ ways.
- **Step 2:** Choose the triplet digit: $10$ ways.
- **Step 3:** Choose the pair digit from the remaining 9: $9$ ways.
- **Step 4:** The remaining 2 positions are forced to hold the pair — $1$ way.

$$\text{Favourable outcomes} = 10 \cdot 10 \cdot 9 \cdot 1 = 900$$

$$P(\text{Full House}) = \frac{900}{100000} = 0.0090$$

### F. P(Four of a Kind) — Exactly 4 identical + 1 distinct

- **Step 1:** Choose 4 of 5 positions for the quadruple: $\binom{5}{4} = 5$ ways.
- **Step 2:** Choose the quad digit: $10$ ways.
- **Step 3:** Choose the distinct digit from the remaining 9: $9$ ways.
- **Step 4:** The 1 remaining position is forced — $1$ way.

$$\text{Favourable outcomes} = 5 \cdot 10 \cdot 9 = 450$$

$$P(\text{Four of Kind}) = \frac{450}{100000} = 0.0045$$

### G. P(Five of a Kind) — All 5 identical

- **Step 1:** All 5 positions form one group: $1$ way.
- **Step 2:** Choose the common digit: $10$ ways.

$$P(\text{Five of Kind}) = \frac{10}{100000} = 0.0001$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Frequency Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A PRNG produced $n = 1000$ random numbers in $[0,1)$. They were partitioned into $k = 10$ equal intervals. The observed frequencies are:
> $O = [105, 92, 88, 110, 95, 100, 98, 107, 102, 103]$.
> Test uniformity at $\alpha = 0.05$. Use $\chi^2_{0.05, 9} = 16.919$.

**Step 1 — Expected frequency**:
$$E_j = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100 \quad \forall j$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(O_j - E_j)^2 / E_j$ for each interval**:

| Interval $j$ | $O_j$ | $E_j$ | $O_j - E_j$ | $(O_j - E_j)^2$ | $(O_j - E_j)^2 / E_j$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 105 | 100 | +5 | 25 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 92 | 100 | −8 | 64 | 0.64 |
| 3 | 88 | 100 | −12 | 144 | 1.44 |
| 4 | 110 | 100 | +10 | 100 | 1.00 |
| 5 | 95 | 100 | −5 | 25 | 0.25 |
| 6 | 100 | 100 | 0 | 0 | 0.00 |
| 7 | 98 | 100 | −2 | 4 | 0.04 |
| 8 | 107 | 100 | +7 | 49 | 0.49 |
| 9 | 102 | 100 | +2 | 4 | 0.04 |
| 10 | 103 | 100 | +3 | 9 | 0.09 |

**Step 3 — Sum the test statistic**:
$$\chi_0^2 = 0.25 + 0.64 + 1.44 + 1.00 + 0.25 + 0.00 + 0.04 + 0.49 + 0.04 + 0.09 = 4.24$$

**Step 4 — Decision**:
$$\chi_0^2 = 4.24 \le \chi^2_{0.05, 9} = 16.919$$

✅ **Conclusion:** Fail to reject $H_0$. The PRNG is **uniformly distributed** at the 5% significance level.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Poker Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** From $n = 5000$ random numbers in $[0,1)$, take the first 5 decimal digits of each. Thus $N = 5000$ groups of $m = 5$ digits. The observed category counts are:
>
> | Category | Observed $O_i$ |
> | :--- | :---: |
> | All Different | 1480 |
> | One Pair | 2540 |
> | Two Pairs | 560 |
> | Three of a Kind | 360 |
> | Full House | 38 |
> | Four of a Kind | 18 |
> | Five of a Kind | 4 |
>
> Test independence at $\alpha = 0.05$. $\chi^2_{0.05, 6} = 12.592$.

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Frequencies** $E_i = N \cdot P_i$ with $N = 5000$:

| Category | $P_i$ | $E_i = 5000 \cdot P_i$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| All Different | 0.3024 | **1512.00** |
| One Pair | 0.5040 | **2520.00** |
| Two Pairs | 0.1080 | **540.00** |
| Three of Kind | 0.0720 | **360.00** |
| Full House | 0.0090 | **45.00** |
| Four of Kind | 0.0045 | **22.50** |
| Five of Kind | 0.0001 | **0.50** |

> [!WARNING]
> The expected frequency for "Five of a Kind" is only 0.5, which is **far below 5**. The standard KTU rule requires us to **pool this category** with another rare one (typically "Four of a Kind" + "Five of a Kind" combined) to satisfy the rule. Let us merge the last two:
> New category "≥ Four of a Kind" → $O = 18 + 4 = 22$, $E = 22.50 + 0.50 = 23.00$.

**Step 2 — Recompute the table with $k_c = 6$ categories**:

| # | Category | $O_i$ | $E_i$ | $O_i - E_i$ | $(O_i-E_i)^2/E_i$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | All Different | 1480 | 1512.00 | −32.00 | 0.6772 |
| 2 | One Pair | 2540 | 2520.00 | +20.00 | 0.1587 |
| 3 | Two Pairs | 560 | 540.00 | +20.00 | 0.7407 |
| 4 | Three of Kind | 360 | 360.00 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 5 | Full House | 38 | 45.00 | −7.00 | 1.0889 |
| 6 | ≥ Four of Kind | 22 | 23.00 | −1.00 | 0.0435 |
| | **SUM** | 5000 | 5000.00 | — | **2.7090** |

Detailed calculations:
$$\frac{(-32)^2}{1512} = \frac{1024}{1512} = 0.6772$$
$$\frac{(20)^2}{2520} = \frac{400}{2520} = 0.1587$$
$$\frac{(20)^2}{540} = \frac{400}{540} = 0.7407$$
$$\frac{0^2}{360} = 0$$
$$\frac{(-7)^2}{45} = \frac{49}{45} = 1.0889$$
$$\frac{(-1)^2}{23} = 0.0435$$

**Step 3 — Sum**:
$$\chi_0^2 = 0.6772 + 0.1587 + 0.7407 + 0 + 1.0889 + 0.0435 = 2.7090$$

**Step 4 — Decision**:
$$\chi_0^2 = 2.709 \le \chi^2_{0.05,\ 6-1} = \chi^2_{0.05,\ 5} = 11.070$$

✅ **Conclusion:** Fail to reject $H_0$. The digits appear **independent** at the 5% level.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow of Statistical Testing of Random Numbers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: PRNG produces sequence of n random numbers in 0,1"] --> B["Choose appropriate test: Frequency or Poker"]
    B --> C["Partition interval 0,1 into k equal subintervals"]
    C --> D["Count observed frequency Oj in each subinterval"]
    D --> E["Compute expected frequency Ej = n / k"]
    E --> F["Compute chi-square statistic chi0 squared"]
    F --> G["Determine degrees of freedom = k minus 1"]
    G --> H{"Compare chi0 squared with chi alpha k-1 critical value"}
    H --> I["chi0 squared less than or equal critical: ACCEPT H0"]
    H --> J["chi0 squared greater than critical: REJECT H0"]
    I --> K["RNG is uniformly distributed"]
    J --> L["RNG is non-uniform, reject the generator"]
    K --> M["End: Generator passes Frequency Test"]
    L --> N["End: Diagnose and replace generator"]
```

```mermaid
    F -- No --> G{"Exactly one pair"}
    G -- Yes --> G1["One Pair: P = 0.5040"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Frequency Test for random numbers. State the test statistic and the decision rule used.**

**Model Answer:**
The Frequency Test (also called the **Uniformity Test**) checks whether the generated random numbers are uniformly distributed over $[0, 1)$. The interval $[0, 1)$ is divided into $k$ equal sub-intervals, and the observed frequency $O_j$ in each sub-interval is compared with the expected frequency $E_j = n/k$.

The test statistic is:
$$\chi_0^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}$$

**Decision Rule:** At significance level $\alpha$ with degrees of freedom $k-1$:
