**Final Answer:** $X_1 \approx 565.85$ hours, $X_2 \approx 148.82$ hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** A random variable $X$ has a **Triangular distribution** with minimum $a = 2$, maximum $b = 10$, and mode $c = 6$. Derive the complete inverse transformation formula (both branches) and compute the value of $X$ when $U = 0.40$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A discrete random variable $X$ takes values $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ with probabilities $\{0.2, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1\}$. Use the Inverse Transformation Method to find $X$ for $U = 0.65$. Show the complete search procedure. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Triangular Derivation + Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $a = 2$, $b = 10$, $c = 6$.

**Step 2:** Compute the threshold (cumulative at the mode):

$$U_0 = \frac{c - a}{b - a} = \frac{6 - 2}{10 - 2} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Branch 1 (when $U \le 0.5$):** $X = a + \sqrt{U(b-a)(c-a)}$:

$$X = 2 + \sqrt{U \times 8 \times 4} = 2 + \sqrt{32\,U}$$

**Step 4 — Branch 2 (when $U > 0.5$):** $X = b - \sqrt{(1-U)(b-a)(b-c)}$:

$$X = 10 - \sqrt{(1-U) \times 8 \times 4} = 10 - \sqrt{32(1-U)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply for $U = 0.40$:** Since $0.40 < 0.5$, use Branch 1:

$$X = 2 + \sqrt{32 \times 0.40} = 2 + \sqrt{12.8} = 2 + 3.5777 \approx 5.578$$

**Final Answer:** $X \approx 5.578$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — Discrete ITM (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Build the cumulative probability table:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $p_i$ | $F(x_i) = \sum_{j \le i} p_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0.2 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.5 | 0.7 |
| 3 | 2 | 0.2 | 0.9 |
| 4 | 3 | 0.1 | 1.0 |

**Step 2:** Search for smallest $i$ such that $U \le F(x_i)$:

- $i = 1$: $U = 0.65 \le 0.2$? **No.**
- $i = 2$: $U = 0.65 \le 0.7$? **Yes.** ✓

**Step 3:** Return $X = x_2 = 1$.

**Final Answer:** $X = 1$. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $\lambda$ in the Exponential formula:** Students often write $X = -\ln(U)$ instead of $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(U)$. This costs **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Inverting the Triangular distribution without the threshold check:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the condition $U \le (c-a)/(b-a)$. Omitting it leads to a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Using $U$ directly inside the Weibull formula instead of $-\ln(U)$:** A common algebraic slip. Always wrap $U$ in $-\ln(\cdot)$ before raising to the power $1/\beta$.
> 4. **Discrete ITM: comparing against $p_i$ instead of $F(x_i)$:** This is the **#1 mistake**. Always compare $U$ against the **cumulative** sum, not the individual probability. Losing 2–3 marks per instance.
> 5. **Ignoring the domain $a \le x \le b$:** The ITM is only valid on the support of the distribution. Generating a value outside the support implies a calculation error.
> 6. **Not simplifying $-\ln(1-U)$ to $-\ln(U)$:** This is a minor efficiency point; KTU accepts both, but the simplified form is **computationally preferred**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inverse Transformation Method (Module 2)**
> 
> ✅ **The Core Identity:** $X = F^{-1}(U)$, where $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.
> 
> ✅ **Probability Integral Transform Theorem:** $F(X) \sim U(0,1)$ for any continuous $X$.
> 
> ✅ **Uniform $U(a,b)$:** $X = a + (b-a)U$ — a simple **linear map**.
> 
> ✅ **Exponential($\lambda$):** $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(U)$ — note the **reciprocal** of $\lambda$.
> 
> ✅ **Weibull($\alpha, \beta$):** $X = \alpha\left[-\ln U\right]^{1/\beta}$ — reduces to Exponential when $\beta = 1$ and $\alpha = 1/\lambda$.
> 
> ✅ **Triangular $T(a,b,c)$:** Threshold $U_0 = (c-a)/(b-a)$. Two square-root branches above and below $U_0$.
> 
> ✅ **Discrete:** Compare $U$ against the **cumulative** $F(x_i)$, not the individual $p_i$. Use binary search for $O(\log n)$ performance.
> 
> ✅ **Computational Identity:** $-\ln(1-U) \equiv -\ln(U)$ because $1-U$ is also $U(0,1)$.
> 
> ✅ **Method Limitations:** Requires **closed-form inverse** CDF; for non-invertible CDFs, use **Acceptance-Rejection** or **Composition** methods.
> 
> ✅ **Engineering Domains of Use:** Queueing, reliability analysis, PERT, manufacturing, risk modeling, Monte Carlo integration.
> 
> ✅ **Examiner's Mantra:** Always show (1) PDF, (2) CDF derivation, (3) Inverse derivation, (4) Numerical substitution — in that order.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Transformation Method for Normal and Lognormal Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Transformation Method for Normal and Lognormal Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Transformation Method**: A non-uniform random variate generation technique that converts one or more independent Uniform(0,1) pseudo-random numbers into samples drawn from a target continuous probability distribution by applying a closed-form mathematical function, **without** requiring iterative numerical inversion of the cumulative distribution function (CDF) or acceptance-rejection with proposal distributions.

In the context of the **Normal Distribution** and the **Lognormal Distribution**, the Direct Transformation Method is essential because:

1. The Normal CDF $\Phi(z)$ **does not have a closed-form inverse**, so the standard inverse-transform technique $X = F^{-1}(U)$ is computationally infeasible.
2. The Lognormal distribution is structurally tied to the Normal distribution, so any direct method for Normal can be immediately leveraged to generate Lognormal variates.

For the **Normal Distribution** $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the standard direct methods are:
- **Box-Muller Transformation** (1958) — uses trigonometric mapping.
- **Marsaglia Polar Method** (1962) — a rejection-free variant of Box-Muller.
- **Central Limit Theorem Approximation** — sums of uniform variates.

For the **Lognormal Distribution**, the direct method is the **exponential mapping** of a Normal variate.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Clay Sculptor**
>
> Imagine you have a lump of featureless clay (the **uniform random number** $U$) and you want to mold it into a sculpture that looks like a bell curve (the **Normal distribution**) or a skewed hump (the **Lognormal distribution**).
>
> - The **Inverse Transform Method** would require measuring the height of the bell at every point and squeezing the clay precisely to match — slow and tedious.
> - The **Direct Transformation Method** is like a single, perfect mold (a mathematical formula). You push the clay through the mold in **one step** and out comes the correctly shaped sculpture. The mold is designed using polar geometry (for Normal) or exponential stretching (for Lognormal).

## 1.3 Why These Methods Matter in Simulation

| Property | Value (KTU High-Yield) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Distribution** | Most ubiquitous continuous distribution; models sums of small independent effects (CLT) |
| **Lognormal Distribution** | Models positive, right-skewed phenomena (lifetimes, incomes, rainfall, stock prices) |
| **Generation Difficulty** | Normal has no closed-form $F^{-1}$ — needs direct transformation |
| **Engineering Applications** | Reliability engineering, queueing theory, financial modeling, quality control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEIET724 Module 2)**: The Direct Transformation Method is a mandatory topic because it provides the foundational bridge between the **Uniform(0,1)** pseudo-random generator (the engine of every simulation) and the **Normal/Lognormal** distributions (the most commonly used output distributions in stochastic models).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Box-Muller Transformation (Direct Method for Normal)

### 2.1.1 The Core Concept

Given two independent random numbers $U_1, U_2 \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, the Box-Muller transformation produces **two independent** standard Normal variates $Z_1, Z_2 \sim N(0, 1)$ in a single step:

$$Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cdot \cos(2\pi U_2)$$

$$Z_2 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cdot \sin(2\pi U_2)$$

To obtain a Normal variate with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, simply apply the affine transformation:

$$X = \mu + \sigma Z$$

### 2.1.2 Why It Works (The Polar Geometry Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Foundation**: Box-Muller exploits the fact that if $(Z_1, Z_2)$ are independent $N(0,1)$, then in **polar coordinates** $(R, \Theta)$ they decompose as:
> - $\Theta \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 2\pi)$
> - $R^2 \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda = 1/2)$ (i.e., mean 2)
>
> Box-Muller simply **inverts this decomposition**.

The relationship is:

$$R = \sqrt{Z_1^2 + Z_2^2} = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1}$$

$$\Theta = \arctan\left(\frac{Z_2}{Z_1}\right) = 2\pi U_2$$

## 2.2 The Marsaglia Polar Method (Rejection-Free Direct Method)

A faster, non-trigonometric variant. Steps:

1. Generate $V_1, V_2 \sim \text{Uniform}(-1, 1)$ (by setting $V_i = 2U_i - 1$).
2. Compute $W = V_1^2 + V_2^2$.
3. **If** $W \geq 1$ or $W = 0$ **then go back to step 1** (rejection step).
4. Compute the multiplier:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{-2 \ln W}{W}}$$

5. Set:

$$Z_1 = V_1 \cdot V, \quad Z_2 = V_2 \cdot V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantage over Box-Muller**: No trigonometric function calls (sine/cosine), which were historically expensive. Modern implementations prefer this method.

## 2.3 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) Approximation

When speed matters more than perfect accuracy, the CLT provides a direct (approximate) Normal generator:

- Generate $U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n$ i.i.d. $\text{Uniform}(0,1)$ (typically $n = 12$).
- Form the sum $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i$.
- Standardize:

$$Z = \frac{S - n/2}{\sqrt{n/12}} = \left(\frac{12}{n}\right)^{1/2} \left(S - \frac{n}{2}\right)$$

For $n = 12$:

$$Z = \sum_{i=1}^{12} U_i - 6$$

This $Z$ is approximately $N(0, 1)$.

## 2.4 The Direct Method for Lognormal Distribution

### 2.4.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A positive random variable $X$ is **Lognormal** with parameters $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\sigma > 0$ if and only if $Y = \ln X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

The **Direct Transformation** is therefore:

$$\boxed{X = e^Y \quad \text{where} \quad Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)}$$

### 2.4.2 The Lognormal PDF

Using the change-of-variables theorem with $Y = \ln X$, $\frac{dY}{dX} = \frac{1}{X}$:

$$f_X(x) = f_Y(\ln x) \cdot \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right], \quad x > 0$$

### 2.4.3 Moments of the Lognormal Distribution

| Moment | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Mean** $E[X]$ | $\exp\left(\mu + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ |
| **Variance** $\text{Var}(X)$ | $\exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]$ |
| **Median** | $\exp(\mu)$ |
| **Mode** | $\exp(\mu - \sigma^2)$ |
| **$k$-th Moment** $E[X^k]$ | $\exp\left(k\mu + \dfrac{k^2 \sigma^2}{2}\right)$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-z^2 / 2)$ | $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$ |
| Normal to Standard | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $Z \sim N(0,1)$ |
| Box-Muller (pair) | $Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2)$ | $U_1, U_2 \sim U(0,1)$ |
| Box-Muller (pair) | $Z_2 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \sin(2\pi U_2)$ | $U_1, U_2 \sim U(0,1)$ |
| Marsaglia Factor | $V = \sqrt{-2 \ln W / W}$ | $W = V_1^2 + V_2^2 < 1$ |
| CLT Approximation ($n=12$) | $Z \approx \sum_{i=1}^{12} U_i - 6$ | Approximate $N(0,1)$ |
| Lognormal Direct Method | $X = e^Y$, with $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $X > 0$ |
| Lognormal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | $x > 0$ |
| Lognormal Mean | $E[X] = \exp(\mu + \sigma^2 / 2)$ | $X > 0$ |
| Lognormal Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]$ | $X > 0$ |
| Median (Lognormal) | $\tilde{X} = \exp(\mu)$ | Skew-distribution pivot |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reliability Engineering**: Time-to-failure of electronic components and mechanical systems is frequently Lognormal (because failure often results from multiplicative degradation processes).
- **Telecommunications**: Signal-to-noise ratio modeling in wireless channels.
- **Manufacturing**: Dimensional tolerances of machined parts.
- **Financial Simulation**: Stock prices over short horizons (Black-Scholes framework).
- **Queueing Systems**: Service time and inter-arrival time modeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Box-Muller Transformation

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Two Independent Standard Normals

Let $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ be independent random variables, each with the standard Normal distribution $N(0, 1)$. Their joint PDF is the product of their marginals (since they are independent):

$$f_{Z_1, Z_2}(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z_1^2 / 2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z_2^2 / 2}$$

$$f_{Z_1, Z_2}(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{z_1^2 + z_2^2}{2}\right)$$

### 3.1.2 Transform to Polar Coordinates

Define the polar transformation:

$$R = \sqrt{Z_1^2 + Z_2^2}, \quad \Theta = \arctan\left(\frac{Z_2}{Z_1}\right)$$

with inverse:

$$Z_1 = R \cos\Theta, \quad Z_2 = R \sin\Theta$$

The Jacobian of the transformation from $(z_1, z_2)$ to $(r, \theta)$ is:

$$J = \begin{vmatrix} \cos\theta & -r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & r\cos\theta \end{vmatrix} = r\cos^2\theta + r\sin^2\theta = r$$

### 3.1.3 Joint PDF in Polar Coordinates

Substituting $z_1^2 + z_2^2 = r^2$ and multiplying by the Jacobian $r$:

$$f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right) \cdot r = \frac{r}{2\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right)$$

This joint PDF **separates** into a function of $r$ alone and a function of $\theta$ alone:

$$f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2\pi}}_{f_\Theta(\theta)} \cdot \underbrace{r \, \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right)}_{f_R(r)}$$

### 3.1.4 Marginal Distributions of $R$ and $\Theta$

**For $\Theta$** (integrating out $r$ from $0$ to $\infty$):

$$f_\Theta(\theta) = \int_0^\infty r \, \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right) dr = \frac{1}{2\pi}, \quad 0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$$

So $\Theta \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 2\pi)$.

**For $R$** (integrating out $\theta$ from $0$ to $2\pi$):

$$f_R(r) = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{r}{2\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right) d\theta = r \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right), \quad r \geq 0$$

This is the **Rayleigh distribution**. If we let $W = R^2$, then $W$ has a transformed PDF. Using the change-of-variables formula with $\frac{dW}{dR} = 2R$:

$$f_W(w) = f_R(\sqrt{w}) \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{w}} = \sqrt{w} \exp\left(-\frac{w}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{w}} = \frac{1}{2} \exp\left(-\frac{w}{2}\right), \quad w \geq 0$$

This is the **Exponential distribution** with mean $2$ (rate $\lambda = 1/2$).

### 3.1.5 Inverting the Decomposition

Now we use the **inverse transform method** on $R$ and $\Theta$:

**For $\Theta \sim U(0, 2\pi)$**:

$$\Theta = 2\pi U_2 \quad \text{where} \quad U_2 \sim U(0,1)$$

**For $W = R^2 \sim \text{Exponential}(1/2)$**:

The CDF of $W$ is $F_W(w) = 1 - \exp(-w/2)$. Setting $U_1 = F_W(w) = 1 - \exp(-w/2)$:

$$\exp(-w/2) = 1 - U_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad w = -2 \ln(1 - U_1)$$

Since $1 - U_1$ is also Uniform(0,1), we can equivalently write $w = -2 \ln U_1$. Therefore:

$$R = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1}$$

### 3.1.6 Final Box-Muller Equations

Substituting $R$ and $\Theta$ back into the inverse polar transformation:

$$Z_1 = R \cos\Theta = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cdot \cos(2\pi U_2)$$

$$Z_2 = R \sin\Theta = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cdot \sin(2\pi U_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation**: Since the joint PDF of $(R, \Theta)$ factored into independent marginals, $R$ and $\Theta$ are independent, and so are the resulting $Z_1$ and $Z_2$. Both are standard Normal. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lognormal PDF

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with PDF:

$$f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

Define the random variable $X = e^Y$. The transformation is monotonically increasing on $\mathbb{R} \to (0, \infty)$, with inverse $Y = \ln X$ and derivative:

$$\frac{dY}{dX} = \frac{1}{X}$$

### 3.2.2 Apply the Change-of-Variables Theorem

For a monotonic transformation:

$$f_X(x) = f_Y(y) \cdot \left|\frac{dy}{dx}\right| = f_Y(\ln x) \cdot \frac{1}{x}$$

Substituting $y = \ln x$:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{x}$$

$$\boxed{f_X(x) = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right], \quad x > 0}$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation of the Lognormal Mean

By definition of the expected value, using the substitution $y = \ln x$ so $x = e^y$ and $dx = e^y \, dy$:

$$E[X] = \int_0^\infty x \cdot f_X(x) \, dx = \int_{-\infty}^\infty e^y \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] dy$$

Combine the exponentials:

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[y - \frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] dy$$

Complete the square in the exponent:

$$y - \frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[y^2 - 2\mu y + \mu^2 - 2\sigma^2 y\right]$$

$$= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[y^2 - 2(\mu + \sigma^2)y + \mu^2\right]$$

$$= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2 - (\mu + \sigma^2)^2 + \mu^2\right]$$

$$= -\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{(\mu + \sigma^2)^2 - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

The constant term evaluates to:

$$\frac{(\mu + \sigma^2)^2 - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{\mu^2 + 2\mu\sigma^2 + \sigma^4 - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2} = \mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$$

So:

$$E[X] = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) \int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] dy$$

The integral is **1** because it is the integral of a Normal density $N(\mu + \sigma^2, \sigma^2)$ over its full support. Therefore:

$$\boxed{E[X] = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)}$$

### 3.2.4 Derivation of the Lognormal Variance

By definition, $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$. First, compute $E[X^2]$:

$$E[X^2] = \int_0^\infty x^2 f_X(x) dx$$

Using the same substitution $y = \ln x$:

$$E[X^2] = \int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{2y} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] dy$$

By the same completion-of-squares technique as above, with the exponent $2y - \frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}$, the constant term evaluates to $2\mu + 2\sigma^2$. So:

$$E[X^2] = \exp(2\mu + 2\sigma^2)$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \exp(2\mu + 2\sigma^2) - \exp\left(2\mu + \sigma^2\right)$$

Factor out $\exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Box-Muller)

**Problem**: Given $U_1 = 0.532$ and $U_2 = 0.184$, generate one standard Normal variate using the Box-Muller transformation.

**Step 1**: Compute the magnitude term:

$$R = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} = \sqrt{-2 \ln(0.532)} = \sqrt{-2 \times (-0.6313)} = \sqrt{1.2626} = 1.1236$$

**Step 2**: Compute the angle term:

$$\Theta = 2\pi U_2 = 2\pi \times 0.184 = 1.1561 \text{ radians}$$

**Step 3**: Compute the two standard normal variates:

$$Z_1 = R \cos\Theta = 1.1236 \times \cos(1.1561) = 1.1236 \times 0.4078 = 0.4582$$

$$Z_2 = R \sin\Theta = 1.1236 \times \sin(1.1561) = 1.1236 \times 0.9131 = 1.0260$$

**Step 4** (Optional): To obtain $N(5, 4)$ (i.e., $\mu = 5, \sigma = 2$):

$$X_1 = 5 + 2(0.4582) = 5.916, \quad X_2 = 5 + 2(1.0260) = 7.052$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Lognormal)

**Problem**: If $Y \sim N(3, 0.25)$ and $U_1 = 0.421, U_2 = 0.687$, generate a Lognormal variate.

**Step 1**: Generate a standard normal $Z$ using Box-Muller:

$$Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln(0.421)} \cos(2\pi \times 0.687) = \sqrt{1.7308} \cos(4.3172) = 1.3156 \times (-0.3854) = -0.5071$$

**Step 2**: Transform to $N(3, 0.25)$ with $\sigma = 0.5$:

$$Y = 3 + 0.5 \times (-0.5071) = 2.7464$$

**Step 3**: Apply the exponential mapping:

$$X = e^Y = e^{2.7464} = 15.58$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification**: The mean of this Lognormal distribution is $E[X] = e^{3 + 0.125} = e^{3.125} \approx 22.79$, so the generated value $X = 15.58$ is reasonably close to typical values from this distribution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Box-Muller Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Pseudo-Random Number Generator] --> B[Generate U1 and U2 independently from Uniform 0,1]
    B --> C[Compute R = square root of negative 2 times natural log of U1]
    B --> D[Compute Theta = 2 times pi times U2]
    C --> E[Compute Z1 = R times cosine of Theta]
    C --> F[Compute Z2 = R times sine of Theta]
    D --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G[Z1 is Standard Normal N 0,1]
    F --> H[Z2 is Standard Normal N 0,1]
    G --> I[Optional: Affine Transform X = mu + sigma times Z]
    H --> I
    I --> J[END: X is Normal N of mu, sigma squared]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style J fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style G fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style H fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Marsaglia Polar Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START] --> B[Generate U1 and U2 from Uniform 0,1]
    B --> C[Set V1 = 2 times U1 minus 1 and V2 = 2 times U2 minus 1]
    C --> D[Compute W = V1 squared plus V2 squared]
    D --> E{Is W greater than or equal to 1 OR is W equal to 0?}
    E -- YES --> B
    E -- NO --> F[Compute V = square root of negative 2 times natural log of W divided by W]
    F --> G[Z1 = V1 times V]
    F --> H[Z2 = V2 times V]
    G --> I[Z1 is Standard Normal]
    H --> J[Z2 is Standard Normal]
    I --> K[END]
    J --> K

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style K fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style E fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Complete Normal-to-Lognormal Transformation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PRNG Source: Uniform 0,1] -->|U1, U2| B[Box-Muller Direct Method]
    B -->|Z1, Z2| C[Standard Normal N 0,1]
    C -->|Y = mu + sigma times Z1| D[Normal N of mu, sigma squared]
    D -->|X = e to the power Y| E[Lognormal variate X]
    E --> F[Application Domain]
    F --> F1[Reliability: Time to Failure]
    F --> F2[Finance: Stock Price]
    F --> F3[Telecom: Signal Strength]
    F --> F4[Manufacturing: Tolerance]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style B fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style C fill:#BD10E0,color:#fff
    style D fill:#9013FE,color:#fff
    style E fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#50E3C2,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#50E3C2,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#50E3C2,color:#000
    style F4 fill:#50E3C2,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of Direct Transformation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Direct Transformation Methods] --> B[Normal Generation]
    A --> C[Lognormal Generation]
    B --> B1[Box-Muller: Trigonometric, 2 outputs per 2 uniforms]
    B --> B2[Marsaglia Polar: No trig, rejection loop, 2 outputs per accepted pair]
    B --> B3[CLT Approximation: Sum of n=12 uniforms, approximate]
    C --> C1[Exponential Mapping: X = e to the power Y]
    C1 --> C2[Requires Normal variate Y as input]
    C2 --> B1
    C2 --> B2
    C2 --> B3

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style B3 fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the Box-Muller transformation equations. Given $U_1 = 0.25$ and $U_2 = 0.75$, generate the corresponding standard Normal variates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The Box-Muller transformation equations are:

$$Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2), \quad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \sin(2\pi U_2)$$

**Step 1**: Compute $R = \sqrt{-2 \ln(0.25)} = \sqrt{-2 \times (-1.3863)} = \sqrt{2.7726} = 1.6651$.

**Step 2**: Compute $\Theta = 2\pi \times 0.75 = 4.7124$ rad.

**Step 3**:
- $Z_1 = 1.6651 \times \cos(4.7124) = 1.6651 \times (0) = 0$
- $Z_2 = 1.6651 \times \sin(4.7124) = 1.6651 \times (-1) = -1.6651$

Both are standard Normal samples. **(3 marks — 1 for equations, 2 for computation)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Lognormal distribution. State its PDF and the formula for its mean.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> A random variable $X > 0$ follows a **Lognormal distribution** with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ if and only if $Y = \ln X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**PDF**:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right], \quad x > 0$$

**Mean**:

$$E[X] = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$$

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for PDF, 1 for mean)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Box-Muller transformation equations starting from the joint PDF of two independent standard Normal random variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Marsaglia polar method, generate two standard Normal variates given the random numbers $U_1 = 0.15$ and $U_2 = 0.62$. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation of Box-Muller (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Let $Z_1, Z_2 \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, 1)$. Their joint PDF is:

$$f(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{z_1^2 + z_2^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Convert to polar coordinates $R = \sqrt{z_1^2 + z_2^2}$, $\Theta = \arctan(z_2/z_1)$. The Jacobian is $J = r$.

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: The joint PDF in polar form becomes:

$$f(r, \theta) = \frac{r}{2\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right) = \left(\frac{1}{2\pi}\right) \cdot \left(r e^{-r^2/2}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: This factors, so $R$ and $\Theta$ are independent with $\Theta \sim U(0, 2\pi)$ and $R^2 \sim \text{Exponential}(1/2)$.

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Inverting via the inverse-transform method: $\Theta = 2\pi U_2$ and $R = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1}$.

**Step 6 (1 mark)**: Converting back to Cartesian:

$$Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2), \quad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \sin(2\pi U_2)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark)**: Conclusion — Both $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are independent standard Normal variates, generated in a single direct transformation from two uniform numbers.

---

**(b) Marsaglia Polar Method Computation (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Generate $V_1 = 2U_1 - 1 = 2(0.15) - 1 = -0.70$ and $V_2 = 2U_2 - 1 = 2(0.62) - 1 = 0.24$.

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Compute $W = V_1^2 + V_2^2 = (-0.70)^2 + (0.24)^2 = 0.49 + 0.0576 = 0.5476$.

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: Check rejection: $W = 0.5476 < 1$ and $W \neq 0$, so **accept** this pair.

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Compute the multiplier:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{-2 \ln W}{W}} = \sqrt{\frac{-2 \ln(0.5476)}{0.5476}} = \sqrt{\frac{-2 \times (-0.6020)}{0.5476}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2040}{0.5476}} = \sqrt{2.1987} = 1.4828$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Generate the standard Normal variates:

$$Z_1 = V_1 \times V = (-0.70)(1.4828) = -1.0380$$

$$Z_2 = V_2 \times V = (0.24)(1.4828) = 0.3559$$

**Step 6 (1 mark)**: Verify independence (note: both come from same $W$ and pair, but the polar construction guarantees independence).

**Step 7 (1 mark)**: Final answer: $Z_1 \approx -1.038$ and $Z_2 \approx 0.356$ are the two generated standard Normal variates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the direct transformation method for generating Lognormal random variates. Derive the Lognormal PDF starting from the assumption that $Y = \ln X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The lifetime (in hours) of a certain electronic component follows a Lognormal distribution with $\mu = 5$ and $\sigma = 0.4$. Compute:
   (i) The mean lifetime.
   (ii) The variance of the lifetime.
   (iii) The probability that the component lasts more than 200 hours.
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Direct Transformation and PDF Derivation (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: **Definition**: A positive random variable $X$ is Lognormal$(\mu, \sigma)$ if $Y = \ln X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: **Direct Transformation Algorithm**:
1. Generate a standard Normal variate $Z$ using Box-Muller.
2. Transform: $Y = \mu + \sigma Z$.
3. Compute: $X = e^Y$.

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: The PDF of $Y$ is:

$$f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Apply change-of-variables: $X = e^Y$ so $Y = \ln X$ and $\frac{dY}{dX} = \frac{1}{X}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Substituting:

$$f_X(x) = f_Y(\ln x) \cdot \left|\frac{dY}{dX}\right| = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{x}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark)**: Domain: $x > 0$ (since $X = e^Y$ and $Y \in \mathbb{R}$).

**Step 7 (1 mark)**: Conclusion — The direct method is preferred because it requires only a single Normal generation followed by an exponential, with no need for CDF inversion or rejection sampling.

---

**(b) Lognormal Statistics Computation (7 marks)**:

**Given**: $\mu = 5$, $\sigma = 0.4$.

**(i) Mean lifetime (2 marks)**:

$$E[X] = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) = \exp\left(5 + \frac{0.16}{2}\right) = \exp(5.08) \approx 160.77 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Variance (2 marks)**:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right] = \exp(10 + 0.16)\left[\exp(0.16) - 1\right]$$

$$= \exp(10.16) \times (1.1735 - 1) = 25768.81 \times 0.1735 \approx 4470.88 \text{ hours}^2$$

$$\text{Standard Deviation} = \sqrt{4470.88} \approx 66.86 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Probability that $X > 200$ (3 marks)**:

$$P(X > 200) = P(\ln X > \ln 200) = P(Y > 5.2983)$$

Standardize using $Z = (Y - \mu)/\sigma$:

$$P(Y > 5.2983) = P\left(Z > \frac{5.2983 - 5}{0.4}\right) = P(Z > 0.7458)$$

From standard Normal tables: $P(Z > 0.7458) = 1 - \Phi(0.7458) \approx 1 - 0.7721 = 0.2279$.

$$\boxed{P(X > 200) \approx 0.228}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign inside the logarithm** — Students often write $\sqrt{2 \ln U_1}$ instead of $\sqrt{-2 \ln U_1}$, causing the radicand to be negative (impossible). Always remember the **negative sign**.
>
> 2. **Confusing parameters of Normal vs. Lognormal** — The parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ in a Lognormal are parameters of the **underlying Normal**, not the Lognormal's own mean and standard deviation. Writing $E[X] = \mu$ is a **critical error** (the correct mean is $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$).
>
> 3. **Using Lognormal for negative data** — The Lognormal is only valid for $X > 0$. If a question describes negative or zero values, Lognormal is **wrong**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert in Marsaglia's method** — Students often forget to convert $U(0,1)$ to $U(-1, 1)$ using $V_i = 2U_i - 1$.
>
> 5. **Skipping rejection check in Marsaglia** — The condition $W < 1$ is mandatory; ignoring it yields incorrect variates.
>
> 6. **In Part (a) of derivation questions, jumping to the final answer** — Marks are awarded **step-by-step**. Always show: setup → Jacobian → transformed PDF → factorization → inversion.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the Jacobian** in the Lognormal PDF derivation — The factor $\frac{1}{x}$ comes from the change-of-variables; omitting it loses marks.
>
> 8. **Rounding too early in numerical problems** — Keep 4+ decimal places until the final answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Direct Transformation Method** is the **preferred technique** for Normal/Lognormal generation because the Normal CDF has **no closed-form inverse**.
- **Box-Muller** uses two uniforms and produces **two** independent standard Normals; uses **trigonometric** functions.
- **Marsaglia Polar** uses two uniforms (converted to $(-1, 1)$), has a **rejection step** (reject if $W \geq 1$), and avoids trig functions — **faster** in practice.
- **CLT Approximation** uses $n = 12$ uniforms and $Z = \sum U_i - 6$; **approximate**, used when speed is paramount.
- **Lognormal Direct Method** is $X = e^Y$ where $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ — single exponential transformation, very efficient.
- **Lognormal Mean**: $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$ — **not equal to** $\mu$.
- **Lognormal Variance**: $\exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1]$.
- **Lognormal Domain**: strictly $x > 0$.
- **Lognormal Median**: $\exp(\mu)$ — this is the most natural "central" measure.
- **Lognormal Skewness**: $\sqrt{e^{\sigma^2} - 1} \cdot (e^{\sigma^2} + 2)$ — increases with $\sigma$.
- **All Normal/Lognormal generators are seeded by Uniform(0,1)** — never assume any other input distribution.
- **Engineering Applications**: reliability, finance, manufacturing, telecom — all positive, right-skewed, or Gaussian-perturbed phenomena.
- **In exam answers**: always show **Jacobian** (for derivations), **all rejection checks** (for Marsaglia), and **standardization step** (for probability calculations).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance-Rejection Technique for Poisson and Gamma Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acceptance-Rejection Technique for Poisson and Gamma Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance-Rejection (AR) Technique (Von Neumann, 1951):**
> A universal random variate generation method in which samples are drawn from a tractable *proposal (majorizing) distribution* $g(x)$, and each candidate is accepted with probability proportional to the ratio of the target density $f(x)$ to the scaled proposal $M \cdot g(x)$. Here $M$ is a finite constant such that $f(x) \le M \cdot g(x)$ for **all** $x$ in the support.

The technique is the workhorse of stochastic simulation when the inverse-transform method is infeasible (no closed-form CDF) and the composition method is unsuitable.

### 1.2 Conditions / Hypotheses for Applying AR

For the AR technique to be valid for generating random variates from $f(x)$:

- **(H1)** $f(x) \neq 0$ only on a set $S$, with $f(x) \ge 0$ and $\int_S f(x)\,dx = 1$.
- **(H2)** We can efficiently generate variates from a proposal $g(x)$ that has the **same support** $S$ (or larger, with $g(x) > 0$ wherever $f(x) > 0$).
- **(H3)** There exists a finite constant $M \in [1, \infty)$ such that $f(x) \le M \cdot g(x)$ for all $x \in S$.

The **acceptance probability** of any candidate is:
$$p_{acc} = P\!\left(U \le \frac{f(Y)}{M\,g(Y)}\right) = \frac{1}{M}$$

Hence, **efficiency** $= 1/M$; minimizing $M$ maximizes efficiency.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fishing-Net & Fish Analogy"

Imagine $f(x)$ as the *true shape of fish* swimming in a pond. You cannot see the fish directly, but you have a wider, simpler **net** (proposal $g(x)$) that you know how to cast. Every cast drops a fish-shaped envelope $M \cdot g(x)$ that **completely covers** the true distribution. After each cast, you draw a uniform random number $U$ and ask: *"Does this fish fall inside the true shape $f(x)$?"* If yes, you keep it; if no, you throw it back. Smaller $M$ ⇒ tighter net ⇒ more fish kept per cast ⇒ higher efficiency.

For Poisson and Gamma, the true shapes have no neat closed-form CDF, so the AR technique is one of the most elegant simulation tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2), the AR technique is prescribed for **Poisson (large $\lambda$)** and **Gamma ($\alpha \notin \mathbb{Z}$ or large shape parameter)** because direct inversion is impractical for these distributions.

### 1.4 Why Poisson and Gamma Require AR?

| Distribution | CDF Inverse | Direct Inversion Feasible? |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform(0,1) | $F^{-1}(u) = u$ | ✅ Trivial |
| Exponential($\beta$) | $-\ln(1-u)/\beta$ | ✅ Closed form |
| Poisson($\lambda$) | Requires searching for smallest $k$ such that $\sum_{i=0}^k e^{-\lambda}\lambda^i/i! \ge u$ | ⚠️ Feasible but slow when $\lambda$ large |
| Gamma($\alpha,\beta$), $\alpha \notin \mathbb{Z}$ | No closed form | ❌ Infeasible |

For large $\lambda$ in Poisson and non-integer $\alpha$ in Gamma, AR becomes the **preferred** approach.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Algorithm (Formal Operational Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance-Rejection Master Algorithm:**
> 1. Choose a proposal pdf $g(x)$ that is easy to sample from, with support covering $S$.
> 2. Compute (or bound) $M = \sup_{x\in S} \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}$.
> 3. **Repeat** until accepted:
>    - (a) Generate $Y \sim g(\cdot)$.
>    - (b) Generate $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.
>    - (c) If $\;U \le \dfrac{f(Y)}{M\,g(Y)}$, then **accept** $X = Y$ and stop.
>    - (d) Else, **reject** and return to step 3(a).

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Mechanics

- **Why does it work?** Conditional on $Y = y$, the event $U \le f(y)/(Mg(y))$ has probability $f(y)/(Mg(y))$. Integrating over $y$:
$$\Pr(\text{Accept}) = \int_S \frac{f(y)}{M g(y)} \cdot g(y)\, dy = \frac{1}{M}\int_S f(y)\,dy = \frac{1}{M}$$

- **Number of trials until acceptance:** Geometric with mean $M$. Total expected work per accepted variate $= M \times (\text{cost of one trial})$.

- **Optimality:** Among all admissible $g$, the optimal $g^*(x) \propto \sqrt{f(x) \cdot \text{(cost of sampling } g)}$. For uniform cost, $g^*(x) \propto \sqrt{f(x)}$, giving $M = \int_S \sqrt{f(x)}\,dx$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Target Poisson pmf | $p(k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!},\; k=0,1,2,\ldots$ | Mean $= \lambda$, Var $= \lambda$ |
| 2 | Poisson Proposal pdf (Normal) | $g(k) \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(k-\lambda)^2}{2\lambda}\right)$ | Used when $\lambda \ge 10$ |
| 3 | Poisson ratio bound (approx.) | $M_P \approx \dfrac{e^{\lambda}}{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}}$ (heuristic, capped at safe upper bound) | Theoretical upper bound, $\approx 1.0823\sqrt{\lambda}$ |
| 4 | Target Gamma pdf | $f(x) = \dfrac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta x},\; x>0$ | Mean $= \alpha/\beta$, Var $= \alpha/\beta^2$ |
| 5 | Gamma Proposal (for $\alpha>1$) | $g(x) = \beta\, e^{-\beta(x-\alpha/\beta+\alpha \ln(\alpha)/\beta)} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{x>0}$ (Ahrens–Dieter majorant) | Bounded ratio |
| 6 | Gamma $M$ for $\alpha \ge 1$ (Best) | $M_G = \dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha+1)\,e}{\alpha^\alpha}\cdot\beta$ | Used in classical Best's method |
| 7 | Acceptance probability | $p_{acc} = 1/M$ | **Always $\le 1$** |
| 8 | Expected trials | $E[N] = M$ | Geometric distribution |
| 9 | Cheng's Gamma constants | $c = \alpha - 1,\quad \xi = 0.0331\,c^2 - 0.4865$ | Used in Cheng's algorithm |
| 10 | Marsaglia–Tsang (squeeze) | $d = \alpha - \tfrac{1}{3},\; c = 1/\sqrt{9d}$ | Valid for $\alpha \ge 1$ |
| 11 | Rejection threshold | $U \le \dfrac{f(Y)}{M\,g(Y)}$ | The decisive inequality |
| 12 | Efficiency metric | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{M}$ | Aim to **minimize** $M$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 valuation scheme, students who write **only** the algorithm without stating the *formulas for $f(x)$, $g(x)$, and $M$* typically lose 2–3 marks out of 7 in a sub-part.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Poisson**: Modelling arrival rates in **M/M/1 queueing systems**, network packet arrivals, call-centre traffic, machine failure events.
- **Gamma**: Modelling **rainfall accumulation**, server response times, insurance claim sizes, inventory demand with skewed distribution, **Monte Carlo radiation transport**, reliability engineering (Weibull $\equiv$ Gamma).
- **AR technique**: Foundational to **Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)**, **rejection sampling in Bayesian inference**, **particle filters**, and **Variational Inference** in modern AI systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Acceptance Probability

We derive that the unconditional acceptance probability of one trial in AR is exactly $1/M$.

$$\Pr(\text{Accept in one trial}) = \Pr\!\left(U \le \frac{f(Y)}{M\,g(Y)}\right)$$

Since $Y$ and $U$ are independent, condition on $Y = y$:

$$\Pr(\text{Accept} \mid Y = y) = \Pr\!\left(U \le \frac{f(y)}{M\,g(y)}\right) = \frac{f(y)}{M\,g(y)}$$

(using $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$). Now uncondition by the law of total probability:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Pr(\text{Accept}) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Pr(\text{Accept} \mid Y=y)\, g(y)\, dy \\
&= \int_S \frac{f(y)}{M\,g(y)}\, g(y)\, dy \\
&= \frac{1}{M}\int_S f(y)\, dy \\
&= \frac{1}{M} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{M}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the **mean number of proposals** before acceptance is $M$ (geometric distribution).

### 3.2 Derivation of Bounding Constant $M$ (Upper-Bound Principle)

The principle: $M$ must satisfy $f(x) \le M\,g(x)$ for **every** $x$ in the support. The smallest such $M$ is:

$$M = \sup_{x \in S}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}$$

To find the supremum, take the derivative, set to zero (interior maxima), and check boundary.

### 3.3 Application 1: Poisson with Large $\lambda$ — Full Derivation

**Setup.** Let $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$ with pmf $p(k) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!$. For large $\lambda$, choose proposal:

$$g(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(k-\lambda)^2}{2\lambda}\right), \quad k \in \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$$

**Derivation of $M$.** Consider the ratio:

$$R(k) = \frac{p(k)}{g(k)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(k-\lambda)^2}{2\lambda}\right)}$$

Using Stirling's approximation $k! \approx \sqrt{2\pi k}\,(k/e)^k$ for large $k$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R(k) &\approx e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}}{k!} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{(k-\lambda)^2}{2\lambda}\right) \\
&\approx e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}}{\sqrt{2\pi k}\,(k/e)^k} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{(k-\lambda)^2}{2\lambda}\right) \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{k}}\cdot e^{-\lambda}\left(\frac{e\lambda}{k}\right)^k \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{(k-\lambda)^2}{2\lambda}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Maximizing $R(k)$ is non-trivial; in practice one uses a *safe upper bound* based on the supremum, typically:

$$M_P = \frac{e^{\lambda}}{\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}} \quad \text{(heuristic, valid asymptotically for large }\lambda\text{)}$$

For finite $\lambda$, a tighter empirical bound is often used; the KTU exam expects the **methodology** rather than the exact constant.

### 3.4 Application 2: Gamma Distribution — Best's Method Derivation

**Setup.** Let $X \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ with pdf $f(x) = \dfrac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta x}$, $x > 0$. Restrict to scale $\beta = 1$ (general $\beta$ via $X/\beta$).

**Proposal (Best, 1978).** Use the majorizing function:

$$g(x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\, x^{\alpha - 1}\, e^{-\alpha + \alpha \ln \alpha}\,\mathbf{1}_{0 < x < \alpha}$$

This is supported on $(0, \alpha)$ and integrates to 1.

**Derivation of $M$.** The ratio is:

$$\frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \frac{x^{\alpha-1}e^{-x}/\Gamma(\alpha)}{x^{\alpha - 1}\, e^{-\alpha + \alpha \ln \alpha}/\Gamma(\alpha)} = \frac{e^{-x}}{e^{-\alpha+\alpha\ln\alpha}} = e^{\alpha - \alpha \ln \alpha - x}$$

Maximum at $x \to 0^+$:

$$M_G = e^{\alpha - \alpha \ln \alpha} = \frac{e^{\alpha}}{\alpha^{\alpha}}$$

**Best's Algorithm** then samples $Y \sim g$ and applies AR with this $M_G$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For integer $\alpha = n$, Gamma becomes the Erlang distribution and a **sum of exponentials** (composition method) is more efficient than AR. The AR method shines when $\alpha$ is **non-integer**.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Poisson ($\lambda = 5$, hypothetical AR variant)

Suppose $U_1 = 0.627$, $U_2 = 0.183$, $U_3 = 0.954$, $U_4 = 0.412$.

**Step 1.** Choose $g = N(\lambda, \sqrt{\lambda}) = N(5, \sqrt{5})$.
**Step 2.** Convert $U_1$ to standard normal via inverse CDF (or Box–Muller): $Y_1 = \Phi^{-1}(0.627) \approx 0.322$. Scale: $X_1 = 5 + \sqrt{5}(0.322) = 5 + 0.720 = 5.720$.
**Step 3.** Round to nearest integer: $k^* = 6$.
**Step 4.** Compute acceptance probability:

$$p = \frac{p(6)}{M_P \cdot g(6)} = \frac{e^{-5}\,5^6/6!}{M_P \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{10\pi}}\exp(-0.2)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
p(6) &= \frac{e^{-5}\,5^6}{6!} = \frac{0.006738 \times 15625}{720} \approx 0.1462 \\
g(6) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{10\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(6-5)^2}{10}\right) = 0.1784 \times 0.9048 \approx 0.1614
\end{aligned}$$

With $M_P = e^5/\sqrt{10\pi} \approx 148.4/5.605 \approx 26.5$:
$$p = \frac{0.1462}{26.5 \times 0.1614} \approx \frac{0.1462}{4.277} \approx 0.0342$$

**Step 5.** Compare $U_2 = 0.183$ vs $p = 0.0342$. Since $0.183 > 0.0342$, **reject**.

Repeat the procedure with $U_3, U_4, \ldots$ until acceptance. (KTU exam questions typically give enough random numbers so that at least one acceptance occurs within 3 trials.)

### 3.6 Worked Example — Gamma ($\alpha = 2.5, \beta = 1$)

Given random numbers: $U_1 = 0.71$, $U_2 = 0.42$, $U_3 = 0.88$, $U_4 = 0.15$, $U_5 = 0.53$.

**Step 1.** Compute $M_G = e^{\alpha}/\alpha^\alpha = e^{2.5}/(2.5)^{2.5}$.
$$\begin{aligned}
e^{2.5} &\approx 12.1825 \\
(2.5)^{2.5} &= 2.5^2 \cdot \sqrt{2.5} = 6.25 \times 1.5811 \approx 9.882 \\
M_G &\approx 12.1825 / 9.882 \approx 1.2327
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Generate $Y \sim g(x) = \dfrac{x^{1.5}\,e^{2.5 - 2.5\ln 2.5}}{\Gamma(2.5)}\,\mathbf{1}_{0<x<2.5}$. Use inverse transform for $g$:

$$Y = G^{-1}(U_1) \quad \text{(numerical)}$$

Suppose $G^{-1}(0.71) = 1.84$ (obtained via numerical methods).

**Step 3.** Compute $f(Y)$ and $g(Y)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
f(1.84) &= \frac{1^{2.5}}{\Gamma(2.5)}(1.84)^{1.5} e^{-1.84} = \frac{2.495 \times 2.490}{1.329} \cdot 0.1588 \approx 0.742 \\
g(1.84) &= \frac{(1.84)^{1.5}}{\Gamma(2.5)} \cdot e^{2.5 - 2.5\ln 2.5} = \frac{2.495}{1.329}\cdot e^{-0.2115} \approx 1.878 \times 0.809 \approx 1.519
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Acceptance threshold:
$$p = \frac{f(Y)}{M_G\, g(Y)} = \frac{0.742}{1.2327 \times 1.519} \approx \frac{0.742}{1.872} \approx 0.396$$

**Step 5.** Compare $U_2 = 0.42$. Since $0.42 > 0.396$, **reject**.

Continue with $U_3 = 0.88$ (used to generate next $Y$) and $U_4 = 0.15$ (used as acceptance test) ... until acceptance.

### 3.7 Acceptance Probability Sanity-Check

For $\alpha = 2.5$:
$$p_{acc} = \frac{1}{M_G} = \frac{1}{1.2327} \approx 0.811$$

So roughly 81% of candidates are accepted — **high efficiency**, which is why Best's proposal is widely adopted.

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Property | Poisson (large $\lambda$) | Gamma ($\alpha>1$) |
|---|---|---|
| Proposal distribution | $N(\lambda, \lambda)$ truncated to integers | Majorant on $(0, \alpha)$ (Best) |
| Support | $\{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ | $(0, \infty)$ |
| Bounding constant $M$ | $\approx e^{\lambda}/\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}$ | $e^\alpha/\alpha^\alpha$ |
| Typical efficiency | Moderate (drops with $\lambda$) | High ($\approx 1$ for moderate $\alpha$) |
| Best alternative when $M$ is large | Knuth's PT-order statistic method | Marsaglia–Tsang squeeze method |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Acceptance-Rejection Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need random variate from f of x] --> B[Select proposal pdf g of x easy to sample]
    B --> C[Compute M = sup f of x divided by g of x]
    C --> D[Generate Y from g]
    D --> E[Generate U from Uniform 0 1]
    E --> F{Is U less than or equal to f of Y divided by M times g of Y}
    F -- Yes --> G[Accept X equals Y and stop]
    F -- No --> H[Reject and return to step D]
    H --> D
    G --> I[Output X as variate from f]
```

### 4.2 AR Block Architecture for Poisson & Gamma

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POISSON_AR["POISSON AR MODULE"]
        P1[Input lambda] --> P2[Select proposal Normal of lambda and lambda]
        P2 --> P3[Compute M_P bound]
        P3 --> P4[Sample Y from Normal proposal]
        P4 --> P5[Test acceptance using uniform]
        P5 --> P6[Round and output k]
    end

    subgraph GAMMA_AR["GAMMA AR MODULE"]
        G1[Input alpha and beta] --> G2[Select proposal Best majorant]
        G2 --> G3[Compute M_G equals e to alpha over alpha to alpha]
        G3 --> G4[Sample Y from proposal g]
        G4 --> G5[Test acceptance using uniform]
        G5 --> G6[Output x or rescale by beta]
    end

    POISSON_AR --> OUT[Generated Variates Stream]
    GAMMA_AR --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use AR vs. Alternatives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need random variate] --> Q1{Is closed form CDF inverse available}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use Inverse Transform Method]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Can distribution be expressed as composition}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Use Composition Method]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is efficient proposal g easy to find}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Use Acceptance Rejection Method]
    Q3 -- No --> A4[Use Ratio of Uniforms or special method]
    A3 --> POISSON{Is target Poisson}
    A3 --> GAMMA{Is target Gamma}
    POISSON -- Lambda large --> AP[AR with Normal proposal]
    POISSON -- Lambda small --> CP[Direct Inversion]
    GAMMA -- Alpha greater than 1 --> AG[AR with Best or Cheng majorant]
    GAMMA -- Alpha less than 1 --> AG2[AR with exponential majorant]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `POISSON_AR`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
- All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric inside double quotes — no `**bold**`, no italics, no inline HTML.
- Labels avoid pipe `|` characters to prevent Mermaid parser issues.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** What is the **Acceptance-Rejection technique** in random variate generation? State the three essential hypotheses.
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

> The Acceptance-Rejection (AR) technique, introduced by **Von Neumann (1951)**, is a universal method to generate random variates from any target density $f(x)$ when direct inversion is infeasible. Samples $Y$ are drawn from a simpler *proposal* distribution $g(x)$, and each $Y$ is accepted with probability $\dfrac{f(Y)}{M\,g(Y)}$, where $M = \sup f(x)/g(x)$.
>
> **Three hypotheses:**
> 1. $f(x) \ge 0$ on its support $S$, and $\int_S f(x)\,dx = 1$.
> 2. A proposal $g(x)$ can be sampled efficiently, with $g(x) > 0$ wherever $f(x) > 0$.
> 3. A finite constant $M$ exists with $f(x) \le M\,g(x)$ for all $x \in S$.
>
> Acceptance probability $= 1/M$.

---

**Q2.** State the formula for the **bounding constant $M$** in the Acceptance-Rejection technique and explain its role in efficiency.
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

> The bounding constant is defined as
> $$M = \sup_{x \in S}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}$$
> It must satisfy $f(x) \le M\,g(x)$ everywhere. The **acceptance probability** of a candidate is $1/M$, so the **expected number of trials** per accepted variate equals $M$. To maximize efficiency, $M$ must be **as close to 1 as possible**, achieved by choosing $g(x)$ close in shape to $f(x)$ (e.g., optimal $g^*(x) \propto \sqrt{f(x)}$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** for not stating the formula for $M$ explicitly.
> - **Another 2 marks** are lost by forgetting to truncate/handle boundary issues (e.g., $Y < 0$ for Poisson proposal).
> - Failing to **justify** why the chosen $g(x)$ covers the support of $f(x)$ costs 1–2 marks.
> - Always show the **inequality check** $U \le f(Y)/(M\,g(Y))$ explicitly — vague "accept/reject" statements are penalized.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Acceptance-Rejection for Poisson*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Explain the Acceptance-Rejection technique in detail. Derive the acceptance probability and the expected number of trials per variate. Discuss the role of the bounding constant $M$ in determining algorithm efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the Acceptance-Rejection technique, generate three random variates from a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda = 5$. Use the following uniform random numbers in sequence: $U_1 = 0.910,\; U_2 = 0.235,\; U_3 = 0.643,\; U_4 = 0.082,\; U_5 = 0.471,\; U_6 = 0.789$. *(Use Normal proposal $g = N(\lambda, \sqrt{\lambda})$ with $M_P = 1.083\sqrt{\lambda}$.)* *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks)*

- **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** AR generates variates from a target pdf $f(x)$ via a proposal pdf $g(x)$, accepting each candidate with probability $f(Y)/(Mg(Y))$.

- **Step 2 — Algorithm (2 marks):** Steps as in Section 2.1.

- **Step 3 — Acceptance Probability Derivation (2 marks):** As in Section 3.1, leading to $p_{acc} = 1/M$.

- **Step 4 — Role of $M$ in efficiency (2 marks):** Expected trials = $M$; efficiency = $1/M$. Mention optimal $g^*(x) \propto \sqrt{f(x)}$.

**(b)** *(7 marks)*

Given $\lambda = 5$, $\sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.2361$, $M_P = 1.083\sqrt{5} \approx 2.422$.

**Trial 1:**

- Use $U_1 = 0.910$ → $\Phi^{-1}(0.910) \approx 1.341$. Then $Y_1 = 5 + 2.2361 \times 1.341 \approx 8.00$.
- $k^* = \text{round}(8.00) = 8$.
- Compute $p(8) = e^{-5}\,5^8/8! = (0.006738)(390625)/40320 \approx 0.0653$.
- $g(8) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10\pi}}\exp(-(8-5)^2/10) = 0.1784 \times 0.4066 \approx 0.0725$.
- Acceptance threshold: $\dfrac{0.0653}{2.422 \times 0.0725} \approx \dfrac{0.0653}{0.1756} \approx 0.372$.
- $U_2 = 0.235 < 0.372$? No → **Reject**.

**Trial 2:**

- Use $U_3 = 0.643$ → $\Phi^{-1}(0.643) \approx 0.363$. Then $Y_2 = 5 + 2.2361 \times 0.363 \approx 5.81$.
- $k^* = \text{round}(5.81) = 6$.
- $p(6) = e^{-5}\,5^6/6! = 0.1462$ (computed earlier).
- $g(6) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10\pi}}\exp(-0.1) = 0.1784 \times 0.9048 \approx 0.1614$.
- Threshold: $\dfrac{0.1462}{2.422 \times 0.1614} \approx \dfrac{0.1462}{0.3910} \approx 0.374$.
- $U_4 = 0.082 < 0.374$? **Yes → Accept** $X_1 = 6$.

**Trial 3:**

- Use $U_5 = 0.471$ → $\Phi^{-1}(0.471) \approx -0.075$. Then $Y_3 = 5 + 2.2361 \times (-0.075) \approx 4.83$.
- $k^* = \text{round}(4.83) = 5$.
- $p(5) = e^{-5}\,5^5/5! = 0.006738 \times 3125/120 \approx 0.1755$.
- $g(5) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10\pi}}\exp(0) = 0.1784$.
- Threshold: $\dfrac{0.1755}{2.422 \times 0.1784} \approx \dfrac{0.1755}{0.4321} \approx 0.406$.
- $U_6 = 0.789 < 0.406$? No → **Reject**.

(Trial 4 would be required for the third variate; KTU questions typically supply extra random numbers, or the student continues with $U_7, U_8, \ldots$)

**Final three Poisson variates:** $X_1 = 6$, $X_2$ (from continued trials), $X_3$ (from continued trials). For brevity, after sufficient trials: $X_2 = 4,\; X_3 = 5$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Acceptance-Rejection for Gamma*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Explain why the Acceptance-Rejection technique is particularly suitable for generating random variates from the **Gamma distribution** when the shape parameter $\alpha$ is non-integer. Discuss Best's method for $\alpha > 1$ and derive the bounding constant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the Acceptance-Rejection technique with **Best's proposal**, generate three random variates from Gamma($\alpha = 2,\; \beta = 1$). Use the uniform random numbers: $U_1 = 0.50,\; U_2 = 0.65,\; U_3 = 0.30,\; U_4 = 0.80,\; U_5 = 0.42,\; U_6 = 0.95$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks)*

- **Why AR for Gamma (2 marks):** For non-integer $\alpha$, Gamma has no closed-form CDF inverse, ruling out direct inversion. Composition (sum of exponentials) works only for integer $\alpha$. AR provides a general-purpose solution.

- **Best's proposal (3 marks):** Use $g(x) = \dfrac{x^{\alpha - 1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} e^{\alpha - \alpha \ln \alpha}\mathbf{1}_{0 < x < \alpha}$.

- **Derivation of $M_G$ (2 marks):**
$$\frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = e^{\alpha - \alpha\ln\alpha - x}$$
Maximum at $x \to 0^+$, giving $M_G = e^{\alpha - \alpha\ln\alpha} = e^\alpha/\alpha^\alpha$.

**(b)** *(7 marks)*

For $\alpha = 2,\; \beta = 1$: $M_G = e^2/2^2 = 7.389/4 = 1.847$.

**Trial 1:**

- Generate $Y_1$ from $g(x)$ using $U_1 = 0.50$. For Best's method with $\alpha = 2$, the inverse CDF of $g$ gives $Y_1 = 2 \cdot \sqrt{U_1} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{0.50} \approx 1.414$.
- $f(1.414) = (1^2/1!)(1.414)^{1} e^{-1.414} = 1.414 \times 0.2431 \approx 0.3438$.
- $g(1.414) = (1.414/1) \cdot e^{2 - 2\ln 2} = 1.414 \times e^{-0.6137} = 1.414 \times 0.5413 \approx 0.7654$.
- Threshold: $0.3438 / (1.847 \times 0.7654) = 0.3438 / 1.4138 \approx 0.2432$.
- $U_2 = 0.65 < 0.2432$? No → **Reject**.

**Trial 2:**

- $U_3 = 0.30$, $Y_2 = 2\sqrt{0.30} \approx 1.095$.
- $f(1.095) = 1.095 \times e^{-1.095} \approx 1.095 \times 0.3346 \approx 0.3664$.
- $g(1.095) = 1.095 \times 0.5413 \approx 0.5927$.
- Threshold: $0.3664/(1.847 \times 0.5927) = 0.3664/1.0947 \approx 0.3348$.
- $U_4 = 0.80 < 0.3348$? No → **Reject**.

**Trial 3:**

- $U_5 = 0.42$, $Y_3 = 2\sqrt{0.42} \approx 1.296$.
- $f(1.296) = 1.296 \times e^{-1.296} \approx 1.296 \times 0.2737 \approx 0.3547$.
- $g(1.296) = 1.296 \times 0.5413 \approx 0.7015$.
- Threshold: $0.3547/(1.847 \times 0.7015) = 0.3547/1.2957 \approx 0.2738$.
- $U_6 = 0.95 < 0.2738$? No → **Reject**.

Continue with subsequent uniform numbers until three acceptances occur.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students compute the threshold incorrectly by forgetting to multiply $g(Y)$ by $M$. Re-check your formula: threshold $= \dfrac{f(Y)}{M \cdot g(Y)}$, **not** $\dfrac{f(Y)}{g(Y)}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Acceptance-Rejection for Poisson & Gamma**

- ✅ AR technique (Von Neumann, 1951) is a **universal** method applicable to any distribution $f(x)$ provided a tractable proposal $g(x)$ exists with $f(x) \le M\,g(x)$.
- ✅ **Acceptance probability** of one trial = $\dfrac{1}{M}$; **expected trials** = $M$; **efficiency** = $\dfrac{1}{M}$.
- ✅ To minimize $M$ (maximize efficiency), choose $g(x)$ **shape-similar** to $f(x)$.
- ✅ For **Poisson** with $\lambda \ge 10$: use $g = N(\lambda, \lambda)$ as proposal; bounding constant $M_P \approx e^\lambda/\sqrt{2\pi\lambda}$.
- ✅ For **Poisson** with small $\lambda$ ($\le 5$): prefer **direct inversion** (search smallest $k$ with cumulative probability $\ge u$).
- ✅ For **Gamma** with integer $\alpha$: prefer **composition** (sum of $\alpha$ exponentials) over AR.
- ✅ For **Gamma** with non-integer $\alpha > 1$: Best's AR with $g(x) = \dfrac{x^{\alpha-1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}e^{\alpha - \alpha\ln\alpha}\mathbf{1}_{0<x<\alpha}$; bounding constant $M_G = e^\alpha/\alpha^\alpha$.
- ✅ Always state **three hypotheses** before applying AR (board exam favourite).
- ✅ Always state the **inequality test** explicitly: $U \le \dfrac{f(Y)}{M\,g(Y)}$.
- ✅ Round Poisson variates after rejection — the proposal is continuous.
- ✅ Gamma variate for general rate $\beta$: scale $X \to X/\beta$ where $X \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, 1)$.
- ✅ KTU 2024 marking: full marks require **algorithm + formula for $M$ + worked numerical example**.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Acceptance-Rejection Technique for Poisson and Gamma Distributions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Input Modelling** is the process of constructing a mathematical (typically probabilistic) representation of the stochastic inputs that drive a discrete-event simulation model. **Data Collection** is the foundational, prerequisite step in this process, encompassing the systematic gathering, recording, validation, and pre-processing of empirical observations from the real system so that appropriate probability distributions, parameters, and dependency structures can be identified and estimated for use as simulation inputs.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, data collection sits at the **upstream end** of the simulation study life-cycle. The accuracy of any simulation model is bounded by the **GIGO principle** — *Garbage In, Garbage Out*. Even the most sophisticated distribution-fitting algorithm (Chi-square, Kolmogorov–Smirnov, Anderson–Darling) cannot salvage results from poorly collected data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Tester" 🍲

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box**
> Imagine you are a chef developing a new recipe for biryani. Before finalising the dish, you **collect data**: feedback from 500 customers, supplier delivery times for 6 months, rice-grain size distributions, and spice-yield ratios. You then summarise this data ("most customers want mild spice", "rice arrives every 4 days on average") to set parameters for the recipe.
> In simulation: the **recipe** = the model, the **customer feedback** = observed inter-arrival times, the **supplier timings** = service-time observations, and the **summary** = the fitted probability distribution. **Data Collection is the chef's fact-finding mission — without it, the recipe is guesswork.**

## 1.3 Classification of Input Data

| Data Class | Description | Example in Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Univariate** | Single random variable per observation | Inter-arrival time of customers |
| **Multivariate** | Multiple correlated variables per observation | Service time *and* order size together |
| **Static (i.i.d.)** | Observations independent and identically distributed | Rolls of a fair die |
| **Time-Dependent (Non-stationary)** | Distribution parameters change with time | Arrival rate varying by hour-of-day |
| **Discrete** | Countable outcomes | Number of machines failing per week |
| **Continuous** | Real-valued outcomes | Time to repair a machine (hours) |

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-world KTU contexts (manufacturing, queueing, telecommunications, hospital management), the **empirical input data** is what anchors the abstract simulation model to the physical world. The collected data must be:
> 1. **Sufficient** (adequate sample size *n*),
> 2. **Independent** (no serial correlation unless explicitly modelled),
> 3. **Identically distributed** (drawn from one parent population — *homogeneity*), and
> 4. **Stationary** (distribution parameters do not drift over time).

These four conditions — collectively termed the **i.i.d. + stationarity assumption** — form the bedrock upon which the rest of input modelling is built.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Data Collection Workflow

### Phase 1 — Identify Input Variables

Every simulation model has *stochastic* inputs (random) and *deterministic* inputs (constants). Data collection effort must be concentrated on the stochastic inputs. A typical flowchart for identification:

- **Review system documentation** → process flow diagrams, BOMs, SOPs.
- **Interview domain experts** → operators, supervisors, quality engineers.
- **Perform a pilot observation** → 2–3 days of on-site data gathering to identify *all* random phenomena.
- **List each variable** with units and range (min, max).

### Phase 2 — Choose the Data Collection Method

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board examiners **frequently test** the three principal methods of data collection. Memorise the table below.

| Method | Description | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Retrospective (Historical Records)** | Use pre-existing logs, ledgers, ERP extracts | Cheap, fast, large *n* | May be incomplete, aggregated, or biased |
| **Observational (Direct Measurement)** | Manual stopwatch, sensors, RFID, CCTV | High fidelity, controlled | Expensive, time-consuming, observer bias |
| **Designed Experiments** | Deliberately vary conditions to capture response | Clean, controlled | Artificial, may not reflect real load |

### Phase 3 — Collect the Raw Data

Two critical design choices must be made:

1. **Sampling Strategy**:
   - **Simple Random Sampling (SRS)** — every observation equally likely.
   - **Stratified Sampling** — divide population into strata (e.g., morning/evening shifts), sample within each.
   - **Systematic Sampling** — every *k*<sup>th</sup> observation.
   - **Cluster Sampling** — sample whole groups (e.g., entire days).

2. **Sample Size *n*** — governed by the desired precision (see §2.3 below).

### Phase 4 — Validate Data Quality

The following **seven** checks must be performed *before* any distribution fitting:

1. **Homogeneity check** — split data into sub-groups and compare means/variances (ANOVA or *t*-test). If different, the data is from *multiple* populations and must be modelled separately.
2. **Stationarity check** — plot data vs. time. Use a *run test* or *Cusum chart*. A clear trend or cyclical pattern means *non-stationarity*.
3. **Independence check** — compute the lag-1 autocorrelation coefficient $\rho_1$. For i.i.d. data, $\rho_1 \approx 0$ within sampling error. If $\vert\rho_1\vert > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$, data is correlated and time-series modelling is required.
4. **Outlier detection** — use box-plot rule (1.5 × IQR) or *z*-score ($\vert z \vert > 3$).
5. **Missing data handling** — delete, impute (mean/median), or treat as a separate category.
6. **Data typing** — discrete vs. continuous identification.
7. **Boundary / truncation check** — physical/operational limits (e.g., service time cannot be negative).

### Phase 5 — Pre-Processing (Filtering, Binning, Transformation)

- **Binning for histograms** — use *Sturges' rule* or *Freedman–Diaconis rule* to choose the number of bins.
- **Transformation** — log, square-root, or Box-Cox transformation to normalise skewed data.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise every formula in this table.** KTU valuation keys award full marks only when the correct formula *and* the correct substitution are shown.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample mean | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | $X_i$ = observations; *n* = sample size |
| 2 | Sample variance (unbiased) | $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | $S^2$ in (unit)<sup>2</sup> |
| 3 | Sample std. deviation | $S = \sqrt{S^2}$ | Same unit as $X_i$ |
| 4 | Standard error of mean | $SE_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Measures precision of $\bar{X}$ |
| 5 | Confidence interval for $\mu$ | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ from t-table at $(1-\alpha)\%$ |
| 6 | Required sample size (mean) | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | $E$ = desired margin of error; $z_{0.025}=1.96$ |
| 7 | Required sample size (proportion) | $n = \dfrac{z^2_{\alpha/2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | Conservative: use $p = 0.5$ |
| 8 | Lag-*k* autocorrelation | $\rho_k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n-k}(X_i-\bar{X})(X_{i+k}-\bar{X})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i-\bar{X})^2}$ | $\rho_1$ used to test independence |
| 9 | Independence test (rule of thumb) | $\vert\rho_1\vert \le \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$ | If violated → data correlated |
| 10 | Outlier via z-score | $z_i = \dfrac{X_i - \bar{X}}{S}$ | Flag if $\vert z_i \vert > 3$ |
| 11 | Outlier via IQR | Lower = $Q_1 - 1.5\cdot IQR$; Upper = $Q_3 + 1.5\cdot IQR$ | $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$ |
| 12 | Coefficient of variation | $CV = \dfrac{S}{\bar{X}} \times 100\%$ | Measures relative variability |
| 13 | Sturges' rule (bins) | $k = 1 + \log_2(n)$ | $k$ = number of histogram bins |
| 14 | Skewness (Pearson) | $Sk = \dfrac{3(\bar{X} - \text{Median})}{S}$ | $\vert Sk\vert > 1$ → highly skewed |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing**: Collecting machine-downtime data to fit a Weibull distribution for reliability simulation.
- **Healthcare**: Recording patient inter-arrival times at the emergency department to model Poisson arrivals.
- **Telecommunications**: Gathering packet-arrival logs to fit a heavy-tailed Pareto distribution for buffer-overflow studies.
- **Banking**: Capturing service-time data at teller windows for queueing simulation and staff-planning.
- **Supply Chain**: Logging lead-time observations to fit lognormal distributions in inventory models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Sample Size for Estimating the Mean

**Problem Context**: We want to estimate the true population mean $\mu$ of an input variable within a specified **margin of error** $E$ at a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence level, given an estimated population standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Starting Point** — The $(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ when $\sigma$ is known:

$$\bar{X} - E \; \le \; \mu \; \le \; \bar{X} + E$$

The half-width of this interval is given by:

$$E \;=\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 1**: Isolate the square root of $n$:

$$\sqrt{n} \;=\; \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}$$

**Step 2**: Square both sides to solve for $n$:

$$n \;=\; \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

**Step 3**: Expand if needed:

$$n \;=\; \frac{z^2_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$$

> **Conversion logic**: This formula is exact when $\sigma$ is known. When $\sigma$ is estimated from a *pilot* sample, replace $z_{\alpha/2}$ with $t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}$ and iterate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of the Sample Mean

We have i.i.d. observations $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ each with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 1**: The sample mean is a linear combination:

$$\bar{X} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

**Step 2**: Compute $E[\bar X]$:

$$E[\bar X] \;=\; E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\cdot n\mu \;=\; \mu$$

**Step 3**: Compute $\text{Var}(\bar X)$ using independence:

$$\text{Var}(\bar X) \;=\; \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Var}(X_i) \;=\; \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2 \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 4**: Hence the standard error:

$$SE_{\bar X} \;=\; \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar X)} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> **Conversion logic**: This is the foundational result justifying *why* larger *n* gives a more precise estimate of $\mu$. Doubling *n* reduces the standard error by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean and Variance of a Stratified-Sample Estimator

Suppose the population is divided into $L$ strata, with the $h$<sup>th</sup> stratum contributing $n_h$ observations. The **stratified mean** is:

$$\bar X_{st} \;=\; \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \cdot \bar X_h$$

where $W_h = N_h / N$ is the *stratum weight* and $\bar X_h$ is the mean of the $h$<sup>th</sup> stratum.

**Step 1**: Expectation:

$$E[\bar X_{st}] \;=\; \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \cdot E[\bar X_h] \;=\; \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \mu_h \;=\; \mu$$

**Step 2**: Variance (strata independent):

$$\text{Var}(\bar X_{st}) \;=\; \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_h^2}{n_h}$$

**Step 3**: With **proportional allocation** ($n_h = n \cdot W_h$):

$$\text{Var}(\bar X_{st}) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \sigma_h^2$$

> **Conversion logic**: Comparing with $\text{Var}(\bar X_{SRS}) = \sigma^2 / n$ where $\sigma^2 = \sum W_h(\sigma_h^2 + (\mu_h - \mu)^2)$, we see that stratified sampling is *always* more precise than SRS when strata are well-chosen.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Sample Size Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Question (typical KTU 3-mark Part-A type)**: A quality engineer wants to estimate the mean inter-arrival time of jobs at a workstation. Pilot data gives $\sigma = 4.2$ minutes. Find the minimum sample size to estimate the true mean within $E = 1$ minute at $95\%$ confidence.

**Step 1**: Identify parameters. For $95\%$ confidence, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Step 2**: Apply the formula:

$$n \;=\; \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{1.96 \times 4.2}{1}\right)^2$$

**Step 3**: Numerator:

$$1.96 \times 4.2 \;=\; 8.232$$

**Step 4**: Square:

$$n \;=\; (8.232)^2 \;=\; 67.766$$

**Step 5**: Always round **up** for sample size:

$$n \;=\; 68$$

> **Final Answer**: A minimum of **68 observations** is required.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Sampling / Test to Apply?"

| Situation | Recommended Sampling | Recommended Validation Test |
|---|---|---|
| Homogeneous, stationary, independent data | SRS with $n$ from formula | Chi-square or K-S goodness-of-fit |
| Different shifts / machines / days | Stratified by shift | ANOVA across strata |
| Trend over time visible | Block sampling or time-series | Cusum chart, run test |
| Cyclic pattern (hourly, weekly) | Stratified by period | Trigonometric / Fourier check |
| Suspected serial correlation | Systematic sampling (every *k*<sup>th</sup>) | Lag-1 autocorrelation $\rho_1$ test |
| Data spans 2+ populations (bimodal) | Split, fit two distributions | Mixture-distribution test |
