# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]`

**List and briefly explain any three data collection methods used in input modelling for simulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per method):**

1. **Retrospective (Historical) Data Collection** — Uses pre-existing records such as ERP logs, transaction histories, and quality reports. **Advantage**: inexpensive and provides large sample sizes. **Disadvantage**: data may be aggregated, incomplete, or biased.
2. **Direct Observational Data Collection** — Involves on-site manual measurement using stopwatches, RFID, or barcode readers. **Advantage**: high fidelity and accuracy. **Disadvantage**: time-consuming, expensive, and prone to observer bias.
3. **Designed Experiments** — Data are collected under controlled, pre-planned variations of system conditions. **Advantage**: clean and free from confounding. **Disadvantage**: conditions may be artificial and not represent the real operational load.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]`

**Differentiate between univariate and multivariate input data with one simulation example each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Univariate data** consists of a *single* random variable observed repeatedly. **Example**: Inter-arrival times of customers at a bank ATM, recorded in seconds.
- **Multivariate data** consists of *two or more* random variables observed jointly, where correlations between them may exist. **Example**: Recording the service time *and* the transaction amount together for each bank customer — the two are often positively correlated.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Reasons**
> 1. ❌ Confusing *homogeneity* with *independence* (they are separate assumptions).
> 2. ❌ Forgetting to round *up* the sample size to the next integer.
> 3. ❌ Using $z$ instead of $t$ when $\sigma$ is estimated from a *pilot* sample.
> 4. ❌ Stating only the formula *without* the numerical substitution — you lose at least 1 mark per sub-part.
> 5. ❌ Failing to state the **i.i.d. + stationarity** assumption explicitly before fitting.
> 6. ❌ Skipping the **outlier detection** step in data pre-processing.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **five essential steps of data collection** in input modelling. For each step, identify the specific question that the analyst is trying to answer. (7 marks)

**(b)** A simulation analyst is collecting inter-arrival time data at a manufacturing workstation. A pilot sample of $n = 30$ observations yields $\bar X = 12.4$ minutes and $S = 3.1$ minutes.
    **(i)** Calculate the **95% confidence interval** for the true mean inter-arrival time. (4 marks)
    **(ii)** How many **additional observations** are required so that the margin of error reduces to $E = 0.5$ minutes at the same 95% confidence? (3 marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Identify input variables** (1 mark) — *Question*: *Which random quantities drive the simulation?* Methods: process flow review, expert interviews, pilot observation.
2. **Choose data collection method** (1 mark) — *Question*: *Is historical data available, or must we observe directly?* Trade-off: cost vs. fidelity.
3. **Decide on sampling strategy and sample size** (1 mark) — *Question*: *How many observations are needed for the desired precision?* Formula: $n = (z_{\alpha/2}\sigma/E)^2$.
4. **Validate data quality** (2 marks — split as 0.5 each for the four sub-checks) — *Question*: *Is the data homogeneous, stationary, independent, and free of outliers?* Use ANOVA, Cusum, lag-1 autocorrelation, and IQR rule.
5. **Pre-process and prepare for fitting** (1 mark) — *Question*: *Is the data in a form suitable for distribution fitting?* Binning, transformation, and empirical-CDF construction.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) 95% Confidence Interval (4 marks):**

Degrees of freedom: $n - 1 = 29$.

From the t-table, $t_{0.025, 29} = 2.045$.

$$\bar X \pm t_{0.025,29} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \;=\; 12.4 \pm 2.045 \times \frac{3.1}{\sqrt{30}}$$

Compute the standard error:

$$\frac{3.1}{\sqrt{30}} \;=\; \frac{3.1}{5.477} \;=\; 0.5660$$

Compute the half-width:

$$2.045 \times 0.5660 \;=\; 1.157$$

**Confidence Interval**:

$$(12.4 - 1.157, \;\; 12.4 + 1.157) \;=\; (11.24, \;\; 13.56) \text{ minutes}$$

**(ii) Required Sample Size (3 marks):**

Using the pilot estimate of $S = 3.1$ as proxy for $\sigma$:

$$n \;=\; \left(\frac{z_{0.025} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{1.96 \times 3.1}{0.5}\right)^2$$

Compute the numerator:

$$1.96 \times 3.1 \;=\; 6.076$$

Divide by 0.5:

$$\frac{6.076}{0.5} \;=\; 12.152$$

Square:

$$n \;=\; (12.152)^2 \;=\; 147.67$$

Round up: $n = 148$ observations total.

Since 30 observations are already collected:

$$\text{Additional observations} \;=\; 148 - 30 \;=\; 118$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the **i.i.d. + stationarity assumptions** for simulation input data. For each assumption, describe **one statistical test** to verify it. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hospital records the inter-arrival times (in minutes) of emergency patients for 10 consecutive days. The lag-1 autocorrelation is found to be $\rho_1 = 0.42$ with $n = 240$ observations.
    **(i)** Test, at $\alpha = 0.05$, whether the data is **independent**. (4 marks)
    **(ii)** If independence is violated, what **type of input model** is required? Justify. (3 marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Independence** (2 marks) — Each observation must be unaffected by previous ones (e.g., one customer's service time should not influence another's). **Test**: Compute the lag-1 autocorrelation coefficient $\rho_1$. Reject independence if $\vert\rho_1\vert > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$.

**2. Identical Distribution (Homogeneity)** (2 marks) — All observations must come from the same parent distribution (i.e., the system operates in one regime). **Test**: Split data into sub-groups (e.g., by day or shift) and run a one-way ANOVA on the sub-group means. Reject homogeneity if $F$-statistic exceeds $F_{\alpha, k-1, n-k}$.

**3. Stationarity** (2 marks) — The distribution parameters (mean, variance) must not drift over time. **Test**: Construct a Cusum chart and look for a slope; or use a run test on the sequence above/below the median. Reject stationarity on a significant run count.

**4. (Synthesis — 1 mark)** — All four must hold simultaneously to apply classical goodness-of-fit tests. Violation of any one requires an *alternative input model*: time-series for non-stationary/correlated, mixture for non-homogeneous.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Test of Independence (4 marks):**

**Hypotheses**:
- $H_0$: Data is independent ($\rho_1 = 0$)
- $H_1$: Data is not independent ($\rho_1 \neq 0$)

**Critical bound**:

$$\frac{2}{\sqrt{n}} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{240}} \;=\; \frac{2}{15.492} \;=\; 0.1291$$

**Compare**:

$$\vert \rho_1 \vert \;=\; 0.42 \;>\; 0.1291$$

**Decision**: Reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Conclusion**: The inter-arrival times are **not independent**; they exhibit positive serial correlation. Likely cause: the arrival rate varies by time-of-day (peak hours vs. off-peak).

**(ii) Type of Input Model Required (3 marks):**

Since independence is violated, the classical i.i.d. assumption for distribution fitting **does not hold**. The required input model is a **time-dependent / non-stationary stochastic process**, modelled using one of:

1. **Non-homogeneous Poisson Process (NHPP)** — if arrivals come from a Poisson process with a time-varying rate $\lambda(t)$ (e.g., piecewise-constant or sinusoidal rate).
2. **Autoregressive Time-Series Model (AR, ARMA, ARIMA)** — if the inter-arrival times themselves form a correlated sequence.
3. **State-dependent Arrival Model** — if the correlation arises because the system state (queue length, current load) feeds back to the arrival rate.

**Justification (1 mark)**: The positive value of $\rho_1 = 0.42$ indicates a *persistent* pattern (long runs of high or low inter-arrival times) consistent with **time-of-day variation** in patient inflow. An NHPP with hourly rate function $\lambda(t)$ is the most parsimonious and practically adopted model in hospital simulation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Data Collection is the FIRST step** of input modelling — it precedes distribution identification, parameter estimation, and goodness-of-fit testing.
- ✅ Always verify the **i.i.d. + stationarity** assumption **before** fitting any distribution.
- ✅ **Sample size formula**: $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ — *always round up* the final value.
- ✅ Use $t$-distribution when $\sigma$ is estimated from a pilot sample; use $z$ only when $\sigma$ is *known* from prior studies.
- ✅ **Lag-1 autocorrelation test**: Independence is rejected when $\vert \rho_1 \vert > \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- ✅ **Outlier detection** uses either the IQR rule ($1.5 \times IQR$) or the $z$-score rule ($\vert z \vert > 3$).
- ✅ **Three data collection methods**: Retrospective (cheap, big *n*), Observational (accurate, costly), Designed Experiments (controlled, artificial).
- ✅ **Sturges' rule** for histogram bins: $k = 1 + \log_2(n)$.
- ✅ Skewed data may require **log or square-root transformation** before fitting.
- ✅ If the data is **non-stationary** → use an **NHPP**; if **correlated** → use an **ARIMA** model.
- ✅ Always state the **assumption of i.i.d. + stationarity explicitly** in your KTU exam answer before fitting — examiners look for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Identifying the Distribution with the Collected Data

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Distribution Identification** is the statistical procedure of determining the most appropriate theoretical probability distribution that best represents a collected empirical dataset of random observations from a real-world system. In the context of **System Simulation**, this forms the critical bridge between raw data collection (Input Analysis) and the construction of a valid stochastic simulation model.

The procedure involves:
1. **Visual inspection** of the data using frequency histograms and quantile plots.
2. **Parameter estimation** for candidate theoretical distributions.
3. **Goodness-of-fit (GOF) testing** using formal statistical hypothesis tests such as the **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) test** and the **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test**.
4. **Selection** of the distribution that most parsimoniously represents the underlying stochastic process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module objective is to "identify the distribution with the collected data" using empirical observations and formal statistical tests. Marks are typically awarded for selecting the correct test, computing the test statistic, comparing with critical values, and stating the conclusion at a chosen **significance level ($\alpha$)** (commonly **$\alpha = 0.05$**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a forensic scientist who has collected **500 fingerprints** at a crime scene, and each fingerprint has a measurable ridge width. You do not know what mathematical rule generated these widths. Your job is to **find the rule**.

- **Step 1 — "Eyeball the shape":** Plot a histogram of all 500 widths. If it looks like a "bell," it might be a **Normal distribution**. If it looks like a "decaying slide," it might be an **Exponential distribution**.
- **Step 2 — "Measure the key numbers":** Compute the **mean ($\mu$)** and **variance ($\sigma^2$)** — these tell you *where* the bell is centered and *how wide* it is.
- **Step 3 — "Run a court trial (Hypothesis Test)":** Formally ask: *"Is the data consistent with the bell-shaped Normal distribution?"* The court (statistical test) gives a verdict — **accept** or **reject** the suspect distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Meaning:** Distribution identification = matching observed data to a known theoretical family of probability curves, then proving the match statistically. Without this step, any simulation built on arbitrary assumptions is mathematically unreliable.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Empirical Distribution** | The observed frequency distribution constructed from collected sample data. |
| **Theoretical Distribution** | A mathematically defined probability model (e.g., Normal, Exponential, Poisson). |
| **Goodness-of-Fit (GOF)** | A measure of how well observed data matches a candidate theoretical distribution. |
| **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)** | Statement that the data *follows* the candidate theoretical distribution. |
| **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$)** | Statement that the data does *not* follow the candidate distribution. |
| **Significance Level ($\alpha$)** | Probability threshold for rejecting $H_0$ (typically **0.05** or **0.01**). |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | Number of independent pieces of information used in a statistical test. |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Pipeline of Distribution Identification

The rigorous KTU-endorsed procedure for identifying a distribution from collected data follows this ordered pipeline:

1. **Data Collection & Preparation**
   - Gather $N$ independent observations: $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$.
   - Remove obvious outliers (use boxplots or the $1.5 \times IQR$ rule).
   - Compute summary statistics: **sample mean ($\bar{x}$)**, **sample variance ($s^2$)**, **sample skewness**, and **sample kurtosis**.

2. **Visual Inspection (Heuristic Stage)**
   - Construct a **frequency histogram** with $\approx \sqrt{N}$ equal-width class intervals.
   - Overlay candidate PDF/PMF curves (scaled appropriately).
   - Construct a **Quantile-Quantile (Q-Q) plot**: plot the empirical quantiles against the theoretical quantiles. A near-linear pattern suggests a good fit.

3. **Candidate Selection Based on Shape Descriptors**
   - **Bounded, symmetric, bell-shaped** → Normal / Uniform
   - **Right-skewed, positive support only** → Exponential / Lognormal / Gamma
   - **Discrete, count-based** → Poisson / Geometric
   - **Discrete, trials-until-success** → Binomial / Negative Binomial

4. **Parameter Estimation**
   - **Method of Moments:** Equate sample moments to theoretical moments and solve.
   - **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE):** Maximize the likelihood function $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i;\theta)$.

5. **Formal Goodness-of-Fit Testing**
   - Apply the **Chi-Square test** (for grouped data) and/or the **Kolmogorov-Smirnov test** (for ungrouped data).
   - Compute the test statistic, compare with the critical value at significance level $\alpha$, and accept or reject $H_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world simulation (queue modeling, reliability engineering, traffic flow, manufacturing), correctly identifying inter-arrival and service-time distributions directly determines the accuracy of performance predictions such as average waiting time, system throughput, and resource utilization.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | First moment estimator |
| 2 | Sample Variance | $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Second central moment |
| 3 | Class Frequency | $f_i$ | Number of observations in class $i$ |
| 4 | Class Relative Frequency | $p_i = f_i / N$ | Empirical probability of class $i$ |
| 5 | Expected Frequency | $E_i = N \cdot p_i(\hat{\theta})$ | Theoretical count under $H_0$ |
| 6 | **Chi-Square Statistic** | $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | Test statistic for grouped data |
| 7 | Degrees of Freedom (Chi-Square) | $\nu = k - s - 1$ | Where $k$ = # classes, $s$ = # estimated parameters |
| 8 | **K-S Statistic** | $D = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \vert S_N(x_i) - F(x_i) \vert$ | Maximum vertical deviation |
| 9 | Empirical CDF | $S_N(x) = \dfrac{\text{# observations} \le x}{N}$ | Step function CDF |
| 10 | Critical Value Comparison | Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{\alpha,\nu}$ or $D > D_{\alpha,N}$ | Decision rule |
| 11 | Normal MLE | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Normal distribution parameters |
| 12 | Exponential MLE | $\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{x}$ | Rate parameter estimate |
| 13 | Poisson MLE | $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$ | Mean rate estimate |
| 14 | Critical $\chi^2$ (common) | $\chi^2_{0.05, \nu}$ | Tabulated; e.g., $\chi^2_{0.05,5} = 11.07$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latex Pipe Rule:** All absolute-value expressions in tables and equations use `\vert` (not the unescaped pipe `\|`) to keep markdown tables valid.

### 2.3 Physical Constants & Standard Statistical Thresholds

| Constant / Threshold | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Significance level $\alpha$** | **0.05** (95% confidence) |
| **Confidence level** | $1 - \alpha = 0.95$ |
| **Minimum sample size for $\chi^2$ test** | $N \ge 50$ (and each $E_i \ge 5$) |
| **Critical $\chi^2_{0.05, 5}$** | **11.07** |
| **Critical $\chi^2_{0.05, 4}$** | **9.488** |
| **Critical $D_{0.05, 50}$ (K-S)** | **$\approx 0.192$** |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Statistic

The **Pearson Chi-Square statistic** measures the squared deviation between observed and expected counts, normalized by the expected count.

**Starting Point:** For $k$ mutually exclusive classes with observed frequencies $O_i$ and expected frequencies $E_i$:

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
$$

**Algebraic Expansion of Each Term:**

$$
\frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i} = \frac{O_i^2 - 2 O_i E_i + E_i^2}{E_i}
$$

$$
= \frac{O_i^2}{E_i} - 2 O_i + E_i
$$

**Summing over all $k$ classes:**

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{O_i^2}{E_i} - 2 \sum_{i=1}^{k} O_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i
$$

Since $\sum_{i=1}^{k} O_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i = N$, the last two sums equal $N$ each:

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{O_i^2}{E_i} - 2N + N
$$

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{O_i^2}{E_i} - N
$$

This is the **computationally efficient form** preferred in KTU exam answers.

### 3.2 Complete Worked Example — Chi-Square Test for Exponential Fit

**Problem Statement:**
A bank collects **$N = 100$** inter-arrival times (in minutes). The data is grouped into $k = 6$ classes. The sample mean is computed as $\bar{x} = 2.5$ minutes. Under the null hypothesis $H_0$, the inter-arrival times follow an **exponential distribution**. Test this at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Exponential Parameter (MLE):**

$$
\hat{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\bar{x}} = \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.4 \text{ per minute}
$$

**Step 2 — Class Boundaries and Exponential CDF:**

For an Exponential distribution:

$$
F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}
$$

The expected probability for class $i$ bounded by $[a_i, a_{i+1})$ is:

$$
p_i = F(a_{i+1}) - F(a_i) = e^{-\lambda a_i} - e^{-\lambda a_{i+1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_i$ and Tabulate:**

Suppose the observed and computed values yield the following table (values typical for an exam problem):

| Class Interval | $O_i$ | $p_i$ (computed) | $E_i = 100 p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[0, 1)$ | 38 | 0.330 | 33.0 |
| $[1, 2)$ | 22 | 0.221 | 22.1 |
| $[2, 3)$ | 17 | 0.148 | 14.8 |
| $[3, 4)$ | 10 | 0.099 | 9.9 |
| $[4, 5)$ | 8 | 0.066 | 6.6 |
| $[5, \infty)$ | 5 | 0.136 | 13.6 |
| **Total** | **100** | **1.000** | **100.0** |

**Step 4 — Compute the Chi-Square Statistic (Term-by-Term):**

$$
\frac{(38 - 33.0)^2}{33.0} = \frac{25}{33.0} = 0.758
$$

$$
\frac{(22 - 22.1)^2}{22.1} = \frac{0.01}{22.1} = 0.0005
$$

$$
\frac{(17 - 14.8)^2}{14.8} = \frac{4.84}{14.8} = 0.327
$$

$$
\frac{(10 - 9.9)^2}{9.9} = \frac{0.01}{9.9} = 0.001
$$

$$
\frac{(8 - 6.6)^2}{6.6} = \frac{1.96}{6.6} = 0.297
$$

$$
\frac{(5 - 13.6)^2}{13.6} = \frac{73.96}{13.6} = 5.438
$$

**Summing all terms:**

$$
\chi^2_0 = 0.758 + 0.0005 + 0.327 + 0.001 + 0.297 + 5.438 = 6.821
$$

**Step 5 — Determine Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
\nu = k - s - 1 = 6 - 1 - 1 = 4
$$

where $s = 1$ because $\lambda$ was estimated from the data.

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ and $\nu = 4$:

$$
\chi^2_{0.05, 4} = 9.488
$$

Compare:

$$
\chi^2_0 = 6.821 < 9.488 = \chi^2_{0.05, 4}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\chi^2_0$ does **not exceed** the critical value, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The data is consistent with an Exponential distribution with rate $\lambda = 0.4$ at the 5% significance level.

### 3.3 Complete Worked Example — Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test for Normal Fit

**Problem Statement:**
Five observations: $x = \{2.1, 3.4, 4.0, 5.2, 6.7\}$. Test $H_0$: data is from a $N(\mu = 4, \sigma = 1)$ distribution at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Sort and Compute Empirical CDF:**

Sorted data: $2.1, 3.4, 4.0, 5.2, 6.7$. For sorted data, $S_N(x_i) = i/N$.

**Step 2 — Compute Theoretical CDF $F(x_i)$:**

$$
F(x) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{x - 4}{1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate:**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $S_N(x_i) = i/N$ | $F(x_i)$ | $\vert S_N - F \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 0.20 | $\Phi(-1.9) = 0.0287$ | 0.1713 |
| 2 | 3.4 | 0.40 | $\Phi(-0.6) = 0.2743$ | 0.1257 |
| 3 | 4.0 | 0.60 | $\Phi(0.0) = 0.5000$ | 0.1000 |
| 4 | 5.2 | 0.80 | $\Phi(1.2) = 0.8849$ | 0.0849 |
| 5 | 6.7 | 1.00 | $\Phi(2.7) = 0.9965$ | 0.0035 |

**Step 4 — K-S Statistic:**

$$
D = \max_{i} \vert S_N(x_i) - F(x_i) \vert = 0.1713
$$

**Step 5 — Critical Value:**

For $N = 5$ and $\alpha = 0.05$, $D_{0.05, 5} = 0.565$.

Since $D = 0.1713 < 0.565$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

### 3.4 Method of Moments — Estimation for Normal Distribution

Equate the first two population moments to sample moments:

$$
\mu = E[X] \approx \bar{x}, \quad \sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X) \approx s^2
$$

**Derivation for Exponential:**

For Exponential$(\lambda)$, $E[X] = 1/\lambda$. Equating to $\bar{x}$:

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = \bar{x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\bar{x}}
$$

**Derivation for Poisson:**

For Poisson$(\lambda)$, $E[X] = \lambda$. Equating to $\bar{x}$:

$$
\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the moment-equality step explicitly. Examiners award full marks when they see: *"Equating the theoretical mean to the sample mean: $E[X] = \bar{x}$"* before solving for $\hat{\theta}$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart — Distribution Identification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Raw Collected Data X1 to XN"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Summary Statistics"]
    B --> B1["Sample Mean xbar"]
    B --> B2["Sample Variance s squared"]
    B --> B3["Sample Skewness"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Visual Inspection"]
    C --> C1["Construct Histogram with sqrt N bins"]
    C --> C2["Construct Q-Q Plot"]
    D["Step 3: Candidate Selection by Shape"] --> D1["Symmetric Bell shaped: Normal or Uniform"]
    D --> D2["Right Skewed Positive: Exponential or Lognormal"]
    D --> D3["Discrete Count: Poisson or Binomial"]
    E["Step 4: Parameter Estimation"] --> E1["Method of Moments"]
    E --> E2["Maximum Likelihood Estimation MLE"]
    F["Step 5: Goodness of Fit Testing"] --> F1["Chi Square Test for Grouped Data"]
    F --> F2["Kolmogorov Smirnov Test for Ungrouped Data"]
    F1 --> G{"Test Statistic exceeds Critical Value"}
    F2 --> G
    G -->|Yes| H["REJECT H0: Data does NOT fit distribution"]
    G -->|No| I["FAIL TO REJECT H0: Data FITS distribution"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT: Selected Theoretical Distribution with Estimated Parameters"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing Between Chi-Square and K-S Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["START: Have Observed Data"] --> Q{"Is Data Grouped into Classes"}
    Q -->|"Yes use Chi Square"| R["Step 1: Define k Classes"]
    Q -->|"No use K S"| S["Step 1: Sort Data"]
    R --> R1["Step 2: Estimate Parameters s"]
    R --> R2["Step 3: Compute Expected Frequencies E_i"]
    R --> R3["Step 4: Ensure all E_i greater than or equal to 5"]
    R2 --> T["Step 5: Compute Chi Square Statistic"]
    R3 --> T
    T --> T1["Step 6: DOF equals k minus s minus 1"]
    T1 --> T2{"Compare with chi square table"}
    T2 -->|Reject| U1["Reject H0"]
    T2 -->|Accept| U2["Fail to Reject H0"]
    S --> S1["Step 2: Compute Empirical CDF S_N"]
    S --> S2["Step 3: Compute Theoretical CDF F"]
    S1 --> V["Step 4: Find D equals max vertical gap"]
    S2 --> V
    V --> V1["Step 5: Compare with D alpha N table"]
    V1 -->|Reject| W1["Reject H0"]
    V1 -->|Accept| W2["Fail to Reject H0"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "identifying the distribution" of collected data in system simulation? List any two goodness-of-fit tests used for this purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

Identifying the distribution of collected data refers to the statistical procedure of selecting the most appropriate theoretical probability distribution that best represents an empirical dataset obtained from observations of a real-world system. It involves examining the shape of the data, estimating distribution parameters, and statistically testing the goodness of fit.

Two goodness-of-fit tests are:
1. **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test** — suitable for grouped (binned) data.
2. **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test** — suitable for ungrouped continuous data.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the null and alternative hypotheses for testing whether the collected data follows an Exponential distribution. Why is the degrees of freedom for the Chi-Square test equal to $k - s - 1$?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The collected data follows an Exponential distribution with rate parameter $\lambda$.
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** The collected data does **not** follow an Exponential distribution.

The degrees of freedom $\nu = k - s - 1$ because:
- $k$ classes provide $k$ observed counts.
- One constraint: $\sum O_i = N$ consumes 1 degree of freedom.
- $s$ parameters are estimated from the data itself (using sample moments or MLE), consuming an additional $s$ degrees of freedom.
- Therefore, the independent pieces of information remaining are $k - 1 - s = k - s - 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the step-by-step procedure of the **Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test** for identifying a distribution from collected data. Mention the assumptions and limitations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A manufacturing unit records the number of machine breakdowns per day for **$N = 60$** days. The data is summarized below. Using $\alpha = 0.05$, test whether the data follows a **Poisson distribution** with $\lambda = 2$.

| Breakdowns (X) | Observed Frequency ($O_i$) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 8 |
| 1 | 17 |
| 2 | 20 |
| 3 | 9 |
| 4 | 4 |
| $\ge 5$ | 2 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chi-Square Test Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **State Hypotheses:**
   - $H_0$: The data follows the specified theoretical distribution.
   - $H_1$: The data does not follow the distribution.

2. **Group the data** into $k$ mutually exclusive classes. Each class should have an expected frequency $E_i \ge 5$. Combine tail classes if necessary.

3. **Estimate parameters** ($s$ parameters) from the data using MLE or Method of Moments.

4. **Compute expected frequencies** using the theoretical PMF/PDF:
   $E_i = N \cdot p_i(\hat{\theta})$

5. **Compute the test statistic:**
   $\chi^2_0 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$

6. **Determine degrees of freedom:** $\nu = k - s - 1$.

7. **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{\alpha, \nu}$; otherwise, fail to reject.

**Assumptions:** Random sample, independent observations, sufficiently large $N$, classes with $E_i \ge 5$.

**Limitations:** Requires grouped data, loses information during binning, sensitive to bin-width choice.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** For Poisson($\lambda = 2$):

$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^x}{x!}
$$

Compute each probability:

$$
P(0) = e^{-2} = 0.1353, \quad P(1) = 2e^{-2} = 0.2707
$$

$$
P(2) = \frac{2^2 e^{-2}}{2} = 0.2707, \quad P(3) = \frac{8e^{-2}}{6} = 0.1804
$$

$$
P(4) = \frac{16e^{-2}}{24} = 0.0902, \quad P(\ge 5) = 1 - 0.9473 = 0.0527
$$

**Step 2:** Expected Frequencies $E_i = 60 \cdot p_i$:

| X | $O_i$ | $p_i$ | $E_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 8 | 0.1353 | 8.12 |
| 1 | 17 | 0.2707 | 16.24 |
| 2 | 20 | 0.2707 | 16.24 |
| 3 | 9 | 0.1804 | 10.82 |
| 4 | 4 | 0.0902 | 5.41 |
| $\ge 5$ | 2 | 0.0527 | 3.17 |

**Step 3:** Since $E_i < 5$ for the last class, combine X = 4 and X $\ge 5$:

Combined: $O = 4 + 2 = 6$, $E = 5.41 + 3.17 = 8.58$.

Now $k = 5$ classes.

**Step 4:** Compute $\chi^2_0$:

$$
\frac{(8 - 8.12)^2}{8.12} = 0.0016
$$

$$
\frac{(17 - 16.24)^2}{16.24} = 0.0353
$$

$$
\frac{(20 - 16.24)^2}{16.24} = 0.8694
$$

$$
\frac{(9 - 10.82)^2}{10.82} = 0.3111
$$

$$
\frac{(6 - 8.58)^2}{8.58} = 0.7816
$$

**Sum:**

$$
\chi^2_0 = 0.0016 + 0.0353 + 0.8694 + 0.3111 + 0.7816 = 1.999
$$

**Step 5:** Degrees of freedom: $\nu = k - s - 1 = 5 - 1 - 1 = 3$.

**Step 6:** Critical value: $\chi^2_{0.05, 3} = 7.815$.

**Conclusion:** Since $1.999 < 7.815$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The data is consistent with Poisson($\lambda = 2$) at the 5% significance level.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Kolmogorov-Smirnov test** in detail. Compare it with the Chi-Square test.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The following 8 observations of a process inter-arrival time (in seconds) are collected: $\{1.2, 2.5, 0.8, 3.1, 2.0, 4.2, 1.7, 2.9\}$. Using the K-S test at $\alpha = 0.05$, test whether the data follows an **Exponential distribution with mean 2.5 seconds**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) K-S Test Description (7 Marks):**

The **Kolmogorov-Smirnov test** is a non-parametric test that compares the empirical cumulative distribution function $S_N(x)$ with the theoretical CDF $F(x)$.

**Procedure:**
1. Sort the $N$ observations in ascending order.
2. Compute the empirical CDF: $S_N(x_i) = i/N$.
3. Compute the theoretical CDF at each $x_i$.
4. Find the test statistic: $D = \max \vert S_N(x_i) - F(x_i) \vert$.
5. Compare $D$ with the critical value $D_{\alpha, N}$ from K-S tables.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Chi-Square Test | K-S Test |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | Grouped | Ungrouped |
| Information loss | Yes (binning) | No (uses raw data) |
| Suitable for | Discrete & continuous | Continuous only |
| Sample size | Large ($N \ge 50$) | Any size |
| Sensitivity | Less sensitive in tails | Sensitive across whole range |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Sort data: $\{0.8, 1.2, 1.7, 2.0, 2.5, 2.9, 3.1, 4.2\}$.

**Step 2:** For Exponential with mean = 2.5, $\lambda = 1/2.5 = 0.4$.

$$
F(x) = 1 - e^{-0.4 x}
$$

**Step 3:** Tabulation:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $S_N = i/N$ | $F(x_i) = 1 - e^{-0.4 x_i}$ | $\vert S_N - F \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8 | 0.125 | $1 - e^{-0.32} = 0.2739$ | 0.1489 |
| 2 | 1.2 | 0.250 | $1 - e^{-0.48} = 0.3812$ | 0.1312 |
| 3 | 1.7 | 0.375 | $1 - e^{-0.68} = 0.4934$ | 0.1184 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 0.500 | $1 - e^{-0.80} = 0.5507$ | 0.0507 |
| 5 | 2.5 | 0.625 | $1 - e^{-1.00} = 0.6321$ | 0.0071 |
| 6 | 2.9 | 0.750 | $1 - e^{-1.16} = 0.6869$ | 0.0631 |
| 7 | 3.1 | 0.875 | $1 - e^{-1.24} = 0.7113$ | 0.1637 |
| 8 | 4.2 | 1.000 | $1 - e^{-1.68} = 0.8138$ | 0.1862 |

**Step 4:** Maximum deviation:

$$
D = \max(0.1489, 0.1312, 0.1184, 0.0507, 0.0071, 0.0631, 0.1637, 0.1862) = 0.1862
$$

**Step 5:** Critical value: $D_{0.05, 8} = 0.457$.

**Conclusion:** Since $D = 0.1862 < 0.457$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The data is consistent with Exponential(mean = 2.5) at the 5% significance level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting parameter adjustment in DOF:** When parameters are estimated from data, students often compute DOF as $k - 1$ instead of $k - s - 1$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Combining classes incorrectly:** Tail classes with $E_i < 5$ must be **merged** with adjacent classes, NOT discarded. Failure to merge violates test assumptions.
> 3. **Using the wrong test for small samples:** K-S test is preferred when $N < 50$. Chi-Square is invalid for small $N$.
> 4. **Mis-stating the conclusion:** Students often write "accept $H_0$" instead of the correct statistical phrasing "**fail to reject $H_0$**." Examiners deduct marks for this in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Not specifying the significance level $\alpha$:** Always declare $\alpha$ before stating the decision rule.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Identifying Distributions:**

- [x] **Definition:** Distribution identification = matching empirical data to a theoretical probability model using visual + statistical methods.
- [x] **Two Main Tests:** Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) for grouped data; Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) for ungrouped continuous data.
- [x] **Chi-Square Statistic:** $\chi^2_0 = \sum (O_i - E_i)^2 / E_i$.
- [x] **Chi-Square DOF:** $\nu = k - s - 1$, where $s$ = number of estimated parameters.
- [x] **K-S Statistic:** $D = \max \vert S_N(x) - F(x) \vert$.
- [x] **Empirical CDF:** $S_N(x_i) = i/N$ for sorted data.
- [x] **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if test statistic > critical value at $\alpha$.
- [x] **Standard $\alpha$:** **0.05** unless stated otherwise.
- [x] **Minimum Sample Size for $\chi^2$:** $N \ge 50$, and every $E_i \ge 5$.
- [x] **Estimator Formulas:**
  - Exponential: $\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{x}$
  - Poisson: $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$
  - Normal: $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = s^2$
- [x] **Conclusion Phrasing:** Always say "fail to reject $H_0$" (not "accept $H_0$").
- [x] **Always declare $\alpha$** before applying the test.
- [x] **Practical Use:** Identifying correct input distributions makes simulation output predictions statistically reliable and engineering-grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goodness of Fit Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Goodness of Fit Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goodness of Fit (GOF) Tests** are a class of statistical hypothesis tests used in system simulation to determine whether a sample of observed data is drawn from a population that follows a specific theoretical probability distribution. They quantify the discrepancy between the **empirical distribution function (EDF)** derived from observed/simulated data and the **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** of the hypothesized theoretical distribution.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology (aligned with Banks, Carson, Nelson & Nicol — *Discrete-Event System Simulation*, 5th Ed., Pearson), GOF tests are categorized as **input model validation procedures** within the larger framework of the simulation study methodology.

**Key Formal Properties of a GOF Test:**
- **Null Hypothesis (H₀):** The observed data follow the hypothesized distribution $F_0(x)$.
- **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁):** The observed data do NOT follow the hypothesized distribution $F_0(x)$.
- **Test Statistic ($\mathbf{D}$ or $\mathbf{\chi^2}$):** A scalar quantity measuring the maximum (or aggregate) discrepancy between observed and expected behavior.
- **Significance Level ($\mathbf{\alpha}$):** Commonly chosen as **0.05** or **0.10** in KTU simulation laboratory contexts.
- **p-value:** The probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed, assuming $H_0$ is true. If $p < \alpha$, $H_0$ is **rejected**.

## 1.2 Three Canonical GOF Tests in KTU Curriculum

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2) emphasizes three tests for verifying input probability distributions:

| Test | Statistic | Best Use Case | Sample Size Sensitivity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test** | Aggregate frequency discrepancy | Discrete & binned continuous data | Requires $n \geq 30$ |
| **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test** | Maximum vertical deviation of CDFs | Continuous data, parameter-free | Robust for small $n$ |
| **Anderson-Darling (A-D) Test** | Weighted squared deviation (tail-sensitive) | Continuous data with critical tails | Better power than K-S |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Tailor Measuring a Suit"**
>
> Imagine you order a custom shirt online. When it arrives, you measure it with a tape at several points — shoulder, chest, sleeve, length — and compare to your body's measurements. The **GOF test is the same procedure applied to data**:
> - Your **body's measurements** = the *theoretical CDF* $F_0(x)$ (the expected pattern).
> - The **shirt's measurements** = the *empirical CDF* $S_n(x)$ (the actual sample).
> - The **test statistic** = how badly the shirt "fits" — a single number summarizing overall mismatch.
> - If the discrepancy is **too large**, the "fit" is rejected (H₀ is rejected).

**Engineering Interpretation:** In simulation, we often *assume* inter-arrival times follow an exponential distribution. Before running a 10,000-iteration simulation experiment, we must statistically verify this assumption. GOF tests are the formal validation step that prevents propagation of incorrect distributional assumptions through the entire model.

---

## 1.4 Why GOF Tests are Indispensable in System Simulation

> [!NOTE]
> A simulation model's output is only as credible as its **input distributions**. KTU examiners routinely test whether students understand that:
> - Fitting the wrong distribution leads to **biased performance metrics** (waiting times, queue lengths, throughput).
> - GOF tests are part of **Step 4 (Validating Input Models)** of the simulation study process defined by Banks et al.

The **four-stage input modeling procedure** is:
1. **Collect** raw data from the real system.
2. **Hypothesize** a candidate distribution family.
3. **Estimate** the parameters via MLE or method of moments.
4. **Validate** the fitted model using GOF tests.

Only after **Step 4 succeeds** (i.e., we fail to reject $H_0$) is the model ready to drive the simulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The General GOF Framework

Every GOF test follows the same logical skeleton:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five-Stage GOF Procedure (Universal Algorithm)**
> 1. **Order** the $n$ observations: $x_{(1)} \leq x_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq x_{(n)}$
> 2. **Define** the empirical distribution function $S_n(x) = \frac{\#\{i : x_i \leq x\}}{n}$
> 3. **Specify** the hypothesized CDF $F_0(x; \theta)$ with parameter set $\theta$
> 4. **Compute** the test statistic $D$ measuring $\lVert S_n(x) - F_0(x) \rVert$ in some appropriate norm
> 5. **Compare** $D$ to its sampling distribution under $H_0$ and obtain a $p$-value

The three tests differ **only** in:
- The **norm** used to measure discrepancy (e.g., sup-norm for K-S, $L^2$-weighted for A-D),
- The **binning strategy** (none for K-S/A-D, fixed-width bins for $\chi^2$),
- The **reference distribution** of the statistic under $H_0$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Reference (Examination Quick-Recall Table)**

**Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value bars use $\vert$ (LaTeX) instead of the keyboard pipe `$\vert$` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### A. Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Goodness of Fit Test

| Item | Mathematical Expression | Notes / KTU Requirement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test Statistic** | $\chi^2_0 = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $O_i$ = observed frequency in bin $i$; $E_i$ = expected frequency |
| **Expected Frequency** | $E_i = n \cdot \left[ F_0(x_i^U) - F_0(x_i^L) \right]$ | $n$ = sample size; $k$ = number of bins |
| **Degrees of Freedom** | $\nu = k - s - 1$ | $s$ = number of estimated parameters |
| **Decision Rule** | Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{\alpha, \nu}$ | Compare against critical value from $\chi^2$ table |
| **Bin Width Rule** | $k \approx \sqrt{n}$ or $k \approx 2 \cdot n^{2/5}$ | Each $E_i \geq 5$ (pool bins if necessary) |
| **Test Direction** | **Always right-tailed** | Only upper-tail rejection region |

### B. Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test

| Item | Mathematical Expression | Notes / KTU Requirement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test Statistic** | $D = \displaystyle\max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \left\vert S_n(x_{(i)}) - F_0(x_{(i)}) \right\vert$ | Supremum of vertical deviation |
| **Two-sided variant** | $D^+ = \max_i \left[ i/n - F_0(x_{(i)}) \right]$ | Upper deviation component |
| | $D^- = \max_i \left[ F_0(x_{(i)}) - (i-1)/n \right]$ | Lower deviation component |
| **Asymptotic Critical Value** | $D_{\alpha, n} \approx \sqrt{-\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(\alpha/2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ | For large $n$ |
| **Decision Rule** | Reject $H_0$ if $D > D_{\alpha, n}$ | Use table for small $n$ |
| **Limitation** | Parameters must be **pre-specified** (not estimated from same data) | Critical KTU exam point! |

### C. Anderson-Darling (A-D) Test

| Item | Mathematical Expression | Notes / KTU Requirement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test Statistic** | $A^2 = -n - \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(2i-1)}{n} \left[ \ln F_0(x_{(i)}) + \ln(1 - F_0(x_{(n+1-i)})) \right]$ | Tail-weighted $L^2$ norm |
| **Critical Values** | $A^2_{\alpha}$ tabulated by distribution family | More powerful than K-S in tails |
| **Adjusted Statistic** | $A^{*2} = A^2 \cdot \left( 1 + \tfrac{0.75}{n} + \tfrac{2.25}{n^2} \right)$ | Finite-sample correction |

### D. Universal Symbols Reference

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | Significance level (Type-I error probability), typically **0.05** |
| $1 - \beta$ | Statistical power of the test (probability of rejecting false $H_0$) |
| $\theta$ | Parameter vector of hypothesized distribution |
| $\hat{\theta}$ | MLE estimate of $\theta$ from sample data |
| $S_n(x)$ | Empirical (sample) CDF |
| $F_0(x)$ | Hypothesized theoretical CDF |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where GOF Tests are Used in KTU Industrial Contexts**
>
> - **Manufacturing Queues:** Validating that machine breakdown inter-arrival times follow a Poisson (exponential) process before simulating a production line.
> - **Telecommunications:** Fitting packet inter-arrival distributions before simulating a network router.
> - **Hospital Operations:** Validating patient arrival patterns before simulating an emergency department.
> - **Reliability Engineering:** Verifying Weibull distribution fits for component failure times.
> - **Inventory Systems:** Validating demand distributions before simulating $(s, S)$ policies.

The GOF test bridges the gap between **empirical observation** and **theoretical abstraction** — a fundamental epistemological step in any model-driven engineering discipline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Empirical CDF $S_n(x)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $S_n(x)$ defined as a step function?**
> The empirical CDF assigns to each observed datum its rank-fractional probability mass.

$$
S_n(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}\{x_i \leq x\}
$$

where $\mathbf{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function. By the **Strong Law of Large Numbers (SLLN)**:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n(x) = F(x) \quad \text{almost surely, for every continuity point } x \text{ of } F
$$

This convergence guarantee is the mathematical foundation that justifies GOF testing — as $n$ grows, the empirical distribution converges to the true distribution, so a large discrepancy between $S_n$ and $F_0$ is **statistical evidence** that $F_0 \neq F$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chi-Square Test Statistic Distribution

**Step 1 — Decompose total probability.**
For $k$ mutually exclusive bins partitioning the real line:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i = 1, \quad \text{where } p_i = P(X \in \text{Bin}_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Multinomial observation.**
Each observation falls into exactly one bin. Therefore the vector of bin counts $\mathbf{O} = (O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_k)$ follows a **multinomial distribution**:

$$
\mathbf{O} \sim \text{Multinomial}(n; p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k)
$$

**Step 3 — Expected frequency.**
Under $H_0$, with $p_i = p_i^{(0)}$, the expected count is:

$$
E[O_i] = n \cdot p_i^{(0)} \stackrel{\text{def}}{=} E_i
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic distribution (Pearson's Theorem).**
For large $n$, the standardized deviations converge jointly to a multivariate normal. The sum of squares of these standardized deviations follows a **chi-square distribution**:

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i} \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_{\nu}, \quad \nu = k - 1 - s
$$

where $s$ parameters were estimated from the same data (consuming $s$ degrees of freedom).

> [!NOTE]
> The **$-1$** in $\nu = k - 1 - s$ arises because the bin probabilities must sum to 1 (one linear constraint). The **$-s$** arises because parameter estimation using the same sample introduces additional uncertainty — a subtle but **frequently examined KTU point**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Kolmogorov-Smirnov Asymptotic Distribution

**Step 1 — Glivenko-Cantelli Theorem.**
The K-S test statistic satisfies:

$$
\sup_{x} \vert S_n(x) - F(x) \vert \xrightarrow{a.s.} 0
$$

**Step 2 — Donskin's Theorem.**
Moreover, when $F$ is continuous, the scaled supremum converges in distribution:

$$
\sqrt{n} \cdot D_n \xrightarrow{d} \sup_{0 \leq t \leq 1} \vert B(t) \vert
$$

where $B(t)$ is a **Brownian Bridge** (Gaussian process with $E[B(t)] = 0$, $\text{Var}[B(t)] = t(1-t)$, and $B(0) = B(1) = 0$).

**Step 3 — Kolmogorov's Distribution.**
The CDF of $\sup_{t \in [0,1]} \vert B(t) \vert$ is given by Kolmogorov's formula:

$$
P\!\left(\sqrt{n}\, D_n \leq x\right) \approx 1 - 2 \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{j-1} e^{-2 j^2 x^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Inversion for critical value.**
Solving for the critical value at level $\alpha$ inverts this expression:

$$
D_{\alpha, n} \approx \frac{\sqrt{-0.5 \ln(\alpha/2)}}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

For the common case $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
D_{0.05, n} \approx \frac{1.358}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (Chi-Square Test — KTU Examination Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Complete Worked Solution: Chi-Square GOF Test**

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
A bank observes 200 customer inter-arrival times. After binning into 5 equal-width intervals, the following frequencies are recorded. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the data follow an **exponential distribution with mean $\beta = 4$ minutes** (pre-specified, no parameter estimation).

**Given Data Table:**

| Bin | Range (minutes) | Observed $O_i$ | $F_0(x_i^U)$ | $F_0(x_i^L)$ | $p_i$ | Expected $E_i = 200 p_i$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $[0, 2)$ | **45** | $1 - e^{-2/4}$ | $0$ | $0.3935$ | $78.70$ |
| 2 | $[2, 4)$ | **55** | $1 - e^{-4/4}$ | $1 - e^{-2/4}$ | $0.2387$ | $47.74$ |
| 3 | $[4, 6)$ | **40** | $1 - e^{-6/4}$ | $1 - e^{-4/4}$ | $0.1447$ | $28.94$ |
| 4 | $[6, 8)$ | **35** | $1 - e^{-8/4}$ | $1 - e^{-6/4}$ | $0.0878$ | $17.56$ |
| 5 | $[8, \infty)$ | **25** | $1$ | $1 - e^{-8/4}$ | $0.1353$ | $27.06$ |
| | **Total** | **200** | | | **1.0000** | **200.00** |

**Step A — Verify expected frequencies.**
All $E_i \geq 5$? Yes (78.70, 47.74, 28.94, 17.56, 27.06). Proceed without pooling.

**Step B — Compute the test statistic.**

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1:} & \quad \frac{(45 - 78.70)^2}{78.70} = \frac{(-33.70)^2}{78.70} = \frac{1135.69}{78.70} = 14.430 \\
\text{Term 2:} & \quad \frac{(55 - 47.74)^2}{47.74} = \frac{(7.26)^2}{47.74} = \frac{52.71}{47.74} = 1.104 \\
\text{Term 3:} & \quad \frac{(40 - 28.94)^2}{28.94} = \frac{(11.06)^2}{28.94} = \frac{122.32}{28.94} = 4.227 \\
\text{Term 4:} & \quad \frac{(35 - 17.56)^2}{17.56} = \frac{(17.44)^2}{17.56} = \frac{304.15}{17.56} = 17.321 \\
\text{Term 5:} & \quad \frac{(25 - 27.06)^2}{27.06} = \frac{(-2.06)^2}{27.06} = \frac{4.24}{27.06} = 0.157
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Aggregate.**

$$
\chi^2_0 = 14.430 + 1.104 + 4.227 + 17.321 + 0.157 = 37.239
$$

**Step D — Degrees of freedom.**
Here $k = 5$ bins, $s = 0$ parameters estimated from sample (mean was *given* as $\beta = 4$):

$$
\nu = k - s - 1 = 5 - 0 - 1 = 4
$$

**Step E — Critical value lookup.**
From the chi-square table at $\alpha = 0.05$, $\nu = 4$:

$$
\chi^2_{0.05, 4} = 9.488
$$

**Step F — Decision.**

$$
\chi^2_0 = 37.239 > \chi^2_{0.05, 4} = 9.488
$$

Therefore, **reject $H_0$**. At the 5% significance level, the inter-arrival times do **not** follow an exponential distribution with mean 4 minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation in KTU Context:** The exponential assumption is statistically invalid; the modeler must either (a) collect more data and re-evaluate, (b) try a different distribution family (e.g., Weibull or Gamma), or (c) accept the misfit and proceed with documented caveats.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GOF Test Selection Guide for KTU Examination**

| Selection Criterion | $\chi^2$ Test | K-S Test | A-D Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data type** | Discrete or binned continuous | Continuous only | Continuous only |
| **Minimum sample size** | $n \geq 30$, all $E_i \geq 5$ | No strict minimum | No strict minimum |
| **Tail sensitivity** | Uniform across all bins | Moderate | High (tail-weighted) |
| **Discrete distributions** | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Not applicable | ❌ Not applicable |
| **Pre-estimated parameters** | Must reduce $\nu$ by $s$ | **Inapplicable** unless Lilliefors correction used | Requires critical-value table per family |
| **Computational simplicity** | High | Very high | Moderate |
| **KTU exam frequency** | Most frequently tested | Often tested | Less common but growing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master GOF Test Decision Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block follows the strict naming protocol: all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) and labels contain no markdown formatting characters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Have n observations x1...xn"]
    nodeB["Is data DISCRETE or can be binned?"]
    nodeC["Use CHI SQUARE TEST"]
    nodeD["Is data CONTINUOUS ungrouped?"]
    nodeE["Are tail discrepancies critical?"]
    nodeF["Use ANDERSON DARLING TEST"]
    nodeG["Are parameters KNOWN a priori?"]
    nodeH["Use KOLMOGOROV SMIRNOV TEST"]
    nodeI["Use LILLIEFORS CORRECTION with K S"]
    nodeJ["COMPUTE TEST STATISTIC chi2 or D or A2"]
    nodeK["COMPUTE p value from reference distribution"]
    nodeL{"Compare p value vs significance alpha"}
    nodeM["FAIL TO REJECT H0: Distribution FITS"]
    nodeN["REJECT H0: Distribution does NOT FIT"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL -- p greater than alpha --> nodeM
    nodeL -- p less than alpha --> nodeN
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: GOF Test Subsystem in a Simulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA["Raw Observations x1...xn"] --> procB["Sort and Order Data"]
    procB --> procC["Compute Empirical CDF Sn of x"]
    procD["Hypothesized Distribution F0 with theta"] --> procE["Estimate Parameters theta hat via MLE"]
    rawA --> procE
    procC --> compF["Comparison Layer"]
    procE --> compF
    compF --> statG["Statistic Computation chi2 or D or A2"]
    statG --> refH["Reference Sampling Distribution"]
    refH --> decI{"Decision Threshold alpha"}
    decI -- Accept H0 --> outJ["Validated Input Model"]
    decI -- Reject H0 --> outK["Return to Hypothesization"]
    outK --> procD
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Deviation Geometry of K-S Statistic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisA["X AXIS: Sorted Observations x_i"]
    axisB["Y AXIS: Cumulative Probability 0 to 1"]
    cdfC["Theoretical CDF F0 of x (smooth curve)"]
    edfD["Empirical CDF Sn of x (step function)"]
    dplus["D plus = maximum of Sn minus F0 above"]
    dminus["D minus = maximum of F0 minus Sn above"]
    dmax["D = max of D plus and D minus"]

    cdfC --- axisA
    edfD --- axisA
    cdfC --- axisB
    edfD --- axisB
    cdfC --> dplus
    edfD --> dplus
    cdfC --> dminus
    edfD --> dminus
    dplus --> dmax
    dminus --> dmax
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The K-S statistic $D$ is the **single largest vertical gap** anywhere between the theoretical CDF and the empirical step function. This geometric intuition is a **favorite KTU question type** — students are often asked to sketch the empirical CDF, plot the theoretical CDF, and identify $D$ graphically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks Total)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> Define Goodness of Fit Test. State the null and alternative hypotheses involved in a Chi-Square goodness of fit test.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** A Goodness of Fit Test is a statistical hypothesis test used to determine whether a set of observed data is drawn from a population that follows a specific theoretical probability distribution. It measures the discrepancy between the observed frequency distribution and the expected frequency distribution under the hypothesized model.
>
> **Hypotheses (2 Marks):**
> - **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The observed data follow the hypothesized distribution $F_0(x; \theta)$, i.e., $O_i = E_i$ for all bins.
> - **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** The observed data do **not** follow the hypothesized distribution, i.e., at least one $O_i \neq E_i$.
>
> **Test Statistic:** $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$, which under $H_0$ follows $\chi^2_{\nu}$ with $\nu = k - s - 1$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> Differentiate between the Chi-Square test and the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test as goodness of fit procedures.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Feature | Chi-Square Test | K-S Test |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Data type** | Discrete or grouped continuous | Continuous only |
> | **Discrepancy measure** | Aggregate squared deviation | Maximum vertical deviation |
> | **Sample size** | Requires $n \geq 30$ and $E_i \geq 5$ | Applicable for any $n$ |
> | **Distribution parameters** | Can accommodate estimated parameters | Parameters must be pre-specified |
> | **Bin dependency** | Sensitive to bin width choice | Bin-independent |

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 1 × 14 Marks)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the procedure for performing a Chi-Square goodness of fit test. State clearly the assumptions and the degrees of freedom involved.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] In a manufacturing unit, the number of machine breakdowns per day over 100 days is recorded. The data are grouped as follows. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether breakdowns follow a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda = 2$.
>
> | Breakdowns per day | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | $\geq 5$ |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Observed frequency | 12 | 24 | 30 | 18 | 10 | 6 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure for Chi-Square Goodness of Fit Test (Diagram + Steps)**
>
> ```
>        [Raw Data x1...xn]
>                |
>                v
>       [Define k Bins]
>                |
>                v
>   [Compute Observed O_i per bin]
>                |
>                v
>   [Hypothesize F0 with parameters]
>                |
>                v
>   [Compute Expected E_i = n*p_i]
>                |
>                v
>   [Verify ALL E_i greater than or equal to 5]
>           |              |
>          YES             NO
>           |              |
>           v              v
>   [Compute chi2_0]   [Pool adjacent bins]
>           |
>           v
>   [Compute nu = k - s - 1]
>           |
>           v
>   [Lookup chi2_alpha_nu]
>           |
>           v
>       [Decision]
>    |              |
>   REJECT      FAIL TO REJECT
> ```
>
> **Assumptions (3 Marks):**
> 1. Observations are **independent**.
> 2. The sample is drawn via **random sampling**.
> 3. Each bin's **expected frequency $E_i \geq 5$**.
> 4. The hypothesized distribution $F_0$ is **completely specified** (or its parameters estimated from independent data, not the same sample without correcting $\nu$).
> 5. Sample size is sufficiently large ($n \geq 30$).
>
> **Degrees of Freedom (2 Marks):**
> $$
> \nu = k - s - 1
> $$
> where $k$ = number of bins, $s$ = number of parameters estimated from the same sample.
>
> **Decision Rule (2 Marks):**
> Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{\alpha,\nu}$; otherwise fail to reject.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
> - $H_0$: Breakdowns follow Poisson($\lambda = 2$).
> - $H_1$: Breakdowns do not follow Poisson($\lambda = 2$).
>
> **Step 2 — Compute Poisson probabilities.**
> For Poisson with $\lambda = 2$: $P(X = x) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^x}{x!}$
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P(X=0) &= e^{-2} \cdot 1 = 0.1353 \\
> P(X=1) &= e^{-2} \cdot 2 = 0.2707 \\
> P(X=2) &= e^{-2} \cdot 2 = 0.2707 \\
> P(X=3) &= e^{-2} \cdot \tfrac{8}{6} = 0.1804 \\
> P(X=4) &= e^{-2} \cdot \tfrac{16}{24} = 0.0902 \\
> P(X \geq 5) &= 1 - (0.1353 + 0.2707 + 0.2707 + 0.1804 + 0.0902) = 0.0527
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute expected frequencies ($E_i = 100 \cdot p_i$).**
>
> | Bin | $O_i$ | $p_i$ | $E_i$ |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | 0 | 12 | 0.1353 | 13.53 |
> | 1 | 24 | 0.2707 | 27.07 |
> | 2 | 30 | 0.2707 | 27.07 |
> | 3 | 18 | 0.1804 | 18.04 |
> | 4 | 10 | 0.0902 | 9.02 |
> | $\geq 5$ | 6 | 0.0527 | 5.27 |
> | **Total** | **100** | **1.0000** | **100.00** |
>
> **Step 4 — Verify $E_i \geq 5$.** All $E_i \geq 5.27 > 5$. ✓ (No pooling needed.)
>
> **Step 5 — Compute $\chi^2_0$.**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \chi^2_0 &= \frac{(12-13.53)^2}{13.53} + \frac{(24-27.07)^2}{27.07} + \frac{(30-27.07)^2}{27.07} \\
> &\quad + \frac{(18-18.04)^2}{18.04} + \frac{(10-9.02)^2}{9.02} + \frac{(6-5.27)^2}{5.27} \\
> &= \frac{2.34}{13.53} + \frac{9.42}{27.07} + \frac{8.58}{27.07} + \frac{0.0016}{18.04} + \frac{0.96}{9.02} + \frac{0.53}{5.27} \\
> &= 0.173 + 0.348 + 0.317 + 0.0001 + 0.106 + 0.101 \\
> &= 1.045
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 6 — Degrees of freedom.** $\nu = k - s - 1 = 6 - 0 - 1 = 5$ (since $\lambda$ was pre-specified, $s = 0$).
>
> **Step 7 — Critical value.** From $\chi^2$ table: $\chi^2_{0.05, 5} = 11.070$.
>
> **Step 8 — Decision.**
>
> $$
> \chi^2_0 = 1.045 < \chi^2_{0.05, 5} = 11.070
> $$
>
> **Fail to reject $H_0$**. At the 5% significance level, the breakdown data are consistent with a Poisson distribution having mean $\lambda = 2$.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 14 Marks

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness of fit test. Describe how the test statistic $D$ is computed and its asymptotic distribution.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] The inter-arrival times of 10 customers at a service facility (in minutes) are recorded as: 0.8, 1.2, 0.5, 2.3, 1.7, 0.9, 3.1, 0.6, 2.0, 1.4. Using the K-S test at $\alpha = 0.05$, test whether the data are exponentially distributed with mean $\beta = 1.5$ minutes.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test — Explanation (7 Marks)**
>
> **Concept (2 Marks):** The K-S test compares the **empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) $S_n(x)$** with the **theoretical CDF $F_0(x)$** of the hypothesized distribution. Unlike the Chi-Square test, it does not require binning and works directly with the raw sorted data.
>
> **Test Statistic (3 Marks):**
>
> $$
> D = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \left\vert S_n(x_{(i)}) - F_0(x_{(i)}) \right\vert
> $$
>
> Equivalently:
>
> $$
> D = \max(D^+, D^-)
> $$
>
> where:
>
> $$
> D^+ = \max_{i} \left[ \frac{i}{n} - F_0(x_{(i)}) \right], \quad D^- = \max_{i} \left[ F_0(x_{(i)}) - \frac{i-1}{n} \right]
> $$
>
> **Asymptotic Distribution (2 Marks):** Under $H_0$ (and when $F_0$ is fully specified without estimated parameters):
>
> $$
> \sqrt{n} \cdot D_n \xrightarrow{d} K = \sup_{0 \leq t \leq 1} \vert B(t) \vert
> $$
>
> where $B(t)$ is a standard **Brownian Bridge**. The CDF of $K$ (Kolmogorov's distribution) is:
>
> $$
> P(K \leq x) = 1 - 2 \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{j-1} e^{-2 j^2 x^2}
> $$
>
> For practical use at $\alpha = 0.05$: $D_{0.05, n} \approx \dfrac{1.358}{\sqrt{n}}$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Hypotheses.**
> - $H_0$: Data $\sim$ Exponential($\beta = 1.5$).
> - $H_1$: Data do not follow this distribution.
>
> **Step 2 — Sort the data and compute $F_0(x)$ for Exponential($\beta = 1.5$).**
> CDF: $F_0(x) = 1 - e^{-x/1.5}$
>
> | $i$ | $x_{(i)}$ (sorted) | $F_0(x_{(i)})$ | $i/n$ | $(i-1)/n$ | $\vert i/n - F_0 \vert$ | $\vert F_0 - (i-1)/n \vert$ |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | 1 | 0.5 | 0.2835 | 0.10 | 0.00 | 0.1835 | 0.2835 |
> | 2 | 0.6 | 0.3297 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.1297 | 0.2297 |
> | 3 | 0.8 | 0.4135 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.1135 | 0.2135 |
> | 4 | 0.9 | 0.4512 | 0.40 | 0.30 | 0.0512 | 0.1512 |
> | 5 | 1.2 | 0.5507 | 0.50 | 0.40 | 0.0507 | 0.1507 |
> | 6 | 1.4 | 0.6084 | 0.60 | 0.50 | 0.0084 | 0.1084 |
> | 7 | 1.7 | 0.6988 | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.0012 | 0.0988 |
> | 8 | 2.0 | 0.7634 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.0366 | 0.0634 |
> | 9 | 2.3 | 0.8130 | 0.90 | 0.80 | 0.0870 | 0.0130 |
> | 10 | 3.1 | 0.8736 | 1.00 | 0.90 | 0.1264 | 0.0264 |
>
> **Sample calculation:** $F_0(0.5) = 1 - e^{-0.5/1.5} = 1 - e^{-0.3333} = 1 - 0.7165 = 0.2835$ ✓
>
> **Step 3 — Identify $D^+$, $D^-$, and $D$.**
>
> $$
> D^+ = \max\{0.1835, 0.1297, 0.1135, 0.0512, 0.0507, 0.0084, 0.0012, 0.0366, 0.0870, 0.1264\} = 0.1835
> $$
>
> $$
> D^- = \max\{0.2835, 0.2297, 0.2135, 0.1512, 0.1507, 0.1084, 0.0988, 0.0634, 0.0130, 0.0264\} = 0.2835
> $$
>
> $$
> D = \max(D^+, D^-) = \max(0.1835, 0.2835) = 0.2835
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Critical value.**
>
> $$
> D_{0.05, 10} \approx \frac{1.358}{\sqrt{10}} = \frac{1.358}{3.162} = 0.4295
> $$
>
> (Using exact K-S table: $D_{0.05, 10} = 0.409$.)
>
> **Step 5 — Decision.**
>
> $$
> D = 0.2835 < D_{0.05, 10} = 0.409
> $$
>
> **Fail to reject $H_0$.** At the 5% significance level, the inter-arrival data are consistent with an exponential distribution having mean 1.5 minutes.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Critical Pitfalls)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $-1$ in degrees of freedom:** Writing $\nu = k - s$ instead of $\nu = k - s - 1$. Costs **1 mark** in nearly every Chi-Square question.
>
> 2. **Neglecting the parameter-reduction:** When the mean is estimated *from the same sample*, students must use $\nu = k - 2$ for Exponential (1 parameter). This is the **most frequently failed pitfall** — KTU examiners specifically test whether students realize parameters must be pre-specified.
>
> 3. **Confusing two-sided vs one-sided K-S:** The K-S test is inherently **two-sided** ($D = \max(D^+, D^-)$). Students often compute only one component and report it as $D$.
>
> 4. **Using K-S on binned data:** K-S requires **continuous ungrouped data**. Applying it to a frequency table is mathematically invalid.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $E_i \geq 5$ check:** Failure to verify or pool bins loses **1–2 marks** automatically.
>
> 6. **Reporting the wrong decision direction:** Chi-Square rejection region is **always right-tailed** (large $\chi^2$ values indicate misfit). K-S rejection is **also right-tailed** (large $D$ values).
>
> 7. **Calculation errors in Poisson probabilities:** When $x \geq 5$, students often forget to compute the tail probability $P(X \geq 5) = 1 - \sum_{x=0}^{4} P(X = x)$.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 Examination**

- [ ] **GOF Tests validate input distributions** in the four-stage input modeling procedure (collect → hypothesize → estimate → **validate**).
- [ ] **Three canonical tests:** Chi-Square ($\chi^2$), Kolmogorov-Smirnov ($D$), Anderson-Darling ($A^2$).
- [ ] **Chi-Square statistic:** $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ with $\nu = k - s - 1$.
- [ ] **K-S statistic:** $D = \max \vert S_n(x) - F_0(x) \vert$, requires **continuous data** with **pre-specified parameters**.
- [ ] **Anderson-Darling statistic:** $A^2$ is **tail-weighted** — more powerful when tail behavior matters.
- [ ] **Critical chi-square values at $\alpha = 0.05$ to memorize:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$; $\chi^2_{0.05, 4} = 9.488$; $\chi^2_{0.05, 5} = 11.070$; $\chi^2_{0.05, 10} = 18.307$.
- [ ] **K-S critical value formula (large $n$):** $D_{\alpha, n} \approx \dfrac{\sqrt{-0.5 \ln(\alpha/2)}}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- [ ] **Universal decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{\alpha, \nu}$ OR $D > D_{\alpha, n}$ OR $A^2 > A^2_{\alpha}$.
- [ ] **Bin width rule of thumb:** $k \approx \sqrt{n}$ for $\chi^2$ test; always verify $E_i \geq 5$.
- [ ] **Never apply K-S to binned/grouped data** — use Chi-Square instead.
- [ ] **Always state hypotheses explicitly** before any computation — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing $H_0$/$H_1$.
- [ ] **Lilliefors correction** is required when using K-S with parameters estimated from the same sample.
- [ ] **Type-I error** = probability of rejecting a true $H_0$ = $\alpha$.
- [ ] **Type-II error** = probability of failing to reject a false $H_0$ = $\beta$.
- [ ] **Statistical power** = $1 - \beta$.
- [ ] **Decision is binary:** Either reject $H_0$ or *fail to reject* $H_0$ (never "accept" $H_0$).
- [ ] **Anderson-Darling's key advantage:** detects tail misfit that Chi-Square and K-S may miss.
- [ ] **Final engineering step:** If all GOF tests reject, the modeler must re-examine data collection, try alternative distributions, or accept the misfit with documented limitations.

---

> **END OF NOTES — OEIET724 MODULE 2 — GOODNESS OF FIT TESTS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selecting Input Models Without Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Selecting Input Models Without Data # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In discrete-event simulation (DES), an **input model** is a probability distribution (or a collection of distributions) that mathematically characterizes the stochastic elements of the system being simulated — inter-arrival times, service times, failure times, batch sizes, resource availability, etc. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selecting Input Models Without Data** refers to the formal set of techniques used to specify input probability distributions for a simulation model in the complete **absence of empirical observations** from the real system. In KTU terminology, this is also called the *ex-ante* (before-the-fact) or *a-priori* input modeling problem. When no input data are available, the modeler must rely on: 1. **Theoretical/Physical principles** governing the underlying process. 2. **Published parameter tables** for analogous systems. 3. **Subject-matter expert (SME) elicitation** of distributional parameters. 4. **Maximum-entropy** and **principle-of-insufficient-reason** arguments. 5. **Bounding/distributional-inequality** techniques (e.g., Markov, Chebyshev). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under *Module 2 – Input Modeling* and is assessed at the *Apply* and *Analyze* levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (CO2: *Apply input analysis techniques to construct valid input models for simulation*). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world analogy — The "Blindfolded Chef":** > Imagine you are a chef who must prepare a dish **without ever tasting the ingredients**. You cannot sample them. How do you decide how much salt to add? You fall back on: > - **Physical laws** (salt dissolves in water at a known rate), > - **Cookbook tables** (a published recipe for the *same type* of cuisine), > - **The head chef's experience** (an expert who *knows* the dish), > - **A safety range** (use the *minimum* until proven otherwise). > > **Selecting input models without data is exactly this.** You cannot observe the random process, so you construct the input model from *first principles*, *analogous published data*, and *expert judgment*, while enforcing *safety bounds*. ## 1.3 Why "Without Data" is a Real Engineering Problem In KTU-style exam questions and in real industry practice, the "no-data" situation is not rare. It arises when: - The system is **entirely new** (e.g., a new airport terminal under design). - The system is **not yet operational** (e.g., a prototype manufacturing line). - **Time and cost** prohibit data collection. - The data are **confidential** or **politically inaccessible**. - Data exist but are for an **analogous (not identical)** system. The **two fundamental deliverables** of no-data input modeling are: 1. **Choose the family** of distribution (e.g., Exponential, Normal, Triangular, Beta). 2. **Choose the parameters** of that family (e.g., $\lambda = 2.3$ per hour, $\mu = 50$ seconds, $\sigma = 5$ seconds). ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of No-Data Input Modeling | # | Pillar | Foundation | Example | |---|--------|------------|---------| | 1 | **Theoretical / Physical** | Laws of probability, queueing theory, renewal theory | Inter-arrival times of a Poisson process $\Rightarrow$ Exponential($\lambda$) | | 2 | **Published / Tabulated** | Industry handbooks, vendor specs, census data | Aircraft taxi time $\Rightarrow$ Uniform(60 s, 180 s) per FAA tables | | 3 | **Expert Judgment** | Domain experts, Delphi method, SME elicitation | Customer patience time $\Rightarrow$ Triangular(2, 5, 15) min | These three pillars form the analytical spine of Sections 2 and 3 below. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of No-Data Input Modeling Strategies The modeler's strategy can be hierarchically decomposed as follows. Each strategy is selected based on the **information available** about the underlying stochastic process. ### Strategy A — Distributional Assumption from Process Physics Some random phenomena are **mathematically well-defined** by their generating mechanism. In these cases the distribution is *forced* by theory: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The four classical "physics-forced" distributions:** > > 1. **Exponential** — if inter-event times are *memoryless* (Poisson process, radioactive decay, random arrivals under pure chance). > 2. **Normal** — if the variable is the *sum of many small independent effects* (Central Limit Theorem). > 3. **Uniform** — if the only known information is the *range* [a, b] and nothing else (Principle of Insufficient Reason by Laplace). > 4. **Poisson** — if the variable counts *events in an interval* and counts are rare and independent. ### Strategy B — Maximum-Entropy Distribution The **Principle of Maximum Entropy (POME)**, due to E. T. Jaynes (1957), states: > *Given a set of known constraints, the distribution that best represents the current state of knowledge is the one with the largest entropy.* Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with pdf $f(x)$. $$H(X) \;=\; -\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\,\ln f(x)\,dx$$ The POME selects $f(x)$ that **maximizes** $H(X)$ subject to the known moment constraints: $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\,dx &= 1 \\ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x\,f(x)\,dx &= E[X] \quad \text{(if mean known)} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x^{2}\,f(x)\,dx &= E[X^{2}] \quad \text{(if variance known)} \\ \vdots &= \vdots \end{aligned} $$ Solving via the calculus of variations with Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_0, \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots$ yields the **MaxEnt distribution**: $$f(x) \;=\; \exp\!\left(\lambda_{0} + \lambda_{1}x + \lambda_{2}x^{2} + \cdots\right)$$ Applying POME to common no-data scenarios gives: | Known Information | MaxEnt Distribution | Functional Form | |------------------|--------------------|------------------| | Range $\vert x \in [a, b]\vert$ only | **Uniform** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | | Mean $\mu > 0$ only, $x \ge 0$ | **Exponential** | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$, $\lambda = 1/\mu$ | | Mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$ | **Normal** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-(x-\mu)^{2}/2\sigma^{2}}$ | | Mean $\mu$, $x \in [0, \infty)$ | **Exponential** | Same as above | | Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^{2}$, $x \in [0,\infty)$ | **Gamma** | $f(x) = \dfrac{x^{k-1}e^{-x/\theta}}{\theta^{k}\Gamma(k)}$ | ### Strategy C — Distributional Bounds and Inequalities When parameters are *partially* known, the modeler constructs **bounds** on system performance: - **Markov's inequality:** $P(X \ge a) \le \dfrac{E[X]}{a}$ for $X \ge 0$. - **Chebyshev's inequality:** $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \ge k\sigma) \le \dfrac{1}{k^{2}}$. - **Cantelli's (one-sided Chebyshev) inequality:** $P(X - \mu \ge k\sigma) \le \dfrac{1}{1+k^{2}}$. These bounds are *distribution-free* — they require **no data** and only the mean and/or variance. ### Strategy D — Three-Point Estimation (Triangular / PERT) When an expert can supply only **three values** — *minimum* ($a$), *most likely* ($m$), and *maximum* ($b$) — the recommended no-data distribution is the **Triangular**: $$ \begin{aligned} f(x) &= \begin{cases} \dfrac{2(x-a)}{(b-a)(m-a)} & a \le x \le m \\[4pt] \dfrac{2(b-x)}{(b-a)(b-m)} & m < x \le b \\[4pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\[8pt] E[X] &= \dfrac{a+m+b}{3} \\[4pt] \text{Var}(X) &= \dfrac{a^{2}+m^{2}+b^{2}-am-ab-mb}{18} \end{aligned} $$ The **PERT (Beta-PERT)** modification uses $E[X] = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ to weight the mode four times more. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the *single most important* reference for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the **distributional triggers** and the **moment formulas**. | # | Known About $X$ | Recommended Distribution | Mean Formula | Variance Formula | |---|-----------------|--------------------------|--------------|------------------| | 1 | $\vert x \in [a,b]\vert$, nothing else | **Uniform** $(a,b)$ | $(a+b)/2$ | $(b-a)^{2}/12$ | | 2 | Mean $\mu$ only, $X \ge 0$ | **Exponential** | $\mu$ | $\mu^{2}$ | | 3 | Mean $\mu$, $X \ge 0$, CV known | **Gamma / Erlang** | $k\theta$ | $k\theta^{2}$ | | 4 | Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^{2}$, symmetric | **Normal** | $\mu$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | | 5 | Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^{2}$, $X \ge 0$ | **Log-Normal** | $e^{\mu+\sigma^{2}/2}$ | $(e^{\sigma^{2}}-1)e^{2\mu+\sigma^{2}}$ | | 6 | Min $a$, Mode $m$, Max $b$ (expert) | **Triangular** | $(a+m+b)/3$ | $\frac{a^{2}+m^{2}+b^{2}-am-ab-mb}{18}$ | | 7 | Min $a$, Mode $m$, Max $b$, weighted | **Beta-PERT** | $(a+4m+b)/6$ | $\frac{(b-a)^{2}}{36}$ | | 8 | Counts in interval, rare, independent | **Poisson($\lambda$)** | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | | 9 | Counts in interval, rate $\lambda t$ | **Poisson($\lambda t$)** | $\lambda t$ | $\lambda t$ | | 10 | Bernoulli trials, $n$ trials, $p$ success | **Binomial** $(n,p)$ | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | | 11 | Time-to-first-event, mean $\mu$ | **Exponential** | $\mu$ | $\mu^{2}$ | | 12 | Time-to-$k$-th-event, rate $\lambda$ | **Erlang** $(k, \lambda)$ | $k/\lambda$ | $k/\lambda^{2}$ | **Coefficient of Variation (CV):** $$CV \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\mu}$$ > **Diagnostic rule:** $CV < 0.30$ (low variability) $\Rightarrow$ Normal/Triangular; $0.30 \le CV \le 1.0$ $\Rightarrow$ Gamma/Log-Normal; $CV \ge 1.0$ $\Rightarrow$ Exponential. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utilities | Engineering Field | Typical No-Data Input | Selected Distribution | |-------------------|----------------------|----------------------| | Airport terminal design | Aircraft taxi times (new runway) | Triangular (from FAA tables) | | Manufacturing line | Machine time-to-failure (prototype) | Weibull with $\beta = 1.5$ (expert) | | Hospital ER | Patient arrival rate (new wing) | Poisson process $\Rightarrow$ Exp inter-arrival | | Call center | Service time (new product) | Log-Normal (industry handbook) | | Network router | Packet inter-arrival time | Exponential (memoryless traffic) | | Inventory system | Demand per day | Poisson or Negative Binomial | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum-Entropy Yields Uniform Distribution **Setup:** Suppose the only information about a continuous random variable $X$ is that it lies in a known bounded interval $\vert x \in [a, b]\vert$. We seek the pdf $f(x)$ that maximizes $H(X)$ subject to: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Constraint 1 (normalization):} \quad \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx &= 1 \\ \text{Constraint 2 (range):} \quad f(x) &= 0 \;\;\text{for}\;\; x \notin [a, b] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1.** Write the entropy functional: $$H[f] \;=\; -\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,\ln f(x)\,dx$$ **Step 2.** Construct the Lagrangian with multiplier $\lambda_0$: $$\mathcal{L} \;=\; -\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\ln f(x)\,dx \;+\; \lambda_{0}\!\left(\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx - 1\right)$$ **Step 3.** Take the variational derivative with respect to $f(x)$ and set to zero: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial f(x)} \;=\; -\ln f(x) - 1 + \lambda_{0} \;=\; 0$$ **Step 4.** Solve for $f(x)$: $$f(x) \;=\; e^{\lambda_{0} - 1}$$ This is a **constant** on $[a, b]$. Call it $c$. **Step 5.** Apply the normalization constraint: $$\int_{a}^{b} c\,dx \;=\; c(b-a) \;=\; 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c \;=\; \frac{1}{b-a}$$ **Conclusion:** $$f(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{b-a}, \quad x \in [a, b]$$ This is the **Uniform$(a, b)$** distribution. It is the *most honest* choice when we know only the range. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum-Entropy Yields Exponential Distribution **Setup:** Suppose we know only that $E[X] = \mu$ and that $X \ge 0$. Maximize entropy: $$ \begin{aligned} H[f] &= -\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x)\ln f(x)\,dx \\ \int_{0}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx &= 1 \\ \int_{0}^{\infty} x\,f(x)\,dx &= \mu \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1.** Lagrangian with two multipliers $\lambda_0, \lambda_1$: $$\mathcal{L} \;=\; -\int_{0}^{\infty}\!f\ln f\,dx \;+\; \lambda_{0}\!\left(\int_{0}^{\infty}\!f\,dx - 1\right) \;+\; \lambda_{1}\!\left(\int_{0}^{\infty}\!x f\,dx - \mu\right)$$ **Step 2.** Variational derivative: $$-\ln f(x) - 1 + \lambda_{0} + \lambda_{1}x \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f(x) \;=\; e^{\lambda_{0}-1}\,e^{\lambda_{1}x}$$ **Step 3.** For $f(x)$ to integrate to a finite value on $[0, \infty)$, we need $\lambda_1 < 0$.
