## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> *Differentiate between terminating and steady-state simulations. Give one real-world example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Terminating Simulation | Steady-State Simulation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Run length | Finite, fixed by a natural event | Theoretically infinite; long-run behaviour of interest |
| Initial conditions | Well-defined and important (e.g., empty queue) | Arbitrary; system is already "running" |
| Startup data | Kept (it is part of the run) | Discarded (transient bias) |
| Example | Bank open 9 AM – 4 PM | 24/7 cellular network switch |

**(1 mark for each correct row + 1 mark for valid examples.)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *What is initialization bias? State two methods used to reduce it in steady-state simulations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Initialization bias is the distortion introduced into simulation estimates by the choice of artificial initial conditions (e.g., starting a queue with zero customers when the real system is rarely empty). For steady-state analysis, the early part of the run is *not* representative of long-run behaviour, so it must be removed.

**Two methods (any two of the following — 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Warm-up / initialization-bias deletion** — discard the first $L$ observations.
2. **Pilot run to estimate $L$** — use Welch's moving-average plot or MSER (mean-squared-error rule).
3. **Regenerative method** — start cycles from a known state, naturally removing bias.
4. **Replication with different initial conditions** — averaged across starting states.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 3]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **independent replication method** for output analysis of a terminating simulation. State the assumptions, the formula for the confidence interval, and the conditions under which it is valid.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A single-server queue is simulated 12 times, each run lasting 1000 customer-service completions. The mean waiting times (in minutes) of the 12 replications are: 4.1, 4.5, 3.9, 4.3, 4.7, 4.0, 4.4, 4.2, 4.6, 4.1, 4.5, 4.3. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean waiting time and interpret the result.

#### Model Solution for A(a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** The independent replication method runs the simulation $R$ times, each time with a *different random-number stream* and identical initial conditions, producing $R$ i.i.d. replication means $\bar{Y}_1, \bar{Y}_2, \dots, \bar{Y}_R$.

2. **Procedure [2 marks]:**
   - Decide run length $n$ from the natural terminating horizon.
   - Execute $R$ replications independently.
   - Compute $\bar{Y}_i$ and $S_i^2$ per replication.
   - Compute pooled variance $S_p^2 = \frac{1}{R}\sum S_i^2$ and grand mean $\bar{\bar{Y}}$.
   - Test independence and normality of the $\bar{Y}_i$.
   - Apply the $t$-based CI.

3. **Assumptions [2 marks]:**
   - The replication means are i.i.d. (different RNG streams guarantee independence).
   - The replication means are approximately normal (justified by CLT for moderate $R$, or by formal normality test).
   - Run length $n$ is identical for every replication.

4. **CI formula [1 mark]:**

$$\bar{\bar{Y}} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,R-1}\cdot \frac{S_p}{\sqrt{R}}$$

5. **Validity conditions [1 mark]:** $R \ge 5$ minimum, $R \ge 30$ preferred for $z$-approximation; CLT satisfied; no time-trend in output.

#### Model Solution for A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Sum of the 12 means.

$$\sum \bar{Y}_i = 4.1+4.5+3.9+4.3+4.7+4.0+4.4+4.2+4.6+4.1+4.5+4.3 = 51.6$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Grand mean.

$$\bar{\bar{Y}} = \frac{51.6}{12} = 4.30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Sum of squared deviations about the mean.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(4.1-4.3)^2 = 0.04 \\
&(4.5-4.3)^2 = 0.04 \\
&(3.9-4.3)^2 = 0.16 \\
&(4.3-4.3)^2 = 0.00 \\
&(4.7-4.3)^2 = 0.16 \\
&(4.0-4.3)^2 = 0.09 \\
&(4.4-4.3)^2 = 0.01 \\
&(4.2-4.3)^2 = 0.01 \\
&(4.6-4.3)^2 = 0.09 \\
&(4.1-4.3)^2 = 0.04 \\
&(4.5-4.3)^2 = 0.04 \\
&(4.3-4.3)^2 = 0.00
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum = $0.04+0.04+0.16+0.00+0.16+0.09+0.01+0.01+0.09+0.04+0.04+0.00 = 0.68$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Sample variance.

$$S_p^2 = \frac{0.68}{12-1} = \frac{0.68}{11} = 0.0618 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; S_p = 0.2486$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Critical $t$ value.

For 95% confidence, $t_{0.025,\,11} = 2.201$.

**Step 6 [1 mark]:** Half-width and CI.

$$h = 2.201 \cdot \frac{0.2486}{\sqrt{12}} = 2.201 \cdot 0.07178 = 0.158$$

$$\boxed{\;4.142 \;\le\; \theta \;\le\; 4.458 \text{ minutes with 95 % confidence}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** With 95% confidence, the true mean customer waiting time in this single-server queue lies between **4.14 and 4.46 minutes**. The CI is narrow (~0.16 min half-width), indicating that 12 replications gave reasonably precise results.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / KTU Model Paper 4 — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **regenerative method** of output analysis for steady-state simulations. Derive the regenerative estimator of the long-run mean and explain the construction of its confidence interval.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Common Random Numbers (CRN)** technique for comparing two alternative system designs. Show mathematically why CRN reduces the variance of the estimated difference.

#### Model Solution for B(a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** A *regenerative point* is a system state at which the stochastic evolution of the simulation probabilistically "restarts" — the future evolution depends only on the current state and not on the past. The intervals between successive regenerative points are called *regenerative cycles* and are i.i.d.

2. **Why it is useful [1 mark]:** Cycle means are *exactly* i.i.d. (not just approximately), so the CLT applies cleanly and no warm-up period is required.

3. **Regenerative estimator [3 marks]:** For $C$ cycles, the total reward (sum of observations weighted by interval length) and total time are:

$$Y_c = \sum_{t \in \text{cycle } c} X_t, \qquad T_c = \text{length of cycle } c$$

The regenerative estimator of the long-run mean performance is:

$$\hat{\theta}_C = \frac{\sum_{c=1}^{C} Y_c}{\sum_{c=1}^{C} T_c}$$

4. **Confidence interval [2 marks]:** By the regenerative CLT,

$$\sqrt{C}\cdot(\hat{\theta}_C - \theta) \;\xrightarrow{d}\; N\!\left(0,\,\sigma_{reg}^2\right)$$

where $\sigma_{reg}^2$ is computed from the per-cycle ratios. The $(1-\alpha)$ CI is:

$$\hat{\theta}_C \pm z_{\alpha/2}\cdot \frac{\hat{\sigma}_{reg}}{\sqrt{C}}$$

The **advantage** is the elimination of initialization bias and the removal of autocorrelation.

#### Model Solution for B(b) — 7 marks

1. **Problem context [1 mark]:** When comparing two designs A and B, the simple difference of independent-sample means has variance $\text{Var}(\bar{Y}_A) + \text{Var}(\bar{Y}_B)$. If the two designs are run with *different* RNG streams, a portion of the variability is the same in both — and so cancels out, but only if we synchronize the streams.

2. **Common Random Numbers idea [2 marks]:** Use the *same* random-number stream at corresponding decision points in both designs. This induces a **positive correlation** $\rho > 0$ between the two output means.

3. **Variance reduction derivation [3 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\bar{Y}_A - \bar{Y}_B) &= \text{Var}(\bar{Y}_A) + \text{Var}(\bar{Y}_B) - 2\,\text{Cov}(\bar{Y}_A, \bar{Y}_B) \\
&= \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2\rho\sigma_A\sigma_B \\
&= (\sigma_A - \sigma_B)^2 + 2\sigma_A\sigma_B(1-\rho)
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\rho > 0$ (achieved by CRN), the variance of the difference is strictly less than the sum of the individual variances. The reduction factor is the **positive covariance** term $-2\,\text{Cov}(\bar{Y}_A,\bar{Y}_B)$.

4. **Practical guidance [1 mark]:** CRN works best when the systems have similar structure and when random variates drive similar events (e.g., inter-arrival times, service times). It is most effective for **paired comparisons** rather than screening.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Output Data Analysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S_p$ (pooled) with $S_i$ (single replication).** Using the wrong estimator leads to a wrong CI width. Always pool across replications.
> 2. **Applying the $z$-table when $R < 30$.** The $t$-distribution must be used for small sample sizes; failure to do so produces a falsely narrow CI and the examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract warm-up data** in steady-state problems. The first $L$ observations are *contaminated* and will bias the mean downward (or upward). Always state "after discarding the warm-up period $L$."
> 4. **Treating batch means as fully independent.** They are *approximately* independent, not exactly. Examiners want a sentence acknowledging this approximation.
> 5. **Stating the regeneration estimator without the cycle-length denominator.** A common mark-loser is writing $\hat{\theta} = \bar{Y}_C$ instead of $\hat{\theta} = \sum Y_c / \sum T_c$.
> 6. **Skipping the verification of independence and normality.** KTU 2024 rubrics explicitly award 1 mark for "validation of CLT assumptions" — students who omit it lose easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print before walking into the exam hall):**
>
> - [ ] **Two simulation classes:** Terminating (finite horizon, well-defined start) vs. Steady-state (long-run, initialization-bias removal required).
> - [ ] **Why replications?** They make the replication means **i.i.d.**, which is the only way to apply the Central Limit Theorem cleanly.
> - [ ] **Pooled variance formula:** $S_p^2 = \frac{1}{R}\sum_{i=1}^{R} S_i^2$ — used with $t_{R-1}$ when $R < 30$, with $z$ when $R \ge 30$.
> - [ ] **CI half-width formula:** $h = t_{\alpha/2,\,R-1}\cdot S_p/\sqrt{R}$.
> - [ ] **Sample-size formula:** $R^* = \lceil (z_{\alpha/2}\cdot S_p/h)^2 \rceil$ — use the two-phase pilot procedure.
> - [ ] **Initialization bias:** Always present at the start of steady-state runs; remove via warm-up deletion, regenerative cycles, or batch means.
> - [ ] **Batch means:** Partition one long run into $k$ disjoint batches of size $m$; treat batch means as i.i.d. and apply the standard CI formula.
> - [ ] **Regenerative method:** Cycle-based; estimator is $\hat{\theta} = \sum Y_c / \sum T_c$; CI uses the regenerative CLT.
> - [ ] **Common Random Numbers:** Use the *same* RNG stream for both designs to induce positive covariance and reduce the variance of the difference: $\text{Var}(\bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2) = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\,\text{Cov}(\bar{Y}_1, \bar{Y}_2)$.
> - [ ] **Antithetic variates:** Use $U$ and $1-U$ as a pair; estimator $\bar{Y}_{AV} = (Y(U) + Y(1-U))/2$.
> - [ ] **Standard $z$-values to memorize:** 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.96, 99% → 2.576.
> - [ ] **Always state assumptions** before applying a CI: i.i.d., normality (or large $R$), fixed run length, no time-trend.
> - [ ] **Common $t$-values for CI problems:** $t_{0.025,\,9}=2.262$, $t_{0.025,\,19}=2.093$, $t_{0.025,\,29}=2.045$.
> - [ ] **Final answer format:** Always quote the interval, the confidence level, and a one-sentence interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variance Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variance Reduction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance Reduction Techniques (VRT)** are statistical procedures employed in stochastic (Monte Carlo) simulation to decrease the **variance** of an estimator of a system performance measure, *without* increasing the number of simulation replications. They achieve this by exploiting structural knowledge, correlation, or alternative sampling strategies.

In formal notation, if $\bar{Y}(n) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ is the sample-mean estimator of a performance measure, the goal of VRT is to construct an *improved* estimator $\bar{Y}_{VRT}(n)$ such that:

$$Var(\bar{Y}_{VRT}) \;<\; Var(\bar{Y})$$

for the *same* computational effort $n$. The efficiency gain is quantified by the **variance reduction factor**:

$$\rho = \frac{Var(\bar{Y})}{Var(\bar{Y}_{VRT})}$$

A value $\rho > 1$ indicates a successful VRT. The estimator is said to be **unbiased** because:

$$E[\bar{Y}_{VRT}] = \mu \quad \text{(true system mean)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** VRTs do **NOT** remove systematic bias in the underlying model — they only compress the *statistical noise* around the true expected value. They are therefore classified as **efficiency-improvement tools**, not correctness tools.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are trying to estimate the **average weight of a cricket ball** produced by a factory, but the weights are random (tiny manufacturing variations).

| Strategy (Real-World Analogy) | Equivalent VRT |
| :--- | :--- |
| Weigh 100 balls picked blindly from the conveyor belt. | **Plain (Crude) Monte Carlo** — high variance. |
| Weigh 50 balls, then weigh *the same 50 slots again* on a different machine. | **Common Random Numbers (CRN)** — induced correlation cancels machine-noise. |
| Weigh 50 balls; then weigh 50 balls whose complements are *forced* to be on the opposite end of the tolerance band. | **Antithetic Variates** — negative correlation smooths the average. |
| You happen to know the *exact* average density of rubber. Use weight-vs-density as a co-pivot. | **Control Variates** — leverages a known expectation. |
| Sort balls into light / medium / heavy piles first, then sample equally from each pile. | **Stratified Sampling** — covers the full range, no cluster bias. |
| Sample more balls from the *very-light* pile because that pile is rare but critical for safety. | **Importance Sampling** — focuses effort where it matters. |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Trick:** Whenever the question says *"Estimate the mean / probability / expected cost"*, the *default* answer starts with a **Crude Monte Carlo** estimator, and VRTs are presented as the *upgrade*. Always show the variance before and after.

---

## 1.3 Key Statistical Metrics Used Throughout

These five quantities appear in **every** VRT formula and must be memorized:

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mu = E[Y]$ | True (unknown) expected performance | Often the target of estimation |
| $\sigma^2 = Var(Y)$ | True (unknown) variance of one replication | Estimated by $S^2$ |
| $S^2$ | Sample variance | $\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ |
| $h$ | Half-width of the $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval | Critical for KTU numericals |
| $Cov(X,Y)$ | Covariance between two estimators | The "fuel" of CRN & Control Variates |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Marks are lost when students forget the **degrees of freedom = $n-1$** in the $t$-distribution used for confidence intervals. The $z$-distribution is only acceptable for very large $n$ ($n \geq 30$) as a board approximation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need VRT? — The Cost of Variance

For a fixed replication budget $n$, the **half-width** of the $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is:

$$h \;=\; t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Rearranging, the number of replications required to achieve a target half-width $h_0$ is:

$$n \;=\; \left(\frac{t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot S}{h_0}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Because $n$ grows as the **square** of the sample standard deviation $S$, a VRT that cuts $S$ in half cuts the *required* replication count by a factor of **4**. This is a massive saving for long, expensive simulations (e.g., VLSI, weather, queueing systems).

---

## 2.2 The Seven Standard VRTs (KTU 2024 Syllabus Coverage)

Module 2 of the OEIET724 syllabus prescribes the following seven techniques. Each one attacks variance from a different angle.

### 2.2.1 Common Random Numbers (CRN)

* **Idea:** When comparing *two* system designs $A$ and $B$, drive both with the *same* stream of uniform random numbers $U_i$.
* **Mechanism:** Induces a *positive* covariance $Cov(Y_A, Y_B) > 0$. The variance of the *paired-difference* estimator $\bar{D} = \bar{Y}_A - \bar{Y}_B$ becomes small.
* **When useful:** Comparing alternative designs, sensitivity analysis.

### 2.2.2 Antithetic Variates (AV)

* **Idea:** For every uniform $U_i$, also use $1 - U_i$. Average the two responses.
* **Mechanism:** Induces *negative* covariance, reducing the variance of the average.
* **When useful:** Estimating a single mean, monotonic systems.

### 2.2.3 Control Variates (CV)

* **Idea:** Use a *correlated* variable $X$ whose true mean $\mu_X$ is known analytically, and subtract its deviation from the primary estimate.
* **Mechanism:** The optimal multiplier $c^{*}$ exploits the covariance $Cov(Y, X)$.
* **When useful:** Any system where an analytically tractable "twin" exists (e.g., M/M/1 mean queue length has a known formula).

### 2.2.4 Importance Sampling (IS)

* **Idea:** Sample from a *biased* distribution $g(\cdot)$ that over-samples *important* (rare) events.
* **Mechanism:** Likelihood-ratio weighting compensates the bias.
* **When useful:** Estimating rare-event probabilities (system failures, large insurance claims).

### 2.2.5 Stratified Sampling (SS)

* **Idea:** Partition the input space into $L$ disjoint strata; sample $n_k$ from stratum $k$.
* **Mechanism:** Eliminates *between-stratum* variance.
* **When useful:** When the input random variable has known categorical or bounded regions.

### 2.2.6 Conditional Expectation (CE)

* **Idea:** Condition the response on a related variable and use the analytical conditional mean.
* **Mechanism:** Removes the variance due to the conditioning variable.
* **When useful:** Sum-decomposition problems, e.g., $Y = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$.

### 2.2.7 Indirect Estimation

* **Idea:** Instead of estimating a complex quantity directly, estimate a related quantity that is mathematically equivalent but has lower simulation variance.
* **When useful:** Transforming ratios into products, using unbiased ratio estimators.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important revision artifact** for Module 2. Memorize it.

| # | Technique | Improved Estimator | Variance (Single Rep) | Optimal Parameter | Reduction Achieved When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **CRN** | $\bar{D} = \bar{Y}_1 - \bar{Y}_2$ (paired) | $\sigma_D^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\,Cov(Y_1,Y_2)$ | Use *same* $U_i$ stream | $Cov(Y_1,Y_2) > 0$ |
| 2 | **Antithetic** | $\bar{Y}_{AV} = \dfrac{1}{2}(\bar{Y} + \bar{Y}')$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sigma_Y^2 + Cov(Y, Y')\right)$ | Use $U_i$ and $1-U_i$ | $Cov(Y, Y') < 0$ |
| 3 | **Control Variate** | $\bar{Y}_{CV} = \bar{Y} - c^{*}(\bar{X} - \mu_X)$ | $\sigma_Y^2 - \dfrac{Cov^2(Y,X)}{\sigma_X^2}$ | $c^{*} = -\dfrac{Cov(Y,X)}{\sigma_X^2}$ | $\vert Cov(Y,X) \vert$ large |
| 4 | **Importance Sampling** | $\hat{\mu}_{IS} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{f(Z_i)\,h(Z_i)}{g(Z_i)}$ | Depends on $g$ | $g^{*}(x) = \dfrac{\vert h(x)\vert f(x)}{\int \vert h \vert f \,dx}$ | Rare event under $f$ |
| 5 | **Stratified** | $\bar{Y}_{str} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} W_k \bar{Y}_k$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{L} W_k^2 \dfrac{\sigma_k^2}{n_k}$ | $n_k \propto W_k \sigma_k$ (Neyman alloc.) | $\sigma_k \ll \sigma$ within strata |
| 6 | **Conditional Exp.** | $\hat{\mu}_{CE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum E[Y \mid X_i]$ | $\sigma_Y^2 - Var(E[Y \mid X])$ | — | $Var(E[Y \mid X]) > 0$ |
| 7 | **Indirect (Ratio)** | $\hat{R} = \dfrac{\bar{Y}}{\bar{X}}$ (Jackknife bias-corrected) | $Var(\bar{Y}) + R^2 Var(\bar{X}) - 2R\,Cov(\bar{Y},\bar{X})$ | — | High $Cov(Y,X)$, small $R$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safety:** All vertical bars in the table above are rendered as **bold text** or **`vert`** in the LaTeX source to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your answer script, always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of VRT |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Telecommunications (Queues)** | Estimating blocking probability in M/M/c/c systems using **importance sampling** — direct simulation would need millions of calls. |
| **VLSI / Chip Design** | Comparing two gate-sizes using **CRN** — reduces wafer-test cost. |
| **Finance / Risk Analytics** | Estimating Value-at-Risk (VaR) using **importance sampling** and **antithetic variates**. |
| **Supply Chain** | Estimating fill-rate of a warehouse using **stratified sampling** over demand regions. |
| **Aerospace Reliability** | Estimating failure probability of a redundant system using **conditional expectation** on stress cycles. |
| **Manufacturing Yield** | Comparing two production-line layouts using **control variates** anchored on the *known* line-balance throughput. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the *full* derivations that the KTU board expects for a 14-mark question. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance of the Sample-Mean Estimator (Foundation)

Let $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$ be i.i.d. samples from a simulation with true mean $\mu$ and true variance $\sigma^2$. The sample mean is:

$$\bar{Y}(n) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Step 1 — Take the expectation.**

$$E[\bar{Y}(n)] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\right] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[Y_i] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu = \mu$$

So the estimator is **unbiased** — no systematic error.

**Step 2 — Take the variance.**

$$Var(\bar{Y}(n)) = Var\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(Y_i) = \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Standard deviation (the half-width divisor).**

$$\sigma_{\bar{Y}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Variance shrinks *linearly* with $n$, but the standard deviation shrinks only as $1/\sqrt{n}$. This slow convergence is precisely what VRT accelerates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Antithetic Variates (AV) in Full

Let $U_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ and $Y_i = h(U_i)$ for some response function. The **antithetic** response uses the complement:

$$Y_i' = h(1 - U_i)$$

**Step 1 — Construct the AV estimator (using $n/2$ pairs to keep total work = $n$).**

$$\bar{Y}_{AV} = \frac{1}{n/2}\sum_{j=1}^{n/2} \frac{Y_j + Y_j'}{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n/2}\bigl(Y_j + Y_j'\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Verify unbiasedness.**

$$E[Y_j'] = E[h(1 - U_j)] = E[h(U_j)] = E[Y_j] = \mu$$

Therefore:

$$E\!\left[\frac{Y_j + Y_j'}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{2}(\mu + \mu) = \mu \quad \Rightarrow \quad E[\bar{Y}_{AV}] = \mu \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance of one pair average.**

$$Var\!\left(\frac{Y + Y'}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(Var(Y) + Var(Y') + 2\,Cov(Y, Y')\bigr)$$

Since $Var(Y') = Var(Y) = \sigma^2$:

$$Var\!\left(\frac{Y + Y'}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(\sigma^2 + Cov(Y, Y')\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Variance of the AV mean (over $n/2$ independent pairs).**

$$Var(\bar{Y}_{AV}) = \frac{1}{n/2}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\bigl(\sigma^2 + Cov(Y, Y')\bigr) = \frac{\sigma^2 + Cov(Y, Y')}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Variance reduction factor.**

$$\rho_{AV} = \frac{Var(\bar{Y})}{Var(\bar{Y}_{AV})} = \frac{\sigma^2/n}{(\sigma^2 + Cov(Y, Y'))/n} = \frac{2\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + Cov(Y, Y')}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Result:** Variance is reduced if and only if $Cov(Y, Y') < 0$. For a **monotonic** response function $h$, $Y$ and $Y'$ are *negatively* correlated, so AV is guaranteed to help. For non-monotonic $h$, AV may *increase* variance — a common KTU follow-up trap question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Control Variates (CV) in Full

Let $Y$ be the response of interest, $X$ be the **control** variate with known mean $\mu_X = E[X]$, and let $\rho$ be their correlation. Construct:

$$\bar{Y}_{CV} = \bar{Y} - c(\bar{X} - \mu_X)$$

**Step 1 — Unbiasedness.**

$$E[\bar{Y}_{CV}] = E[\bar{Y}] - c(E[\bar{X}] - \mu_X) = \mu - c(\mu_X - \mu_X) = \mu \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Variance of the CV estimator (using $n$ independent pairs).**

$$Var(\bar{Y}_{CV}) = \frac{1}{n}Var\bigl(Y - c(X - \mu_X)\bigr) = \frac{1}{n}\bigl(Var(Y) + c^2 Var(X) - 2c\,Cov(Y, X)\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Find the optimal $c$ that minimizes the variance.**

Differentiate the inner bracket with respect to $c$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dc}\bigl(Var(Y) + c^2 Var(X) - 2c\,Cov(Y, X)\bigr) = 2c\,Var(X) - 2\,Cov(Y, X) = 0$$

Solving:

$$c^{*} = \frac{Cov(Y, X)}{Var(X)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $c^{*}$ back to obtain the minimum variance.**

$$Var(\bar{Y}_{CV}^{*}) = \frac{1}{n}\bigl(Var(Y) - c^{*2} Var(X)\bigr) = \frac{1}{n}\!\left(Var(Y) - \frac{Cov^2(Y, X)}{Var(X)}\right)$$

Factoring:

$$Var(\bar{Y}_{CV}^{*}) = \frac{\sigma_Y^2}{n}\left(1 - \rho^2\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \rho = \frac{Cov(Y, X)}{\sigma_Y \sigma_X}$$

**Step 5 — Reduction factor.**

$$\rho_{CV} = \frac{Var(\bar{Y})}{Var(\bar{Y}_{CV}^{*})} = \frac{1}{1 - \rho^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Favorite:** A correlation of $\rho = 0.95$ between $Y$ and $X$ gives $\rho_{CV} = 1/(1 - 0.9025) \approx 10.26$, i.e., CV is over **10× more efficient** than crude MC. The same simulation time now yields a 10× tighter confidence interval.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stratified Sampling (Neyman Allocation)

Partition the support of $U$ into $L$ strata, each with weight $W_k$ and within-stratum variance $\sigma_k^2$. Draw $n_k$ samples from stratum $k$.

**Step 1 — Unbiased stratified estimator.**

$$\bar{Y}_{str} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} W_k \bar{Y}_k \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{Y}_k = \frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_k} Y_{kj}$$

**Step 2 — Variance.**

$$Var(\bar{Y}_{str}) = \sum_{k=1}^{L} W_k^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_k^2}{n_k}$$

**Step 3 — Minimize the variance subject to the constraint $\sum n_k = n$.** Use Lagrange multipliers.

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \frac{W_k^2 \sigma_k^2}{n_k} + \lambda\!\left(\sum_{k=1}^{L} n_k - n\right)$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial n_k = 0$:

$$-\frac{W_k^2 \sigma_k^2}{n_k^2} + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_k = \frac{W_k \sigma_k}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

Substituting back into the budget constraint:

$$n = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \frac{W_k \sigma_k}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}\sum_{k=1}^{L} W_k \sigma_k$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,n_k^{*} = n \cdot \frac{W_k \sigma_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{L} W_j \sigma_j}\,} \quad \text{(Neyman Allocation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Allocate *more* samples to strata that are *wider* (high $W_k \sigma_k$). This is the opposite of importance sampling for variance minimization.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Common Random Numbers (CRN) — Paired Estimator

For two system configurations $A$ and $B$, let $Y_{1i}$ and $Y_{2i}$ be paired responses driven by the *same* $U_i$.

**Step 1 — The paired-difference estimator.**

$$\bar{D} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \quad \text{where} \quad D_i = Y_{1i} - Y_{2i}$$

**Step 2 — Variance of the difference per replication.**

$$Var(D_i) = Var(Y_{1i}) + Var(Y_{2i}) - 2\,Cov(Y_{1i}, Y_{2i}) = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\,Cov(Y_1, Y_2)$$

**Step 3 — Variance of the mean difference.**

$$Var(\bar{D}) = \frac{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\,Cov(Y_1, Y_2)}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Reduction factor (relative to independent sampling, where $Cov = 0$).**

$$\rho_{CRN} = \frac{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\,Cov(Y_1, Y_2)} > 1 \quad \text{when } Cov(Y_1, Y_2) > 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (often asked as a 7-mark sub-part):** If the systems have *synchronization breaks* — e.g., $A$ uses a different number of uniform variates than $B$ per replication — CRN is **invalid**. One must use the **same random-number stream at the *same* call points** in both systems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Board-Exam Ready)

| Technique | Computes | Required Knowledge | Failure Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CRN | Difference of two means | Common $U_i$ stream | Different RNG call counts |
| Antithetic | Single mean | Monotonicity of $h(\cdot)$ | Non-monotonic response |
| Control Variate | Single mean | Known $E[X]$, $\sigma_X$ | Weak correlation $\vert\rho\vert \approx 0$ |
| Importance Sampling | Rare-event probability | Good proposal $g(\cdot)$ | Bad choice of $g$ (unbounded variance) |
| Stratified | Single mean | Disjoint strata partition | Forgetting to weight by $W_k$ |
| Conditional Exp. | Sum of random count | Known $E[Y \mid X]$ | Conditioning set too large |
| Indirect | Ratio of two means | Mathematical identity | Mis-specifying the ratio form |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, the following Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node identifiers** prefixed with letters and **plain uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting inside node text) to guarantee clean compilation.

## 4.1 Master Decision Flow: Which VRT to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Simulation Study] --> A1{What is the estimation goal?}
    A1 -- Compare two designs --> A2[Use Common Random Numbers CRN]
    A1 -- Estimate single mean --> A3{Is the response monotonic in U?}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Use Antithetic Variates AV]
    A3 -- No --> A5{Is a correlated variable X with known mean available?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Use Control Variates CV]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Use Stratified Sampling SS]
    A1 -- Estimate rare event probability --> A8[Use Importance Sampling IS]
    A8 --> A9[Choose proposal g via cross entropy]
    A2 --> A10[Run paired replications]
    A4 --> A11[Generate U and 1 minus U]
    A6 --> A12[Compute optimal c from pilot run]
    A7 --> A13[Partition support into L strata]
    A10 --> A14[Compute confidence interval]
    A11 --> A14
    A12 --> A14
    A13 --> A14
    A9 --> A14
    A14 --> A15[Report reduced variance estimator]
```

## 4.2 Antithetic Variates: Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Uniform RNG U sub i] --> B1[Response h U sub i]
    B0 --> B2[Complement 1 minus U sub i]
    B2 --> B3[Response h of 1 minus U sub i]
    B1 --> B4[Adder Sum]
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5[Divider by 2]
    B5 --> B6[Pair Average Y bar sub i]
    B6 --> B7[Accumulator over n by 2 pairs]
    B7 --> B8[Final AV Estimator Y bar sub AV]
    B8 --> B9[Variance Cov Y Y prime term captured]
```

## 4.3 Control Variate Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Run pilot n sub 0 replications] --> C1[Compute sample Cov of Y and X]
    C1 --> C2[Compute sample Var of X]
    C2 --> C3[c sub star = Cov / Var sub X]
    C3 --> C4[Run main n replications]
    C4 --> C5[Collect Y bar and X bar]
    C5 --> C6[Y bar sub CV = Y bar minus c star X bar minus mu sub X]
    C6 --> C7{Is variance reduced by factor greater than 1?}
    C7 -- Yes --> C8[Adopt CV estimator]
    C7 -- No --> C9[Fallback to Crude Monte Carlo]
```

## 4.4 Stratified Sampling: Allocation & Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Identify L strata of U] --> D1[Estimate W sub k and sigma sub k from pilot]
    D1 --> D2[Apply Neyman allocation n sub k proportional to W sub k sigma sub k]
    D2 --> D3[Draw n sub k samples from each stratum k]
    D3 --> D4[Compute stratum mean Y bar sub k]
    D4 --> D5[Weighted sum: Y bar sub str = sum W sub k Y bar sub k]
    D5 --> D6[Variance: sum W sub k squared sigma sub k squared / n sub k]
    D6 --> D7[Compare with crude MC variance sigma squared / n]
```

## 4.5 Importance Sampling for Rare Events

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[Target: Estimate P of rare event E] --> E1[Choose biased proposal density g]
    E1 --> E2[Draw Z sub i from g]
    E2 --> E3[Evaluate likelihood ratio L sub i = f of Z sub i divided by g of Z sub i]
    E3 --> E4[Indicator I of event E on Z sub i]
    E4 --> E5[Weighted sample: L sub i times I sub i]
    E5 --> E6[IS estimator: average over n]
    E6 --> E7[Check effective sample size]
    E7 -- Adequate --> E8[Report IS estimate]
    E7 -- Poor --> E1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
