```mermaid
    class PR1 flow;
    class DM1 flow;
    class AX1 aux;
```

> [!NOTE]
> This depicts a **Balancing (B) Loop**: when inventory falls, the reorder decision increases production, which raises inventory — counteracting the original disturbance.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) Notation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Birth Rate] -->|plus| nodeB[Population]
    nodeB -->|plus| nodeA
    nodeB -->|plus| nodeC[Death Rate]
    nodeC -->|minus| nodeB
    nodeD[Resource Availability] -->|minus| nodeB
    nodeB -->|minus| nodeD
    classDef causal fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD causal;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLD Polarity Logic:**
> - **Birth Rate → (+) → Population** → **Population → (+) → Birth Rate** ⇒ **Reinforcing Loop (R)**.
> - **Population → (+) → Death Rate** AND **Death Rate → (−) → Population** ⇒ **Balancing contribution**.
> - **Population → (−) → Resources** AND **Resources → (−) → Population** ⇒ **Balancing (B)** loop providing *carrying-capacity* feedback.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by confusing the signs of *inflow* and *outflow* in the stock equation. Always write $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$, **never** the reverse.
> - In **Euler integration** problems, students forget to update the rate using the **new** time value for time-varying functions (e.g., $R(t) = 40 + 2t$).
> - In **CLD** problems, the most common mistake is **loop polarity miscount** — count the **negative links carefully** and remember the parity rule.
> - Always include **units** in numerical simulation answers; KTU examiners often deduct marks for missing units.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate clearly between a Stock and a Flow in the context of System Dynamics. Give one engineering example of each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):
A **stock** (or *level*) is an **accumulation** within a system at a specific instant of time. It represents the *state* of the system and has memory — its current value depends on the entire history of inflows and outflows. Stocks can only be changed by flows. *Example:* the number of finished goods in a factory warehouse.

A **flow** (or *rate*) is an **activity** measured per unit of time that causes a stock to increase or decrease. *Example:* the production rate of 100 units/day feeding the warehouse.

| Feature | Stock | Flow |
|---------|-------|------|
| Measured at | An instant | Over an interval |
| Units | "items", "₹", "persons" | "items/day", "₹/month" |
| Can change instantly? | **No** | **Yes** (instantaneously) |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between a positive (reinforcing) feedback loop and a negative (balancing) feedback loop. State the loop polarity rule used in Causal Loop Diagrams.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):
- A **positive (reinforcing) feedback loop** amplifies change in the same direction, producing either exponential *growth* or exponential *collapse*. It contains an **even number (including zero) of negative causal links**. *Example:* compound interest, viral spread.

- A **negative (balancing) feedback loop** counteracts change and drives the system toward an *equilibrium* or *goal*. It contains an **odd number of negative causal links**. *Example:* thermostat-controlled room temperature.

**Loop Polarity Rule:**
> *If the number of negative links in a closed loop is **odd** → the loop is **Balancing (B)**. If **even** → the loop is **Reinforcing (R)**.*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**For a manufacturing unit, the production rate (inflow) is given by $P(t) = 50 + 5t$ units/day and the demand rate (outflow) is $D(t) = 80 - 2t$ units/day. The initial finished-goods stock is $I_0 = 100$ units. Using Euler integration with $\Delta t = 1$ day, simulate the inventory for 5 days. Tabulate and comment on the trend.**

#### ✅ Solution:

**Step 1 — Set Up the Stock Equation:**

$$
I_{n+1} \;=\; I_n + \Delta t \cdot \bigl[P(n) - D(n)\bigr]
$$

**Step 2 — Day-by-Day Calculation:**

| Day $n$ | $P(n) = 50+5n$ | $D(n) = 80-2n$ | Net = $P-D$ | $I_{n+1}$ (units) |
|---------|----------------|----------------|--------------|--------------------|
| 0       | 50             | 80             | $-30$        | $100 - 30 = 70$    |
| 1       | 55             | 78             | $-23$        | $70 - 23 = 47$     |
| 2       | 60             | 76             | $-16$        | $47 - 16 = 31$     |
| 3       | 65             | 74             | $-9$         | $31 - 9 = 22$      |
| 4       | 70             | 72             | $-2$         | $22 - 2 = 20$      |
| 5       | 75             | 70             | $+5$         | $20 + 5 = 25$      |

**Step 3 — Trend Analysis:**
- Days 0–4: Inventory *declines* because demand exceeds production.
- Day 4: Net flow crosses zero (crossover point).
- Day 5: Inventory *begins recovering* as production overtakes demand.

> This is a **classic U-shaped inventory pattern** characteristic of a **balancing loop** reaching its equilibrium.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a Causal Loop Diagram, model the population dynamics of a city where births are proportional to population and resources (food) limit the population through a balancing feedback. Identify all loops and their polarity.**

#### ✅ Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the Variables:**
- $P$ = Population (Stock)
- $B$ = Birth Rate (Auxiliary)
- $D$ = Death Rate (Auxiliary)
- $R$ = Resource Availability (Stock)
- $b$, $d$, $\rho$ = Constants

**Step 2 — Causal Relationships:**
1. $P \xrightarrow{+} B$ — More population, more births.
2. $B \xrightarrow{+} P$ — More births, more population.
3. $P \xrightarrow{+} D$ — More population, more deaths.
4. $D \xrightarrow{-} P$ — More deaths, less population.
5. $P \xrightarrow{-} R$ — More population, more resource consumption.
6. $R \xrightarrow{-} D$ — Fewer resources, more deaths.

**Step 3 — Loop Identification:**

| Loop | Path | # of Negative Links | Polarity | Type |
|------|------|---------------------|----------|------|
| Loop 1 | $P \to B \to P$ | 0 | R | Reinforcing |
| Loop 2 | $P \to D \to P$ | 1 | B | Balancing |
| Loop 3 | $P \to R \to D \to P$ | 2 | R | Reinforcing (carrying-capacity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Net Behaviour** depends on which loop dominates. Initially, the reinforcing loop (Loop 1) dominates, producing growth. As $P$ approaches the carrying capacity, the balancing loops take over, producing **logistic (S-shaped) growth**.

**Step 4 — Governing Equation:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} \;=\; (b - d) \cdot P \;=\; r \cdot P \!\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right)
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the major elements of System Dynamics modelling. With a neat diagram, distinguish between a Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) and a Stock-Flow Diagram.**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Major Elements:**

1. **Stocks (Levels):** Accumulations representing system state. *Example:* inventory level.
2. **Flows (Rates):** Activities that change stocks per unit time. *Example:* production rate.
3. **Auxiliary Variables:** Intermediate computations needed to define flows. *Example:* desired inventory = `target stock − current stock`.
4. **Constants:** Fixed numerical parameters. *Example:* production capacity.
5. **Feedback Loops:** Closed causal chains — either reinforcing (R) or balancing (B).
6. **Delays:** Time lags between cause and effect (material, information, response).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) | Stock-Flow Diagram |
|---------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Nature | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| Shows | Direction of influence | Actual magnitudes |
| Symbols | Words + arrows with (+/−) | Stocks (rectangles), Flows (valves), Connectors (arrows) |
| Use | Conceptual understanding | Mathematical simulation |
| Loops marked | R or B | Implicit through equations |

*(Refer to SECTION 4 diagrams for visual reference.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A bank account contains ₹10,000 and earns a continuous compound interest of **8% per annum**. Withdrawals occur at a constant rate of ₹600/year. Using the differential equation approach, derive the balance after $t$ years and find when the balance becomes zero.**

#### ✅ Solution:

**Step 1 — Formulate the Stock Equation:**
Let $C(t)$ = account balance at time $t$.

$$
\frac{dC(t)}{dt} \;=\; \underbrace{0.08 \cdot C(t)}_{\text{interest inflow}} \;-\; \underbrace{600}_{\text{withdrawal outflow}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} - 0.08 \, C \;=\; -600
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Linear ODE using Integrating Factor:** $\mu(t) = e^{-0.08 t}$.

Multiplying both sides:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(C \cdot e^{-0.08 t}\right) \;=\; -600 \, e^{-0.08 t}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate:**

$$
C \cdot e^{-0.08 t} \;=\; -600 \cdot \frac{e^{-0.08 t}}{-0.08} + K \;=\; 7500 \, e^{-0.08 t} + K
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C(t)$:**

$$
C(t) \;=\; 7500 + K \, e^{0.08 t}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Initial Condition** $C(0) = 10{,}000$:

$$
10{,}000 \;=\; 7500 + K \quad \Rightarrow \quad K \;=\; 2500
$$

**Step 7 — Final Equation:**

$$
C(t) \;=\; 7500 + 2500 \, e^{0.08 t}
$$

**Step 8 — Wait — this grows, not decays!** Let us re-check the sign convention. Actually, the differential equation is:

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} \;=\; 0.08 C - 600
$$

Solving gives:

$$
C(t) \;=\; 7500 + A \, e^{0.08 t}
$$

But initial value $10{,}000 > 7500$, so $A = 2500$, meaning balance *grows* because interest initially exceeds withdrawals.

**Corrected Insight:** At $C = \dfrac{600}{0.08} = 7500$, the system reaches equilibrium. If $C_0 > 7500$, balance grows; if $C_0 < 7500$, balance decays.

**Step 9 — Re-evaluate the Question** (Balance reaches zero from *below* the equilibrium):

If instead withdrawals dominate, say $C_0 < 7500$, set $C(t) = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; 7500 - 2500 \, e^{0.08 t} \quad \text{(assuming } K = -2500 \text{)}
$$

$$
2500 \, e^{0.08 t} \;=\; 7500 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{0.08 t} \;=\; 3
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\ln 3}{0.08} \;\approx\; \frac{1.0986}{0.08} \;\approx\; 13.73 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** If the balance starts below ₹7,500, it will deplete to zero in approximately **13.73 years**. Otherwise, it grows unboundedly — a **reinforcing loop** dominated by compounding interest.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential KTU Rapid-Revision Points:**

- ⭐ **System Dynamics** was founded by **Jay W. Forrester (MIT, 1956)** to model complex feedback systems.
- ⭐ The **fundamental stock equation** is $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$ — memorize this in both continuous and discrete forms.
- ⭐ **Stocks** have memory and can only be changed by **flows**; they cannot jump instantaneously.
- ⭐ **Flows** are rates measured per unit time; they CAN change instantaneously.
- ⭐ **Auxiliary variables** are intermediate computational quantities that help define flows.
- ⭐ **Constants** are unchanging parameters throughout the simulation.
- ⭐ A **reinforcing (R) loop** has an *even* number of negative links → exponential growth/decay.
- ⭐ A **balancing (B) loop** has an *odd* number of negative links → equilibrium-seeking behaviour.
- ⭐ **Exponential growth** $P(t) = P_0 e^{rt}$ — classic J-curve.
- ⭐ **Logistic growth** $P(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + A e^{-rt}}$ — S-shaped, bounded by carrying capacity $K$.
- ⭐ **Euler integration** formula: $L_{n+1} = L_n + \Delta t \cdot R_n$ — always update the rate at the **current** time $n$.
- ⭐ **Delays** cause oscillations and instability; classify them as *material*, *information*, or *response*.
- ⭐ Always **sign your arrows correctly** in CLDs and **count negative links carefully** for polarity.
- ⭐ Engineering applications: inventory, supply chains, project management, epidemiology, environmental systems, macroeconomic policy.
- ⭐ Carrying capacity $K = \dfrac{\text{birth rate constant}}{\text{death rate constant per capita}}$ in simple logistic models.
- ⭐ KTU favours numerical simulation tables in Part B — present your work in a **clean tabular format** with units.
- ⭐ Don't confuse the **equilibrium value** of a balancing loop with the **initial value** — they may differ.
- ⭐ In compound-interest type problems, always check whether the equilibrium threshold is *above* or *below* the initial value before deciding whether the balance grows or decays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Flows** in System Simulation refer to the discrete-event modeling of systems in which **physical entities** (raw materials, components, jobs, documents, vehicles, or people) traverse a network of **processing stations, queues, transportation links, and storage buffers**. The flow of entities is governed by **service times, routing probabilities, resource availability, and topological network structure**.

In the context of KTU Module 3, Physical Flows constitute the **tangible material movement dimension** of a simulated system — as opposed to *information flows* (signals, data, messages) or *control flows* (logic, decisions). They are studied using **Queueing Networks**, **Petri Nets**, and **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** paradigms.

**Standard KTU Metrics Associated with Physical Flows:**

- **Throughput (X)** — entities processed per unit time (jobs/hour, items/min).
- **Work-In-Process (WIP)** — instantaneous count of entities inside the system.
- **Cycle Time (CT)** — total time an entity spends in the system.
- **Queue Length (Lq)** — average number of entities waiting.
- **Resource Utilization (ρ)** — fraction of time a server is busy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a **post office counter system**. Customers (entities) walk in from the street, form a queue if counters are busy, get served at one of the counters, and leave. The customer is a *physical entity*, the waiting line is a *queue*, the counter is a *server*, and the entire arrangement is a *physical flow network*. If you add a second service window (parallel server), a stamp-purchase counter after document verification (tandem queue), or a lunch break for staff (server downtime), the system's behavior changes — and that's exactly what simulation of physical flows helps you analyze *before* building the real system.

A more engineering-oriented analogy is an **automobile assembly line**: a chassis moves through welding, painting, and assembly stations. Each station acts as a server, the conveyor belt acts as a transport delay, and the buffers between stations act as queues. The entire factory is a **physical flow system**.

## 1.3 Why Physical Flows Matter in Simulation

> [!TIP]
> In KTU's OBE framework, Physical Flows map directly to **CO3: Model real-world physical systems using discrete-event simulation tools and analyze performance metrics.** Studying this topic enables engineers to:
> - Predict **bottlenecks** before system construction.
> - Optimize **buffer sizes** between stations.
> - Balance **line capacities** in manufacturing.
> - Evaluate **throughput vs. WIP trade-offs** (Little's Law territory).
> - Test **"what-if" scenarios** without disturbing the real plant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Taxonomy of Physical Flow Systems

Physical flow systems are classified along four axes:

1. **Topology**
   - **Open Network:** Entities enter from an external source, traverse the network, and exit to an external sink. (e.g., a job shop processing customer orders.)
   - **Closed Network:** A fixed population of entities circulates indefinitely. (e.g., pallets moving on a conveyor loop with no external entry.)

2. **Flow Logic**
   - **Tandem / Series Flow:** Entity passes through stations sequentially (Station 1 → Station 2 → Station 3).
   - **Fork-Join Flow:** Entity splits into parallel sub-entities for concurrent processing and later rejoins.
   - **Recirculating Flow:** Output of a station may re-enter earlier stations.

3. **Server Configuration**
   - **Single-Server (M/M/1)**
   - **Multi-Server (M/M/c)**
   - **Parallel / Redundant Servers**

4. **Buffer Discipline**
   - **Infinite Buffer:** No blocking (theoretical ideal).
   - **Finite Buffer:** When full, upstream station is **blocked** — common in real manufacturing.

## 2.2 Building Blocks of a Physical Flow Model

| Block | Function | Real-World Example |
|-------|----------|--------------------|
| **Source** | Generates entities at stochastic or deterministic inter-arrival times | Raw material dock |
| **Queue** | Holds entities awaiting service | Parts waiting at CNC machine |
| **Server** | Processes entity for a service time | Welding robot, inspection booth |
| **Sink / Exit** | Removes entity from system | Shipping bay |
| **Transport Delay** | Models movement time between stations | Conveyor belt segment |
| **Decision / Route** | Selects next station per probability | Branching conveyor, traffic junction |
| **Resource Pool** | Shared resource (crane, operator) used by multiple servers | Overhead crane servicing 3 machines |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core equations** examined in KTU ESE for Physical Flows. Memorize every row — questions often combine two or more formulas.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Meaning |
|---|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | Long-run average entities in system = arrival rate × avg. time in system |
| 2 | Little's Law (Queue only) | $L_q = \lambda \cdot W_q$ | Average entities waiting |
| 3 | Utilization (single server) | $\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{S}$ | Server busy fraction (must be < 1 for stability) |
| 4 | Utilization (c servers) | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \bar{S}}{c}$ | Per-server busy fraction |
| 5 | **Throughput (open system)** | $X = \lambda$ | In long-run steady state, throughput = arrival rate |
| 6 | M/M/1 Avg. # in system | $L = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$ | Average total entities (waiting + being served) |
| 7 | M/M/1 Avg. time in system | $W = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{1 - \rho}$ | Average sojourn time |
| 8 | M/M/1 Queue length | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho}$ | Average number waiting |
| 9 | M/M/1 Waiting time | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \bar{S}}{1 - \rho}$ | Average waiting time in queue |
| 10 | **Closed Network Throughput** | $X = \dfrac{N}{W}$ | Inverse form of Little's Law |
| 11 | Effective service time (multi-server) | $\bar{S}_{eff} = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{c}$ | Only when servers identical & parallel |
| 12 | Blocking Probability (finite buffer K) | $P_{block} = \rho^{K+1} \cdot \dfrac{1-\rho}{1-\rho^{K+2}}$ | Prob. an arriving entity is rejected |
| 13 | Stability Condition | $\rho < 1$ | Required for any single-server queue to be ergodic |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Derive throughput of a closed network," always begin by stating $X = N / W$ (inverse Little's Law) — this single line shows the examiner you know the foundational identity.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Physical Flow Simulation

A discrete-event simulation of a physical flow executes the following logical sequence:

1. **Initialization:** Set clock $t = 0$, declare empty queues, load entity-generation schedule.
2. **Event Scheduling:** Generate the next event — either an *arrival event* (entity enters) or a *departure event* (server completes).
3. **Arrival Handling:**
   - If server idle → start service immediately, schedule departure.
   - If server busy → entity joins the queue (FIFO/LIFO/priority discipline).
4. **Departure Handling:**
   - Remove entity from server; route to next station via probability matrix.
   - If queue non-empty → promote next entity, schedule its departure.
5. **Statistical Update:** Increment area-under-curve counters for $L$, $L_q$, $\rho$ (using time-weighted averages).
6. **Termination:** Stop when simulated time reaches $T_{end}$, or $N_{entities}$ reached.
7. **Report Generation:** Compute time-averaged metrics, confidence intervals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Physical Flow Simulation |
|--------|------------------------------------------|
| **Manufacturing** | Production line balancing, buffer sizing, bottleneck identification |
| **Logistics & Warehousing** | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) routing, conveyor design |
| **Healthcare** | Patient flow through OPD, emergency room triage optimization |
| **Transportation** | Traffic flow on highways, airport runway scheduling |
| **Supply Chain** | Inventory movement across distribution echelons |
| **Computer Networks** | Packet flow through routers (mapped as queues) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Little's Law (The Cornerstone Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** Over a long period $T$, the time-average number of entities in a system $L$ equals the arrival rate $\lambda$ multiplied by the average time each entity spends in the system $W$.
> $$L = \lambda W$$

### Step 1 — Define the Integral Form of $L$

Let $N(t)$ denote the number of entities in the system at time $t$. The time-average number of entities over $[0, T]$ is:

$$L_T = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} N(t)\, dt$$

### Step 2 — Define Individual Sojourn Times

For entity $i$ that arrives at time $a_i$ and departs at time $d_i$, its sojourn time is:

$$W_i = d_i - a_i$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Entity-Time Area (Alternate Form)

The integral $\int_{0}^{T} N(t)\, dt$ also equals the **sum of sojourn times** of all entities that have departed within $[0, T]$ (with appropriate treatment of residual entities). Let $N_T$ be the number of completed sojourns in $[0, T]$:

$$\int_{0}^{T} N(t)\, dt = \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} W_i$$

### Step 4 — Average Sojourn Time

$$W_T = \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} W_i$$

### Step 5 — Throughput Definition

The long-run throughput is:

$$\lambda_T = \frac{N_T}{T}$$

### Step 6 — Combine Steps 1, 3, 4, and 5

$$L_T = \frac{1}{T} \cdot N_T \cdot W_T = \left(\frac{N_T}{T}\right) \cdot W_T = \lambda_T \cdot W_T$$

### Step 7 — Take the Steady-State Limit

As $T \to \infty$, $L_T \to L$, $W_T \to W$, $\lambda_T \to \lambda$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{L = \lambda \, W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** Little's Law holds for **any** stationary system — regardless of arrival distribution, service distribution, number of servers, or network topology. This universality makes it the most frequently tested equation in this module.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of M/M/1 Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions:** Poisson arrivals (rate $\lambda$), Exponential service (mean $\bar{S} = 1/\mu$), single server, FIFO, infinite buffer.

### Step 1 — Define Utilization

A single server is busy whenever there is at least one entity. In steady state, the fraction of time busy equals the **utilization**:

$$\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{S} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$$

For system stability, $\rho < 1$.

### Step 2 — State Probability (Balance Equations)

Let $P_n$ = probability of $n$ entities in the system. In steady state, flow balance between states $n$ and $n+1$ gives:

$$\lambda P_n = \mu P_{n+1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{n+1} = \rho \cdot P_n$$

Iterating from $P_0$:

$$P_n = \rho^n \cdot P_0$$

### Step 3 — Normalization Constraint

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = 1$$

Using geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = \dfrac{1}{1-\rho}$ for $\rho < 1$:

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

### Step 4 — Expected Number in System

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n$$

Using identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^2}$ with $x = \rho$:

$$L = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Little's Law to Obtain $W$

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \frac{\bar{S}}{1-\rho}$$

### Step 6 — Queue-Specific Metrics (Subtract the One in Service)

$$L_q = L - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$

$$W_q = W - \bar{S} = \frac{\bar{S}}{1-\rho} - \bar{S} = \frac{\rho \bar{S}}{1-\rho}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Squaring a queue"* — the waiting metric always carries an extra factor of $\rho$ compared to the in-service factor. So $W_q = \rho \cdot W \cdot (\lambda/\lambda)$ simplifies mentally as: "wait is service time stretched by utilization."

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Two-Station Tandem Line

**Problem (KTU-Style):** Parts arrive at Station 1 with exponential inter-arrival time mean $1/\lambda = 2$ min. Station 1 service time is exponential with mean $\bar{S}_1 = 1$ min. Station 2 service time is exponential with mean $\bar{S}_2 = 1.5$ min. Infinite buffers. Compute steady-state $W$, $L$, $W_q$, $L_q$ for each station and for the overall line.

### Solution Walk-Through

**Step 1 — Per-Station Parameters**

For Station 1:

$$\lambda_1 = 0.5 \text{ /min}, \quad \bar{S}_1 = 1 \text{ min}, \quad \rho_1 = 0.5$$

For Station 2 (in an open tandem, departure rate from Station 1 = arrival rate to Station 2 in steady state):

$$\lambda_2 = 0.5 \text{ /min}, \quad \bar{S}_2 = 1.5 \text{ min}, \quad \rho_2 = 0.75$$

**Step 2 — Station 1 Metrics (M/M/1)**

$$W_1 = \frac{1}{1-0.5} = 2 \text{ min}$$

$$L_1 = \frac{0.5}{1-0.5} = 1.0$$

$$W_{q1} = \frac{0.5 \cdot 1}{1-0.5} = 1 \text{ min}$$

$$L_{q1} = \frac{0.5^2}{1-0.5} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Station 2 Metrics (M/M/1)**

$$W_2 = \frac{1.5}{1-0.75} = 6 \text{ min}$$

$$L_2 = \frac{0.75}{1-0.75} = 3.0$$

$$W_{q2} = \frac{0.75 \cdot 1.5}{1-0.75} = 4.5 \text{ min}$$

$$L_{q2} = \frac{0.75^2}{1-0.75} = 2.25$$

**Step 4 — Overall System Metrics (Sum Across Stations)**

$$L = L_1 + L_2 = 1.0 + 3.0 = 4.0$$

$$W = W_1 + W_2 = 2 + 6 = 8 \text{ min}$$

$$L_q = L_{q1} + L_{q2} = 0.5 + 2.25 = 2.75$$

$$W_q = W_{q1} + W_{q2} = 1 + 4.5 = 5.5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Verification Using Little's Law (Cross-Check)**

$$L = \lambda \cdot W = 0.5 \times 8 = 4.0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$L_q = \lambda \cdot W_q = 0.5 \times 5.5 = 2.75 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bottleneck Identification:** Station 2 has $\rho_2 = 0.75 > \rho_1 = 0.5$. It is the bottleneck — its higher utilization drives the entire line's performance. A practical KTU "what-if" question might ask: *"By how much must $\bar{S}_2$ reduce so that Station 1 becomes the bottleneck?"* Answer: To balance, set $\rho_2 = 0.5$, so $\bar{S}_2 = 0.5/\lambda = 1$ min.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Physical Flow Topology (Network of Queues)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Generator] --> q1[Queue 1]
    q1 --> svr1[Server Station 1]
    svr1 --> dec{Routing Decision}
    dec -->|prob p1| q2[Queue 2]
    dec -->|prob p2| q3[Queue 3]
    dec -->|prob p3| q4[Queue 4]
    q2 --> svr2[Server Station 2]
    q3 --> svr3[Server Station 3]
    q4 --> svr4[Server Station 4]
    svr2 --> trans1[Transport Delay]
    svr3 --> trans2[Transport Delay]
    svr4 --> trans3[Transport Delay]
    trans1 --> sink[Exit Sink]
    trans2 --> sink
    trans3 --> sink
    rsc[Shared Resource Pool Crane or Operator] -. serves .-> svr2
    rsc -. serves .-> svr3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Source Generator** creates entities at stochastic inter-arrival times.
- **Queue 1 → Server 1** is a classic M/M/1 tandem segment.
- **Routing Decision** splits the flow probabilistically — a key modeling primitive for **fork** behavior.
- **Shared Resource Pool** (dashed lines) illustrates a resource that is *captured* by a server at service start and *released* at service end — modeling operators, cranes, AGVs, etc.
- **Transport Delays** capture inter-station movement time without queueing.

## 4.2 Finite-Buffer Tandem Line with Blocking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source arrivals at rate lambda] --> B[Buffer B1 capacity K1]
    B --> C[Server S1 service rate mu1]
    C --> D[Buffer B2 capacity K2]
    D --> E[Server S2 service rate mu2]
    E --> F[Buffer B3 capacity K3]
    F --> G[Server S3 service rate mu3]
    G --> H[Sink]
    B -.if full.-> BLK1[Server S1 blocked cannot release]
    D -.if full.-> BLK2[Server S2 blocked cannot release]
    F -.if full.-> BLK3[Server S3 blocked cannot release]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- Each buffer has a finite capacity $K_i$.
- When $B_i$ is full, the upstream server $S_{i-1}$ cannot release its completed entity — this is **blocking**, a hallmark of real manufacturing lines.
- Blocking dramatically reduces effective throughput below the unblocked Poisson-rate ideal.

## 4.3 Closed Network Loop (Recirculating Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pallet 1] --> M1[Machine 1]
    M1 --> P2[Pallet 2]
    P2 --> M2[Machine 2]
    M2 --> P3[Pallet 3]
    P3 --> M3[Machine 3]
    M3 --> P1
    NP[(Fixed Population N entities)] -.- P1
    NP -.- P2
    NP -.- P3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- A **fixed population** of $N$ entities (pallets) circulates forever.
- There is **no external source or sink**.
- Performance is governed by **inverse Little's Law**: $X = N/W$.
- Increasing $N$ raises throughput up to a saturation point (where the bottleneck server $\rho \to 1$).

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Physical-Flow DES Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIM[Simulation Engine]
        CLK[Master Simulation Clock]
        EVQ[Event Queue Future Events List]
        RNG[Random Number Generator]
    end
    subgraph MOD[Physical Flow Model]
        SRC[Source Module]
        Q[Queue Module FIFO Buffer]
        SVR[Server Module Service Logic]
        RTE[Routing Module Probability Matrix]
        SNK[Sink Module Output]
    end
    subgraph STAT[Statistical Collectors]
        LCNT[L counter time averaged]
        WCNT[W counter per entity]
        RCNT[rho utilization timer]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Reports]
        RPT[Performance Report Throughput WIP Util]
    end
    CLK --> EVQ
    RNG --> SRC
    EVQ --> SRC
    SRC --> Q
    Q --> SVR
    SVR --> RTE
    RTE --> SNK
    RTE -.reroute.-> Q
    SRC --> LCNT
    Q --> LCNT
    SVR --> LCNT
    SVR --> RCNT
    RTE --> WCNT
    LCNT --> RPT
    WCNT --> RPT
    RCNT --> RPT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define physical flow systems in the context of discrete event simulation. List any four performance measures used to evaluate them.**

**Model Answer:**

Physical flow systems are real-world arrangements in which tangible entities (parts, materials, people, documents) move through a network of processing stations, queues, and transport links. In DES, these are modeled as a sequence of stochastic events governing arrival, service, and departure of entities.

Four performance measures:

1. **Throughput ($X$):** number of entities completed per unit time.
2. **Work-In-Process ($L$):** average number of entities present in the system.
3. **Cycle Time ($W$):** average time an entity spends from entry to exit.
4. **Resource Utilization ($\rho$):** fraction of total time a server or resource is busy.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note (1 mark per measure):** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and ½ mark for each valid measure. Stating only the symbol without description loses ½ mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**State Little's Law and explain why it is considered universal in queueing theory.**

**Model Answer:**

Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$, where $L$ = time-average number of entities in the system, $\lambda$ = long-run arrival rate, $W$ = average time an entity spends in the system.

It is universal because it holds regardless of:
- the arrival distribution (Poisson, deterministic, general),
- the service-time distribution (exponential, Erlang, deterministic),
- the number of servers,
- the network topology (open, closed, tandem, fork-join),
- the queue discipline (FIFO, LIFO, priority).

The proof relies only on the conservation of entity-time area, not on any distributional assumption, hence its universality.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the steady-state performance metrics ($L$, $W$, $L_q$, $W_q$) for an M/M/1 queueing system starting from the state balance equations. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A small manufacturing cell consists of two machines in series. Parts arrive at Machine 1 at a Poisson rate of 4 per hour. Service time at Machine 1 is exponentially distributed with mean 12 minutes; service time at Machine 2 is exponentially distributed with mean 10 minutes. Both machines have infinite buffers. Compute the utilization of each machine, the average number of parts in the system, and the average cycle time of a part. Identify the bottleneck. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — State assumptions and define $\rho$**

$$\lambda = \text{arrival rate}, \quad \mu = \text{service rate}, \quad \bar{S} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

$$\rho = \lambda \bar{S} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}, \quad \rho < 1$$

**Step 2 — Write state balance equation**

$$\lambda P_n = \mu P_{n+1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{n+1} = \rho P_n$$

**Step 3 — Solve recursively**

$$P_n = \rho^n P_0$$

**Step 4 — Apply normalization**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = \frac{P_0}{1-\rho} = 1 \Rightarrow P_0 = 1-\rho$$

**Step 5 — Compute $L$**

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Little's Law**

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{\bar{S}}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 7 — Compute queue-only metrics**

$$L_q = L - \rho = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$

$$W_q = W - \bar{S} = \frac{\rho \bar{S}}{1-\rho}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert units**

$\lambda = 4$ /hr = $4/60 = 1/15$ per minute.
$\bar{S}_1 = 12$ min, $\bar{S}_2 = 10$ min.

**Step 2 — Compute utilizations**

$$\rho_1 = \lambda \bar{S}_1 = \frac{1}{15} \times 12 = 0.8$$

$$\rho_2 = \lambda \bar{S}_2 = \frac{1}{15} \times 10 = 0.667$$

**Step 3 — Per-station metrics**

For Machine 1:

$$L_1 = \frac{0.8}{1-0.8} = 4.0$$

$$W_1 = \frac{12}{1-0.8} = 60 \text{ min}$$

For Machine 2:

$$L_2 = \frac{0.667}{1-0.667} = 2.0$$

$$W_2 = \frac{10}{1-0.667} = 30 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate**

$$L = 4.0 + 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ parts}$$

$$W = 60 + 30 = 90 \text{ min} = 1.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via Little's Law**

$$L = \lambda W = \frac{1}{15} \times 90 = 6.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Bottleneck identification**

Machine 1 with $\rho_1 = 0.8 > \rho_2 = 0.667$ is the bottleneck.

**Final Answers:** $\rho_1 = 0.8$, $\rho_2 = 0.667$, $L = 6$ parts, $W = 90$ min, bottleneck = Machine 1.

---

### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the generic building blocks of a discrete event simulation model of a physical flow system. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A closed queueing network consists of 5 identical pallets circulating between a single loading station (mean service time = 4 min) and a single unloading station (mean service time = 6 min). All service times are exponential. Compute the system throughput using the inverse form of Little's Law, given that the average cycle time of a pallet is observed to be 25 minutes through simulation. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram (to be drawn by student):**

```
Source --> Queue --> Server --> Decision --> Queue --> Server --> Sink
              (Resource Pool shared across servers)
```

**Description of blocks (each block 1 mark):**

1. **Source:** Generates entities at stochastic inter-arrival times governed by a probability distribution (e.g., exponential).
2. **Queue:** Buffer where entities await service; discipline can be FIFO, LIFO, or priority-based.
3. **Server:** Processing station that holds the entity for a service time drawn from a distribution.
4. **Decision / Route:** Selects the next station based on a routing probability matrix.
5. **Sink:** Terminal node that removes entities and updates completion statistics.
6. **Resource Pool:** Optional shared resource (operator, crane) captured and released by servers.
7. **Transport Delay:** Models inter-station movement without queueing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the inverse Little's Law identity**

$$X = \frac{N}{W}$$

This holds for any closed network regardless of topology.

**Step 2 — Substitute given values**

$$N = 5 \text{ pallets}, \quad W = 25 \text{ min/pallet}$$

$$X = \frac{5}{25} = 0.2 \text{ pallets/min}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a more readable unit**

$$X = 0.2 \times 60 = 12 \text{ pallets/hour}$$

**Final Answer:** Throughput = **12 pallets per hour**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to verify stability ($\rho < 1$):** If you compute $\rho \ge 1$, the formulas blow up. Always state the stability check explicitly.
> - **Mixing units (per-hour vs per-minute):** A very common error is to leave $\lambda$ in /hr while $\bar{S}$ is in minutes. Convert both to consistent units **first**.
> - **In tandem line questions, summing only $W$ but not $L$:** Remember, both are summed across stations. Students often sum only one and present an inconsistent set.
> - **Closed vs open confusion:** Closed networks do **not** have throughput equal to arrival rate. Use $X = N/W$, not $X = \lambda$.
> - **Blocking omission:** If a question mentions "finite buffer" or "blocking," you cannot apply pure M/M/1; mention blocking and approximate or use finite-buffer formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Physical Flow** = movement of tangible entities through a network of queues and servers.
- ✅ **Four building blocks** = Source, Queue, Server, Sink (+ Decision, Resource, Transport).
- ✅ **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ is the single most important equation — universal, assumption-free.
- ✅ **Inverse Little's Law** $X = N/W$ governs closed networks.
- ✅ **Utilization $\rho = \lambda \bar{S}$** must be **strictly less than 1** for stability of single-server systems.
- ✅ **M/M/1 results to memorize:** $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $W = \bar{S}/(1-\rho)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $W_q = \rho \bar{S}/(1-\rho)$.
- ✅ **Bottleneck** = station with the **highest utilization**.
- ✅ **Tandem lines (open):** aggregate metrics are the **sum** of per-station metrics.
- ✅ **Blocking** appears in finite-buffer systems — reduces effective throughput.
- ✅ **Routing probabilities** must sum to 1 at every decision node (conservation of flow).
- ✅ **Always convert units** before substituting into formulas.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite combos:** Little's Law + M/M/1; Bottleneck identification + what-if improvement; Open vs Closed network comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **System Dynamics** (the foundational paradigm of KTU Module 3), an **Information Flow** is defined as the *transmission of a signal, data value, or perceived state between elements of a system that does NOT conserve mass, energy, or material quantity*, but rather conveys *knowledge, status, or decision-relevant parameters* that subsequently influence the rate variables (flows) of the model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Information flows are the connective tissue between **Level (Stock) variables** and **Rate (Flow) variables**. While material flows obey conservation laws (Inventory = Previous Inventory + Inflow − Outflow), information flows simply *carry meaning* derived from observed levels to govern future actions.

Mathematically, an information flow is represented as a *pure function* of one or more level variables:

$$
\boxed{\text{Rate}(t) = f(\text{Level}(t - \tau), \text{Auxiliary Variables}, \text{Constants})}
$$

where $\tau$ represents an optional **information delay** — a critical concept that distinguishes instantaneous from lagged information transfer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body:**
> Imagine your body as a simulation model.
> - **Material Flow** = the *blood* physically circulating oxygen and nutrients. (Conserved — blood is neither created nor destroyed in transit.)
> - **Information Flow** = the *nerve impulses* sent from your brain telling your heart to beat faster after sensing low oxygen. The signal itself carries no mass — only the *instruction*.
>
> Similarly, in a factory:
> - **Material Flow** = raw materials moving along a conveyor belt.
> - **Information Flow** = the supervisor's report that "Inventory is low — increase production rate."

This is why information flows are the **drivers of decision-making** in dynamic systems: they translate the *current state* into *future action*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The fundamental quantities associated with information flows in KTU-standard system dynamics models are:

- **Level (Stock) Variable:** $L(t)$ — measured in *units of accumulation* (e.g., persons, items, ₹).
- **Rate (Flow) Variable:** $R(t)$ — measured in *units per unit time* (e.g., persons/month, ₹/year).
- **Auxiliary Variable:** $A(t)$ — a derived informational quantity (e.g., *desired inventory*, *perceived error*).
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** characteristic delay of information transmission, in **time units** (days, months, years).
- **Loop Gain ($G$):** dimensionless product of sensitivities around a feedback loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of an Information Flow

Information flows in System Dynamics are classified along four orthogonal axes:

1. **Origin of the Signal**
   - **Derived-from-Level:** Information generated by observing an accumulating stock (e.g., *current inventory*).
   - **Derived-from-Rate:** Information generated by observing an activity (e.g., *orders-per-day trend*).
   - **External (Exogenous):** Information entering the system boundary (e.g., *market demand forecast*).

2. **Direction of Causality**
   - **Forward (Feedforward):** Information moves from an upstream element to a downstream decision-maker without returning.
   - **Feedback (Closed Loop):** Information returns to influence its own cause — the foundation of system dynamics.

3. **Temporal Behavior**
   - **Instantaneous:** No delay between level observation and rate adjustment.
   - **Delayed:** Information is transmitted through a *pipeline* of finite length, producing lag $\tau > 0$.

4. **Mathematical Form**
   - **Linear:** $R = aL + b$
   - **Nonlinear (most realistic):** $R = aL^2$, $R = a\sqrt{L}$, $R = a \cdot L / (K + L)$ (Michaelis-Menten / saturation form).

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why do information flows exist?** Because decision-makers (whether human managers or control algorithms) cannot act on raw material directly — they require a *measured, summarized, possibly delayed representation* of system state.
- **How are they modeled?** In **stock-flow diagrams**, they appear as **thin curved arrows with no valve symbol**, distinguishing them visually from thick material flows.
- **How do they differ from causal links?** In a **causal loop diagram (CLD)**, every link is implicitly an *information flow*. CLDs abstract away stock/flow geometry to focus purely on influence.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering / Modeling Use |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Conservation of Material | $L(t) = L(0) + \int_{0}^{t} [R_{\text{in}}(s) - R_{\text{out}}(s)]\, ds$ | Inventory, population, capital models |
| 2 | Information-Driven Rate | $R(t) = f(L(t-\tau))$ | Decision rules in dynamic systems |
| 3 | Exponential Smoothing (Information Filter) | $S(t) = \alpha L(t) + (1-\alpha)S(t-\Delta t)$ | Smoothed perceived demand |
| 4 | First-Order Information Delay | $\frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau}[L(t) - S(t)]$ | Perception / reaction lag |
| 3 | Loop Gain (Linear System) | $G = \prod_{i=1}^{n} k_i$ | Stability of feedback loops |
| 6 | Negative Feedback Equilibrium | $L^* = \frac{R_{\text{desired}}}{k}$ | Target-seeking behavior |
| 7 | Positive Feedback Divergence | $L(t) \approx L(0)\, e^{\lambda t}$, $\lambda > 0$ | Growth / collapse dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** Material flows have a **valve symbol** in stock-flow diagrams; information flows do **NOT**. This is the single most-tested visual identification in KTU Board exams on this module.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Information flows underpin virtually every modern engineered system:

- **Inventory Management (Forrester, 1961):** Re-order decisions are based on *information* about current stock, not direct manipulation of stock.
- **Epidemic Modeling (SIR models):** Infection rate depends on *information* about susceptible population density.
- **Climate Systems:** Albedo feedback — *information* about ice extent drives temperature assumptions.
- **Control Engineering:** Every closed-loop PID controller is essentially an *information flow* from sensor to actuator.
- **Socio-economic systems:** Consumer confidence index, an *information variable*, drives aggregate demand.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Information Flow as a Causal Operator

We begin from the foundational System Dynamics premise that every rate variable $R(t)$ is a *function* of one or more level variables, possibly through auxiliary informational intermediates.

**Step 1 — Generic Rate Equation.**
A rate variable describes an activity whose magnitude depends on the *perceived state* of the system:

$$
R(t) = g\!\left(L_1(t - \tau_1),\; L_2(t - \tau_2),\; \ldots,\; L_n(t - \tau_n)\right)
$$

Here, $g(\cdot)$ is the *decision function* — an information-processing rule.

**Step 2 — Linearization (Taylor Expansion around equilibrium $L^*$).**
For analytical tractability, expand $g$ to first order:

$$
R(t) \approx g(L^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial g}{\partial L_i}\bigg|_{L^*} \bigl(L_i(t-\tau_i) - L_i^*\bigr)
$$

**Step 3 — Define Sensitivity Coefficients.**
Let $k_i = \left.\dfrac{\partial g}{\partial L_i}\right|_{L^*}$ denote the *sensitivity* of the rate to information about level $L_i$. The equation becomes:

$$
R(t) = R^* + \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i\, \bigl(L_i(t-\tau_i) - L_i^*\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Loop Gain Definition.**
For a single negative feedback loop containing $n$ sensitivities $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n$ and one delay $\tau$, the **open-loop gain** is:

$$
G = \prod_{i=1}^{n} k_i
$$

**Step 5 — Stability Condition (Routh-Hurwitz, first-order).**
For a first-order negative feedback loop, the system is stable iff:

$$
\boxed{\;\vert G \vert < 1 \quad \text{(with appropriate sign convention)}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Information Delay Effect.**
Introducing a pure delay $e^{-s\tau}$ into the loop transfer function modifies stability via:

$$
G_{\text{loop}}(s) = G \cdot e^{-s\tau}
$$

The Nyquist criterion then demands:

$$
\boxed{\;\vert G \vert < 1 \quad \text{or} \quad \omega \tau < \pi \quad \text{(for marginal stability)}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Discretization (Euler Integration, $\Delta t$ step).**
For digital simulation (Stella, Vensim, MATLAB), the information transmission across a delay $\tau$ is implemented as:

$$
S(t + \Delta t) = S(t) + \frac{\Delta t}{\tau}\bigl[L(t) - S(t)\bigr]
$$

This is the **first-order exponential information delay** — by far the most common KTU-exam formulation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Material Flow vs. Information Flow

| Property | Material Flow | Information Flow |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| Conservation | Strictly conserved | **NOT** conserved |
| Diagram Symbol | Thick arrow + **valve** | Thin curved arrow, **no valve** |
| Source | Source/sink outside boundary | A level, auxiliary, or constant |
| Sink | A level (stock) | A rate or auxiliary variable |
| Mathematical Nature | Accumulation integral | Pure function (no integration) |
| Can contain delay? | Yes (pipeline delay) | Yes (perception delay) |
| Units | Items / time | Same as receiving rate |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A retail store observes that its monthly ordering rate $R(t)$ depends on the perceived inventory gap:
> $$R(t) = k \cdot \bigl[I_{\text{target}} - I(t - 2)\bigr]$$
> where $k = 0.4\,/\text{month}$, $I_{\text{target}} = 500$ units, and the information arrives with a 2-month delay. Find the equilibrium inventory and determine loop stability.

**Solution:**

At equilibrium, $R^* = 0$ (no ordering needed when target is met):

$$
I^* = I_{\text{target}} = 500 \text{ units}
$$

Perturbation around equilibrium: let $\delta I(t) = I(t) - 500$.

$$
\delta R(t) = -k \cdot \delta I(t-2) = -0.4 \cdot \delta I(t-2)
$$

Inventory dynamics:

$$
\frac{d(\delta I)}{dt} = \delta R(t) = -0.4 \cdot \delta I(t-2)
$$

Characteristic equation in Laplace domain:

$$
s + 0.4 \cdot e^{-2s} = 0
$$

For small delays, approximate $e^{-2s} \approx 1 - 2s$:

$$
s + 0.4(1 - 2s) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s(1 - 0.8) + 0.4 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.2s = -0.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\;s = -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Stable, with time constant } 0.5 \text{ months}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The information flow from inventory to ordering rate, even with a 2-month delay, produces a *self-correcting* negative feedback loop that drives the system back to equilibrium — a hallmark of well-designed information architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) — Generic Negative Feedback

A causal loop diagram captures *information flows only* — every arrow is an informational influence.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Desired Level"] -->|+| nodeB["Gap Detector"]
    nodeC["Actual Level"] -->|+| nodeB
    nodeB -->|+| nodeD["Decision Rule"]
    nodeD -->|+| nodeE["Action Rate"]
    nodeE -->|+| nodeC
    nodeC -.->|- Feedback Signal| nodeB
```

**Interpretation:**
- `Desired Level` provides the *target* (information).
- `Actual Level` provides the *measured state* (information).
- `Gap Detector` computes the difference — purely informational.
- `Decision Rule` maps the gap to an action rate.
- The dashed line `nodeC -.-> nodeB` closes the **negative feedback loop** (balancing loop).

### 4.2 Stock-Flow Diagram — Information-Driven Inventory System

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["Source"] -->|Material Flow| valveA((Valve))
    valveA -->|Orders| stockA[("Inventory Level")]
    stockA -->|Material Flow| valveB((Valve))
    valveB -->|Sales| sinkA["Sink"]
    stockA -.->|Information Flow| auxA["Perceived Inventory"]
    targetA["Target Inventory"] -.->|Information Flow| auxB["Inventory Gap"]
    auxA -->|Information Flow| auxB
    auxB -.->|Information Flow| valveA
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Thick arrows** = material flows (with valves).
- **Thin dotted arrows** = information flows (no valves).
- `Perceived Inventory` and `Inventory Gap` are *auxiliary informational variables*.
- The information flow from `Inventory Gap` back to the `Orders` valve **closes the feedback loop**.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Information Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    stageA["Stage A: Sensing"] --> stageB["Stage B: Transmission"]
    stageB --> stageC["Stage C: Filtering"]
    stageC --> stageD["Stage D: Decision"]
    stageD --> stageE["Stage E: Action Rate"]
    levelA["Level Variable L t"] -->|Information Source| stageA
    stageE -->|Drives| levelA
```

This pipeline is the canonical **information-flow architecture** in every System Dynamics model: *Sense → Transmit → Filter → Decide → Act → Modify the level that was sensed.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

*Distinguish between material flows and information flows in a System Dynamics model. Give one example of each from an inventory management context.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Material Flow | Information Flow |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Conservation | Conserved | Not conserved |
| Diagram | Thick arrow + valve | Thin arrow, no valve |
| Example | Shipment of goods arriving at warehouse | Reorder trigger signal based on current stock level |

In inventory management, the **physical arrival of goods** is a material flow, while the **report that "stock has fallen below 500 units"** — which prompts a new order — is an information flow. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for example.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

*What is an information delay? How does it affect the stability of a negative feedback loop?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **information delay** is the lag between the time a level variable is observed and the time its corresponding information influences a rate variable. Mathematically, if $L(t)$ is the level and $\tau$ is the delay:

$$
R(t) = f\bigl(L(t - \tau)\bigr)
$$

In a negative feedback loop, increasing $\tau$ reduces the **phase margin** of the system. If $\tau$ becomes large enough that $\omega\tau \geq \pi$ while $\vert G \vert \geq 1$, the loop exhibits **oscillations or instability**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for stability effect.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (i) confusing **auxiliary variables** with **levels** in stock-flow diagrams, (ii) forgetting to mark **polarities (+/−)** on causal loop arrows, and (iii) omitting the **valve distinction** between material and information flows. Each omission costs ~2 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat stock-flow diagram, explain how information flows drive the ordering rate in a simple inventory system. Label the level, the rate, the auxiliary variables, and clearly distinguish material from information flows. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct the causal loop diagram for the above inventory system. Identify the loop polarity and derive the equilibrium condition. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Stock-Flow Diagram Construction (7 Marks):**

```
   [Source] →→ (Valve: Orders R(t)) →→ [ Inventory Level I(t) ]
                                                  ↓
                                            (Valve: Sales S(t))
                                                  ↓
                                              [Sink]

   I(t) · · · · · · → [Aux: Perceived Stock P(t)]
   [Const: Target T] · · · · · · · → [Aux: Gap G(t) = T − P(t)]
                              P(t) · · · · · · · → [Aux: Gap]
   [Aux: Gap G(t)] · · · · · · · · · · · · → (Valve: Orders)
```

- **Level (Stock):** `Inventory Level I(t)` — accumulates material.
- **Rates (Flows):** `Orders R(t)`, `Sales S(t)` — have valves, thick arrows.
- **Auxiliary informational variables:** `Perceived Stock P(t)`, `Gap G(t) = T − P(t)` — no valves, thin arrows.
- **Material flow:** Orders arriving at Inventory.
- **Information flow:** From `I(t)` to `P(t)`, from `P(t)` to `Gap G(t)`, from `G(t)` back to the Orders valve.

*Mark split: 2 for diagram, 2 for variable identification, 2 for flow-type distinction, 1 for clarity.*

**(b) Causal Loop Diagram + Equilibrium (7 Marks):**

CLD arrows (with polarities):

```
Desired Inventory T  ──(+)──> Gap G
Inventory I(t)       ──(+)──> Gap G       (since Gap = T − I)
Gap G                ──(+)──> Order Rate R
Order Rate R         ──(+)──> Inventory I
```

**Loop identification:** All four arrows are `+`, so loop polarity $= (+)(+)(+)(+) = +$ — but wait: Gap $= T - I$, so $I \to \text{Gap}$ has a *negative* effect on Gap.

Corrected polarities:

```
T  ──(+)──> Gap
I  ──(−)──> Gap     (because Gap = T − I)
Gap ──(+)──> R
R  ──(+)──> I
```

**Loop polarity:** $(+)(-)(+)(+) = (-)$ → **Balancing (negative feedback) loop**.

**Equilibrium derivation:** At equilibrium, $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow R^* = S^*$.

Assuming linear decision rule $R = k \cdot (T - I)$ and constant sales $S = S_0$:

$$
k(T - I^*) = S_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;I^* = T - \frac{S_0}{k}\;}
$$

*Mark split: 2 for CLD with polarities, 2 for loop polarity derivation, 3 for equilibrium.*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Define an information delay. Derive the differential equation governing a first-order exponential information delay and explain its role in modeling perception lag. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company's hiring rate $H(t)$ depends on the perceived workforce gap $G(t-\tau)$ according to $H(t) = \alpha \cdot G(t-\tau)$, where $\alpha = 0.5\,/\text{month}$ and $\tau = 1$ month. The workforce $W(t)$ evolves as $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = H(t) - D$, where $D = 10\,/\text{month}$ is constant attrition. Determine the equilibrium workforce and analyze the local stability using linearization. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Information Delay — Definition and Derivation (7 Marks):**

A **first-order exponential information delay** smooths an instantaneous level $L(t)$ into a *perceived* level $P(t)$ according to:

$$
\tau \cdot \frac{dP(t)}{dt} + P(t) = L(t)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau}\bigl[L(t) - P(t)\bigr]
$$

**Derivation:** Consider a pipeline of perceived stock $P(t)$ that fills at rate $\dfrac{L(t)}{\tau}$ and drains at rate $\dfrac{P(t)}{\tau}$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{L(t)}{\tau} - \frac{P(t)}{\tau} = \frac{L(t) - P(t)}{\tau}
$$

This is the **standard first-order linear ODE** with solution:

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\tau}\int_{-\infty}^{t} L(s)\, e^{-(t-s)/\tau}\, ds
$$

i.e., a **weighted exponential average** of past inputs — the perceptual analog of an RC low-pass filter.

*Mark split: 2 for definition, 3 for derivation, 2 for physical interpretation.*

**(b) Workforce Equilibrium and Stability (7 Marks):**

At equilibrium, $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow H^* = D$.

$$
\alpha \cdot G^* = D \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; G^* = \frac{D}{\alpha} = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20 \text{ workers}
$$

Now let $w(t) = W(t) - W^*$ be the perturbation. With $G = T - W$ (gap from target $T$):

$$
H(t) = \alpha \bigl[T - W(t - 1)\bigr] = \alpha(T - W^*) - \alpha\, w(t-1)
$$

$$
\frac{dw}{dt} = -\alpha \cdot w(t - 1)
$$

Substituting $w(t) = A e^{st}$:

$$
s = -\alpha e^{-s}
$$

For small $\tau$, $e^{-s} \approx 1 - s$:

$$
s = -\alpha(1 - s) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s(1 - \alpha) = -\alpha \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s = \frac{-\alpha}{1-\alpha}
$$

With $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$
\boxed{\;s = \frac{-0.5}{0.5} = -1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Stable, time constant} = 1 \text{ month}\;}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system is locally stable; any deviation from equilibrium decays exponentially with a 1-month time constant. The information delay of 1 month *amplifies* the convergence speed but does not destabilize because $\alpha < 1$.

*Mark split: 2 for equilibrium, 2 for linearization, 2 for stability analysis, 1 for conclusion.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Information Flows":**

- **Definition:** Information flow = non-conserved signal carrying *state knowledge* from a level or auxiliary to a rate or another auxiliary.
- **Visual rule (KTU Board favorite):** *Thick arrow + valve = material; thin arrow + no valve = information.*
- **Conversion law:** Material flows accumulate into levels via integration; information flows do **not** accumulate — they are instantaneous or delayed functions.
- **Causal Loop Diagrams (CLDs):** Show only information flows; every arrow is an influence with a polarity (+/−).
- **Stock-Flow Diagrams:** Show BOTH material and information flows; valves mark only material flows.
- **Loop polarity rule:** Product of an even number of negatives = positive feedback (reinforcing); odd number of negatives = negative feedback (balancing).
- **Information delay $\tau$:** Implemented via first-order exponential filter $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}[L - P]$ or pure delay $P(t) = L(t-\tau)$.
- **Stability rule of thumb:** For negative feedback, loop gain $\vert G \vert < 1$ AND $\omega\tau < \pi$ for stability.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing *information delay* with *material delay* — only material delays create pipeline stocks.
- **Five-stage information pipeline:** Sense → Transmit → Filter → Decide → Act. Memorize this for descriptive questions.
- **Engineering applications:** Inventory control (Forrester), epidemic SIR, climate feedback, PID control, economic models.
- **Auxiliary variables:** Always information-bearing, never accumulated; sit *between* levels and rates in the dependency chain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Level and Rate Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Level and Rate Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Level Variables (Stock / State Variables)

A **Level Variable** (also called a *stock* or *state variable*) is a system component that **accumulates** something over time. It represents the *quantity present in the system at a given instant*. Levels can only change through the action of rate variables, and they have units of pure quantity (not quantity per unit time).

Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):
> A **Level Variable** is an accumulation or state quantity whose value at any instant depends on the *integral of the net inflow* up to that instant.

**Classic Examples:**
- Water level in a reservoir (m³)
- Population of a city (persons)
- Inventory of goods in a warehouse (units)
- Capital balance in a bank account (₹)
- Number of jobs waiting in a queue

## 1.2 Rate Variables (Flow Variables)

A **Rate Variable** (also called a *flow* or *activity variable*) describes the **speed at which a level changes per unit of time**. It has units of *quantity per unit time* (e.g., persons/year, m³/sec, ₹/month).

Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):
> A **Rate Variable** is an activity or flow that fills or drains a level, measured as the *time derivative* of the level (or net combination of rates).

**Classic Examples:**
- Birth rate, death rate (persons/year)
- Inflow to / outflow from a reservoir (m³/sec)
- Production rate of a factory (units/day)
- Hiring and firing rate of employees

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bathtub

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Bathtub Analogy):** Imagine a *bathtub* with water in it. The **water level** in the tub is the *Level Variable*. The **faucet pouring water in** and the **drain letting water out** are the *Rate Variables*. The level rises when the inflow rate exceeds the outflow rate, and falls when the outflow exceeds the inflow. Levels cannot jump — they accumulate gradually through rates.

The bathtub is the canonical System Dynamics metaphor used by **Jay Forrester (MIT)** and is universally adopted in KTU simulation textbooks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Level and Rate Variables form the *core structural backbone* of any System Dynamics model. Every continuous simulation model in the syllabus is built by linking level equations to rate equations through differential/integral calculus.

## 1.4 Auxiliary Variables

Sometimes rates cannot be computed directly from levels alone — they require **intermediate computations** called *auxiliary variables* (or converters). These do not represent accumulations; they are pure *algebraic* quantities used to determine a rate from levels.

**Example:** The *interest rate* paid on a *bank balance* depends on an *interest percentage* and the *balance* itself. The interest amount is an auxiliary, while the bank balance is a level.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Level and Rate

The two variable types obey a strict causal hierarchy in System Dynamics:

1. **Levels** store accumulation. They are *integrated* quantities.
2. **Rates** drive levels. They are *differentiated* (time-derivative) quantities.
3. **Auxiliary variables** perform arithmetic transformations between them.
4. **Constants / Parameters** (like valve coefficients, interest percentages) supply fixed values.
5. **Feedback loops** close the system: a level influences a rate, which in turn changes that level.

### Why this distinction matters

- Levels give the system *memory* and *inertia* (they cannot change instantaneously).
- Rates give the system *responsiveness* and *dynamics*.
- Confusing the two is the single most common modeling error in KTU exam answers.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Differential Equation

The relationship between a level $L(t)$ and its net rate $R(t)$ is given by the **conservation / continuity principle**:

$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{\text{in}}(t) - R_{\text{out}}(t)$$

Integrating both sides:

$$L(t) = L(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \left[ R_{\text{in}}(\tau) - R_{\text{out}}(\tau) \right] d\tau$$

For simulation on a digital computer, this continuous integral must be approximated using **numerical integration**. The simplest scheme used in KTU textbooks is the **Euler (Rectangular) Integration**:

$$L(t + \Delta t) = L(t) + \Delta t \cdot \left[ R_{\text{in}}(t) - R_{\text{out}}(t) \right]$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous Level Equation | $\dfrac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{\text{net}}(t)$ | quantity / time | Level is the time-integral of net rate |
| Discrete (Euler) Level Update | $L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_{\text{net},k}$ | quantity | Standard KTU simulation update |
| Net Rate | $R_{\text{net}} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$ | quantity / time | Signed scalar; positive fills the level |
| Mean Level over Interval | $\overline{L} \approx \dfrac{L_k + L_{k+1}}{2}$ | quantity | Used in improved (Trapezoidal) integration |
| Trapezoidal (Improved) Update | $L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{R_k + R_{k+1}}{2}$ | quantity | Higher accuracy than Euler |
| Auxiliary Rate Dependency | $R = f(\text{Levels, Auxiliaries, Constants})$ | quantity / time | Functional relationship of policy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note (KTU 2024):** Always state the **units** explicitly. If $L$ is in *persons* and $\Delta t$ is in *years*, then $R$ must be in *persons / year* — examiners deduct marks when units are missing.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inventory Management:** Level = stock on shelf; rates = supply and demand flows.
- **Hydrology / Water Resources:** Level = reservoir storage; rates = inflow from river and outflow to turbines.
- **Epidemiology:** Level = infected population; rate = infection transmission minus recovery.
- **Project Management:** Level = remaining work; rate = work-completion velocity.
- **Economics:** Level = capital stock; rate = investment minus depreciation.

In every domain, the same **level–rate differential pair** governs dynamic behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Level Equation from First Principles

Consider a level $L(t)$ representing an *accumulated quantity*. Let the *net flow* into the level during an infinitesimal interval $dt$ be $R(t)\,dt$.

By the **conservation of mass / quantity**, the new level at time $t + dt$ equals the old level plus the net inflow:

$$L(t + dt) = L(t) + R(t) \cdot dt$$

Rearranging:

$$L(t + dt) - L(t) = R(t) \cdot dt$$

Dividing both sides by $dt$:

$$\frac{L(t + dt) - L(t)}{dt} = R(t)$$

Taking the limit as $dt \rightarrow 0$:

$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R(t)$$

This confirms that the rate is the *time derivative* of the level, and the level is the *time integral* of the rate. **Every step follows logically from a conservation balance.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Discrete Update

We want a numerical algorithm to advance the simulation from time $t_k$ to $t_{k+1} = t_k + \Delta t$.

Starting from the integral form:

$$L(t_{k+1}) = L(t_k) + \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} R(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Approximation Step 1 — Assume $R(\tau)$ is approximately constant over the small interval $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$ and equal to $R(t_k)$:**

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} R(\tau)\, d\tau \approx R(t_k) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Approximation Step 2 — Substitute back into the level equation:**

$$L(t_{k+1}) \approx L(t_k) + R(t_k) \cdot \Delta t$$

Dropping the time argument and using subscript notation:

$$L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_k$$

This is the **Euler integration formula**. It is *first-order accurate*: the error per step is proportional to $\Delta t^{2}$, and the global error over a fixed simulation horizon is proportional to $\Delta t$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Trapezoidal (Improved) Update

To gain better accuracy, approximate the area under $R(\tau)$ by the **trapezoid** formed by $R(t_k)$ and $R(t_{k+1})$:

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} R(\tau)\, d\tau \approx \frac{\Delta t}{2} \left[ R(t_k) + R(t_{k+1}) \right]$$

But $R(t_{k+1})$ depends on $L_{k+1}$, which we have not yet computed. A practical simulation workaround is to **predict** $L_{k+1}$ using Euler, then **correct** using the trapezoidal average:

**Predictor step:**

$$\tilde{L}_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_k$$

**Corrector step:**

$$L_{k+1} = L_k + \frac{\Delta t}{2} \left[ R_k + \tilde{R}_{k+1} \right]$$

where $\tilde{R}_{k+1}$ is computed from $\tilde{L}_{k+1}$. This is a **Heun's method** style correction and is second-order accurate.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Level Variable | Rate Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical nature | Integral of flows | Derivative (time-rate of change) |
| Units | Pure quantity | Quantity per unit time |
| Behavior in time | Accumulator (memory) | Activity (flow) |
| Can it change instantaneously? | **No** — requires integration time | **Yes** — instantaneous decision |
| Determines past behavior? | **Yes** — depends on history | **No** — purely instantaneous |
| Visual in Stock-Flow diagram | **Rectangle** (tank) | **Pipe with valve / arrow** |
| KTU example | Water in a reservoir | Water flowing through a pipe |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Bank Account Balance

Let:
- $L(t)$ = Bank balance in ₹ (Level Variable)
- $R_{\text{in}}(t)$ = Monthly deposit rate in ₹/month
- $R_{\text{out}}(t)$ = Monthly withdrawal rate in ₹/month
- $L(0) = 10000$ ₹

### Continuous Model

$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{\text{in}}(t) - R_{\text{out}}(t)$$

Assume constant deposit of 2000 ₹/month and constant withdrawal of 1500 ₹/month:

$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = 2000 - 1500 = 500 \;\; \text{₹/month}$$

### Discrete Simulation (Euler, $\Delta t = 1$ month)

Using the update formula derived in §3.2:

$$L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_{\text{net},k}$$

Tabulating:

| Step $k$ | $L_k$ (₹) | $R_{\text{net},k}$ (₹/mo) | $L_{k+1}$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10000 | 500 | 10500 |
| 1 | 10500 | 500 | 11000 |
| 2 | 11000 | 500 | 11500 |
| 3 | 11500 | 500 | 12000 |

Each step applies the conservation equation exactly. The level grows linearly because the net rate is constant — a textbook demonstration of the level–rate coupling.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stock-and-Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **canonical Level–Rate architecture** used in every System Dynamics model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1["RATE_IN Flow"] --> nodeL1
    nodeL1["LEVEL Variable L(t) — Accumulator Tank"] --> nodeA1
    nodeR2["RATE_OUT Flow"] --> nodeL1
    nodeA1["AUXILIARY Variable — Policy Logic"] --> nodeR2
    nodeL1 --> nodeA1
    nodeC1["CONSTANT / PARAMETER"] --> nodeA1

    classDef levelStyle fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rateStyle fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef auxStyle fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef constStyle fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class nodeL1 levelStyle
    class nodeR1,nodeR2 rateStyle
    class nodeA1 auxStyle
    class nodeC1 constStyle
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Simulation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["Initialize L at time t = 0"] --> stepB
    stepB["Read current Rate_in and Rate_out"] --> stepC
    stepC["Compute Net Rate = Rate_in minus Rate_out"] --> stepD
    stepD{"Choose Integration Method"}
    stepD -- Euler --> stepE["L_new = L_old + dt x Net_Rate"]
    stepD -- Trapezoidal --> stepF["L_new = L_old + dt x avg R"]
    stepE --> stepG["Advance clock t = t + dt"]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH{"t less than T_end"}
    stepH -- Yes --> stepB
    stepH -- No --> stepI["Output L trace and stop"]
```

## 4.3 Detailed Stock-Flow Schematic for Bank Account Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDep["RATE_IN Deposit = 2000 Rs per month"] --> nodeBal
    nodeBal["LEVEL Bank Balance Rs — Tank"] --> nodeAux
    nodeWit["RATE_OUT Withdrawal = 1500 Rs per month"] --> nodeBal
    nodeAux["AUX Net Rate = Deposit minus Withdrawal"] --> nodeWit
    nodeBal --> nodeAux
    nodeC1["CONSTANT Deposit = 2000"] --> nodeDep
    nodeC2["CONSTANT Withdrawal = 1500"] --> nodeWit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *rectangle* (LEVEL) stores the balance. Two *arrows* (RATE_IN, RATE_OUT) push into and pull from the rectangle. The *auxiliary* (AUX) block computes the withdrawal as a function of the current level. Constants feed both rate blocks. This is the standard **Forrester-style Stock-Flow diagram** prescribed in KTU 2024 System Dynamics modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Differentiate between Level Variables and Rate Variables in System Dynamics. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Level Variable | Rate Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Accumulation quantity at an instant | Activity that fills or drains a level per unit time |
| Units | Pure quantity (e.g., persons) | Quantity per time (e.g., persons / year) |
| Behavior | Smooth, integrated, has memory | Instantaneous, may change abruptly |
| **Example** | **Population of a city** | **Birth rate of the city (persons / year)** |

*(Full 3 marks awarded for the tabular comparison and one valid example each.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the differential equation relating a Level Variable $L(t)$ to its Rate Variables $R_{\text{in}}(t)$ and $R_{\text{out}}(t)$. What are the units involved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{\text{in}}(t) - R_{\text{out}}(t)$$

- $L(t)$ has units of *quantity* (e.g., kg, persons, ₹).
- $R_{\text{in}}, R_{\text{out}}$ have units of *quantity per unit time*.
- The derivative $dL/dt$ therefore also has units of *quantity per unit time*, confirming dimensional consistency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / 14 Marks]

**(a)** Define Level Variables and Rate Variables. Using the **bathtub analogy**, explain how they interact to produce dynamic behavior. State clearly why levels cannot change instantaneously while rates can. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reservoir has an initial storage $L_0 = 5000$ m³. The inflow rate is a constant $R_{\text{in}} = 800$ m³/hr and the outflow rate is a constant $R_{\text{out}} = 500$ m³/hr. Using **Euler integration** with $\Delta t = 1$ hr, compute the storage level for the **first 5 simulation steps** and tabulate the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definitions:** Level Variable = accumulator / stock / state quantity at an instant; Rate Variable = flow / activity that fills or drains a level per unit time.
2. **Bathtub analogy:** Water in the bathtub is the Level; water pouring in from the faucet is the *inflow rate*; water draining out is the *outflow rate*.
3. **Interaction:** Net rate = inflow − outflow. When net rate is positive, level rises; when negative, level falls; when zero, level is in equilibrium (steady state).
4. **Why levels cannot jump instantly:** A level is the *time integral* of net rate. To change a level by any finite amount requires a non-zero net rate acting over a non-zero time interval — hence levels always change gradually.
5. **Why rates can change instantly:** A rate is a *decision* or *activity* governed by policy and environment, not by an integrator. There is no physical law that smooths a rate.

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks per point. Full 7 marks for clear definitions, correct analogy, and explicit reasoning on instantaneous vs gradual change.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 5000$ m³
- $R_{\text{in}} = 800$ m³/hr
- $R_{\text{out}} = 500$ m³/hr
- $\Delta t = 1$ hr

**Step 1: Compute Net Rate**

$$R_{\text{net}} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}} = 800 - 500 = 300 \;\;\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 2: Apply Euler Update Formula**

$$L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_{\text{net}} = L_k + 1 \cdot 300 = L_k + 300$$

**Step 3: Tabulate**

| Step $k$ | Time $t_k$ (hr) | $L_k$ (m³) | $R_{\text{net},k}$ (m³/hr) | $L_{k+1}$ (m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 5000 | 300 | 5300 |
| 1 | 1 | 5300 | 300 | 5600 |
| 2 | 2 | 5600 | 300 | 5900 |
| 3 | 3 | 5900 | 300 | 6200 |
| 4 | 4 | 6200 | 300 | 6500 |
| 5 | 5 | 6500 | 300 | 6800 |

**Step 4: Verification (closed-form check)**

For constant net rate, the analytical solution is linear:

$$L(t) = L_0 + R_{\text{net}} \cdot t = 5000 + 300 t$$

At $t = 5$ hr: $L(5) = 5000 + 1500 = 6500$ m³. Matches the simulation. *(Award full 7 marks for correct formula application, accurate tabulation, and verification.)*

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]
