**(a)** Derive the **Euler integration formula** for updating a Level Variable $L(t)$ from a known Rate Variable $R(t)$. Discuss **one advantage** and **one limitation** of this method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A production system has an inventory level $L_0 = 200$ units. The production rate is $R_p = 50$ units/day and the demand rate is $R_d = 40$ units/day. Simulate for **6 days** using Euler integration with $\Delta t = 1$ day. Plot (describe in a table) how the inventory evolves and comment on the **steady-state behavior**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (5 marks for derivation; 1 mark each for advantage and limitation):**

Starting from the integral form of the level equation:

$$L(t_{k+1}) = L(t_k) + \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} R(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Approximating the area under the rate curve by a rectangle of height $R(t_k)$ and width $\Delta t$:

$$\int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} R(\tau)\, d\tau \approx R(t_k) \cdot \Delta t$$

Substituting:

$$L(t_{k+1}) \approx L(t_k) + \Delta t \cdot R(t_k)$$

In subscript notation:

$$L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_k$$

**Advantage:** Simple, computationally cheap, requires only one rate evaluation per step — ideal for textbook simulation.

**Limitation:** Only *first-order accurate*; for large $\Delta t$, error accumulates and the simulation may drift from the true solution, especially for stiff or oscillating systems.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 200$ units
- $R_p = 50$ units/day (production / inflow)
- $R_d = 40$ units/day (demand / outflow)
- $\Delta t = 1$ day
- Simulate for 6 days

**Step 1: Net Rate**

$$R_{\text{net}} = R_p - R_d = 50 - 40 = 10 \;\;\text{units/day}$$

**Step 2: Euler Update**

$$L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_{\text{net}} = L_k + 10$$

**Step 3: Tabulate**

| Day $k$ | $L_k$ (units) | $R_{\text{net}}$ (units/day) | $L_{k+1}$ (units) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 200 | 10 | 210 |
| 1 | 210 | 10 | 220 |
| 2 | 220 | 10 | 230 |
| 3 | 230 | 10 | 240 |
| 4 | 240 | 10 | 250 |
| 5 | 250 | 10 | 260 |
| 6 | 260 | 10 | 270 |

**Step 4: Steady-State Comment**

Because $R_p > R_d$, the net rate is positive and *constant*. Therefore the inventory grows **unboundedly** in linear fashion — there is **no steady state** in this scenario. A steady state would be reached only if $R_p = R_d$, in which case the inventory level would remain at its initial value forever. *(Award full 7 marks for the correct table, interpretation, and explicit mention that $R_p \ne R_d$ precludes equilibrium.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Writing $L(t)$ without specifying *what quantity* loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Confusing level and rate.** Treating a level as a flow (or vice versa) is a conceptual error worth 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not stating Euler formula explicitly.** Examiners require the boxed update equation before plugging numbers.
> 4. **Skipping the steady-state comment in part (b).** Even when the question awards marks for "comment," students often ignore it — losing up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Using wrong sign convention.** Net rate = IN − OUT. Mixing the order is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Level Variable** = *accumulator / stock / state*; units are pure quantity; has memory; cannot jump.
- **Rate Variable** = *flow / activity*; units are quantity per time; can change instantly; drives levels.
- **Differential backbone:** $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$.
- **Integral form:** $L(t) = L(t_0) + \displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} R_{\text{net}}(\tau)\,d\tau$.
- **Euler update (discrete simulation):** $L_{k+1} = L_k + \Delta t \cdot R_{\text{net},k}$.
- **Trapezoidal update (more accurate):** $L_{k+1} = L_k + \dfrac{\Delta t}{2}(R_k + R_{k+1})$.
- **Auxiliary Variables** are *algebraic intermediates* used to compute rates from levels.
- **Constants / Parameters** supply fixed inputs (e.g., pipe diameters, interest rates).
- **Stock-Flow diagram convention:** *rectangles* for levels, *pipes with valves* for rates.
- **Bathtub analogy** is the universally accepted intuition — always use it in 7-mark answers.
- **Dimensional consistency** between $L$, $R$, and $\Delta t$ is a frequent KTU checker.
- **Steady state** occurs when $R_{\text{in}} = R_{\text{out}}$, making $dL/dt = 0$ and $L$ constant.
- **Forrester (MIT)** is the originator of System Dynamics and the Stock-Flow notation — credited in KTU textbook references.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Delays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Discrete Event System Simulation (DESS)** and **GPSS (General Purpose Simulation System)**, a **Delay** is a controlled, time-consuming operation during which a transaction (or entity) remains *inactive* in a facility or queue, holding resources for a specified duration before being released to the next block in the model. 

Formally, a delay is defined as the time interval between an entity's *arrival* at a service facility and its *departure* from that facility. Mathematically, if $t_{arrival}$ is the entry time and $t_{departure}$ is the exit time, then:

$$D = t_{departure} - t_{arrival}$$

The delay duration $D$ is sampled from a probability distribution function $F(d)$ that characterizes the stochastic nature of real-world service or processing times.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Module 3 (GPSS World and Simulation of Simple Systems), "Delays" refers specifically to the **ADVANCE block** and the associated **stochastic service-time distributions** that govern how long transactions are held in facilities.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **customer standing in a queue at a bank cashier**. The moment they reach the counter, the *service* begins, and the customer is "delayed" — they cannot leave until the cashier finishes. Whether the service takes 30 seconds (a simple withdrawal) or 5 minutes (a complex loan form), that time spent at the counter is the **delay**.

In a simulation model, the customer is a **TRANSACTION**, the cashier is a **FACILITY**, and the duration of service is a **DELAY**. The simulation clock simply jumps forward by the delay duration while the customer is "held" at the counter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A delay does NOT change the value of system parameters. It simply consumes simulated time. The simulation engine fast-forwards the clock through the delay interval, and only upon expiry does the transaction proceed to its next logical step.

## 1.3 Classification of Delays

Delays in simulation are broadly classified into two categories:

| Type | Symbol | Behavior | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic Delay** | $D = C$ (constant) | Fixed, known time | Traffic light stays red for exactly 30 s |
| **Stochastic (Probabilistic) Delay** | $D \sim F(d)$ | Random, drawn from a distribution | Customer service time at a bank |

**Standard delay-generation metric:** The **mean inter-arrival time** and **mean service time** are expressed in *time units* and are sampled using the system's **random number generator (RNG)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the ADVANCE Block (GPSS)

In **GPSS World**, the `ADVANCE` block is the sole mechanism for introducing a delay. Its syntax is:

```
ADVANCE    A, B
```

Where:
- **Operand A (Mean Delay):** The average time the transaction is held.
- **Operand B (Half-Range / Modifier):** An optional uniform spread around A.

### Operational Step-by-Step Logic

1. A transaction enters the `ADVANCE` block at simulated time $t$.
2. The simulator draws a uniform random number $U \in [0, 1]$.
3. The actual delay $D$ is computed:
   - If **B is omitted** (deterministic): $D = A$.
   - If **B is present** (uniform): $D = A - B + 2 \cdot B \cdot U$.
4. The transaction is scheduled to leave the block at time $t + D$.
5. The simulation clock is advanced, and all concurrent block operations proceed in parallel.
6. When $t + D$ is reached, the transaction moves to the *Next Sequential Block*.

## 2.2 Stochastic Distributions Used for Delays

In real-world simulations, delays are rarely uniform. Engineers model them using appropriate probability distributions that match observed empirical data.

### (a) Uniform Distribution
Used when all outcomes in an interval $[a, b]$ are equally likely.

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{b-a}, \quad a \le x \le b$$

### (b) Exponential Distribution
Used for modeling **memoryless** service processes such as inter-arrival times in Poisson processes (e.g., M/M/1 queues).

$$f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0$$

### (c) Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
Used for processes where the mean dominates and small deviations are common (e.g., machining time).

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

### (d) Triangular Distribution
Used when min, mode, and max service times are known from field data.

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2(x-a)}{(b-a)(c-a)}, & a \le x \le c \\[6pt] \dfrac{2(b-x)}{(b-a)(b-c)}, & c < x \le b \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Random-Delay Generation

Real systems are **non-deterministic**. A bank teller does not serve every customer in exactly 3 minutes. If we use a *fixed* delay in simulation, we get a **biased, overly-optimistic** model. By drawing delays from a probability distribution, we capture:

- **Variability** in real-world service times.
- **Queue build-up** under high load.
- **Tail effects** where a few extreme delays create bottlenecks.
- Accurate **confidence intervals** for performance metrics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Parameters / Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic Delay** | $D = t_{departure} - t_{arrival}$ | Time units (s, min, hr) | Foundation |
| **GPSS Uniform Delay** | $D = A - B + 2 B U$ | $U \sim U(0,1)$ | `ADVANCE A,B` block |
| **GPSS Deterministic** | $D = A$ | Constant | `ADVANCE A` block |
| **Uniform PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $a, b$ are bounds | Bounded service time |
| **Uniform Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | Time | Average delay |
| **Uniform Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | Time² | Spread of delay |
| **Exponential PDF** | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $\lambda > 0$ (rate) | Memoryless process |
| **Exponential Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Time | Average inter-arrival |
| **Inverse Transform (Exponential)** | $D = -\dfrac{\ln(1-U)}{\lambda}$ | $U \sim U(0,1)$ | Sample exponential delay |
| **Normal PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\mu, \sigma$ | Central tendency |
| **Triangular Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{a+m+b}{3}$ | min, mode, max | Empirical modeling |
| **M/M/1 Utilization** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Queueing stability |
| **M/M/1 Avg Delay in Queue** | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Time | Performance metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Notation Rule:** In any markdown table, all absolute value bars are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking table syntax (e.g., $\vert x - \mu \vert$ instead of $\vert x - \mu \vert$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Delay Application |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Machine cycle time, conveyor transfer time |
| **Telecommunications** | Packet transmission delay, propagation delay |
| **Healthcare** | Patient waiting time, surgery duration |
| **Logistics** | Truck loading/unloading time, customs clearance |
| **Computer Networks** | Router queueing delay, processing delay |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Uniform Random Variable to Exponential Delay

A **GPSS engine** internally generates *uniformly distributed* random numbers $U \sim U(0,1)$. To produce a delay drawn from an **exponential distribution** (often used for inter-arrival and service times), we use the **Inverse Transform Method**.

### Step 1 — Start with the CDF of the Exponential Distribution

$$F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0$$

### Step 2 — Equate CDF to a Uniform Random Variate

We set $U = F(x)$, where $U \in [0, 1]$ is the uniform random number.

$$U = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the Exponential Term

Subtract 1 from both sides:

$$U - 1 = -e^{-\lambda x}$$

Multiply by $-1$:

$$1 - U = e^{-\lambda x}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Natural Logarithm to Both Sides

Take $\ln$ of both sides (logarithm is a strictly monotonic function for $x > 0$):

$$\ln(1 - U) = -\lambda x$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $x$ (the desired delay)

$$x = -\frac{\ln(1 - U)}{\lambda}$$

This is the **inverse transform** formula. The final expression for the exponential delay is:

$$D = -\frac{\ln(1 - U)}{\lambda}$$

**Conversion logic note:** Since $U$ and $1 - U$ both have identical uniform distributions on $[0, 1]$, the formula is often written as $D = -\ln(U)/\lambda$ for computational simplicity.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Sampling an Exponential Delay

**Problem:** Suppose inter-arrival times of customers at a call center follow an exponential distribution with mean $\mu = 5$ minutes. Compute the actual delay value when the random number drawn is $U = 0.382$.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1 — Identify the rate parameter $\lambda$:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ per minute}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the inverse transform formula:**

$$D = -\frac{\ln(1 - U)}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $U = 0.382$:**

$$D = -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.382)}{0.2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the argument of the logarithm:**

$$1 - 0.382 = 0.618$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(0.618) \approx -0.4809$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back and divide:**

$$D = -\frac{(-0.4809)}{0.2} = \frac{0.4809}{0.2} = 2.4045 \text{ minutes}$$

**Final Answer:** The sampled delay is approximately **2.40 minutes**.

## 3.3 Worked Example: GPSS Uniform ADVANCE Block

**Problem:** In a GPSS model, a `SEIZE` block grabs a machine, followed by `ADVANCE 12,4`. Compute three sampled delay values for random numbers $U_1 = 0.15$, $U_2 = 0.50$, $U_3 = 0.92$.

**Formula for GPSS uniform ADVANCE:**

$$D = A - B + 2 \cdot B \cdot U = 12 - 4 + 2 \cdot 4 \cdot U = 8 + 8U$$

**Step-by-step evaluation matrix:**

| Trial | $U_i$ | Computation | Resulting Delay $D$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.15 | $8 + 8(0.15) = 8 + 1.20$ | **9.20** |
| 2 | 0.50 | $8 + 8(0.50) = 8 + 4.00$ | **12.00** |
| 3 | 0.92 | $8 + 8(0.92) = 8 + 7.36$ | **15.36** |

**Verification of bounds:**
- When $U = 0$: $D = 8$ (lower bound, $A - B$).
- When $U = 1$: $D = 16$ (upper bound, $A + B$).
- The range is correctly $[8, 16]$ minutes. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Distribution

| Empirical Observation in Real System | Recommended Distribution | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| All values in a bounded interval equally likely | **Uniform** | Maximum entropy under known bounds |
| Process has no memory (e.g., radioactive decay) | **Exponential** | Markov / memoryless property |
| Mean dominates, small symmetric deviations | **Normal** | Central Limit Theorem applies |
| Min, mode, max are known from field data | **Triangular** | Simple empirical fit |
| Most delays are small, few are extreme | **Log-normal / Pareto** | Heavy-tailed behavior |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Delay-Generation Flow in GPSS

The following block diagram captures the GPSS simulation flow when a transaction encounters a delay:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Enters SEIZE Block] --> B[Facility Captured]
    B --> C[Transaction Reaches ADVANCE Block]
    C --> D{Is Operand B Present?}
    D -- No --> E[Deterministic Delay D equals A]
    D -- Yes --> F[Draw Uniform Random U]
    F --> G[Compute D equals A minus B plus 2 B U]
    E --> H[Transaction Scheduled to Leave at t plus D]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Simulation Clock Advances]
    I --> J[Transaction Released to RELEASE Block]
    J --> K[Next Sequential Block]
```

## 4.2 Architecture: Inverse Transform Method for Delay Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RNG[Random Number Generator] --> U[Uniform U in 0 1]
    U --> CDF[CDF F of Target Distribution]
    CDF --> INV[Apply Inverse F inverse]
    INV --> D[Delay Value D]
    D --> SIM[Insert into Simulation Clock]
    SIM --> NEXT[Advance Transaction]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Discrete Event Simulation with Delays

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Phase]
        S1[Statistical Data Collection]
        S2[Distribution Fitting]
        S3[Parameter Estimation]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Simulation Engine]
        E1[Clock Manager]
        E2[Transaction Manager]
        E3[Delay Generator ADVANCE]
        E4[Random Number Stream]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Phase]
        O1[Queue Length Statistics]
        O2[Average Delay in System]
        O3[Utilization Metrics]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 --> E3
    E4 --> E3
    E1 --> E3
    E2 --> E1
    E3 --> O1
    E3 --> O2
    E3 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **Delay Generator (ADVANCE)** is the bridge between the statistical inputs (distribution + parameters) and the simulation outputs (queue length, waiting time, utilization). Every delay sampled propagates through the clock manager and ultimately shapes the performance metrics reported by the model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a "Delay" in the context of GPSS system simulation. List the two types of delays supported by the `ADVANCE` block.**

**Model Answer:**

A **delay** in GPSS is the time interval during which a transaction is held in a block (typically `ADVANCE`) while the simulation clock advances. The transaction is *inactive* for the duration of the delay and is released automatically when the delay expires.

The two types of delays supported by the `ADVANCE` block are:

1. **Deterministic Delay** — Specified by a single operand $A$, so $D = A$.
2. **Stochastic (Uniform) Delay** — Specified by two operands $A$ and $B$, so the delay is uniformly distributed in the range $[A - B, A + B]$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: What is the purpose of the Uniform Random Number Generator (URNG) in modeling delays? Give its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer:**

The **URNG** is the foundational engine for producing pseudo-random numbers $U \in [0, 1]$ that are subsequently transformed (via inverse transform, convolution, or acceptance-rejection) into samples from any desired delay distribution such as exponential, normal, or triangular.

**Mathematical form of the uniform PDF:**

$$f(u) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le u \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The corresponding CDF is $F(u) = u$, and the mean is $\mu = 0.5$, with variance $\sigma^2 = 1/12$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the operational mechanism of the `ADVANCE` block in GPSS. Discuss the role of Operands A and B. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-server queuing system has exponential inter-arrival times with mean **6 minutes** and exponential service times with mean **4 minutes**. Using the inverse transform method, compute **two sample delays** for arrivals when the random numbers drawn are $U_1 = 0.435$ and $U_2 = 0.721$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The `ADVANCE` block holds a transaction for a specified time and then releases it to the next block.

**Operational mechanism (refer to Section 4.1 flowchart):**

1. A transaction enters the `ADVANCE A, B` block at simulated clock time $t$.
2. The simulator checks whether **Operand B** is present:
   - If **B is absent** (only A is given), the delay is **deterministic**: $D = A$.
   - If **B is present**, the simulator draws a uniform random number $U$.
3. The actual delay is computed as:

$$D = A - B + 2 \cdot B \cdot U$$

4. The transaction's *scheduled departure time* is set to $t + D$.
5. The clock advances; the transaction becomes a *future event* and waits.
6. When clock reaches $t + D$, the transaction exits the block and proceeds to the *Next Sequential Block*.

**Role of operands:**

- **Operand A** specifies the *mean* (or central value) of the delay.
- **Operand B** specifies the *half-range* of uniform variation around the mean. When B = 0, the delay becomes deterministic.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given data:**

- Inter-arrival mean $\mu_{IA} = 6$ min, so arrival rate $\lambda_{IA} = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$ per min.
- Service mean $\mu_S = 4$ min, so service rate $\mu = 1/4 = 0.25$ per min.

**Step 1 — Apply inverse transform for exponential:**

$$D = -\frac{\ln(1 - U)}{\lambda}$$

**Sample 1: For inter-arrival delay with $U_1 = 0.435$:**

$$D_1 = -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.435)}{1/6} = -6 \cdot \ln(0.565)$$

$$\ln(0.565) \approx -0.5709 \implies D_1 = -6 \cdot (-0.5709) = 3.425 \text{ min}$$

**Sample 2: For inter-arrival delay with $U_2 = 0.721$:**

$$D_2 = -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.721)}{1/6} = -6 \cdot \ln(0.279)$$

$$\ln(0.279) \approx -1.2763 \implies D_2 = -6 \cdot (-1.2763) = 7.658 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answers:**

- $D_1 \approx 3.43$ minutes.
- $D_2 \approx 7.66$ minutes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Differentiate between deterministic and stochastic delays. Explain with an example how the **Exponential distribution** is used to model inter-arrival delays. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank has a single ATM. Customer inter-arrivals follow a uniform distribution in $[2, 8]$ minutes, and service times follow a triangular distribution with parameters $a = 1$, $m = 3$, $b = 6$ minutes. Compute the **mean inter-arrival delay** and the **mean service delay**. Verify whether the system is stable using the M/M/1 stability criterion analogy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Difference between deterministic and stochastic delays:**

| Parameter | Deterministic Delay | Stochastic Delay |
|---|---|---|
| Value | Fixed, single value | Random, drawn from a distribution |
| Variability | Zero variance | Non-zero variance |
| Realism | Low (overly idealized) | High (matches real systems) |
| GPSS Form | `ADVANCE A` | `ADVANCE A, B` |
| Example | Traffic signal fixed 30 s | Customer service time at a bank |

**Modeling inter-arrival delays with Exponential distribution:**

In a Poisson arrival process, inter-arrival times are exponentially distributed with parameter $\lambda$ (the arrival rate). The PDF is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

The mean inter-arrival time is $\mu = 1/\lambda$, and the CDF is $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Sampling via inverse transform:**

$$D_{arrival} = -\frac{\ln(1 - U)}{\lambda}$$

**Example:** If customers arrive at a store at a rate of $\lambda = 10$ per hour, then mean inter-arrival delay is $\mu = 1/10$ hour $= 6$ minutes. To generate a sample delay, draw $U = 0.5$:

$$D = -\frac{\ln(0.5)}{10} = \frac{0.6931}{10} = 0.06931 \text{ hours} \approx 4.16 \text{ min}$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Mean of uniform inter-arrival distribution $[2, 8]$:**

$$\mu_{IA} = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{2 + 8}{2} = 5 \text{ min}$$

**Arrival rate:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\mu_{IA}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ per min}$$

**Step 2 — Mean of triangular service distribution $(a=1, m=3, b=6)$:**

$$\mu_S = \frac{a + m + b}{3} = \frac{1 + 3 + 6}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33 \text{ min}$$

**Service rate:**

$$\mu = \frac{1}{\mu_S} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3 \text{ per min}$$

**Step 3 — Stability check (treating it as an M/M/1 analogue):**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{0.2}{0.3} = 0.6667$$

Since $\rho < 1$, the system is **stable** and the queue will not grow unbounded.

**Step 4 — Average delay in queue (M/M/1 formula, as analytical benchmark):**

$$W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{0.6667}{0.3 - 0.2} = \frac{0.6667}{0.1} = 6.667 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answers:**

- Mean inter-arrival delay = **5 min**.
- Mean service delay ≈ **3.33 min**.
- Utilization $\rho = 0.667 < 1 \Rightarrow$ **Stable system**.
- Average delay in queue ≈ **6.67 min**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the operand $B$ in `ADVANCE A, B` as the *upper bound*. It is the **half-range**, not the upper bound. The actual range is $[A - B, A + B]$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the formula $D = -\ln(U) / \lambda$ and writing $U$ instead of $(1 - U)$. While mathematically equivalent for a fresh uniform stream, students must show the derivation starting from $U = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ to earn full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In triangular distribution, the **mode** is denoted $m$ or $c$, *not* the mean. Mean of a triangular distribution is $(a + m + b)/3$, not $m$.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to invert the rate parameter. If the *mean* is given, then $\lambda = 1/\mu$, not $\lambda = \mu$.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** When asked for "stability," students often compute $\rho$ correctly but fail to state the criterion ($\rho < 1$) explicitly. Always state the rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Delay** = elapsed time a transaction is held in a simulation block.
- The `ADVANCE` block is the **only** block in GPSS that introduces a delay.
- `ADVANCE A` → **deterministic delay** of $A$ units.
- `ADVANCE A, B` → **uniform stochastic delay** in range $[A - B, A + B]$.
- Formula: $D = A - B + 2BU$ where $U \sim U(0,1)$.
- The **Inverse Transform Method** is used to convert uniform $U$ into exponentially distributed delays: $D = -\ln(1 - U)/\lambda$.
- **Exponential** is used for memoryless processes (M/M/1 queues, Poisson arrivals).
- **Normal** is used for processes with central tendency and symmetric variation.
- **Triangular** is used for empirical modeling with known min, mode, and max.
- **Stability** of a single-server queue requires $\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$.
- Always state the **distribution** assumed before drawing samples — partial credit is heavily penalized in KTU valuation if the assumption is implicit.
- Mean inter-arrival = $1/\lambda$ in exponential context; never confuse $\lambda$ and $\mu$ in the same equation.
- Uniform mean = $(a + b)/2$; uniform variance = $(b - a)^2 / 12$.
- For GPSS `ADVANCE`, $B$ is **half-range**, *not* the upper bound.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Smoothing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Information Smoothing # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Information Smoothing** is a class of time-series analysis techniques used in system simulation to filter out random fluctuations (noise) from observed data, thereby revealing the underlying systematic pattern — such as **level, trend, and seasonality** — and producing reliable forecasts. In the formal theory of discrete-event and continuous simulation, a simulation output stream $Y_1, Y_2, Y_3, \dots, Y_t$ is regarded as a **noisy realization of a latent signal**. Information Smoothing (often synonymously used with **Exponential Smoothing** in the operations-research literature by Makridakis, Wheelwright, & Hyndman) is the family of recursive filters that recursively update a smoothed estimate $S_t$ such that: $$S_t = f(Y_t, S_{t-1}, \theta)$$ where $\theta$ is the smoothing constant. The objective is to **minimize the expected squared forecast error** $E[(Y_{t+1} - F_{t+1})^2]$. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — "The Water Tank Float":** Imagine a water tank whose level is fluctuating wildly due to waves (noise). An *information smoother* is like the calm average of the water height you would feel if you had a damped float — it does not react to every splash, but responds to a sustained change (the true water level). Similarly, a forecaster does not blindly follow yesterday's sales spike; he/she smooths past data to reveal the true demand trend. Another analogy: **The Stylus on a Vinyl Record.** As the stylus plays, the grooves contain both the music (signal) and surface dust (noise). A *smoother* is the equivalent of a low-pass filter that lets the bass and rhythm pass through while suppressing high-frequency pops. The smoother, like a good analyst, is **adaptive** — it changes the amount of damping (smoothing constant $\alpha$) depending on the level of disturbance. ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Information Smoothing Techniques | **#** | **Technique** | **Suitable When** | **Parameters** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Naïve Forecast | Stationary, no pattern | None | | 2 | Simple / Single Moving Average (MA) | Stationary random walk | Window $n$ | | 3 | Weighted Moving Average (WMA) | Recent observations matter more | Weights $w_i$ | | 4 | Single Exponential Smoothing (SES) | Stationary, no trend | $\alpha$ | | 5 | Double Exponential Smoothing (DES / Holt's) | Linear trend present | $\alpha, \beta$ | | 6 | Triple Exponential Smoothing (TES / Holt–Winters) | Trend + Seasonality | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ | | 7 | Adaptive Smoothing | Time-varying noise level | $\alpha(t)$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Modules under "Information Smoothing" in *OEIET724 System Simulation* focus primarily on **Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing (Single, Double, Holt–Winters)**, and **forecast error metrics (MAD, MSE, MAPE, Tracking Signal)**. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Do We Need Smoothing? — The "Why" Behind It - **Noise Suppression:** Real-world data and simulation outputs are corrupted by stochastic disturbances. - **Pattern Extraction:** Reveals the *signal* (level, trend, seasonality) hidden in data. - **Forecasting:** Generates reliable predictions $F_{t+m}$ for $m$ periods ahead. - **Decision-Support:** Critical for inventory control, capacity planning, simulation validation. ## 2.2 The Core Logic of Exponential Smoothing The defining recursion of **Single Exponential Smoothing (SES)** is derived from a weighted sum of all past observations, where weights decay geometrically: $$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Geometric Decay Property:** Expanding the recursion gives $S_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i Y_{t-i}$. Older observations receive exponentially smaller weights — this is the mathematical "memory" of the smoother. - $\alpha \to 1$: Smoother reacts to recent changes (low memory, high responsiveness). - $\alpha \to 0$: Smoother responds slowly, gives more weight to the past (high memory, low responsiveness). - **Optimal** $\alpha$ minimizes the in-sample **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** or **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Holt–Winters (Triple ES) The **Holt–Winters method** is the most general and is the heart of KTU-level questions. It has three coupled recursions: 1. **Level equation** $L_t$: weighted blend of the deseasonalized observation and the previous level-trend pair. 2. **Trend equation** $T_t$: weighted blend of the change in level and the previous trend. 3. **Seasonal equation** $I_t$: weighted blend of the seasonal residual and the previous seasonal index from the same season a full cycle ago. > **How to use it:** Initialize $L_0, T_0$ and the first $p$ seasonal indices. For each new $Y_t$, compute $(L_t, T_t, I_t)$, then forecast $m$ periods ahead using the appropriate level + trend + seasonal combination. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📊 > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **master cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 board exams. All notations follow Makridakis & Hyndman conventions. | **Smoothing Model** | **Core Recursion(s)** | **Forecast Equation** | **Initial Conditions** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Simple MA (n-period)** | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} Y_{t-i}$ | $F_{t+1}$ as above | Use first $n$ values | | **Weighted MA** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} w_i Y_{t-i}$ | Same | $\sum w_i = 1$ | | **Single ES (SES)** | $S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$ | $F_{t+1} = S_t$ | $S_0 = Y_1$ | | **Double ES (Holt)** | $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$ | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m\,T_t$ | $L_0 = Y_1$
$T_0 = Y_2 - Y_1$ | | **Triple ES (Holt–Winters, Additive)** | $L_t = \alpha(Y_t - I_{t-p}) + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$
$I_t = \gamma(Y_t - L_t) + (1-\gamma)I_{t-p}$ | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m\,T_t + I_{t-p+m}$ | $L_0, T_0$ from trend; $I_0$ from first $p$ periods | | **Triple ES (Multiplicative)** | $L_t = \alpha(Y_t / I_{t-p}) + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$
$I_t = \gamma(Y_t / L_t) + (1-\gamma)I_{t-p}$ | $F_{t+m} = (L_t + m\,T_t) \cdot I_{t-p+m}$ | Same as additive | ### Forecast Error / Performance Metrics | **Metric** | **Formula** | **Purpose** | **Units** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Error** | $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ | Signed residual | Same as $Y$ | | **MAD** | $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t \vert$ | Average magnitude of error | Same as $Y$ | | **MSE** | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2$ | Penalizes large errors | $(unit)^2$ | | **MAPE** | $MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{e_t}{Y_t} \right\vert$ | Scale-independent % error | Percent | | **Tracking Signal** | $TS = \frac{\sum e_t}{MAD}$ | Detects bias in forecast | Dimensionless | | **Cumulative Error** | $E_t = \sum e_i$ | Sum of all errors | Same as $Y$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb for Tracking Signal (KTU 2024):** If $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ (or within $\pm 3$ to $\pm 4$ MAD), the model is acceptable. If $\vert TS \vert > 4$, the forecasting model is **biased** and must be revised. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications 🌍 - **Inventory & Supply-Chain Simulation:** Demand smoothing in a manufacturing SIMULINK/ARENA model. - **Network Traffic Forecasting:** Predicting packet arrival rates. - **Weather & Climate Modeling:** Filtering sensor noise. - **Econometric & Financial Engineering:** Asset price prediction. - **Digital Signal Processing:** Direct analog of single-pole IIR low-pass filter. # 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Single Exponential Smoothing from the Weighted Average Starting from the definition: $S_t$ is a weighted sum of all past observations, with geometrically decaying weights. **Step 1:** Begin with the recursive definition. $$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $S_{t-1}$ with its own recursion. $$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) \left[ \alpha Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) S_{t-2} \right]$$ **Step 3:** Continue the substitution iteratively to obtain the infinite-series expansion. $$S_t = \alpha Y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) Y_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 Y_{t-2} + \dots + \alpha(1-\alpha)^{n} Y_{t-n} + \lim_{n\to\infty}(1-\alpha)^{n+1} S_{0}$$ **Step 4:** Since $0 < \alpha < 1$, the term $(1-\alpha)^{n+1} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Hence, the initial value $S_0$ becomes irrelevant for large $t$. $$\boxed{S_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i\, Y_{t-i}}$$ **Interpretation:** Each past observation $Y_{t-i}$ carries weight $\alpha(1-\alpha)^i$, which sums to unity (since $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i = 1$). This proves that SES is a **proper weighted average** of all past data. ## 3.2 Derivation of Double Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Method) Holt's method is built to handle **linear trend** $Y_t = a + b t + \epsilon_t$. **Step 1:** Recognize that SES assumes no trend, so it consistently **lags behind** a trended series. Holt introduces *two* recursions — one for level $L_t$ and one for trend $T_t$. $$L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$ **Step 2:** The new level is a weighted average of the **observed level** $Y_t$ and the **predicted level** $L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}$. **Step 3:** The new trend is a weighted average of the **change in level** $L_t - L_{t-1}$ and the **previous trend** $T_{t-1}$. $$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$$ **Step 4:** The $m$-period ahead forecast adds $m$ multiples of the current trend to the current level. $$F_{t+m} = L_t + m\, T_t$$ > [!NOTE] > **Justification for the Forecast:** Substituting the recursions repeatedly shows that the forecast at horizon $m$ extrapolates the latest straight-line fit $(L_t, T_t)$, which is the best linear projection of the trended series. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Single Exponential Smoothing **Problem:** Given the time series $Y_t = \{100, 110, 120, 130, 140\}$ for $t = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$, compute the SES with $\alpha = 0.3$ and initial value $S_0 = Y_1 = 100$. Also compute the **MAD**. **Step 1 — Compute the smoothed values** $S_t$ using $S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$. $$S_1 = 0.3(100) + 0.7(100) = 100.00$$ $$S_2 = 0.3(110) + 0.7(100) = 33.00 + 70.00 = 103.00$$ $$S_3 = 0.3(120) + 0.7(103) = 36.00 + 72.10 = 108.10$$ $$S_4 = 0.3(130) + 0.7(108.1) = 39.00 + 75.67 = 114.67$$ $$S_5 = 0.3(140) + 0.7(114.67) = 42.00 + 80.27 = 122.27$$ **Step 2 — Compute the one-step-ahead forecasts** $F_{t+1} = S_t$. $$F_2 = 100.00, \quad F_3 = 103.00, \quad F_4 = 108.10, \quad F_5 = 114.67, \quad F_6 = 122.27$$ **Step 3 — Compute the forecast errors** $e_t = Y_t - F_t$. $$e_2 = 110 - 100 = 10.00$$ $$e_3 = 120 - 103 = 17.00$$ $$e_4 = 130 - 108.10 = 21.90$$ $$e_5 = 140 - 114.67 = 25.33$$ **Step 4 — Compute the MAD** (using $n = 4$ observed errors). $$MAD = \frac{1}{4}(10.00 + 17.00 + 21.90 + 25.33) = \frac{74.23}{4} = 18.56$$ **Step 5 — Compute the MSE.** $$MSE = \frac{1}{4}\left(10^2 + 17^2 + 21.9^2 + 25.33^2\right) = \frac{1722.19}{4} \approx 430.55$$ **Step 6 — Compute the MAPE.** $$MAPE = \frac{100}{4}\left(\frac{10}{110} + \frac{17}{120} + \frac{21.9}{130} + \frac{25.33}{140}\right)$$ $$MAPE = 25 \times (0.0909 + 0.1417 + 0.1685 + 0.1810) = 25 \times 0.5821 = 14.55\%$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Double ES (Holt's Method) **Problem:** Using the same data, apply Holt's method with $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$. Initialise $L_1 = Y_1 = 100$, $T_1 = Y_2 - Y_1 = 10$. Compute the forecasts $F_2, F_3, F_4, F_5$. **Step 1:** For $t = 1$, we have $L_1 = 100$ and $T_1 = 10$. **Step 2 — Compute $L_2, T_2$:** $$L_2 = 0.4(110) + 0.6(100 + 10) = 44.0 + 66.0 = 110.00$$ $$T_2 = 0.3(110 - 100) + 0.7(10) = 3.0 + 7.0 = 10.00$$ **Step 3 — Compute $L_3, T_3$:** $$L_3 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(110 + 10) = 48.0 + 72.0 = 120.00$$ $$T_3 = 0.3(120 - 110) + 0.7(10) = 3.0 + 7.0 = 10.00$$ **Step 4 — Compute $L_4, T_4$:** $$L_4 = 0.4(130) + 0.6(120 + 10) = 52.0 + 78.0 = 130.00$$ $$T_4 = 0.3(130 - 120) + 0.7(10) = 3.0 + 7.0 = 10.00$$ **Step 5 — Compute $L_5, T_5$:** $$L_5 = 0.4(140) + 0.6(130 + 10) = 56.0 + 84.0 = 140.00$$ $$T_5 = 0.3(140 - 130) + 0.7(10) = 3.0 + 7.0 = 10.00$$ **Step 6 — Compute the forecasts $F_{t+1} = L_t + T_t$:** $$F_2 = 110, \quad F_3 = 120, \quad F_4 = 130, \quad F_5 = 140$$ **Step 7 — Forecast for 2 periods ahead at $t = 5$:** $$F_{5+2} = L_5 + 2 \cdot T_5 = 140 + 20 = 160$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Notice how Holt's method **perfectly tracks** the perfect linear trend $Y_t = 90 + 10 t$ (it absorbs the trend completely), whereas SES produced persistent errors because it was structurally incapable of following the linear growth. ## 3.5 Selection Matrix — When to Use What | **Data Pattern** | **Recommended Smoother** | **Number of Parameters** | |:---|:---|:---:| | Constant level, white noise | Single ES or MA | **1** | | Linear trend, no seasonality | Double ES (Holt) | **2** | | Trend + Seasonality (additive) | Triple ES (Holt–Winters Additive) | **3** | | Trend + Seasonality (multiplicative) | Triple ES (Holt–Winters Multiplicative) | **3** | | Time-varying noise level | Adaptive Smoothing | **2+** | | Non-linear, complex pattern | ARIMA / Neural Net (out of scope) | — | # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single Exponential Smoothing Computational Flow The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the recursive information flow of SES, showing how the previous smoothed value $S_{t-1}$ and the current observation $Y_t$ are blended to produce the next smoothed value $S_t$ and the forecast $F_{t+1}$. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Observed Value Y sub t] --> C[Multiplier alpha] A1[Previous Smoothed Value S sub t minus 1] --> D[Multiplier 1 minus alpha] C --> E[Summing Junction] D --> E E --> F[New Smoothed Value S sub t] F --> G[Forecast Block F sub t plus 1 equals S sub t] F --> A1 G --> H[Error Calculator e sub t plus 1 equals Y sub t plus 1 minus F sub t plus 1] H --> I[MAD MSE MAPE Computation Module] I --> J[Tracking Signal Output] J --> K{Is TS within plus or minus 4} K -- Yes --> L[Model Acceptable] K -- No --> M[Revise Smoothing Constants alpha beta gamma] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The closed loop from $S_t$ back to $S_{t-1}$ is the defining **recursion** of exponential smoothing. The feedback to the error block allows **adaptive** tuning of $\alpha$ via the tracking-signal logic. ## 4.2 Comparative Architecture — Smoothing Family Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Information Smoothing Family] --> A1[Moving Average] R --> A2[Exponential Smoothing] A1 --> B1[Simple MA] A1 --> B2[Weighted MA] A1 --> B3[Double MA] A2 --> C1[Single ES] A2 --> C2[Double ES Holt] A2 --> C3[Triple ES Holt Winters] C3 --> D1[Additive Seasonality] C3 --> D2[Multiplicative Seasonality] C1 --> E1[Stationary Series] C2 --> E2[Linear Trend Series] C3 --> E3[Trend plus Seasonality Series] ``` ## 4.3 Signal Flow — Holt–Winters Triple Exponential Smoothing ```mermaid flowchart LR Y[Input Y sub t] --> S1[Subtract Seasonal Index I sub t minus p] S1 --> M1[Multiply by alpha] PrevL[L sub t minus 1 plus T sub t minus 1] --> M2[Multiply by 1 minus alpha] M1 --> Sum1[Sum to produce L sub t] M2 --> Sum1 Sum1 --> DL[Compute L sub t minus L sub t minus 1] DL --> M3[Multiply by beta] PrevT[T sub t minus 1] --> M4[Multiply by 1 minus beta] M3 --> Sum2[Sum to produce T sub t] M4 --> Sum2 Sum1 --> SI[Subtract L sub t from Y sub t] Y --> SI SI --> M5[Multiply by gamma] PrevI[I sub t minus p] --> M6[Multiply by 1 minus gamma] M5 --> Sum3[Sum to produce I sub t] M6 --> Sum3 Sum1 --> FC[Forecast F sub t plus m] Sum2 --> FC Sum3 --> FC FC --> OUT[Output F sub t plus m equals L sub t plus m T sub t plus I sub t minus p plus m] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three coupled feedback loops:** The diagram above reveals the three recursive loops of Holt–Winters — **level, trend, and seasonal** — that operate in parallel. The forecast stage combines all three to project the series $m$ periods ahead. # 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Information Smoothing and list its main categories used in system simulation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Information Smoothing is a class of time-series analysis techniques used to filter noise from observed data and reveal the underlying level, trend, and seasonal pattern. The main categories used in system simulation are: 1. **Moving Averages** — Simple MA, Weighted MA, Double MA. 2. **Exponential Smoothing** — Single ES (level only), Double ES / Holt's (level + trend), Triple ES / Holt–Winters (level + trend + seasonality). 3. **Adaptive Smoothing** — Time-varying smoothing constants. Each technique assigns weights to past observations to balance **responsiveness** with **stability**, with exponential methods using geometrically decaying weights. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the Tracking Signal? State its significance in evaluating a forecasting model. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Tracking Signal (TS)** is a statistical indicator that measures whether the forecast is consistently over- or under-predicting the actual values. It is defined as: $$TS = \frac{\sum e_t}{MAD} = \frac{\text{Cumulative Error (RSFE)}}{\text{Mean Absolute Deviation}}$$ **Significance:** - If $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ (rule of thumb), the model is **acceptable** — the errors are random and unbiased. - If $\vert TS \vert > 4$, there is a **systematic bias** in the model and the smoothing constant $\alpha$ or the model itself must be revised. - It is widely used in **inventory control systems** to trigger automatic re-forecasting. --- ## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **Internal Choice Structure (KTU ESE Module Pattern):** Each long-answer question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. --- ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable mathematical derivation, explain Single Exponential Smoothing. Given a time series $Y = \{50, 55, 52, 60, 58, 65\}$, apply SES with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $S_0 = 50$. Compute the forecasts $F_2$ to $F_6$ and find the **MAD** and **MSE**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Tracking Signal** with formula. If the cumulative forecast error is **20** and MAD is **5**, comment on the model's accuracy. What corrective action is recommended? --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) **Step 1 — Derivation of SES:** (Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation.) The recursion $S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$, when expanded, gives the **weighted infinite series**: $$S_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i Y_{t-i}$$ This shows that the most recent observation carries weight $\alpha$, the previous one $\alpha(1-\alpha)$, and so on, with weights summing to unity. **Step 2 — Apply SES to the data with $\alpha = 0.4$, $S_0 = 50$:** $$S_1 = 0.4(50) + 0.6(50) = 50.00$$ $$S_2 = 0.4(55) + 0.6(50) = 22.00 + 30.00 = 52.00$$ $$S_3 = 0.4(52) + 0.6(52) = 52.00$$ $$S_4 = 0.4(60) + 0.6(52) = 24.00 + 31.20 = 55.20$$ $$S_5 = 0.4(58) + 0.6(55.2) = 23.20 + 33.12 = 56.32$$ $$S_6 = 0.4(65) + 0.6(56.32) = 26.00 + 33.79 = 59.79$$ **Step 3 — Forecasts $F_{t+1} = S_t$:** $$F_2 = 50.00, \quad F_3 = 52.00, \quad F_4 = 52.00, \quad F_5 = 55.20, \quad F_6 = 56.32$$ **Step 4 — Forecast errors $e_t = Y_t - F_t$:** $$e_2 = 55 - 50 = 5.00$$ $$e_3 = 52 - 52 = 0.00$$ $$e_4 = 60 - 52 = 8.00$$ $$e_5 = 58 - 55.2 = 2.80$$ $$e_6 = 65 - 56.32 = 8.68$$ **Step 5 — Compute MAD and MSE:** $$MAD = \frac{1}{5}(5.00 + 0.00 + 8.00 + 2.80 + 8.68) = \frac{24.48}{5} = 4.896$$ $$MSE = \frac{1}{5}(5^2 + 0^2 + 8^2 + 2.8^2 + 8.68^2) = \frac{194.84}{5} = 38.97$$ #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) **Tracking Signal Definition:** $$TS = \frac{\sum e_t}{MAD}$$ **Given:** Cumulative error $= 20$, $MAD = 5$. $$TS = \frac{20}{5} = 4$$ **Comment on Model Accuracy:** Since $\vert TS \vert = 4$ lies on the **upper bound** of the acceptable range ($\vert TS \vert \le 4$), the model is **marginally acceptable** but on the verge of bias detection. It is producing slightly **under-forecasts** (positive errors dominate). **Recommended Corrective Action:** - Slightly **increase** the smoothing constant $\alpha$ so that the smoother reacts more quickly to new data. - Or switch to a **trend-based** model (Holt's method) if the series is trending. - Re-estimate $\alpha$ by minimising MSE over a larger in-sample window. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to compute **all** error terms (some miss $e_1$ because $F_1$ is undefined). The correct count is $n-1$ errors when $F_{t+1} = S_t$. Also, MAD is computed using $\vert e_t \vert$ — forgetting the modulus gives wrong sign and penalized marks. --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Double Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Method)** with its three recursions. Apply Holt's method to the data $Y = \{10, 15, 20, 25, 30\}$ with $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta = 0.4$, initial values $L_1 = 10$, $T_1 = 5$. Compute the forecast for $t = 7$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Single**, **Double**, and **Triple Exponential Smoothing** in terms of their components (level, trend, seasonality) and the number of parameters. List the formula for each forecast. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) **Holt's Method Recursions:** $$L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$ $$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$$ **Forecast:** $F_{t+m} = L_t + m\,T_t$ **Step 1 — Apply to $t = 1$:** $L_1 = 10$, $T_1 = 5$. **Step 2 — Compute $L_2, T_2$:** $$L_2 = 0.5(15) + 0.5(10 + 5) = 7.5 + 7.5 = 15.00$$ $$T_2 = 0.4(15 - 10) + 0.6(5) = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.00$$ **Step 3 — Compute $L_3, T_3$:** $$L_3 = 0.5(20) + 0.5(15 + 5) = 10.0 + 10.0 = 20.00$$ $$T_3 = 0.4(20 - 15) + 0.6(5) = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.00$$ **Step 4 — Compute $L_4, T_4$:** $$L_4 = 0.5(25) + 0.5(20 + 5) = 12.5 + 12.5 = 25.00$$ $$T_4 = 0.4(25 - 20) + 0.6(5) = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.00$$ **Step 5 — Compute $L_5, T_5$:** $$L_5 = 0.5(30) + 0.5(25 + 5) = 15.0 + 15.0 = 30.00$$ $$T_5 = 0.4(30 - 25) + 0.6(5) = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.00$$ **Step 6 — Forecast for $t = 7$ (i.e., $m = 2$ steps ahead from $t = 5$):** $$F_7 = L_5 + 2 \cdot T_5 = 30.00 + 2(5.00) = 40.00$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** Holt's method **exactly tracks** the perfect linear series $Y_t = 5 + 5t$, recovering both the level and the trend perfectly because the data is noise-free and exactly linear. #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) | **Method** | **Components Handled** | **# Parameters** | **Forecast Formula** | |:---|:---|:---:|:---| | **Single ES** | Level only | 1 ($\alpha$) | $F_{t+1} = S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}$ | | **Double ES (Holt)** | Level + Trend | 2 ($\alpha, \beta$) | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m\,T_t$ | | **Triple ES (Holt–Winters)** | Level + Trend + Seasonality | 3 ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m\,T_t + I_{t-p+m}$ (additive) | **Comparison Summary:** - **SES** suits data with no systematic pattern beyond a constant level. - **DES (Holt)** adds a linear-trend term to handle directional growth. - **TES (Holt–Winters)** adds a seasonal index for cyclic patterns; the additive form works when seasonal fluctuations are roughly constant in magnitude, while multiplicative form is for proportional seasonal effects. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a) Traps:** > 1. **Initial value misuse:** Some students treat $T_1 = Y_2 - Y_1$ blindly even when the problem gives $T_1$ explicitly. Always honour the given initial values. > 2. **Forecast formula confusion:** A common error is writing $F_{t+1} = L_t$ for Holt's method, but the correct general form is $F_{t+m} = L_t + m T_t$. **Marks lost: 2.** > 3. **Missing the $m$ multiplier** for multi-step forecasts: in this question $m = 2$ for $F_7$ from $t = 5$. Forgetting the multiplier gives $F_7 = 30$ — wrong. > 4. **Confusing LHS indices:** Always verify whether the recursion is at index $t$ or $t-1$ before substituting values. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Information Smoothing** filters noise and reveals level, trend, and seasonality. - [x] **Single ES (SES)** uses one parameter $\alpha$ and suits stationary data. - [x] **Double ES (Holt's)** adds the trend component $T_t$ and uses two parameters $\alpha, \beta$. - [x] **Triple ES (Holt–Winters)** adds seasonality $I_t$ and uses $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$. - [x] **Smoothing constant** $\alpha \in (0, 1)$: higher $\alpha$ → more responsive, lower $\alpha$ → smoother. - [x] **Holt's forecast formula:** $F_{t+m} = L_t + m T_t$ (always include the $m$ multiplier for $m$-period-ahead forecasts). - [x] **MAD =** $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t \vert$ — average magnitude of error. - [x] **MSE =** $\frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2$ — penalizes large errors quadratically. - [x] **MAPE =** $\frac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{e_t}{Y_t} \right\vert$ — percentage form, scale-free. - [x] **Tracking Signal** $TS = \frac{\sum e_t}{MAD}$; acceptable range $\vert TS \vert \le 4$. - [x] **Geometric decay property** of SES: weights on past observations decay as $\alpha(1-\alpha)^i$. - [x] **Initial conditions:** $S_0 = Y_1$ for SES; $L_1 = Y_1$, $T_1 = Y_2 - Y_1$ for DES. - [x] **Selection rule:** Trend + Seasonality → Holt–Winters; Trend only → Holt; Level only → SES. - [x] **Adaptive smoothing** varies $\alpha$ over time using TS feedback to control responsiveness. - [x] **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always show the **forecast formula derivation** for Holt's/Holt–Winters, not just numerical substitution — that alone carries 3–4 marks. - [x] **Common Mistake:** Writing $F_{t+1} = L_t$ instead of $F_{t+m} = L_t + m T_t$ for Holt's method. - [x] **Convergence of SES** depends on $0 < \alpha < 1$ for the limit to exist; $\alpha = 0$ is degenerate and $\alpha = 1$ is the naïve forecast.

topics.title: Table Functions and Table Function Multipliers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Table Functions and Table Function Multipliers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In **GPSS (General Purpose Simulation System)**, a **Table Function** is a built-in discrete-valued empirical model used to represent a relationship between a single independent variable (the *argument*) and a single dependent variable (the *function result*) through a finite, ordered set of **(X, Y) coordinate pairs**. Linear interpolation is automatically performed between successive pairs, so any continuous-looking functional form can be approximated from tabulated data.

The general GPSS statement takes the form:

$$ \text{label} \; \textbf{FUNCTION} \; A, B $$

where:
- **A** is an SNA (Standard Numerical Attribute) supplying the *argument* value at run time.
- **B** specifies the *type* of function (Integer, Real, or Half-Integer) followed by the *number of (X, Y) pairs* in the table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Limit of Pairs:** A single GPSS FUNCTION block may contain **up to 250 coordinate pairs** (i.e., 500 values: 250 X-values + 250 Y-values). This is a hard upper bound often asked in 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

### 1.2 Three Permitted Function Types (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Type Code | Argument Nature | Result Nature | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RN** (Real Number) | Real-valued X | Real-valued Y | Continuous physical mappings |
| **IN** (Integer) | Integer-valued X | Integer-valued Y | Discrete-count tables |
| **HN** (Half-Integer) | Half-integer X | Half-integer Y | Server time patterns |

The X-values in the table must be **monotonically non-decreasing**; otherwise GPSS issues a runtime error.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Table Function as a "Lookup Table with Smart Guessing".**

Imagine you have a tax-rate table: *Income of ₹2.5 Lakh → 0% tax, ₹5 Lakh → 5% tax, ₹10 Lakh → 20% tax*. If someone earns ₹7.5 Lakh (a value not in your table), you don't want to fail — you want to **linearly guess** the rate, giving something close to 12.5%. That "smart guessing between entries" is exactly what a GPSS Table Function does. It is essentially a **piecewise-linear empirical model** stored compactly inside the simulator.

### 1.4 What is a Table Function *Multiplier*?

A **Table Function Multiplier** is a scaling/conditional-scaling operator that is applied to the *argument* (or, in some GPSS dialects, the *result*) of a function at the moment it is invoked through its SNA. The multiplier enables:

1. **Re-scaling the input domain** without rewriting the table (e.g., converting seconds to minutes before lookup).
2. **Dynamic switching between sub-functions** by setting the multiplier to a near-zero value (within ±0.001), which forces the function result to zero — effectively disabling that branch.
3. **Scaling the lookup output** for unit conversions or scenario analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Numerical Attribute (SNA) for accessing a function:** Use the form `FN$<label>`. To apply a multiplier, the expression becomes `FN$<label> * <multiplier>`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Table Function Resolves a Lookup

When the GPSS evaluator encounters `FN$F1` (or any function reference) during a transaction's journey through the model, the following **six sequential steps** are executed:

1. **Fetch Argument** — Read the current numeric value of the argument SNA specified in the FUNCTION statement.
2. **Range Validation** — Locate the bracketing pair $(X_i, X_{i+1})$ such that $X_i \le \text{arg} \le X_{i+1}$.
   - If `arg < X_min` or `arg > X_max`, GPSS typically extrapolates using the *boundary segment slope*.
3. **Linear Interpolation** — Compute the linearly-interpolated Y-value using the working formula (derived in §3).
4. **Multiplication by Multiplier** — If a multiplier is attached in the referencing expression, multiply the interpolated Y by that multiplier.
5. **Range Clamping** — If the multiplier satisfies $\vert M \vert < 0.001$, the engine forces the **result to zero** regardless of the interpolated value (this is the "switching" trick).
6. **Return** — Place the final numeric value into the calling storage location (or use it inline in a BLOCK operand).

### 2.2 The Core Linear-Interpolation Formula

For two adjacent tabulated points $(X_1, Y_1)$ and $(X_2, Y_2)$ bracketing an argument $A$:

$$ Y(A) \;=\; Y_1 \;+\; \dfrac{(A - X_1)\,(Y_2 - Y_1)}{(X_2 - X_1)} $$

Equivalently, using the slope $m = \dfrac{Y_2 - Y_1}{X_2 - X_1}$:

$$ Y(A) \;=\; Y_1 \;+\; m \cdot (A - X_1) $$

This is the **two-point point-slope form** of a straight line — the same equation students encounter in coordinate geometry.

### 2.3 Multiplier Semantics — The "Near-Zero Switch"

| Multiplier Value Range | Effect on Function Result | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\vert M \vert \ge 0.001$ | Normal scaled value returned | Function actively contributing |
| $\vert M \vert < 0.001$ (including exactly 0) | Result forced to **0** | Function branch disabled |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume that setting a multiplier to exactly 0 causes a *division by zero* or a runtime crash. **It does not.** It is the *intended mechanism* for conditional switching of table functions inside a simulation model.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Linear interpolation | $Y = Y_1 + \dfrac{(A - X_1)(Y_2 - Y_1)}{X_2 - X_1}$ | Argument $A$ must lie within $[X_1, X_2]$ |
| 2 | Slope of segment | $m = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X} = \dfrac{Y_2 - Y_1}{X_2 - X_1}$ | Dimensionless if X & Y share units |
| 3 | Multiplier scaling | $Y_{out} = M \cdot Y(A)$ | $M \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 4 | Zero-suppress rule | If $\vert M \vert < 0.001 \Rightarrow Y_{out} = 0$ | Switch / disable mechanism |
| 5 | Maximum pair count | $N_{pairs} \le 250$ | Per FUNCTION block |
| 6 | Monotonicity constraint | $X_{i+1} \ge X_i$ for all $i$ | Strictly required by GPSS |
| 7 | SNA accessor | `FN$<label>` | Returns current lookup |
| 8 | Number of values | $2 \times N_{pairs}$ | X-values + Y-values |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Table functions are indispensable in:

- **Manufacturing simulation:** machine cycle time as a function of part-type code.
- **Queueing models:** service time distributions converted from empirical histograms.
- **Reliability engineering:** hazard-rate functions versus operating hours.
- **Computer networks:** packet-loss probability as a function of offered load.
- **Econometric micro-simulation:** tax brackets, demand curves, and elasticities stored as sampled pairs.

The multiplier mechanism, in particular, is heavily used in **selective routing** and **mode-switching** (e.g., "if Machine A is down, route through Machine B by zeroing its function's argument multiplier").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Interpolation Formula

**Given:** Two tabulated points $(X_1, Y_1)$ and $(X_2, Y_2)$ with $X_1 < X_2$, and an arbitrary argument $A$ such that $X_1 \le A \le X_2$.

**To Prove:** $Y(A) = Y_1 + \dfrac{(A - X_1)(Y_2 - Y_1)}{X_2 - X_1}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the two-point slope (definition).**

The slope between any two points on a straight line is constant. Using the tabulated endpoints:

$$ m \;=\; \dfrac{Y_2 - Y_1}{X_2 - X_1} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the slope to the bracketing interval.**

The line passing through $(X_1, Y_1)$ with slope $m$ has the point-slope form. For any point $(A, Y(A))$ on this line:

$$ Y(A) - Y_1 \;=\; m \cdot (A - X_1) $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the slope definition from Step 1.**

$$ Y(A) - Y_1 \;=\; \left(\dfrac{Y_2 - Y_1}{X_2 - X_1}\right) \cdot (A - X_1) $$

**Step 4 — Add $Y_1$ to both sides to isolate $Y(A)$.**

$$ Y(A) \;=\; Y_1 \;+\; \dfrac{(A - X_1)(Y_2 - Y_1)}{X_2 - X_1} $$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Interpolation Walkthrough

**Problem:** A GPSS table function models *machine cycle time (sec) vs. part weight (kg)* with the following pairs: $(2, 10)$, $(4, 14)$, $(6, 17)$, $(8, 19)$.

A transaction arrives with a part weight of **5 kg**. Compute the interpolated cycle time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bracketing pair.**

Since $4 \le 5 \le 6$, we have $X_1 = 4$, $X_2 = 6$, $Y_1 = 14$, $Y_2 = 17$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the interpolation formula.**

$$ Y(5) \;=\; 14 \;+\; \dfrac{(5 - 4)(17 - 14)}{(6 - 4)} $$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator and denominator.**

$$ Y(5) \;=\; 14 \;+\; \dfrac{(1)(3)}{2} $$

$$ Y(5) \;=\; 14 \;+\; 1.5 $$

$$ Y(5) \;=\; 15.5 \; \text{seconds} $$

The cycle time for a 5 kg part is therefore **15.5 seconds**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Multiplier Application

**Problem:** Using the same function from §3.2, a designer applies a multiplier of **$M = 0.0005$** to disable this function dynamically (simulating "machine in maintenance"). What is the final reported value?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the unmultiplied function value.**

From §3.2, $Y(5) = 15.5$.

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute multiplier magnitude.**

$$ \vert M \vert \;=\; \vert 0.0005 \vert \;=\; 0.0005 $$

**Step 3 — Apply the zero-suppress rule.**

Since $0.0005 < 0.001$, the rule activates:

$$ Y_{out} \;=\; 0 $$

The function effectively returns zero, and any downstream BLOCK using `FN$<label> * 0.0005` will see a zero contribution, effectively routing all traffic away from this branch.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Multipliers

| Argument $A$ | Multiplier $M$ | $\vert M \vert \ge 0.001$? | Interpolated $Y(A)$ | Final Output |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 5.0 | 1.0 | Yes | 15.5 | 15.5 |
| 5.0 | 2.0 | Yes | 15.5 | 31.0 |
| 5.0 | 0.5 | Yes | 15.5 | 7.75 |
| 5.0 | 0.0005 | **No** | 15.5 (irrelevant) | **0** |
| 5.0 | -0.5 | Yes ($\vert M \vert = 0.5$) | 15.5 | -7.75 |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **negative multipliers with magnitude $\ge 0.001$** still pass through — they simply negate the function value, which can model *discounts* or *offsetting effects* in economic simulations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Table Function Lookup Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction arrives at BLOCK referencing FN-dollars-LABEL] --> B[Read current Argument SNA value A]
    B --> C{Is A within X-min to X-max range?}
    C -- No --> D[Extrapolate using boundary segment slope]
    C -- Yes --> E[Locate bracketing pair Xi and Xi+1]
    D --> F[Compute slope m = dY by dX]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply linear interpolation formula]
    G --> H[Compute raw interpolated value Y of A]
    H --> I{Is multiplier M present in expression?}
    I -- No --> K[Return Y of A directly]
    I -- Yes --> J{Absolute value of M less than 0.001?}
    J -- Yes --> L[Force output to zero]
    J -- No --> M[Return Y of A times M]
    L --> N[Pass value to next BLOCK]
    M --> N
    K --> N
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Function Block Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FUNCBLOCK[GPSS FUNCTION Block]
        XVALS["Tabulated X-values: X1, X2, X3, ..., Xn"]
        YVALS["Tabulated Y-values: Y1, Y2, Y3, ..., Yn"]
        TYPE["Type Specifier: RN / IN / HN"]
        NPAIRS["Number of Pairs: N"]
    end
    ARGIN[Argument SNA] --> FUNCBLOCK
    FUNCBLOCK --> INT[Interpolated Y of A]
    MULT[Multiplier M] --> SCALER[Multiplier Scaler]
    INT --> SCALER
    SCALER --> ZCHECK{Absolute M less than 0.001}
    ZCHECK -- Yes --> ZERO[Output equals 0]
    ZCHECK -- No --> OUT[Output equals Y of A times M]
    ZERO --> FINAL[Final returned value]
    OUT --> FINAL
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Multiplier Roles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Table Function Multiplier Roles]
    R --> R1[Role 1: Input Domain Rescaling]
    R --> R2[Role 2: Output Scaling for Unit Conversion]
    R --> R3[Role 3: Dynamic Branch Switching via Near Zero]
    R --> R4[Role 4: Sign Inversion for Offset Effects]
    R1 --> E1[Example: Convert argument from seconds to minutes]
    R2 --> E2[Example: Scale cycle time by efficiency factor]
    R3 --> E3[Example: Disable a machine function during maintenance]
    R4 --> E4[Example: Apply negative demand in economic model]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Table Function in GPSS. Mention the three standard types and state the maximum number of coordinate pairs allowed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Table Function in GPSS is a discrete empirical model that represents the relationship between a single argument and a single result using ordered (X, Y) coordinate pairs, with automatic linear interpolation between consecutive entries. (1 mark)

The three standard types are:
1. **RN** — Real-valued argument, Real-valued result.
2. **IN** — Integer-valued argument, Integer-valued result.
3. **HN** — Half-integer argument, Half-integer result. (1.5 marks — 0.5 each)

The maximum number of coordinate pairs permitted in a single FUNCTION block is **250**. (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a Table Function Multiplier? Explain the "near-zero switching" behaviour.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Table Function Multiplier is a numeric scaling factor that is applied to the result of a Table Function at the time the function is invoked through its Standard Numerical Attribute (`FN$<label>`). (1 mark)

Its primary roles include input-domain rescaling, output-value scaling for unit conversion, and dynamic conditional switching. (1 mark)

**Near-zero switching rule:** If the absolute magnitude of the multiplier satisfies $\vert M \vert < 0.001$, the GPSS evaluator **forces the function's output to zero** irrespective of the interpolated value. This is the standard mechanism used to disable a functional branch inside a running simulation. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]*

**(a)** Define the GPSS FUNCTION statement with its general syntax. Explain the role of the **Standard Numerical Attribute (SNA)** `FN$` in invoking a Table Function. State any two restrictions imposed by GPSS on the coordinate pairs stored in a FUNCTION block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GPSS model uses the following Table Function to model *service time (minutes)* against *queue length*:

| Queue Length $X$ | Service Time $Y$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 2 |
| 2 | 5 |
| 5 | 9 |
| 8 | 12 |

A transaction arrives when the queue length is **6**. Using the linear interpolation formula, compute the service time. Also determine the final service time reported if a multiplier of **$M = 0.0008$** is applied. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

The GPSS FUNCTION statement has the general form:

$$ \text{label} \; \textbf{FUNCTION} \; A, B $$

where:
- `label` is the symbolic name of the function used as its identifier.
- **A** is an SNA — the *argument* whose current value drives the lookup.
- **B** specifies the *type* (RN/IN/HN) and the *number of coordinate pairs*. (3 marks)

The SNA `FN$<label>` is used at any point inside the model where the function's value is required. For example, in an `ADVANCE` block: `ADVANCE 5,FN$<label>` will hold the transaction for a time equal to 5 plus the function's current lookup value. (2 marks)

**Restrictions on coordinate pairs:**
1. The X-values must be **monotonically non-decreasing** ($X_{i+1} \ge X_i$).
2. The maximum number of pairs is **250**, equalling **500 numeric values** total in one block. (2 marks)

---

**(b)**

**Step 1 — Identify the bracketing pair for $A = 6$.**

From the table, $5 \le 6 \le 8$. Therefore $X_1 = 5$, $X_2 = 8$, $Y_1 = 9$, $Y_2 = 12$. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Apply the interpolation formula.**

$$ Y(6) \;=\; 9 \;+\; \dfrac{(6 - 5)(12 - 9)}{(8 - 5)} $$

$$ Y(6) \;=\; 9 \;+\; \dfrac{(1)(3)}{3} \;=\; 9 + 1 \;=\; 10 \; \text{minutes} $$

(2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for simplification)

**Step 3 — Apply the multiplier $M = 0.0008$.**

Check the switching rule:

$$ \vert M \vert \;=\; 0.0008 \;<\; 0.001 $$

Since the threshold is breached, the **near-zero switching rule** activates, and the final reported service time is **0 minutes**, regardless of the interpolated 10 minutes. (2 marks)

**Final Answer:** Interpolated service time = 10 minutes; Multiplier-applied final value = 0 minutes (function branch disabled).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** With a neat block-level architecture diagram, explain the **six operational steps** executed by GPSS when it encounters a Table Function reference (`FN$<label>`). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine's *failure rate (failures per 1000 hours)* is modelled by the Table Function:

| Operating Hours $X$ | Failure Rate $Y$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 1 |
| 1000 | 3 |
| 2000 | 6 |
| 3000 | 11 |

Compute the failure rate at **1500 hours** using linear interpolation. What multiplier should be applied so that the function returns **zero**? Justify your answer using the near-zero switching rule. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

The six operational steps (each ~1 mark with brief explanation, totalling 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the diagram):

1. **Argument Fetch** — GPSS reads the current numeric value of the argument SNA declared in the FUNCTION statement.
2. **Range Validation** — It identifies the bracketing pair $(X_i, X_{i+1})$ such that $X_i \le \text{arg} \le X_{i+1}$.
3. **Slope Computation** — The segment slope $m = \dfrac{Y_{i+1} - Y_i}{X_{i+1} - X_i}$ is evaluated.
4. **Interpolation** — The linearly-interpolated value $Y(A) = Y_i + m(A - X_i)$ is computed.
5. **Multiplier Application** — If a multiplier $M$ is attached in the referencing expression, the value is scaled: $Y_{out} = M \cdot Y(A)$.
6. **Zero Suppression Check** — If $\vert M \vert < 0.001$, the engine forces $Y_{out} = 0$ and returns; otherwise returns the scaled value.

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in §4.1 for the visual schematic.) (1 mark for the diagram)

---

**(b)**

**Step 1 — Identify bracketing pair for $A = 1500$.**

From the table, $1000 \le 1500 \le 2000$. Thus $X_1 = 1000$, $X_2 = 2000$, $Y_1 = 3$, $Y_2 = 6$. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute the interpolated failure rate.**

$$ Y(1500) \;=\; 3 \;+\; \dfrac{(1500 - 1000)(6 - 3)}{(2000 - 1000)} $$

$$ Y(1500) \;=\; 3 \;+\; \dfrac{(500)(3)}{1000} $$

$$ Y(1500) \;=\; 3 + 1.5 \;=\; 4.5 \; \text{failures per 1000 hours} $$

(3 marks — 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for simplification, 1 mark for final answer)

**Step 3 — Identify a multiplier that forces zero output.**

By the near-zero switching rule, the GPSS engine suppresses output to zero whenever the absolute multiplier magnitude is strictly less than $0.001$. Therefore, **any multiplier $M$ satisfying $\vert M \vert < 0.001$** will cause the function to return zero.

For example, $M = 0.0005$ is a valid choice. With this multiplier:

$$ \vert 0.0005 \vert = 0.0005 < 0.001 $$

The condition is satisfied, and the reported failure rate becomes **0**. (3 marks — 1 mark for stating the rule, 1 mark for the example multiplier, 1 mark for the verification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the monotonicity condition** — Many students write X-values that decrease, costing 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Misapplying the multiplier threshold** — Students often write $\vert M \vert \le 0.001$ (with equality). The strict inequality $\vert M \vert < 0.001$ is correct.
> 3. **Confusing SNA with the FUNCTION label** — `FN$<label>` is an attribute, *not* a block. The FUNCTION statement is the block that *defines* the function.
> 4. **Ignoring the 250-pair limit** — Always quote "**maximum 250 pairs**" verbatim.
> 5. **Skipping the extrapolation case** — When the argument falls outside the X-range, GPSS uses boundary-segment extrapolation, not an error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Table Function** = discrete empirical model with (X, Y) pairs + **automatic linear interpolation**.
- **Maximum pairs per FUNCTION block = 250** (i.e., 500 numeric values).
- **Three function types:** RN (Real), IN (Integer), HN (Half-integer).
- **X-values must be monotonically non-decreasing.**
- **SNA accessor:** `FN$<label>`.
- **Linear interpolation formula:** $Y(A) = Y_1 + \dfrac{(A - X_1)(Y_2 - Y_1)}{X_2 - X_1}$.
- **Table Function Multiplier** scales the lookup result at invocation time.
- **Near-zero switching rule:** If $\vert M \vert < 0.001$, output is **forced to zero**.
- **Engineering uses:** empirical mappings, queueing service times, machine cycles, demand curves, network loss models.
- **Multipliers enable:** domain rescaling, unit conversion, branch switching, sign inversion.
- **Boundary behaviour:** GPSS **extrapolates** (does not error) when argument is outside X-range, using the slope of the nearest boundary segment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causal Loop Diagramming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Causal Loop Diagramming

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** is a qualitative conceptual modelling tool used in **System Dynamics** that maps the interdependencies among the key variables of a dynamic system through causal links annotated with **polarity indicators** (`+` or `-`). Each causal link expresses how a *cause variable* influences an *effect variable*, and the polarity describes the *direction* of that influence. When links are chained to form closed paths, they generate **feedback loops**, which are classified as **Reinforcing (R)** loops — which amplify system behaviour — or **Balancing (B)** loops — which counteract and stabilise system behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OEIET724 / Module 3):**
> Causal Loop Diagramming is the **foundational qualitative modelling step** that precedes quantitative stock-and-flow modelling in any system simulation study. It is a **mandatory** preliminary analysis before building any differential-equation-based simulation model in tools such as Vensim, Stella, or AnyLogic.

### 1.2 The Building Blocks of a CLD

A CLD is constructed from three atomic elements:

| Element | Symbol Used | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable (Node)** | A labelled noun (e.g., `Population`, `Revenue`) | A measurable system quantity that can increase or decrease over time |
| **Causal Link (Arrow)** | Directed edge → | An asserted cause-and-effect relationship between two variables |
| **Polarity Mark** | `+` or `-` placed on the arrow | `+` ⇒ *same direction* of change; `-` ⇒ *opposite direction* of change |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Thermostat in Your Home

Imagine the thermostat on your wall. When the **room temperature drops**, the **heater switches on**, which causes the **room temperature to rise**, which then causes the **heater to switch off**. This entire narrative is a **closed circular chain of cause and effect** — exactly what a CLD captures. If you redraw that story using variables and arrows with polarity marks, you have just constructed a *Balancing Causal Loop*: a negative feedback loop that *resists* deviation and drives the system back toward the desired set-point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CLDs Matter in Simulation:** A CLD reveals the *feedback structure* of a system — the very mechanism that generates its dynamic behaviour (growth, decay, oscillation, overshoot, collapse). Without understanding feedback structure, no mathematical simulation model can be correctly formulated.

### 1.4 Core Conventions in CLD Notation

> [!NOTE]
> - A **positive link** `A →(+ ) B` means: *If A increases, then B increases (more than proportionally) — all else equal. Equivalently, if A decreases, B decreases.*
> - A **negative link** `A →(−) B` means: *If A increases, then B decreases (more than proportionally) — all else equal. Equivalently, if A decreases, B increases.*
> - The phrase "**all else equal**" (the *ceteris paribus* assumption) is implied on every link.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Polarity Rules — The Algebra of Causation

Given any link `A → B` with polarity `s ∈ {+1, −1}`, the rule for change interpretation is:

- If `A` *rises* (above its prior value), and the link polarity `s = +`, then `B` *rises*.
- If `A` *rises*, and the link polarity `s = −`, then `B` *falls*.
- The *opposite* effect follows when `A` falls.

**Why this matters:** Polarity is *not* a measure of magnitude. A `+` link does **not** mean "good" and a `−` link does **not** mean "bad". Polarity strictly encodes the **direction of co-movement** between two variables.

### 2.2 Loop Classification — The Core Theorem

A **feedback loop** is any closed directed cycle in a CLD in which you can start at a variable, follow the arrows, and return to the same variable. The **type** of the loop is determined by a single, deterministic rule:

$$\text{Loop Type} = \begin{cases} \textbf{Reinforcing (R)} & \text{if the number of negative } (-) \text{ links in the loop is } \textbf{EVEN} \\[4pt] \textbf{Balancing (B)} & \text{if the number of negative } (-) \text{ links in the loop is } \textbf{ODD} \end{cases}$$

**Intuition behind the rule:** Multiplying the polarities around a closed loop gives the loop's net polarity. Since `(+1) × (+1) = +1` and `(−1) × (−1) = +1`, an even count of negatives multiplies to a net `+1` (Reinforcing). An odd count of negatives multiplies to a net `−1` (Balancing).

### 2.3 Mathematical Expression of Loop Polarity

Let the loop $L$ consist of $n$ causal links with polarities $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$ where each $s_i \in \{+1, -1\}$. The **loop polarity** is:

$$P(L) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} s_i$$

Then the loop type is governed by:
