> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET724 - SYSTEM SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: CASE STUDIES — COMPREHENSIVE STUDY NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Study (Simulation Context):** A case study in system simulation is a detailed, real-world (or realistic hypothetical) investigation of a system where simulation modeling and analysis techniques are applied to study its behavior, predict performance, and support decision-making. A case study typically documents the **problem definition, model formulation, data collection, computer implementation, validation, experimentation, and interpretation of results** for a specific engineering or management system.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *OEIET724 – System Simulation*, case studies constitute the application-level module where the theoretical simulation methodology studied in Modules 1–3 is exercised on representative systems such as **queuing systems, inventory systems, manufacturing systems, and computer/communication networks**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room**
> Imagine you are the administrator of a busy hospital. You cannot physically experiment with the number of doctors on duty because lives are at stake. Instead, you build a "virtual hospital" on a computer — patients arrive randomly, doctors take varying time to treat them, ambulances arrive at intervals. By running thousands of simulated days, you discover that *adding one more doctor during peak hours reduces average waiting time by 40%*. This entire workflow — from problem to validated results — is a **Simulation Case Study**.

A case study is essentially the **"proof by example"** of simulation methodology. It demonstrates that the abstract statistical and modeling tools of simulation have **practical, monetary, and operational value** when applied to real systems.

### 1.3 Classification of Simulation Case Studies (KTU Syllabus)

| Category | Representative System | Typical Performance Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Queuing Systems** | Bank tellers, toll booths, call centers | Average wait time, queue length, server utilization |
| **Inventory Systems** | Warehouse, retail stock, supply chain | Reorder point, stockout probability, carrying cost |
| **Manufacturing Systems** | Assembly line, job shop, flexible manufacturing | Throughput, machine utilization, WIP |
| **Computer / Network Systems** | LAN, packet routers, server farms | Response time, throughput, packet loss |
| **Project / Business Systems** | R&D project, RMC plant, airline check-in | Project completion time, resource conflicts |

### 1.4 Why Case Studies are Important in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - They test the student's ability to **integrate** random-variate generation, input modeling, output analysis, and verification/validation.
> - They appear frequently in **Part B (14-mark)** questions and as full **Case-Study scenarios** in Part A.
> - Examiner marks are awarded for **structured problem formulation**, **correct use of distributions**, and **logical interpretation of simulation results**.
]<]minimax[>[</section>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Case-Study Methodology

Every case study, irrespective of the domain, follows the **unified simulation methodology** defined by Banks, Carson, Nelson & Nicol (the standard KTU-recommended textbook lineage).

### 2.1.1 The 8-Step Case-Study Procedure

1. **Problem Formulation** — Define the system's objectives, scope, and decision variables.
2. **Conceptual Model Construction** — Identify entities, attributes, resources, activities, and their logical relationships.
3. **Data Collection & Input Modeling** — Gather raw data on inter-arrival times, service times, demands, etc. Fit probability distributions using **chi-square, KS, or Anderson–Darling** tests.
4. **Computer Model Translation** — Encode the conceptual model in a simulation language/software (Arena, MATLAB, Simul8, or pseudo-code).
5. **Verification & Validation** — Verify (debugging the model) and validate (face validity, historical validation, operational validity).
6. **Pilot Runs & Experimentation** — Determine run length, warm-up period, and number of replications.
7. **Output Analysis** — Compute statistical estimates of performance measures with **confidence intervals**.
8. **Interpretation & Documentation** — Translate numerical findings into managerial/engineering recommendations.

### 2.2 Generic Queuing Case Study (M/M/1 / M/M/c)

Most KTU case studies in Module 4 are based on **single-server or multi-server queuing systems**. Let us derive the core performance metrics.

#### 2.2.1 System Assumptions

- Poisson arrivals at rate $\lambda$ (customers/hour)
- Exponential service times at rate $\mu$ (customers/hour)
- Single or multiple servers
- Infinite queue capacity, FIFO discipline
- Steady-state conditions: $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu} < 1$

#### 2.2.2 Steady-State Performance Formulas

For an **M/M/1** system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \quad \text{(Server Utilization)} \\
L_q &= \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} \quad \text{(Expected number in queue)} \\
L &= \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} \quad \text{(Expected number in system)} \\
W_q &= \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)} \quad \text{(Expected waiting time in queue)} \\
W &= \frac{1}{\mu-\lambda} \quad \text{(Expected time in system)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an **M/M/c** system with $c$ identical servers:

$$
P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}
$$

$$
L_q = \frac{(c\rho)^c \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}\, P_0
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the symbols, formulas, and unit conventions.

| # | Quantity | Formula (M/M/1) | Formula (M/M/c) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Utilization $\rho$ | $\rho = \lambda/\mu$ | $\rho = \lambda/(c\mu)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Idle probability $P_0$ | $1 - \rho$ | $\left[\sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!}+\frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!(1-\rho)}\right]^{-1}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Expected number in system $L$ | $\rho/(1-\rho)$ | $L_q + c\rho$ | customers |
| 4 | Expected number in queue $L_q$ | $\rho^2/(1-\rho)$ | $\dfrac{(c\rho)^c \rho}{c!(1-\rho)^2}\,P_0$ | customers |
| 5 | Waiting time in queue $W_q$ | $\lambda/[\mu(\mu-\lambda)]$ | $L_q/\lambda$ | hours |
| 6 | Time in system $W$ | $1/(\mu-\lambda)$ | $W_q + 1/\mu$ | hours |
| 7 | Inventory reorder point $R$ | $\mu_{DL} + z\sigma_{DL}$ | same | units |
| 8 | Newsvendor optimal service level | $(c_u - c_o)/c_u$ | — | probability |
| 9 | Confidence interval | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1}\,\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ | — | units of $\bar{X}$ |
| 10 | Required replication count | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | — | replications |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Formula Pitfall:** Do **NOT** use vertical pipes `|` inside any markdown table cell. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ for absolute value — otherwise the table breaks.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Case Studies

- **Banking** — Optimal number of teller counters and ATM machines.
- **Airports** — Number of check-in counters vs. passenger waiting time.
- **Hospitals** — Bed allocation, doctor scheduling, ambulance deployment.
- **Manufacturing** — Buffer sizing in production lines, machine-job scheduling.
- **Telecommunications** — Bandwidth allocation, packet routing, server sizing.
- **Supply Chain** — Reorder point, safety stock, warehouse capacity.
- **Transportation** — Traffic signal timing, lane configuration, fleet sizing.

### 2.5 Inventory System Case Study (Newsvendor & Continuous Review)

#### 2.5.1 (s, Q) Continuous Review System

- $s$ = reorder point
- $Q$ = order quantity
- Lead time $L$ deterministic
- Demand $D$ during lead time is **normal**: $D \sim N(\mu_{DL}, \sigma_{DL}^2)$

$$
R = \mu_{DL} + z\,\sigma_{DL}
$$

where $z$ is the standard-normal critical value for the desired **in-stock probability** (also called **service level**).

#### 2.5.2 Newsvendor Single-Period Model

$$
\text{Optimal Service Level} = \frac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}
$$

where $C_u$ = under-stocking cost per unit, $C_o$ = over-stocking cost per unit.

### 2.6 Manufacturing System Case Study (Two-Machine Flow Shop)

- Two machines in series: M1 → M2.
- Jobs arrive with inter-arrival time $A \sim U(a,b)$ and processing times $P_1, P_2$.
- **Bottleneck** = machine with the highest average utilization $\rho_i = \dfrac{E[P_i]}{E[A] + E[P_i]}$ (in single-server equivalent) or computed via simulation.
- **Performance metrics**: Average cycle time, throughput, average work-in-process (WIP), utilization of each machine.

### 2.7 Why and How Each Concept Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind case studies:** Real systems are mathematically intractable because of stochasticity, non-linearity, and complexity. Closed-form queuing theory only solves very simple systems. For everything else, **discrete-event simulation is the only practical tool** — and case studies demonstrate exactly how to apply it.
]<]minimax[>[</section>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of M/M/1 Steady-State Probabilities

The M/M/1 birth-death process has states $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ representing the number of customers in the system.

**Birth rate (arrival):** $\lambda_n = \lambda$ for $n \ge 0$
**Death rate (departure):** $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n \ge 1$ and $\mu_0 = 0$

**Step 1 — Write the birth-death balance equations.**

In steady state, flow-in = flow-out for every state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda\, P_0 &= \mu\, P_1 \\
(\lambda + \mu)\, P_1 &= \lambda\, P_0 + \mu\, P_2 \\
(\lambda + \mu)\, P_n &= \lambda\, P_{n-1} + \mu\, P_{n+1}, \quad n \ge 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the recurrence.**

From the first equation: $P_1 = (\lambda/\mu)\, P_0 = \rho\, P_0$.

Inductively, $P_n = \rho^n P_0$.

**Step 3 — Apply the normalization condition.**

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = 1
$$

Since $\rho < 1$, the geometric series sums to $1/(1-\rho)$, so:

$$
P_0 = 1 - \rho
$$

$$
P_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^n
$$

**Step 4 — Derive $L$ and $L_q$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\, P_n = (1-\rho)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,\rho^n = (1-\rho)\,\frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} \\
L_q &= L - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Little's Law $L = \lambda W$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \\
W_q &= \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the canonical M/M/1 formulas that appear in every KTU case study question.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Case Study — Bank Teller Queue (M/M/1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Customers arrive at a bank at an average rate of 10 per hour. A single teller serves them at an average rate of 15 per hour. Both inter-arrival and service times are exponentially distributed. Compute: (i) server utilization, (ii) probability of zero customers, (iii) expected number in system, (iv) expected waiting time in queue.

**Given:** $\lambda = 10$ customers/hr, $\mu = 15$ customers/hr.

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho$.**

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{10}{15} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_0$.**

$$
P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L$.**

$$
L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.6667}{0.3333} = 2.0 \text{ customers}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $L_q$.**

$$
L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \frac{(0.6667)^2}{0.3333} = \frac{0.4444}{0.3333} = 1.3333 \text{ customers}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $W_q$.**

$$
W_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{10}{15 \times 5} = \frac{10}{75} = 0.1333 \text{ hr} = 8 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $W$.**

$$
W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ hr} = 12 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Managerial Insight:** Since $\rho = 0.667$ is already high, the queue will grow rapidly with any further demand increase. The bank manager may consider adding a second teller.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Case Study — Inventory (s, Q) System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A retail store sells a product with lead time 5 days. Average daily demand = 20 units with standard deviation = 5 units. Management wants a 95 % in-stock probability. Find the reorder point.

**Step 1 — Compute lead-time demand mean.**

$$
\mu_{DL} = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute lead-time demand standard deviation.**

$$
\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{5} \times 5 = 5\sqrt{5} \approx 11.18 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Find $z$ for 95 % service level.**

From standard normal table: $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 4 — Compute reorder point $R$.**

$$
R = \mu_{DL} + z\,\sigma_{DL} = 100 + 1.645 \times 11.18 \approx 100 + 18.39 = 118.39 \text{ units}
$$

The store should reorder when stock reaches **≈ 119 units**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping a Case Study to Model Components

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **revision-ready summary** of how a generic case study maps to simulation model components. It is highly likely to appear in KTU Part-B questions.

| Real-World Element | Simulation Model Element | Variable / Attribute |
|---|---|---|
| Customer, machine, job, packet | Entity | Type, attributes |
| Teller, doctor, machine, server | Resource | Capacity, schedule |
| Inter-arrival, demand | Stochastic input | $\lambda$, $A$ |
| Service time, processing time | Stochastic activity | $S$, $P$ |
| Queue, buffer | Queue | Discipline, capacity |
| Decision rule (when to reorder) | Logic / Condition | $s$, $Q$ |
| Performance metric (e.g., waiting time) | Output statistic | $W_q$, $L$, throughput |
| Operating hours | Simulation clock & schedule | $T_{\text{end}}$ |
| Random behavior | Random-variate generator | Seed, RNG |

## 3.5 Validation Techniques Used in Case Studies

1. **Face validity** — Does the model behave as experts expect?
2. **Historical validation** — Compare simulation output to past data.
3. **Operational validation** — Compare output to real-system measurements.
4. **The Turing test** — Experts cannot distinguish real vs. simulated data.
5. **Sensitivity analysis** — How does output change with input parameters?
]<]minimax[>[</section>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Case-Study Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Real System] --> B[Problem Formulation]
    B --> C[Data Collection]
    C --> D[Input Distribution Fitting]
    D --> E[Conceptual Model]
    E --> F[Computer Model Coding]
    F --> G[Verification]
    G --> H{Validated?}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I[Pilot Runs]
    I --> J[Production Runs]
    J --> K[Statistical Output Analysis]
    K --> L[Confidence Intervals & Reports]
    L --> M[Managerial / Engineering Decision]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Queuing Case Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Arrivals - lambda] --> Q[Queue - FIFO]
    Q --> S[Server - mu]
    S --> D[Departures]
    S -.->|Utilization rho| M[Monitor]
    Q -.->|Lq metric| M
    A -.->|Inter-arrival stat| M
    M --> R[Output Reports: Wq, W, L, Lq]
```

## 4.3 Inventory (s, Q) System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Daily Demand D] --> B[Inventory Level IL]
    B --> C{IL less than s?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Place Order of Size Q]
    C -->|No| E[Wait for Next Day]
    D --> F[Lead Time L Days]
    F --> G[Order Arrives - Q units added]
    G --> B
    E --> B
    B -.->|Track| H[Performance: stockouts, holding cost]
```

## 4.4 Two-Machine Manufacturing Flow Shop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Job Arrives] --> M1[Machine 1 - P1]
    M1 --> B[Buffer Queue]
    B --> M2[Machine 2 - P2]
    M2 --> O[Completed Job]
    M1 -.->|Utilization r1| S[Statistics Collector]
    M2 -.->|Utilization r2| S
    S -.->|Throughput, WIP, Cycle Time| R[Output Report]
```

## 4.5 Verification vs. Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conceptual Model] -->|Coding| B[Computer Model]
    B -->|Verification| C{Code matches design?}
    C -->|No| D[Debug]
    D --> B
    C -->|Yes| E[Validation against Real System]
    E --> F{Output matches reality?}
    F -->|No| G[Refine Model Assumptions]
    G --> A
    F -->|Yes| H[Approved for Experimentation]
```
]<]minimax[>[</section>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a "case study" in the context of system simulation. List the main steps involved in conducting a simulation case study.**

**Model Answer:**
A case study in system simulation is the detailed application of simulation methodology to a real or hypothetical system to study its behavior and support decisions. The main steps are:

1. Problem formulation
2. Conceptual model construction
3. Data collection and input modeling
4. Computer model translation
5. Verification and validation
6. Pilot runs and experimentation design
7. Output statistical analysis
8. Interpretation and documentation of results.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly listed steps.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] In a queuing case study, what performance measures are typically estimated? Why is the utilization $\rho$ constrained to be less than 1?**

**Model Answer:**
Typical performance measures in a queuing case study include: average waiting time in queue $W_q$, average time in system $W$, average number in queue $L_q$, average number in system $L$, server utilization $\rho$, and probability of zero customers $P_0$.

The utilization $\rho < 1$ is required for **steady-state stability**. If $\rho \ge 1$, the arrival rate equals or exceeds the service rate, the queue grows without bound, $W_q \to \infty$, and the system never reaches a stationary distribution. This is reflected mathematically in the geometric-series convergence condition for the M/M/1 probability distribution.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
>
> Customers arrive at a single-server ATM kiosk at a rate of **20 per hour** following a Poisson process. Service times are **exponentially distributed** with a mean of **2 minutes**.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the system as an M/M/1 queue and determine the **server utilization, average number of customers in the system, average number in the queue, and average time a customer spends in the system.** *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** The bank is considering adding a second identical ATM. Compute the **average waiting time in the queue and the average number in the queue for the M/M/2 system.** Compare both configurations and recommend which option the bank should choose. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$\lambda = 20$ customers/hr; mean service time = 2 min = $1/30$ hr, so $\mu = 30$ customers/hr.

**Step 2 — Utilization.**

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{20}{30} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 3 — $L$.**

$$
L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.6667}{0.3333} = 2.0 \text{ customers}
$$

**Step 4 — $L_q$.**

$$
L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{(0.6667)^2}{0.3333} = 1.3333 \text{ customers}
$$

**Step 5 — $W$.**

$$
W = \frac{1}{\mu-\lambda} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ hr} = 6 \text{ minutes}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — For M/M/2, $c=2$.**

Traffic intensity per server: $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \dfrac{20}{60} = 0.3333$.

**Step 2 — Compute $P_0$.**

$$
P_0 = \left[1 + \frac{(c\rho)^1}{1!} + \frac{(c\rho)^2}{2!\,(1-\rho)}\right]^{-1}
$$

With $c\rho = 2 \times 0.3333 = 0.6667$ and $1 - \rho = 0.6667$:

$$
P_0 = \left[1 + 0.6667 + \frac{(0.6667)^2}{2 \times 0.6667}\right]^{-1} = \left[1 + 0.6667 + 0.3333\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L_q$.**

$$
L_q = \frac{(c\rho)^c \rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}\, P_0 = \frac{(0.6667)^2 \times 0.3333}{2 \times (0.6667)^2} \times 0.5
$$

$$
L_q = \frac{0.4444 \times 0.3333}{2 \times 0.4444} \times 0.5 = \frac{0.1481}{0.8889} \times 0.5 = 0.0833 \text{ customers}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $W_q$.**

$$
W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{0.0833}{20} = 0.00417 \text{ hr} \approx 15 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Recommendation.**

| Metric | M/M/1 | M/M/2 |
|---|---|---|
| $L_q$ | 1.3333 | 0.0833 |
| $W_q$ | 0.0667 hr (4 min) | 0.00417 hr (15 s) |
| $\rho$ (per server) | 0.6667 | 0.3333 |

**Recommendation:** Although the M/M/2 system involves additional capital cost, the waiting time drops from 4 minutes to 15 seconds — a **94 % reduction**. The bank should install the second ATM.

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
>
> A retail store follows a continuous-review **(s, Q) inventory policy** for a single product. The **lead time is 7 days**, **average daily demand is 30 units** with a **standard deviation of 6 units** (assumed normal), and management wants a **97 % in-stock probability**.
>
> **(a)** Determine the **reorder point** $R$ and the **expected number of units short per replenishment cycle.** *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** The store is evaluating whether to switch to a **periodic-review (R, S) policy** with a review period of 7 days and the same 97 % service level. Compute the **order-up-to level $S$** and discuss the trade-offs between (s, Q) and (R, S) policies. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Lead-time demand mean.**

$$
\mu_{DL} = 7 \times 30 = 210 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Lead-time demand standard deviation.**

$$
\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{7} \times 6 = 6\sqrt{7} \approx 15.87 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Reorder point for 97 % service level.**

$z_{0.03} = 1.88$ (from standard normal table).

$$
R = 210 + 1.88 \times 15.87 \approx 210 + 29.84 = 239.84 \approx 240 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected shortage per cycle.**

For the standard normal, with $z = 1.88$, the expected shortage function $L(z)$ is given by:

$$
L(z) = \phi(z) - z[1 - \Phi(z)]
$$

where $\phi$ is the PDF and $\Phi$ the CDF. With $z = 1.88$:

$\phi(1.88) \approx 0.0681$, $\Phi(1.88) \approx 0.9699$.

$$
L(1.88) = 0.0681 - 1.88 \times (1 - 0.9699) = 0.0681 - 1.88 \times 0.0301 = 0.0681 - 0.0566 = 0.0115
$$

$$
E[\text{Shortage per cycle}] = L(z) \times \sigma_{DL} = 0.0115 \times 15.87 \approx 0.183 \text{ units}
$$

So the store is short by approximately **0.18 units per cycle on average**, which is consistent with the 97 % service level.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Order-up-to level $S$.**

For an (R, S) policy with review period $T = 7$ days and lead time $L = 7$ days, the **protection interval is $T + L = 14$ days**.

$$
\mu_{DL}^{R+S} = 14 \times 30 = 420 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\sigma_{DL}^{R+S} = \sqrt{14} \times 6 = 6\sqrt{14} \approx 22.45 \text{ units}
$$

$$
S = \mu_{DL}^{R+S} + z\,\sigma_{DL}^{R+S} = 420 + 1.88 \times 22.45 \approx 420 + 42.21 = 462.21 \approx 463 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Trade-off discussion.**

| Aspect | (s, Q) Continuous Review | (R, S) Periodic Review |
|---|---|---|
| Order frequency | Variable, whenever stock falls to $s$ | Fixed (every $T$ days) |
| Average inventory | Lower | Higher (because of longer protection interval) |
| Ordering cost | Lower (smaller, more frequent orders) | Higher (larger, less frequent orders) |
| Stockout risk | Lower | Higher |
| Administrative effort | High (continuous monitoring) | Low (periodic review) |
| Suitability | High-value, fast-moving items | Low-value, slow-moving items |

**Recommendation:** The store should keep the (s, Q) policy if the item is high-value or fast-moving, because it maintains lower average inventory. The (R, S) policy is preferable for low-value, slow-moving items where ordering and administrative simplicity matter more.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Case-Study Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units.** Mean service time of 2 minutes must be converted to $\mu = 30$/hr before plugging into M/M/1 formulas. Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Mixing up $\rho$ and $c\rho$ in M/M/c.** The utilization per server is $\rho = \lambda/(c\mu)$, but the term inside the factorial expansion is $c\rho$, not $\rho$. This is a **favourite examiner trap**.
> 3. **Not stating the steady-state condition.** Always explicitly state $\rho < 1$ (or $\lambda < c\mu$) before quoting any M/M/1 or M/M/c formula. **−1 mark** if missing.
> 4. **Skipping the managerial interpretation.** Case-study questions explicitly test whether the student can translate numbers into decisions. Always end with a one-line recommendation.
> 5. **Wrong service-level table lookup.** For 97 %, use $z = 1.88$ (not 1.96, which is 95 %). For 99 %, use $z = 2.33$. Mixing these up costs **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Not mentioning verification vs. validation.** A complete case study answer must distinguish these two terms.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 Case Studies**

- ✅ A **case study** = real-system application of simulation methodology (8-step procedure).
- ✅ Most KTU case studies are **queuing or inventory** systems.
- ✅ **M/M/1 formulas**: $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $W = 1/(\mu-\lambda)$, $W_q = \lambda/[\mu(\mu-\lambda)]$.
- ✅ **Steady-state condition**: $\rho < 1$ (or $\lambda < c\mu$ for M/M/c) — always state explicitly.
- ✅ **M/M/c utilization per server** is $\rho = \lambda/(c\mu)$; the term in factorials is $c\rho$.
- ✅ **Inventory reorder point**: $R = \mu_{DL} + z\sigma_{DL}$; $\mu_{DL} = L \cdot \mu_D$, $\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{L} \cdot \sigma_D$.
- ✅ **Newsvendor optimal service level** = $C_u/(C_u + C_o)$.
- ✅ **Service level → z-value**: 90 % → 1.28; 95 % → 1.645; 97 % → 1.88; 99 % → 2.33.
- ✅ **Little's Law**: $L = \lambda W$ — universal for any queue.
- ✅ **Verification** = building the model correctly; **Validation** = building the correct model.
- ✅ **Confidence interval**: $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1} \cdot S/\sqrt{n}$ for finite replications.
- ✅ **Required replication count**: $n = (z_{\alpha/2} \sigma / E)^2$.
- ✅ Always end a case study answer with a **managerial/engineering recommendation**.
- ✅ **Validation techniques**: face validity, historical, operational, Turing test, sensitivity analysis.
- ✅ **Input-modeling distributions commonly used**: exponential, normal, uniform, Poisson, Weibull, lognormal.
